Appian Interview Experience
About the Company:
Appian Corp (Appian) is an enterprise application platform. The company’s main activities
include delivering solutions for process orchestration, automation, and intelligence. Its
software allows users to build task forms, process solutions, and dashboards by considering
the business policies, models, and procedures and enables users to monitor and respond to
instantaneous critical events. The company works majorly on low code development.
Role: Software Development Engineer
Package: 25.5 LPA
Location: Chennai
Selection Process:
Online Test: 1 hr
Coding Questions : 3
The test was conducted on an Glider AI platform.
Question 1: Generating the Next Unique Booking Reference Code
Input:
curr = sequence of integers representing the unique booking reference
Output:
next = next greater ordered arrangement
Example:
Input: 1, 2, 3 Output: 1, 3, 2
Question 2: Mall Treasure Hunt
1. Online Test
2. Technical Round - 1
3. HR Round - 1
4. HR Round - 2
You are working for an airline reservation system.
Each booking gets a unique reference number that is a permutation of digits.
The system must generate the next greater ordered arrangement each time.
If the current code is the last possible arrangement, reset to the smallest order.
A shopping mall is divided into a grid of rooms.
Rooms can be:
'S' = start (entrance)
'O' = open/accessible
'X' = blocked
'T' = treasure chest
Task:
Find the shortest path (minimum steps) from 'S' to any 'T'.
Example:
Input:
[
['S','O','O','O','O'],
['X','O','X','X','O'],
['X','O','T','X','X'],
['O','O','O','O','O']
]
Output: 5
Question 3: Reorganize Queue with Multiple Team Assignment
Input:
Output:
Reorganized list with team names and/or students.
Example:
`Input: Teams = { "T1": ["Bob", "Carol", "Dave"], "T2": ["Alice", "Eve"], "T3": ["Frank",
"Grace"], "T4": ["Heidi", "Ivan", "Judy", "Eve"] } Queue = ["Alice", "Bob", "Ivan", "Frank"]
You are given a list of students in a queue and a mapping of project teams.
Reorganize the queue such that:
1. Students from the same team are grouped together.
2. If all members are present, replace them with the team name.
3. Students not in any team remain as is.
4. A student in multiple groups can only be claimed by one (the team with earliest
occurrence wins).
1. List of names in queue order
2. Mapping of students to project teams
Output: ["Alice", "Bob", "Ivan", "Frank"]`
Technical Round - 1: 1 hr 30 mins
This round was primarily focused on Data Structures & Algorithms, Projects and Resume
based questions.
The interviewer started with a short intro about himself. Then I gave my intro and I had
mentioned my passion for "Astrophysics". The interviewer seemed interested in
Astrophysics too. So he asked questions from that. He asked about the recent trends in the
field of space for which I mentioned about the different countries of the world trying to prove
that America hasn't reached space yet. (Regarding the landing on the moon)
After this, the interviewer went to the DSA part of the interview. The DSA question asked
was:
Given an array of  intervals  where intervals[i] = any integer, merge all overlapping
intervals, and return _an array of the non-overlapping intervals in the form of {start,end} that
cover all the intervals in the input.
Example:
intervals = [3,6,2,5,8,1,9,11]
output = [{1,3},{5,6},{8,9},{11}]
My approach to the problem: I proposed an approach of sorting the array and then checking
whether there exists an overlap and in case of that add it to the existent output variable.
The approach had a time complexity of O(n log n). The interviewer helped me with an
approach of using a stack. However, I pointed out an edge case with a time complexity of
O(n^2) in that and so he proceeded with questions from my resume. It was very much
shocking that Appian had only one DSA question for the technical round.
After the DSA questions, the questions were based on my intern experience at Amazon. I
was asked about the task I did in Amazon and also how I performed in the team and about
my experience at Amazon. After this, the interviewer moved to the tech stack used in
Amazon and how I made use of Artificial Intelligence in my intern days.
Project based Questions:
I was asked which was the project in the resume I had worked on and is considered my
personal favorite. I chose my Railway DBMS project. I explained what inspired me to do it
and the things I tried to do in the project. After which I was asked about the ER structure of
the project and the tech stack used. Also the interviewer suggested few more things I could
add on to the existing project.
Finally he asked for any questions to him and I asked few questions about work-life balance
and career growth at Appian.
HR Round - 1: 15 minutes
The questions were very general
HR Round - 2: 15 minutes
This round was conducted via online mode at CUIC. Started with an introduction.
Questions asked:
General Tips:
Finally, don't feel disheartened if you get rejected by a company. You still got lot options
coming. Put your 100% effort and stay consistent. Your effort would guide you to your
company. My first rejection was at Appian and now I am placed at Appian.
Aashin A P (2022506072)
Ex-Amazon SDE Intern, Placed at Appian.
MIT IT 2026
Reach out @ [https://www.linkedin.com/in/aashin-a-p-21jan2005/](LinkedIn | Aashin-A-P)
Gmail : apaashin@gmail.com
1. What do you know about Appian?
2. What is the meaning of your name?
3. What is your thought about Artificial Intelligence?
4. What AI tools have you used?
5. Regarding school and college CGPA and rankings?
6. What will you choose : Appian or Amazon?
1. What was implemented in the Railway DBMS project?
2. How was the large amount of data was stored for the railway project?
3. Amazon or Appian - Choice?
1. Be consistent and stay positive.
2. Be clear in DSA concepts and other core concepts.
3. Practice DSA questions in Leetcode and HackerRank.
4. Have a good resume with clear understanding of all the terms you have put into it.
5. The way you present your approach matters -> Start with the brute force and then
optimise the solution and accept feedbacks from the interviewer.

Summer Internship Interview Experience – American Express

 Guharaj T G M

2023115093

About The Company

American Express (Amex) is an American bank holding company and a global financial
services corporation specializing in payment cards. It leverages advanced data
analytics, cloud infrastructure, secure APIs, and machine learning to deliver
personalized experiences. A hybrid cloud environment ensures secure, seamless, and
efficient operations. Amex invests heavily in fraud prevention, regulatory compliance,
and customer engagement, making it a leader in the digital finance space.

Position: Technology Intern/Apprentice
No. of Applicants: 472
Students Selected for Interview: 16
Students Selected for Internship: 4

Out of 472 students who applied for the internship at American Express, only 16 were
shortlisted based on their performance in an Online Coding Round. I was fortunate to
be one among them. The interview process then comprised two rounds – a Technical
Round and an HR Round, both conducted online.

Online Coding Round

1.  Largest Subarray Sum with Equal Ends – You are given an array of positive

integers. Find a subarray (at least 2 elements) where the first and last elements
are the same. Among all such subarrays, return the maximum possible sum.

2.  Trash Collection Time – There are N houses on a street, each storing bags of

trash: Plastic (P), Glass (G), and Metal (M). Three trucks (one per trash type) start
from the beginning, collect their respective bags, and return. Travel between
houses takes given times, and loading each bag takes 1 minute. Find the
minimum time needed until all trucks finish.

 3.  Assassin Sneak on Guarded Board – You have an N×M board. Each cell may be:
empty ., obstacle X, a guard facing <, >, ^, or v (seeing straight until blocked), or
an assassin A. The assassin can move up, down, left, or right on empty cells.
Determine if the assassin can reach the bottom-right cell without being seen by
any guard.

Technical Round

The technical interview began with my self-introduction. The session mainly focused on
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) concepts and Data Structures & Algorithms (DSA).

Key Technical Questions Asked:

•  What is a constructor and destructor?

•  What is the use of the scope resolution operator?

•  What is the use of the this operator?

•  Explain the diamond problem. How is it overcome in Java and C++?

•  What is the volatile keyword?

•  Difference between malloc and new.

•  What does malloc return?

•  Use of the delete keyword.

•  Types of inheritance.

•  Operators that cannot be overloaded.

•  Types of constructors. What is a copy constructor?

•  Types of polymorphism.

•  Static vs. Dynamic binding.

•  Use of static keyword.

•  Call by value vs. Call by reference.

•  What is a friend class and friend function?

•  Function overloading vs. Operator overloading.

•  What is an abstract class?

•  What is a virtual function? Difference between virtual and pure virtual function.

 •  Can we instantiate an abstract class?

•  Can destructors be overloaded?

•  What are access modifiers?

Coding Questions:

1.  Array Problem: Given an array, return another array where each index contains

the count of elements lesser than the element at that index.

2.  String Problem: Find the first non-repeating character in a string.

I was asked to write the solutions in Notepad and share my screen.

Outcome: Out of 16 candidates, 10 were shortlisted for the HR round, including myself.

HR Round

The HR round was conducted by a Director of Software Development at American
Express. The environment was friendly, and the questions were mostly resume-based
and situational.

Key HR Questions:

•  Explanation of my Machine Learning project (Hate Speech Detection) and

justification for the chosen model.

•  Why I used Bootstrap in my e-commerce frontend project and how I made the

website responsive.

•  Questions related to my awards and justification of soft skills like leadership,

time management, and team management.

Situational Questions:

1.  If you are on the way to catch a flight for an interview, but due to traffic, you can

either break rules and reach on time or miss the flight, what will you do?

2.  In a team project, if there is a conflict where two innovative solutions are
presented, both equally good, which solution will you choose and why?

Personal Questions:

•  Asked about my 12th-grade scores and whether I regretted losing marks.

•  Resume-oriented discussions on achievements, skills, and experiences.

 Candidate’s Question to HR:
I asked, “What skills should I develop to cope with the AMEX environment and
contribute effectively to the team?”

The HR responded:
“What I’m about to say isn’t just for AMEX—it applies to every company. Always question
everything. If you are asked to do a task, first ask why it is needed. Questioning is not a
sign of doubt, it’s a sign of clarity and ownership. This mindset is essential anywhere you
work.”

Outcome: Out of 10 candidates, only 4 were selected for the internship. I was privileged
to be among them.

The internship interview at American Express was a highly valuable experience that
tested both my technical knowledge (OOPs, DSA, Coding) and personal attributes
(decision-making, teamwork, adaptability, and communication skills). Being selected
among 4 out of 472 applicants was an achievement that boosted my confidence and
reinforced my passion for learning and contributing to challenging environments.

Tips for Students

I started my internship preparation during my 4th semester holidays, but I strongly
recommend you to start right from the 4th semester itself. Early preparation helps you
build consistency and reduces stress later.

Here are some key takeaways from my experience that might help future aspirants:

1.  Strengthen Your Fundamentals – OOP, DSA, DBMS, and core programming
concepts are always tested. Don’t just memorize; understand them deeply.

2.  Follow a Structured Plan + Practice Consistently – I used Striver’s A–Z DSA

Sheet for a systematic approach and solved problems regularly on LeetCode to
get hands-on practice.

3.  Solve at Least One Problem Daily – Consistency matters. Even one problem

every day improves your problem-solving skills over time.

4.  Be Comfortable Explaining Your Code – Interviews often involve live coding.

Practice explaining your thought process while solving problems.

5.  Know Your Resume Inside-Out – Projects, skills, and achievements you
mention will definitely be asked. Be prepared to justify your choices.

 6.  Prepare for Project-Related Questions – Even if you did the project yourself,
you should be ready to answer any question about it. Anticipate questions and
prepare in advance.

7.  Work on Soft Skills – Communication, teamwork, and decision-making are

equally important in HR rounds.

8.  Prepare for Situational Questions – Think logically and ethically when

answering real-life scenarios.

9.  Ask Good Questions – Show curiosity about the company’s work culture and

values. It leaves a strong impression.

10. Stay Calm and Confident – Even if you don’t know an answer, explain your

approach. Confidence matters as much as correctness.

11. Learn to Question – As the HR said, questioning isn’t negativity; it’s about clarity,

ownership, and professional growth.

12. Take Every Interview as a Learning Opportunity – Whether selected or not,

each experience adds value to your journey.

Feel free to reach out anytime on LinkedIn :)

Guharaj Muralitharan

My SAP Labs Interview Experience  
Total Rounds: 5 
Date: 18th August 2025 
Hi everyone! I wanted to share my SAP Labs interview journey because when I was 
preparing, I used to read these kinds of experiences and they helped me a lot. So, here’s my 
detailed story — from the online test to the HR round. Hopefully, it gives you some idea of 
what to expect and how to prepare. 😊 
 
🖥️ Round 1: Online Assessment (60 mins) 
The very first hurdle was the online coding test, which was conducted in a virtual mode at 
CUIC. We had to solve two coding questions in one hour. 
🔹 Question 1: Bank Transfers Simulation 
We were given: 
 
A string R and an array V. 
 
If R[i] = 'A', transfer V[i] amount from Bank A to Bank B. 
 
If R[i] = 'B', transfer V[i] amount from Bank B to Bank A. 
The task was to find the final balance of both banks after all transactions. 
Constraints were huge (N up to 100,000), so efficiency was key. 
🔹 Question 2: Balancing the Board 
We had two strings (row1, row2) representing a board: 
 
Cells contained 'R', 'W', or '?'. 
 
'?' could be replaced with 'R' or 'W'. 
We had to make the board “balanced” with the minimum replacements. If impossible, 
return "not possible", else return the count of replacements. 
This round tested both logic and optimization skills. After completing it, I felt quite 
confident and fortunately, I cleared it. 
 
 
 
 
⚙️ Round 2: Technical Interview – 1 
From hundreds of students, 90 were shortlisted. 
This round started casually with: 
 
Self-introduction 
 
A few questions about my internship experience 
After this warm-up, we quickly moved to problem-solving. 
💻 SQL Questions I faced: 
1. From the Employees table, find the top-most manager of a given employee. 
2. From Orders and Restaurants tables, find the Top 5 restaurants with the 
maximum orders in the last 7 days. 
This round was a mix of SQL + clarity of thought. I cleared it and was super excited to move 
forward. 
 
⚡ Round 3: Technical Interview – 2 
Now things got serious. Out of 90, only 30 candidates moved ahead, and this round was the 
most grilling one. 
I was interviewed by two experienced professionals who really tested me. It started with 
another introduction (yes, again 😅), and then they literally went line by line through my 
resume. 
Here’s what happened: 
 
I had to explain my projects in detail — not just what I built, but also how I built 
them and why I chose certain approaches. 
 
Then came the conceptual questions: 
o Difference between a process and a task. 
o Internal working of compiler vs interpreter. 
 
One coding task: Implement a basic sorting algorithm on the spot. 
 
Some deep Operating Systems concepts: mutex, critical section, producer-consumer 
problem — and I had to explain them with real-life examples (for example, producer-
consumer like “chef cooking food and waiter serving customers”). 
This round went on for almost 1 hour 30 minutes. Honestly, it was tough, but I stayed calm 
and answered with examples whenever possible. Thankfully, I got through! 🙌 
 
🧑💼 Round 4: Managerial Round 
Now only 15 candidates were left. This round was more about my personality and 
behavior rather than coding. 
The panel was quite friendly (and luckily, one of them had already interviewed me in a 
previous round, so I felt comfortable). 
They asked: 
 
Why do you want to join SAP? 
 
Where do you see yourself in 5 years? 
 
What are your strengths and weaknesses? 
 
How are you working on improving your weaknesses? 
This was more like a conversation than an interview. It went on for about 40 minutes and I 
came out smiling. 
 
👥 Round 5: HR Round 
Finally, 13 candidates were shortlisted for this stage. 
This was the most relaxed and informal round. It started with some storytelling questions 
like: 
 
How did I choose MIT? 
 
My college journey so far. 
After that, the HR explained the compensation details, the internship structure, and the job 
location. I also got a chance to ask my own questions about the role and internship. 
It ended smoothly, and after about 15 minutes of waiting… the results were out! 
🎉 Out of 13, 10 were selected. I was thrilled to be the 4th person on the list. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
💡 Suggestions 
 
Resume is everything – Every line will be questioned. Be honest and well-
prepared. Don’t put something unless you are ready to defend it. 
 
Simple questions matter – They’re not checking if you know “rocket science”. They 
want to see if you understand basics and can explain them clearly. 
 
Use real-life examples – Whenever possible, connect your answers to real-world 
scenarios. It shows you really understand the concept. 
 
Communication is key – Speak confidently but don’t bluff. If you don’t know, it’s 
okay to admit and move on. 
 
📌 My Details 
Name: Anselm Flavian P 
Roll No: 2022506101 
Batch: 2026 
Department: Information Technology 
College: Madras Institute of Technology (Anna University) 
LinkedIn: linkedin.com/in/anselm1305 
 
✨ That was my SAP Labs journey! If this experience helps you and you get placed at SAP, 
do remember to thank me later 😉

# **Appian Interview Experience**

**Product Engineer (On-Campus Placement)**– *Rakshitha T*

## **About Appian:**

**![Image](data:image/jpeg;base64...)**

Appian is a global enterprise software company that provides a **low-code automation platform** to help organizations design, automate, and optimize business processes.

The platform integrates **workflow automation, data fabric, AI, and case management**, enabling companies to rapidly build scalable enterprise applications.

# **Selection Process**

### **1️ Technical Online Test**

### **2️ Technical Interview – Round 1**

### **3️ Managerial / Leadership Round (US Head) – Round 2**

### **4️ HR Round – Round 3**

All three interview rounds were conducted **on the same day** after shortlisting from the online test.

# **Technical Online Test**

**Focus: DSA (3–4 Coding Questions)**

The online assessment was purely technical and DSA-focused.

### **Topics Covered:**

* Arrays
* Strings
* Recursion
* Sorting
* Hashing
* Time & Space Complexity

The questions were easy - medium level and required clear logic building.

Strategy that worked for me:

* Started with brute-force approach
* Clearly optimized step-by-step
* Paid attention to edge cases
* Managed time effectively

Shortlisted candidates moved to interviews.

# **Round 1: Technical Interview (Core + Resume + OOPS)**

**Duration: ~40–45 minutes**

This round was conducted offline.

### **🔹 Part 1 – Resume Deep Dive (First 20 Minutes)**

The interviewer spent significant time discussing my resume:

* Detailed explanation of my projects
* Tech stack used
* Architecture decisions
* Challenges faced
* My leadership role as **President of IEEE WIE**
* Research wing initiatives under IEEE

They were particularly impressed with:

* My research initiatives
* My club leadership experience
* How I balanced technical and organizational responsibilities

They evaluated:

* Depth of understanding
* Ownership
* Communication clarity
* Real contribution vs superficial involvement

### **🔹 Part 2 – OOPS (Python Focus) (Next 20 Minutes)**

They asked me which language I was comfortable with. I chose **Python**.

Interestingly:

* They already had a **printed sheet of OOPS questions in Python**.
* I was asked to **write code on paper** in front of them.
* It was a very basic fill in the blanks type of question. There were pre-defined Classes and I was asked to write functions for those classes.

### **OOPS Topics Covered:**

* Classes and Objects
* Inheritance
* Polymorphism
* Encapsulation
* Constructors
* Method Overriding
* Practical implementation scenarios

They checked:

* Concept clarity
* Syntax awareness
* Logical structuring
* Clean code thinking

This round tested **fundamentals more than complexity**.

# **Round 2: Managerial / Leadership Round**

**(Google Meet with US Head – Conducted at CUIC)**

This round was more conversational.

Questions asked:

* Why do you want to join Appian?
* What are your future career plans?
* Do you have any other offers?
* What motivates you?

I clearly explained:

* My interest in product-building
* My long-term goal of becoming a **Product Manager**
* How Product Engineering at Appian gives strong product exposure

They appreciated:

* Clarity of vision
* Honest communication
* Long-term alignment

# **Round 3: HR Round**

This round focused on:

* Role explanation
* Offer intention confirmation

I mentioned that:

I aim to transition into Product Management in the future, and starting as a Product Engineer at Appian is the perfect path to build strong technical-product foundations.

The HR appreciated the clarity and ambition.

# **My USP (What Made Me Stand Out)**

**Student Business Card with QR Code**

I had a personal student business card that included:

* My name & role
* A QR code linking directly to my resume

![](data:image/png;base64...)

When they scanned it and saw the resume instantly, it created a strong first impression.

It reflected:

* Professionalism
* Branding mindset
* Attention to detail
* Product thinking

This small detail left a memorable impact.

# **Key Takeaways from My Experience**

### **1️ Be Very Clear About Your Resume**

They can question anything mentioned.
Never bluff.

### **2️ Fundamentals > Fancy Knowledge**

OOPS clarity mattered more than advanced frameworks.

### **3️ Leadership Matters**

My role as IEEE WIE President and research initiatives added strong weight.

### **4️ Career Clarity Impresses**

Having a long-term goal (PM path) showed maturity.

### **5️ Personal Branding Helps**

Something as simple as a QR resume card can differentiate you.

# **Preparation Strategy**

### **DSA:**

* Solved topic-wise problems
* Practiced [neetcode.io](http://neetcode.io) 150 questions
* Focused on explaining approach verbally

### **OOPS:**

* Practiced implementing concepts in Python
* Reviewed inheritance, overriding, polymorphism deeply

### **Resume:**

* Prepared explanations for every single bullet point
* Prepared impact statements for each project
* Don't put anything on your resume that you can't vouch for or explain.

# **Final Advice**

* Start preparation early
* Master fundamentals
* Build leadership exposure
* Be authentic
* Think long-term
* Add small differentiators

Preparation + Clarity + Personal Branding = Strong Impact

I hope this helps you crack your next interview.
Feel free to reach out for any queries!

**Naveen O.T**
**Product Management Intern – Appian**

**About the Company:**

Appian Corporation is a US-based enterprise software company founded in 1999, best known for its low-code automation platform that helps businesses quickly build applications, automate workflows, and integrate data across systems. Headquartered in McLean, Virginia, with global offices including India, Appian combines process orchestration, AI, robotic process automation, and process mining into a single platform, enabling organizations to improve efficiency, reduce costs, and modernize operations.

**About the Role:**

A Product Manager (PM) is responsible for guiding a product through its entire lifecycle, from idea to launch and beyond. They define the vision and strategy of the product based on market research, user feedback, and business goals. PMs prioritize features, create and maintain the product roadmap, and work with cross-functional teams (engineering, design, marketing, sales) to ensure that what gets built delivers value. After launch, they analyze metrics, gather user feedback, and iterate to continuously improve the product.

**The Selection Process:**

**i) Online Round (via Bolt platform)**
The online round lasted around 45 minutes and consisted of system design and behavioural questions.
Examples:

* What is your favourite app? What feature do you like the most and why?
* According to you, what does a day in the life of a PM look like?
* How would you solve team conflicts?

Some questions also required recording and uploading a short video. The round was straightforward and simple.

**ii) In-Person Interview @ CUIC (3 rounds)**

**Round 1 – Technical**

* Focused mainly on system design questions.
* I was first asked to introduce myself, then given a scenario to incorporate multilingual support into an app.
* I had to explain the process—from collecting metrics to designing the feature. The interviewer tested my thought process, articulation, and ability to consider diverse approaches rather than expecting a “right” answer.
* I was also asked about my favourite app and related questions.
* **Tip:** Always ask unique and thoughtful questions at the end of the round.

**Round 2 – HR**

* Lasted 15–20 minutes.
* Included self-introduction and behavioral questions such as:
  + What are your plans for the future?
  + Are you interested in higher studies?
  + What do you think a PM does?

**Round 3 – HR Wrap-up**

* It was a very short and lasted less than 10 minutes.
* The interviewer explained the role and asked a few questions about my background.

**Preparation Approach:**

Since PM internships are rare during placements, I focused on:

* Practicing basic and commonly asked questions.
* Learning to explain scenarios using the **STAR method**.
* Understanding PM metrics, feature analysis, and the design process.
* Practicing mock questions and interviews to build confidence and improve articulation.

The interview process primarily tested communication skills, structured thinking, and openness to learning. There weren’t strict “right” answers—what mattered was clarity, reasoning, and confidence.

**Tip:** Be open, ask questions if you get stuck, and articulate your thoughts clearly.

✨ **ALL THE BEST!!!**

Name: Prithvi S K K
Roll No: 2023115090
Mobile: 7448450523
Mail: prithviskk02@gmail.com

Appian Corporation is an American cloud computing and enterprise software company
headquartered in McLean, Virginia, part of the Dulles Technology Corridor. The company
sells a platform as a service (PaaS) for building enterprise software applications. It is focused
on low-code development, process mining, business process management, and case
management markets in North America, Europe, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia.
Appian offers a low code platform with a visual interface and pre-built development modules.
As with other low-code platforms, it enables businesses to create apps and automations using
little or no code.

There are founds rounds of interview

1.  Coding round
2.  Technical round
3.  Techno - Behavioural round
4.   HR round

Role: Software Engineer

 Coding Round:

Candidates were sent a link to attend the online coding round, which was held on the Glider
platform. We had to solve three questions in 75 mins.

Question 1: Given a sorted array and a value x, find the first and last occurrence of value x in
the array and return them. If x is not found in the array, return {-1, -1}.

 Link : https://leetcode.com/problems/find-first-and-last-position-of-element-in-sorted-
array/description/

This is a well-known binary search problem.

Question 2: Given a sorted array which contains non – overlapping intervals and a new
interval which need to be inserted into the appropriate position and return them . The answer
should also need to be in sorted order, and there should not be any overlapping intervals .

Link : https://leetcode.com/problems/insert-interval/description/

 Since the array is sorted, we can solve this question using a greedy approach

Question 3: Given a 1-D array which contains numbers from 1 to n where n is size of the
array. It must contain a duplicate number and a missing number. A 2-D array in the size of M
* 2 will be given, where M is the number of rows in the 2-D array and this 2-D array remains
the same for every test case. Find the duplicate and missing number from the 1-D array and
check whether both of them occur as a pair in the 2-D array.

 If the pair occurs more than 1 time, return the pair with the string as “Multiple teams found”
Else if the pair occurs only 1 time, then return the pair with the string “A Team found”

Else return {-1 , -1 , “No team found”}

 Link : https://leetcode.com/problems/set-mismatch/description/

This problem involves finding the duplicate and missing number .

I was able to solve all the problems with an optimal solution. Even if you are finding it
difficult to find the optimal solution try brute force method, as submitting the test before time
gives you an edge over  others .  42 of us were shortlisted for the interview process.

Technical Round:

When I walked into the interview room, I’ll admit I was a bit anxious at first. Thankfully, the
interviewer’s friendly demeanour helped me relax. We began with an introduction, after
which he moved on to questions about the skills listed on my resume — and he went deep
into the fundamentals.

Some of the questions I remember included:

  Distributed Systems: What is consistency? What do you think about the consistency in

a distributed environment and type of consistency that MongoDB uses?

  Databases: Difference between SQL and NoSQL, and ACID vs BASE properties.
  Web Technologies: Why HTML replaced XML?


JSON: How would an image be sent in JSON format? If it were sent as a string, how
would you escape double quotes inside the string, and what is that process called?
  Networking: What’s the average latency allowed between a client request and server

response?

  HTTP: Difference between GET and POST; how would an HTTP request look?
  Security: What is JWT and how does it work internally? What are  vulnerabilities?


JavaScript: Difference between setTimeout() and setInterval().How do you limit the
number of times a setInterval() is executed?
I was also asked to walk through the end-to-end flow of how a website works, right from the
user’s request to the server’s response. including security aspects.



Then came the only DSA question:

"If there are crores of numbers in a stream where the range is [1,100], and only one number
is not duplicated while the rest are duplicated multiple times, how would you find it
optimally?"

I first suggested a frequency map (hash map) solution — O(n) time, O(n) space. He asked for
further optimization. I proposed counting occurrences directly using an array/bitset, and since
only one number is non-duplicated, we could maintain a counter — once we’ve seen
duplicates for 99 numbers, the remaining one is the answer. This avoids extra space and
works efficiently for the given range. He seemed satisfied with this approach.

 Solution I suggested :

int count[101] = {0};
int duplicatesFound = 0;
for (number in stream) {
    count[number]++;
    if (count[number]==2){
        duplicatesFound++;
        if (duplicatesFound==99){
            for (int i = 1; i <= 100; i++) {
                if (count[i] == 1) {
                    return i;
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

This round lasted an hour and felt intense but rewarding.

Technical Round II:

This was more of a technical + behavioural conversation. This round was virtual .It started
with my introduction and the interviewer inquired about my area of interest to which I
answered “Web Development"  and he asked some questions related to it , some of the
question  I could recall:

  Why is Next.js preferred over React?
  Difference between var, let, and const in JavaScript.
  Difference between Express.js and Node.js.
  Difference between TCP and UDP
  What are Web Sockets? What is the protocol that uses Web Sockets?

I was also asked “Why Appian?”   to which I was able to answer confidently.

We also had some general web development discussions. The round lasted for about 15
minutes.

HR Round:

This round was casual and focused on my background, interests and some general ideas.The
round lasted for about 10 minutes.

Finally the results were published around 9pm and I was fortunate to be one of them . 12 of
us (8 from CEG and 4 from MIT were selected).

Bonus Tip:

At the end of each round, I was given the chance to ask my own questions. This is a great
opportunity — prepare smart, interesting questions that can leave a lasting impression.

 My Preparation and strategies:



It’s never too late to begin your DSA journey. Consistency matters more than speed
— solving even one or two problems a day is enough if done regularly.

  Strengthen your core fundamentals in OS, DBMS, and Computer Networks.

Platforms like GeeksforGeeks and InterviewBit are great resources to revise and
practice these concepts.

  For structured DSA preparation, I recommend working through Striver’s A2Z Sheet.
If you enjoy reading in depth, explore “Beyond Cracking the Coding Interview” or
curated content from interviewing.io.

  While solving problems, focus on recognizing patterns — most questions revolve

around recurring approaches once you start identifying them.

  Be thorough with your resume. Every single word on it can lead to a question in the

interview. Be transparent, and communicate your thoughts with confidence.

                                                    Kaviya G (2022115106)

About the Company – Aptiv

Aptiv is a global technology company focused on making vehicles safer, greener,
and more connected. It operates primarily in the automotive and mobility domain,
developing advanced solutions for modern and future vehicles.

Aptiv operates across two major business segments:

  Advanced Safety and User Experience: Advanced Driver Assistance Systems

(ADAS), autonomous driving support, and in-vehicle infotainment and
connectivity solutions.

  Smart Vehicle Architecture and Electrical Systems: wiring systems, high-

voltage and low-voltage architectures, and vehicle connectivity platforms.

The company plays a key role in enabling next-generation, software-defined vehicles
by combining software, hardware, and data-driven technologies. Aptiv works closely
with leading OEMs (Original Equipment Manufacturers) worldwide to design
scalable, reliable, and future-ready vehicle platforms.

Aptiv has a strong global presence, operating in nearly 50 countries with
manufacturing and engineering centers across North America, Europe, Asia, and
South America, including India. The company employs around 190,000 professionals
globally, with a significant focus on engineering, innovation, and research.

Preparation Strategy for Aptiv

Coding:
• Focus on basic coding questions rather than very complex problems. Practice
consistently on platforms like LeetCode and HackerRank. Prepare bit manipulation
topics.

Core CS Fundamentals:
• Have a strong conceptual understanding of OS, OOPS, DBMS, and CN.
Interviewers mainly test concept clarity, not definitions.

Resume:
• Keep the resume simple, honest, and relevant to the role.
• Be prepared to explain everything mentioned in the resume, especially projects.

 Round 1: Online Coding Assessment (1 hour 30 minutes)

The first round was an online coding assessment conducted on the test platform.

Assessment Pattern

  20 Multiple Choice Questions (MCQs)

  1 Coding Question

The MCQs mainly tested core programming fundamentals, including:

  C / C++ basics

  Data structures

  Logical reasoning

  Basic problem-solving concepts

Coding Question

  The score of an array is defined as the number of elements whose value is

equal to their 1-based index.

  Given an array of integers, we are allowed to delete any element any number
of times (as long as the array length is greater than 1), without changing the
order of remaining elements.

  The goal was to determine the maximum possible score that can be achieved

after performing optimal deletions.

Round 2: Technical Interview (30–40 minutes)

The interviewer began by giving a brief self-introduction and asked me to introduce
myself as well. He asked about my favorite programming language, to which I replied
C++. Then, he started asking questions related to C programming concepts.

The questions covered (as far as i remember)

  Storage classes in C
  Pointer-related concepts

He then asked me to rate my coding skills out of 5, followed by a few coding
questions.

After that, I was asked to explain my projects in detail. The interviewer patiently
listened to my explanations and waited until I completed each answer without

 interrupting. Toward the end, I was given time to ask my questions, and the round
concluded smoothly.

Round 3: Techno-Managerial Interview (60 minutes)

The interviewer introduced himself and mentioned that he has 18 years of industry
experience. I then gave my self-introduction.

He asked me to show proof of my project work, such as a project video or report. I
shared my GitHub account and showed the video demo, report of my project
available there.

Most of the discussion revolved around my projects He presented a problem
statement and asked how I would approach it. The interview was very interactive, he
kept asking “what if” scenarios and followed up on my answers to understand my
thinking process and decision-making ability.

He also asked a logical puzzle:

There are 3 switches on the ground floor and 1 bulb on the first floor. How will you
identify which switch controls the bulb in just one attempt?

Later, he asked:

  What I know about Aptiv

I answered his question, also mentioned the previous interviewer’s role, his role, and
explained how my projects align with this role. He asked about my location
preferences as well.

The interview was very engaging and conversational. He listened attentively,
appreciated my answers, and even praised my approach. I asked a few questions at
the end, and the round concluded on a positive note.

Round 4: HR Interview (20–30 minutes)

The HR interview was conducted by a Senior HR. This round was non-technical.
After introductions, the questions were mostly based on my responses.

The HR asked about:

  My family background
  What I look for when choosing a problem statement
  How I approach and solve problems
  What are the two skills you would develop before joining Aptiv, and why?

 Although the interviewer maintained a serious expression, I answered confidently
and with a smile. At last, she asked about my location preference.

The final results were announced after one week.

Preparation Resources

DSA:
LeetCode Top 150 Questions (Easy and Medium) and Striver’s SDE Sheet for
building strong problem-solving skills and coding fundamentals.

SQL:
LeetCode Top 50 SQL Questions to practice queries and understand database
concepts.

Core Subjects:
Platforms like InterviewBit and GeeksforGeeks to prepare OOPS,OS,DBMS,CN, with
a focus on concept clarity.

Be confident in whatever you are answering, relate your projects to the company and
the role, and be honest throughout the interview. Make sure you are aware of and
can confidently explain every word mentioned in your resume, as most questions will
be based on your answers and the content of your resume.

If you do not get selected, it does not mean you didn’t work hard or give your best.
Focus on doing your duty by attending the placement process confidently and
sincerely, without being attached to the result.

Role: Software Engineer 
 
Appian Corporation is an American cloud computing and enterprise software 
company headquartered in McLean, Virginia, part of the Dulles Technology

Online Assessment  
The online assessment consisted of 3 coding questions to be solved in 1 hour 
30 minutes. The timestamp of submission is considered during evaluation, so 
solving faster is an advantage. 
1. Insert Interval - Leetcode  
2. Find first and last position of element in sorted array - Leetcode  
3. Find missing and repeating – GeeksForGeeks 
Important: Plagiarism check is done by AI. 
Shortlisting Process 
The shortlisted candidates list was released one day before the interview. 
Be ready with: 
• Updated Resume 3 copies 
• DSA, DBMS, OOPS 
• Real-time puzzles 
Interview Rounds 
There were three rounds, each an elimination round. 
 
Technical Round 
Never lie in your resume 
 every word in it can become a question. 
The interviewer spent about 2 minutes reading my resume and simultaneously 
generating questions based on it. Meanwhile, I gave my self-introduction. 
Questions I Faced: 
1. React: 
•    Implement a list in a React functional component. 
2. Project Discussion: 
• I had mentioned three projects — he asked about every single line in the 
descriptions. 
3. API Questions: 
• Explain what an API is. 
• Types of API and their purposes. 
• Additional: Explain about API fire/hit rate. 
4. Project Purpose:

gave me the table schema).

• Preferred language? → C++ 
• Most interesting problem solved? → I explained constructing a binary 
tree from inorder and preorder traversals. 
 
LeetCode Link: Construct Binary Tree from Preorder and Inorder 
Traversal 
7. DSA Question 1: 
• Get non-duplicated elements from a given array containing integers (both 
duplicates and uniques). 
• Explained my approach and wrote the code. [unordermap] 
 
LeetCode Link: Single Number 
8. OOPs:

9. Security in Project: 
• How did you encrypt the password during login? 
• Write the JSON format for an image.

HR Round 
• Questions were similar to the technical round, with more focus on: 
• My projects 
• DBMS basics 
• Academics

Key Takeaways 
• Never lie in your resume — be prepared for any question based on it. 
• DSA + DBMS + OOPS are must-prepare topics. 
• Be ready to explain every project you mention. 
• Have knowledge of API concepts and real-world scenarios. 
• Solve LeetCode problems regularly — they may appear with slight 
modifications. 
• CGPA plays a vital role in the shortlisting process. 
My tips to juniors…. 
Prepare well. 
When asked "Tell me about yourself", speak clearly and confidently. Also, 
know about the company — their goals and the new steps they are taking. 
While solving DSA problems, keep speaking continuously. Talk about your 
approach, explain whatever you are thinking, and share what is running through 
your mind about the problem. Don’t let the interviewer search for the next 
question — keep the conversation active. 
Highlight the novelty of your project, its purpose, and the target consumers of 
your project. Have these points at your fingertips.

About Wells Fargo 
 
Wells Fargo provides banking, investment and mortgage products and services, as well as 
consumer and commercial finance, through 8,050 locations, 13,000 ATMs, the internet 
(wellsfargo.com) and mobile banking, and has offices in 38 countries and territories to support 
customers who conduct business in the global economy 
Source: Linkedin page of Wells Fargo 
 
Number of rounds 
The recruitment process had a total of 4 rounds. 
1. Online round 
2. Technical round 1 
3. Technical round 2 
4. HR round 
 
Details about the rounds 
 
Round 1: Online round: 
 
This round had questions ranging under different sections to test our skills under 
respective categories. There was a general aptitude section, technical MCQs, a coding section 
that consisted of 1 or 2 questions. There was also a business knowledge section that had 
questions regarding stocks, gain and loss etc,. This round happened on an online platform. The 
students who were shortlisted from this round moved to the next round of interviews. 
 
Round 2: Technical round 1: 
 
This round was conducted on zoom.  Questions on basic and advanced data structures, 
Operating systems, Databases and Computer organisation were the key focus. Few questions on 
specific skills I had mentioned in my resume were also asked. 
 
Round 3: Technical round 2: 
 
This round majorly focussed on skills and projects that were mentioned in my resume. 
These questions were framed in such a way to include further requirements to the problem 
statements I have mentioned and how I would extend my ideas to meet the requirements. 
 
Round 4: HR round: 
 
In this round, communication skills and attitude were the main things checked. Few 
questions on my earlier internships and how I got them were asked. The main point was to check 
how social and communicatively able I was, to voice out my ideas and gel to a team. 
 
 
 
Questions asked 
Though I don't exactly remember all the questions, here is how much I can recollect. 
 
The questions in round 1 had technical MCQs on areas like Databases, Computer 
organisation and Operating systems. Debugging and programming based questions were also 
present. For business analysis related questions, questions on stocks etc were asked.  One coding 
question was based on graph and other on arrays. 
 
 
In the interview round that happened for around an hour, first questions on basic data 
structures, sorting were asked. Then, I was asked to share screen and code basic problems in 
arrays and linked lists. 
 
Next, questions on Operating system, databases, dynamic programming were asked to 
test my understanding in these concepts. Questions specific to the skills I had put in the resume 
were asked. Since my areas of interests were Machine learning and Natural language processing, 
a few questions to test my basic knowledge in this area were asked. I was also asked on what 
kind of technical issues would these technologies, say autonomous driving, face in its efficient 
operation. 
 
The second technical round was majorly focussed on the projects I mentioned in my 
resume. Specific questions on the methodology I used were the main areas tested. The 
interviewer extended the problem statement to include newer details and asked me how I would 
solve them efficiently. A few questions on supervised and unsupervised learning, advanced data 
structures like trees and graphs were asked. 
 
The HR round was like a casual talk where the interviewer asked me about my interests, 
previous internships and family background. The main focus was to test my confidence, attitude 
and communication skills. 
 
Topics to be focussed 
 
 Data structures: Arrays, Linked lists, stacks, queues, trees and graphs 
 A basic understanding of dynamic programming and greedy programming (optimal 
substructures and overlapping subproblems, how to approach a DP problem etc) 
 Virtual memory (paging and segmentation), Relations in databases, Memory organisation 
were the frequently tested areas in MCQs 
 
Take away 
 
 
Usually interviewers expect candidates to be punctual and clearly focused. Answer the 
questions in a precise and detailed manner. Do not abstract or assume their questions, always 
answer in such a way that the central point of the answer is clear and other details are clearly 
explained. 
 
 
For questions in the lines of “Tell me about yourself”, answer in a much realistic way, 
say, a brief introduction on your family and academic background and how you found your area 
of interest etc,.  
 
 
Do not add projects or skills you don’t have. Recollect the details about the projects 
you’ve done so that you can precisely elaborate.  
 
Articulate your thoughts well in mind and start answering, do not be nervous. 
 
Communication matters, but even if your English isn’t great, it’s nothing to worry about. 
All that matters is if you are able to answer their questions and convey your ideas clearly. 
Note down the issues faced in one online exam or interview and try to sort them out in the next 
interviews. 
 
ALL THE BEST!

About Appian Corporation
Appian Corporation is an American cloud computing and enterprise software company
headquartered in McLean, Virginia, part of the Dulles Technology Corridor. The com-
pany sells a Platform as a Service (PaaS) for building enterprise software applications.
It focuses on:
• Low-code development

east Asia
Appian offers a low-code platform with a visual interface and pre-built development
modules, enabling businesses to create apps and automations using little or no code.
Interview Rounds
The selection process consisted of four stages:
1. Online Assessment
2. Technical Round 1
3. Technical Round 2
4. HR Round
A total of 149 students appeared for the assessment.
After the assessment, 18
students were shortlisted for the interview process.
1. Online Assessment
Held on the Glider platform. This stage consisted of:
• Multiple-choice questions on:
– Analytical and logical reasoning
– Software testing fundamentals
– Basic quality concepts
• One coding question:
– Problem: Remove duplicate strings from a string array.
– Languages allowed: Java and Python.
– Note: The program had to accept manual input from the user during execu-
tion; there were no predefined inputs or automatic test cases.
1
2. Technical Round 1
This round included:
• A puzzle: There are nine coins, one heavier than the other eight. Using a two-pan
balance and only two weighings, find the heavier coin.
• A coding question: Write a program to check whether a given string is a palindrome.
– Discussed edge cases such as empty strings, whitespace-only strings, and var-
ious input formats.
– Performed a dry run of the code.
• Discussion of my internship in the CSE Department Internal Automation
Project.
• Discussion of my contributions to the Web Development Team of the CUIC
Portal.
• Questions on the projects listed in my resume.
3. Technical Round 2 (Techno-cum-Behavioural)
This round combined technical testing knowledge and behavioural questions:
• Questions on different types of testing:
– Unit testing
– Integration testing
– System testing
– Acceptance testing
– Regression testing
– White box testing
– Black box testing
• Behavioural questions such as:
– Why Appian?
– Why do you want the QE role?
4. HR Round
The HR round covered:
• Academic details: marks in 10th, 11th, 12th, CGPA (8.29 at the time), and JEE
Mains percentile.
• Motivation: why I want the QE role.
After all the rounds, only 3 out of the 18 interviewees were selected — 2 from CEG
and 1 from MIT. I am fortunate to be one of the three selected candidates for the role of
Quality Engineering Intern.
Key Takeaways
1. Your communication skills matter — express your thoughts clearly.
2. Be genuinely curious to learn. Even if you do not know about something, show
eagerness to understand and explore it.
3. Start your preparation early, and know your resume inside out — every single
point should be explainable.
4. DSA is important for all roles; learning puzzles and aptitude too is important.
2
5. Maintain a good CGPA from the first semester itself; it really does give you
an advantage.
If you have a low CGPA, compensate with relevant experience such as internships
(even short-term or unpaid), open-source contributions, maintaining club or depart-
ment websites, or projects in the CSE department. These activities demonstrate
teamwork, initiative, and practical exposure.
6. Finally, have hope rather than rigid expectations. Keep the mindset: “Whatever
happens, happens,” because luck can also be a factor.
Overall Experience
The interview process was an enriching experience that tested my analytical skills, coding
abilities, testing knowledge, and communication skills.

investment banking, asset management, and credit card services. The bank has been 
involved in various philanthropic initiatives and is known for its commitment to 
sustainability and environmental practices. 
There were 3 rounds namely: 
• Round 1: Online Assessment (Virtual) 
- 
This round mainly focused on fundamentals of Software Engineering(SDLC 
lifecycle), OOPS, DBMS, finding outputs of  c/cpp/Java codes, debugging 
programs, and Aptitude questions. 
- 
There were a total of 25 MCQs and 5 sql queries to be completed within 70 
minutes. 
- 
The test was conducted on the Hacker Earth Platform. 
- 
Note: Out of around 400 students who applied for attempting the OA, only 
around 200 were shortlisted for writing the test. 
- 
46 students proceeded further for the interview process conducted at CUIC. 
 
 
• Round 2: Technical Interview (@ CUIC ) 
- 
This round started off with a brief introduction about myself, and I moved on 
to explain about the project that I was most proud of, since I worked really 
hard to deploy it as well. 
- 
The interviewer asked me why I chose MERN Stack and why MongoDB over 
SQL. (I explained the difference between vertical and horizontal scaling here) 
- 
The interviewer was interested in knowing every nook and corner of how I 
did the project and how I managed to deploy it within a month. 
- 
She asked me what motivated me to take up the projects I invested my time 
in. 
- 
What were the key challenges I faced during my projects. 
- 
Major/Key Take-aways and Learnings made . 
- 
She asked me to give a brief explanation about Gen AI, since I mentioned the 
certification (as part of our Naan Mudhalvan Syllabus) in my resume 
- 
Difference between GPT-5 and GPT-4 , which I couldn’t answer properly, but 
managed to give some convincing differences. 
- 
What Data Structure is used in your phone gallery app? With reference to 
how you can easily scroll the pictures in your gallery without having to wait 
for each picture to load. Explain your choice. 
- 
Finally, she asked why I chose to study in CEG and how these 2 years have 
changed me. 
- 
This round lasted for 1 hour and 15 mins 
- 
25 students proceeded for the HR round. 
 
 
• Round 3: HR Round (@ CUIC ) 
- 
This round was entirely behavioural, where the interviewer presented me 
with various scenarios to assess my crisis management skills and my 
response in critical situations. Some questions include:  
- 
She asked me to elaborate on my hobbies, as I had mentioned them in my 
resume. 
- 
Where do you see yourself in the next 4 years? 
- 
Imagine your team is going to release/deploy a project tomorrow. Today 
night you realise that there is a bug in the code and that it was you who made 
the mistake of creating the bug. What will you do? 
- 
Microsoft or Barclays? 
- 
Materialistic life or Work-Balance life? 
- 
Finally, she asked basic details about my parents and this round ended within 
20 minutes. 
- 
Out of 25 students, 15 students (5 from our dept) were selected for the 2026 
Summer Internship Programme at Barclays and I was lucky to be a part of it. 
 
 
 
• Some Tips  
- 
Be confident while answering the questions. 
- 
Be truthful to your resume. You must be thorough with every nook and 
corner of your resume, especially your projects. 
- 
Have a habit of solving DSA problems, and revising DBMS / OOPS concepts 
regularly.  
- 
In Barclays interviews many were asked on concepts like ER Diagram, 
Normalization, etc. 
- 
Try to maintain a CGPA of about 8+ , if otherwise, focus on other projects to 
compensate. 
- 
Read the job description and you must do a research about the company.  
- 
 Refer to the company's website to learn about RISES. 
• A little intro about myself 
- 
 Looking back, I realize I didn’t fully utilize my summer holidays. I did 
practice a few easy-to-medium problems on LeetCode now and then, but 
even after receiving this offer, I can clearly see that my problem-solving skills 
still have a long way to go. 
- 
Most of my time went into working on projects, while I also made sure to 
spend some leisure moments with my family. 
- 
The seriousness of internship preparation struck me only when internship 
offers started flooding the CUIC WhatsApp group—it was a wake-up call. 
- 
In the end, I managed to make good use of the time I had during the final 
stretch, but if there’s one piece of advice I’d give, it would be to start 
preparing early, ideally from your 3rd or 4th semester itself. 
- 
I was shortlisted for interviews at Appian and Western Digital (the latter 
being heavily focused on C programming, which wasn’t my strong area), but 
my preparation at that stage wasn’t sufficient to clear them. 
- 
Barclays was my 3rd glow of hope, and I was determined to grab that 
opportunity. 
- 
All the very best for your future internship opportunities!! 
 
 
• Contact 
- 
Feel free to reach out to me, I’ll be more than happy to help you in whatever 
way I can!

AMAZON DEVELOPMENT CENTRE 
 
SCREENING ROUND: 
 
All the students who met the eligibility criteria took this test (Online). It was a coding + aptitude + 
behavioural round conducted on AMCAT platform.    
 
Part 1: Debugging: 
 
It had around 7 questions with buggy code snippets. Candidates were expected to clear the bug and 
produce the required output.  
Difficulty Level: EASY 
 
Part 2: Coding: 
 
It had 2 Coding question, the questions may seem complex at first glance, but mostly are direct 
application of well-known data structures and algorithms. Candidates will be provided an hour to 
solve. Remember to handle edge cases as well since u will not be aware of failed test cases as they 
are hidden.  
Difficulty Level: Moderate 
 
Part 3: Behavioural Analysis: 
 
This was more of a personal round. There wasn’t a single right answer to any question. It had 
questions like candidate’s interest, his/her strength/weakness. Do attend this part very carefully since 
this is the only round where you can outperform others, also try to remain consistent throughout all 
the questions and don’t contradict yourself across two different questions.  
Difficulty Level: There isn’t such for this part, may be Moderate for you to pay extra attention.     
 
Part 4: Aptitude: 
 
It was a typical aptitude round. I found it difficult to keep up with the time since it had some questions 
testing comprehension skills. 
Difficulty Level: Moderate 
 
 
 
 
INTERVIEW ROUND(S): 
 
There were 4 rounds of interview, out of which first 3 were Technical and last one was technical cum 
managerial. 
 
First Technical Interview: 
 
I was asked to solve two coding problems in one hour. The proposed solution should be optimized to 
the fullest (minimal time and space complexity). The interviewer will have a conversation to help to 
arrive at the solution. 
The two coding problems were based on the following: 
1. Similar to searching in a row and column wise sorted 2-D Matrix 
2. Similar to finding if one string is a substring of another 
 
Second Technical Interview: 
 
Similar to first round, I was given another two problems and was asked to give an optimal solution 
in one hour. 
The two coding problems were based on the following: 
1. Sorting a stream of integers.( I used  an heap) 
2. Finding longest substring in a dictionary of words.( I used a trie ) 
 
Third Technical Interview: 
 
It was similar to first 2 rounds. 
The 2 coding problems were based on the following: 
1. Process scheduling with custom constraints 
2. Application of Binary Search Tree Searching, Insertion and Deletion 
 
Final Round (Managerial + Technical Round): 
 
Questions from various domains like DBMS, Operating Systems, and Object Oriented Programming 
were asked. Finally a coding question based on Bit-magic was given. 
 
 
Topics to be focussed: 
In decreasing order of importance 
1. Data Structures 
2. Algorithms 
3. Basic knowledge on domains listed in the final round experience.  
 
Use Geeksforgeeks articles for preparation and Leetcode for practice. 
Tip: Watch “Back to Back SWE” videos in youtube  
 
Hope this helps. 
 
Thank you and All the Best.

Interview Experience

Name: Divya Dharshini B
Reg No: 2018115027
Mail ID: diyabalakrishnan2001@gmail.com
Company: Accenture

Accenture-AEH’22 Hiring consists of 3 stages.
Role- Advanced Application Engineering Analyst.
CTC-  9.9 LPA (8 Lacs Fixed).

Stage 1:
Stage 1 consists of two sections - Cognitive and Technical assessment
followed by a Coding assessment.

Cognitive and Technical assessment:
Duration: 90 minutes
Sections: English ability, Critical Reasoning and Problem-solving, Abstract
reasoning, Pseudo Code, Networking Security and cloud, and at last common
applications and ms office.
Totally there were 6 sections and 90 questions in total.

Note: This is an elimination round so try to attend all the sections as much as
possible. Time won’t be enough to answer all the questions but try to complete
them because most of the time they consider sectional cut-offs.

Only shortlisted students from this round can able to attend the Coding
assessment.

Coding assessment:
Duration: 45 minutes
The number of questions: 2
Difficulty level: Easy, Medium.

Questions:

1. Given an array, print the elements in the given range.
2. Given two linked lists, merge them according to the conditions given

below:

i) First part of the result linked lists consists of even nodes of 1st

linked list.

 ii) Second part consists of odd nodes of 2nd linked lists.
iii) Third part consists of even nodes of 2nd linked list.
iv) Fourth part consists of odd nodes of 1st linked list.

Note: I solved both the problems and all the test cases were passed. But
people who have solved one question completely and the other question
partially also cleared this round and were shortlisted for the next stage.

Stage 2:
Communicative assessment:
This assessment is a non-elimination round. An online test to assess your
communication skills like the pronunciation of words, listening ability,
vocabulary and fluency in English. The difficulty level increases with time but
not too hard.

Stage 3:
Virtual interview:
After the completion of the previous stage, we received mail about this
interview after two days. We were asked to block our slot according to our
convenience. It was a 30 minutes interview. The interviewer asked the
following questions:

1. Tell me about yourself.
2. What is the difference between IT and Computer science?
3. Explain each of the projects mentioned in your resume briefly.
4. While I was explaining the technologies behind each of my projects, he

interrupted me asked whether I was unsuccessful in any of the
situations during the development of the project and regained
confidence and become successful.

5. Then he asked me whether I have attended interviews before that.
6. Finally, after a couple of general HR questions, the interviewer asked
whether I have any questions for him and I asked ‘What is the most
important skill that you are expecting from our side for this role?’, he
replied me with ‘We just need humans’. We smiled at each other and
left the meeting.

Note: The questions asked in the interview is completely based on the
interviewer because I wasn’t asked technical questions but focused mainly on
my confidence level and passion but many people have been asked technical
concepts that they have mentioned in their resume.

 Smiling and being confident about yourself would mean a lot.

Intern Drive Experience – Accenture
Manish M (IT Purple)

About the company:

Accenture is a global professional services company offering services in strategy, consulting,
digital, technology, and operations. It helps businesses improve performance and navigate
change through technology and industry expertise. Accenture has a notable presence in India
and across the globe, working in areas such as digital transformation, cloud, and AI to assist
businesses in their growth.

Role: Advanced Application Engineering Intern

Round 1 – Cognitive and Technical Assessment:

The test consisted of 90 questions to be completed in 90 minutes. It was divided into six
sections covering topics like verbal, aptitude, reasoning, pseudocode outputs, MS Office, and
basics of networking and cloud.

Those who cleared the test proceeded to the coding assessment, which took place immediately
afterwards.

Round 2 – Coding Assessment:

There were two coding questions, and 45 minutes were provided. Both the questions were
fairly straightforward and involved arrays and sorting for me. One thing to note is that you had
to handle both the input and output yourself, rather than just filling in a function as in typical
tests. Those who are unfamiliar with this can find similar questions on platforms like CodeChef
and Codeforces.

68 students were shortlisted after this round.

Round 3 – Communication Assessment:

The test took about half an hour and was AI-based. It assessed reading, listening, and speaking
skills through tasks such as reading sentences aloud, repeating phrases, and retelling a narrated
story.

This was not an elimination round.

 Round 4 – Virtual Interview:

The interview was held online on Accenture’s platform and was 25-30 minutes long. My
interview was not too technical, focusing instead on general and behavioural questions.
The interviewer concentrated on my projects, discussing the experiences and skills gained
while working on them. Apart from that, there were questions about academics, as well as
some HR-related ones.

Five students were shortlisted for the internship.

Preparation Tips:

  Keep Leetcoding and keep strengthening your DSA.
  Build a strong resume, highlighting projects, certifications, achievements, and

anything else that adds value.

  Focus on projects and explore trending areas like AI and machine learning.
  For coding rounds, practice a wide range of problems and participate in contests

across various platforms.

  For interviews, first and foremost, ensure you have a strong resume with solid
projects. Be thorough with each of your projects and prepare to answer any
questions about them. Be well-versed in OS, DBMS, OOP, SQL. and have strong
communication skills. Prepare for frequently asked questions and go through past
interview questions and experiences.

MathWorks Internship  
Batch: 2019-2023 
About the company: 
MathWorks is an American privately held corporation that specializes in mathematical 
computing software. Its major products include MATLAB and Simulink, which support data 
analysis and simulation. 
Number of Rounds:  4 
1) Online Coding Round + Mcqs 
2) Group Discussion 
3) Managerial Round 
4) HR Round 
ONLINE ROUND: 
Platform: HackerRank 
This round consists of 10 Multiple choice questions from DSA, C,OS ,DBMS and 2 coding 
questions one of easy level and the other with medium difficulty. The total time given for 
both the sections  was 60 mins. 
Questions Asked: 
1) Make all the array elements equal. 
2) This was a medium level question related to arrays. 
A total of 11 students were shortlisted for the next rounds. 
The next day we had a PPT(Pre placement talk) at 8:30 a.m. 
Note: Listen to the PPT carefully as many questions were asked from that. 
Group Discussion: 
Platform: Zoom 
All the shortlisted students participated in this round.  
TOPIC: Which is more important? Higher Education or Work Experience. 
From 11 students only 2 of us were selected for the further rounds. 
 
Note:   
• I started the conversation which I think gave me an upper hand over my other 
friends. 
• It is always necessary to stand on your point and not get manipulated by the opinion 
of others. 
• My key point with which I proceeded in this GD - Both higher education and work 
experience are inter-dependent and they are not mutually exclusive. 
Managerial Round: 
This round lasted for about 30 -35 mins. 
Platform: Zoom 
The interview started with questions like 
• Which is your favourite subject and why? 
• My favourite project and what did I explore newly while doing that project? 
• I was asked many questions from the PPT like , Name the products of MathWorks? 
• Tell us something about the EDG program and many more 
• What are your strengths and weakness? 
• How are you improving your weakness? 
• The interviewer then asked me a coding question related to stacks. I wasn’t asked to 
code it . I gave my approach and then he asked me an optimized approach, I 
managed to give my approach. 
• Finally the interviewer asked me to choose a branch for work 
(Bangalore/Hyderabad). 
Note: 
• Talk with utmost confidence. 
• Have a nice big smile and positive attitude. 
HR Round: 
This round lasted for 10 – 15 mins. 
Platform: Zoom 
The  interviewer greeted me and asked me general questions like tell me about yourself , 
Why MathWorks?, What do you know about MathWorks EDG program?, Which location did 
you select for your internship and why? And a few more questions. Finally she asked me if I 
had any questions. I asked her how did I perform and where should I improve as it was my 
first HR interview. She said that I  have attentively listened to the PPT and I was well 
prepared. She bid goodbye by saying looking forward to working with you. 
Final Result: 
On the same day around 8 p.m. I got to know that I was the only person from our batch 
selected for the internship at MathWorks. 
 
To keep in mind: 
• Prepare an introduction and speak clearly. 
• Try to maintain a good rapport with the interviewer and show them that your 
excited to be there. 
• Never lie in the resume. 
• Have a thorough knowledge of what you’ve put in the  resume. 
• For coding practice from leetcode explore, gfg. 
• Don’t lose hope . Keep practicing and surely you will be selected by one of the 
companies. 
• Read GFG’s archives of internship experiences of that particular companies which 
will give us an insight of how the process flow will be.

Name: Adhithya K 
Department: Computer Science and Engineering 
Company placed: Walmart Global Tech India 
Job Position: Software Engineer 2 
 
Round 1: Online Test 
Time Duration: 60 minutes 
Platform: HackerEarth 
Pattern: 
1) 10 technical MCQs belonging to OOPS, OS, DBMS, Networks and SQL 
2) 2 Coding questions of medium-hard level 
 
Level of difficulty: Medium-Hard 
 
Resources you referred to: GeeksforGeeks, Interviewbit. 
How did you handle the questions: I read the entire question twice. Then I tried to rephrase 
the questions and recalled the concepts related to it. By doing this I was able to answer all the 
questions with good accuracy. 
 
Tips you would suggest for your juniors to pass this round: This is the most important 
round hence one should be strong in CS fundamentals and data structures. 
Count of people shortlisted after this round: 20 students were shortlisted for the next round 
 
Round 2 : Technical Interview 
Time Duration: 50-60 minutes 
Platform: Zoom 
Level of difficulty: Medium-Hard 
I was asked to introduce myself and then we went straight to the technical questions. 
 
Coding questions asked: 
1) Find if a number is prime number or not (I was asked to explain all possible solutions 
with detailed time and space complexities). 
2) Given a binary tree in which each node element contains a number. Find the maximum 
possible path sum from one leaf node to another leaf node (I was asked to explain all 
possible solutions with detailed time and space complexities). (Tree) 
3) Max length Chain - You are given N pairs of numbers. In every pair, the first number is 
always smaller than the second number. A pair (c, d) can follow another pair (a, b) if b < 
c. Chain of pairs can be formed in this fashion. You have to find the longest chain which 
can be formed from the given set of pairs. (DP) 
 
4) I was given 3 aptitude questions based on speed-distance-time, LCM-HCF and Profit-
Loss. 
5) Various questions on Threading, Paging, Java fundamentals, OOPS, SQL were asked. 
 
Tips: Prepare the CS core subjects very well as they are equally important as DSA. 
 
Round 3: Technical Interview 2 
Time Duration: 1.5 hours 
Platform: Zoom 
Level of difficulty: Medium-Hard 
I was asked to introduce myself and then we went straight to the technical questions. 
 
Coding questions asked: 
1) Print the tree nodes in zigzag manner. (Tree DS) 
2) Write a function to get the intersection point of two Linked Lists (I was asked to explain 
all possible solutions with detailed time and space complexity analysis). (Linked List DS) 
3) Binary Tree to Doubly Linked List (Tree and DLL) 
4) Given a list of unique words, return all the pairs of the distinct indices (i, j) in the given 
list, so that the concatenation of the two words (words[i] + words[j]) is a palindrome. (Trie 
DS). 
5) Various questions related to Java, OS, OOPS and Networks. 
 
Tips: Always start answering the questions with Brute-Force approach, and then optimize the 
solutions. 
 
Round 4: HR Interview 
Time Duration: 30 mins 
Platform: Zoom 
 
1) I was asked questions based on my resume. 
2) We had a long discussion about Walmart, innovations at Walmart and technologies 
which Walmart was working in. 
 
List of HR questions asked: 
1) Difference between Hardwork and SmartWork 
2) I was given 2 scenarios and asked how would I react to them. 
 
Your suggestions for juniors in cracking this round: 
1) Always give honest answers and be confident while you answer. 
 
Tips on resume preparation: 
1) Update Your Contact Details (Add Linkedin, GeeksforGeeks, Leetcode, Github, 
Codechef, Codeforces links if available). 
2) Present Your Education Properly. 
3) Layout and Format is important. 
4) List your experiences in reverse chronological format. 
5) Add experiences that are relevant to the job description. 
6) Customize your resume to each company and each job position. 
 
Overall tips how to ace the placements: 
1) Don’t be nervous or stressed during interviews. Ask your friends or seniors to conduct 
mock interviews. This will help you gain confidence. 
2) Always be ready with your real life stories for the soft skills that you have mentioned in 
your resume. 
3) Be consistent in your preparations. 
4) Take care of all the edge cases while writing code. You must know how to calculate time 
and space complexities.
