This round was conducted in Aspiring Minds (SHL) platform. This round was 
conducted offline in IST department LAB. The round consisted of 3 sections 
namely verbal aptitude, business aptitude and coding. Both aptitude sections 
had MCQs which had to be answered compulsorily and free navigation was 
prohibited. Everyone got different sets of questions for coding section. Mostly 
the questions will be some commonly asked questions, but it will be written in 
the form of a scenario, a paragraph. Do be prepared to quickly grasp the 
constraints of the question. 
Verbal Aptitude: Questions like choose the correct meaning, fill in the blank 
with appropriate words were asked. Mostly like reading Comprehension, 
general English grammar.  
 Business Aptitude: Questions were largely based on graph interpretations (pie 
charts, bar graphs, line graphs) and also data interpretation . Mostly like stock 
analytics, basic mathematics and understanding of profit – loss. 
Coding Round: It has 2 parts 
Part 1: 2 Questions (Easy-Medium level)- 40 Minutes 
1. The question is not directly mentioned. It is to quite related to find the 
number of character in a string which are consonants. 
2. The mother starts x1 meters away from the house. The child starts x2 
meters away from the house. The mother walks at a speed of v1 meters 
per second and takes s1 total steps. The child is walking at a speed v2 
and takes s2 total steps. Your goal is to determine the optimal speed v2 
and number of steps s2 for the child such that the number of steps 
where the child steps exactly on the positions where the mother has 
stepped earlier is maximized. 
Part 2: 1 Question – 10 minutes 
Given a start year and an end year, write a function to count the number of 
months within that range (inclusive) where the first day of the month is a 
Sunday.  
For this question , only C++,Java and python IDE was available. 
 
Example: 
Input: 1901-2000 
Output: 171 
Explanation: January 1, 1900, was a Monday (since 1900 is not a leap year, 
January 1, 1901, will be a Tuesday). Use Zeller's Congruence or any standard 
day-of-the-week calculation method to determine which day the first of each 
month falls on for every year in this range. 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/zellers-congruence-find-day-date/ 
 
 
 
Even passing partial test cases is often given added weightage. If you can't 
arrive at an optimal solution, just try a brute force approach to pass as many 
test cases as possible. 
22 students were shortlisted for the Technical and HR interview. I am one 
among them. 
Round 2: Technical Interview 
The technical interview lasted approximately 40-50 minutes, covering a broad 
spectrum of computer science fundamentals and practical problem-solving 
skills. 
Motivation and Background: The interviewer was keen to understand my 
reasons for choosing Computer Science and my self-learning journey. My focus 
on problem-solving platforms like LeetCode and HackerRank during my pre-
college days was highlighted. 
SQL Fundamentals: Given my expressed interest in SQL, I was questioned 
about core concepts like Inner JOINs and Outer JOINs and was asked to 
construct a basic query using JOIN. 
Database Management: A practical scenario involving concurrent transactions 
in a hotel or restaurant setting was introduced. I was expected to propose 
solutions for handling such scenarios, which included concepts like locking and 
abstraction levels. 
Indexing in DBMS:  I explained that its purpose is to speed up data retrieval in 
databases by improving query performance. 
Programming Proficiency: A simple coding challenge involving string reversal 
was presented. Additionally, I was expected to identify potential exceptions 
that could arise during string manipulation. 
List VS Tuples: The interviewer sought to assess my understanding of 
fundamental data structures by comparing lists and tuples in Python, including 
their real-world applications. 
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP): My understanding of OOPS concepts 
was assessed. I explained the core principles - abstraction, encapsulation, 
inheritance, and polymorphism and was asked to implement a simple class in 
C++ or Python, as well as explain how abstraction is utilized in this 
implementation. 
Memory Management: Questions on deep vs. shallow copy and garbage 
collection highlighted key concepts in memory management. 
Ended with the interviewer asking if I had any questions. 
The round concluded with a positive note, advancing me to the next round. 
Few candidates were eliminated after this round. 
Round 3: Technical Interview 
The interview commenced with a focus on understanding my career aspirations 
and identifying my key strengths. To assess problem-solving abilities, the 
interviewer presented real-world scenarios. 
• I mentioned my expertise in database management systems, operating 
systems, and data structures.  
• The discussion then focused on SQL and NoSQL databases, including 
when to use each. 
• Why NoSQL Databases Were Developed and Implemented? 
The round was characterized by a focus on learning and understanding 
rather than solely assessing. 
Round 4: HR Interview 
• During the interview, I was asked to introduce myself. I mentioned my 
strengths, technical interests, club responsibilities, and personal 
interests.  
• I highlighted my role as Head of the Dance Club, which prompted a 
follow-up question about my interest in dance.  
• I elaborated on my passion for dance and how it has contributed to my 
personal growth and leadership abilities. 
The interview proceeded smoothly, with discussions covering various 
aspects of my background and technical knowledge. I felt confident in my 
responses and engaged in meaningful conversations about my experiences 
and skills. 
At the end of the selection process, a total of 12 students were chosen for 
the internship. I am pleased to share that I was among those selected, 
marking a successful conclusion to the interview process. 
Key takeaways from my interview experiences:-  
• Focus on problem-solving and DSA fundamentals. Utilize your summer 
holidays to brush up on the basics. I followed C.O.D.E by ACM Club, 
LeetCode, and Take U Forward on YouTube. 
• Practice aptitude questions using resources like IndiaBix. 
• Build confidence in expressing your thought process during interviews, 
as interviewers often assist when you’re stuck. 
• Be ready for both concept and application-oriented questions in OS, DSA, 
DBMS, and OOP. 
• Prepare answers for common non-technical questions, such as your 
strengths/weaknesses and reasons for wanting the internship. 
 
All the Best !!!

SCHEDULE: 
• ONLINE ASSESSMENT (16/08/24) 
 
Coding Round: Conducted in Hacker-rank Website  
Test Duration: 120 mins Camera On 1 easy, 2 medium and 1 hard question.  
 
 
1. BALANCING ELEMENTS  
When an element is deleted from an array, the higher-indexed elements shift down one 
index to fill the gap, A “balancing element” is defined as an element that, when deleted 
from the array, results in the sum of the even-indexed elements being equal to the sum 
of the odd-indexed elements. Determine how many balancing elements a given array 
contains.  
Example: Input: n=5 arr = [5,5,2,8]  
Output: 2 When the first or second 5 is deleted, the array becomes [5, 2, 5, 8].  
The sum[even] = 5+5=10 and sum[odd]=-2+ 8-10.  
No other elements of the original array have that property.  
There are 2 balancing elements: arr[0] and arr[1]. 
 
              2.MINIMUM FUEL COST 
                     There are n cars located on a 2 dimensional plane at positions (x[i],y[i]) where 0<=i<=n. 
                     They need to be parked in a straight line parallel to the x axis with no spaces between  
                     them. The fuel consumed to move a car is abs(x[finish]-x[start])+abs(y[finish]-y[start]). 
                      Determine the minimum fuel cost to arrange the cars side by side in a row parallel to the  
                      X axis  
                      Example: X=[1,4] , Y=[1,4] Total fuel : 2+3=5 
 
 
 
3.PRODUCT DEPENDENCY TREE 
Given a network of product dependencies represented as a tree. The tree consists of 
treeNodes vertices and the relationships between products are defined by two arrays: 
treeFrom and treeTo, both of size treeNodes – 1. Thes arrays specify the connections, 
where treeFrom[i] is connected to treeTo[i]. 
 
For this product dependency tree, the task is to determine the number of triplets of 
vertices (i ,j ,k) that meet the following conditions : 
0 <= i <j <k < treeNodes 
There should be no simple path connecting vertices i, j and k. 
Due to the potentially large number of triplets, return the answer module ( 10^9 +7) 
 
4.PORTFOLIO BACKTESTING : 
 
                    The traders of Hacker-land have stock prediction model that they want to backtest. 
                    The predicted profit earned every month by a certain portfolio is given by an array 
                    pnl of n integers where a negative integer denotes loss.  
 
                   The traders decide to iterate through the months from 1 to n, and if the pnl is positive, 
                   they add it to their profit and if the pnl is negative, they can either subtract it or skip it.  
                   The net profit must always be greater than or equal to 0.  
                    
                   Given the array pnl and an integer k, find the maximum total profit that can be earned 
                   by the portfolio after subtracting at least k losses. 
                   If it is not possible to take at least k losses, report -1 as the answer.  
 
     Example: Suppose n=5, k=2 and pnl=[4, -3, 6, -2, -1]  
 
     The optimal strategy is to add the profits 4 and 6, and the losses [-2, -1] to get a net  
     profit of 7 which is the maximum possible.

About the company 
The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation, is an American investment banking services 
holding company headquartered in New York City. BNY Mellon was formed from the merger 
of The Bank of New York and the Mellon Financial Corporation in 2007. It is the world's 
largest custodian bank and securities services company. The bank's primary functions are 
managing and servicing the investments of institutions and high-net-worth individuals. Its 
two primary businesses are Investment Services and Investment Management. The bank's 
clients include 80 percent of Fortune 500 companies. The Mumbai Representative Office of 
The Bank of New York Mellon was set up in 1983 and continues to serve as BNY Mellon's 
main liaison point for Indian client base. In 2013, Pershing India Operational Services Private 
Limited (Pershing India) was established in Chennai. Currently, the company have over

No of Rounds: 3 (Online Test + Technical interview + Technical & HR interview) 
Stipend: 75,000 INR per month 
Duration of internship: 7 weeks 
Round 1 – Online Assessment 
The online assessment was conducted on the Hackerrank platform, there were a total of 4 
questions that were asked, which consisted of 1 easy, 2 medium and 1 hard question 
Duration: 2 hours 
The questions that I had gotten are as follows: 
1. Category: Medium 
Topic: Graphs  
 
A server network is represented as a tree of g_nodes servers indexed from 1 to 
g_nodes and g_nodes-1 edges where ith edges connect the servers g_from[i] and 
g_to[i] the transfer time between any 2 connected servers is 1 unit. Given the graph 
g find maximum time taken to transfer the data between any 2 servers in the system. 
Write a function getMaxTime in python with the parameters:  
int g_nodes: the no of servers in the network  
int g_from[n]: the end point of edges  
int g_to[n]: the end point of edges  
 
Function returns: 
int: the maximum time to transfer data between 2 servers 
 
 
2. Category: Hard 
Topic: Graphs  
 
A game contains a maze represented as a graph with n vertices numbered 1 to n and 
m bidirectional edges numbered 1 to m. The ith edge connects endpoint1[i] and 
endpoint2[i]. It takes edheLength[i] units of time to traverse the ith edge. Each vertex 
I will disappear at time t[i] and will not reappear. If a visit occurs at the same time the 
vertex disappears, the vertex cannot be reached. The game starts at vertex 1 and 
time 0. Find the earliest visit time for each vertex starting from vertex 1. If a vertex 
cannot be reached the arrival time is -1 Write python function mazeGame that has 
following parameters  
Int t[n] : array of integers the ith vertex goes away at t[i] 1 based index 
             int endpoint1[m]: vertex no of 1 end of an edge[i] 1 based index  
             int endpoint2[m]: vertex number of the other end edge[i] 1 based index  
             Int edheLength[m] : edheLength[i] is the time it takes to traverse edge[i] 1 based 
index  
Returns:  
int[m]: the arrival times at each vertex I or -1 if visit is not possible 
3. Category: Easy 
Topic: Single Dimensional Arrays 
 
There are n devices in the network where the vulnerability of ith device is 
represented by vulnerability[i]. All devices in a security level must have the same 
vulnerability level and the number of devices in any 2 security levels should not differ 
by more than 1 Given the array vulnerability, find the maximum no of security levels 
needed to ensure protection of network Write a function getMinimumGroups that 
has the parameter:  
Int vulnerability[i] : the vulnerabilities of devices  
 
Returns  
Int: the minimum no of groups required 
 
4. Category: Medium 
Topic: Multi-Dimensional Arrays 
 
There are n customers and m shops in HackerMall. The no of items of each type in a 
shop is unlimited so several customers can buy items of same type in a shop A 
customer is considered as served if all types of items the customer wishes to 
purchase are available in open shops. Initially the shops are closed. Find maximum 
no of customers who can be served by the mall if almost 2 shops are opened to serve 
the customers Write function countmaximumcustomers has the following 
parameters:   
int customer[m][p] : the types of items each customer wishes to purchase  
int shop[m][k]: the types of items offered by each shop  
 
Returns  
Int: the maximum no of customers that can be served  
 
Round 2 – Technical Interview 
The interview was conducted in person at CUIC. It went for about 35-40 min.  
The interviewer first introduced herself and in turn asked for my introduction. Then she 
went through my resume and asked me which programming language I’m proficient in. I 
told Python, since I was very familiar with it.  
She then asked me why I chose Python over the other languages, and I told her its because 
its an interpreted language and its easy and user-friendly nature and syntax. 
The interviewer started to test me in Data Structures. She gave me a piece of paper and 
asked me to write down each data structure that I have learnt, draw its representation and 
write functions to perform basic operations like creation, insertion, deletion and search. I 
started off with arrays, how to access elements in an array and wrote functions for 
performing the elementary operations. I explained every line of the code as I was writing. I 
did this for every data structure upto graphs.  
While doing this task, I was asked many questions like: 
 
1. What is the advantage of linked list over other linear data structures? 
2. Given a binary tree, I was asked to write its inorder, preorder and postorder traversal, and 
given two out of three traversals, I was used to construct the corresponding binary tree 
4. What is the difference between tree and graph 
5. Given a graph, write its BFS and DFS traversals and explain the procedure to perform the 
same 
After this was done, the interviewer moved on to asking me basic questions in Python 
programming, to test my knowledge. I was then asked to write an optimal solution to 
remove duplicates in a string, and to perform basic arithmetic operations on matrices. I 
wrote down the programs on paper and explained my approach to her step by step. She was 
satisfied with it. 
Next, the interviewer tested me in DBMS. She gave me a Student database with 4-5 tables in 
it, and asked me to write SQL queries to retrieve appropriate records from the table. This 
task involved the use of joins – equijoin, left join, right join, inner join, natural join, triggers 
and cursors. Given an equijoin query, I was asked to translate it to natural join, left join, 
right join and natural join. This extensively tested my understanding of join operations and 
the basic syntax of various types of joins in SQL. It was a bit challenging but interesting.  
 
This was followed by a few general questions in SQL: 
1. Difference between order by and group by 
2. When do we use having clause 
3. Aggregate functions  
4. Difference between drop and truncate command 
The interviewer concluded by asking me what my favourite subject was, in Computer 
Science, and I said DBMS. She then asked me if I have any questions for her, and I asked her 
what tech stacks we’ll be generally using for doing projects during the summer internship, 
and what prior knowledge we are expected to have.  
Overall, the interview went well and I was shortlisted for the next round 
 
Round 3 – Technical + HR Interview 
It took place in person at CUIC. It went for about 45 min. I was asked to bring my laptop 
along with resume. 
The interviewer greeted me and asked me to introduce myself. Then he went through my 
resume thoroughly. 
Since I’m doing the BS Data Science and Applications program concurrently with my college 
degree and I had mentioned it in my resume, the interviewer was curious to know more 
about the program and its modalities, so I spent a lot of time explaining how I got into the 
program, why I wanted to pursue it and how I had benefited out of the program overall. He 
then asked me a few questions he had about the IITM program and I answered them. 
After this, the interviewer gave me three questions, I was asked to open my laptop, use any 
editor of my choice to type out the code and run the programs in front of him. The 
questions that were given to me were: 
1. Given some numbers in an array, use bubble sort and merge sort to sort the 
numbers in the array, and show the results obtained after every pass 
2. Write code to perform division of 2 numbers without using the ‘/’ operator, and 
multiplication of 2 numbers without using ‘*’ operator 
3. Write optimal solution to reverse a number and string, and to check whether it is a 
palindrome or not 
I had to code in the presence of the interviewer and explain every part of the code as I 
typed. I was asked to write separate functions to perform each operation in a program for 
modularity. 
Following this, I was questioned extensively about the projects that I had put on my resume. 
I had a music management system project and hotel booking system project, and I was 
asked to describe what tech stacks I had used for frontend and backend development. I was 
also told to describe the structure of the database created and how the tables were linked, 
what was the choice of primary key, foreign key and why. I was also asked which normal 
form the tables existed in, and how to normalise it completely. The interviewer was also 
curious to know the unique selling point of my project and what added benefits it offers 
compared to traditional systems 
After this was done, the interviewer looked into my resume and asked me about my 
positions of responsibilities. Since I was a Deputy Director in CSAU, ACM-CEG, Guindy Times, 
I explained about my role in each of these clubs and told him what kind of sessions and 
programs each of these clubs conduct in college. He was eager to know more about these 
clubs and also suggested a few sessions and events that technical clubs in our college could 
conduct, to attract many people. It was a very friendly conversation overall.  
The interviewer also asked me how I was able to manage being in so many clubs while 
pursuing a dual degree, and I told him that managing time wisely was the key. 
Finally, the interviewer asked me whether I have any questions for him. I asked him about 
the various teams for summer internship at BNY and what each team does. I then thanked 
the interviewer and came out. 
After an hour, the results were announced.  
Overall, 9 of us from CEG and MIT got selected for the summer internship program at BNY 
Mellon, and I was one among them. 
Tips: 
1. Be confident with what you put on your resume. Do not fake anything there as you 
can be questioned on anything there, and you are expected to be thorough with 
what is mentioned there. 
2. Have 2-3 solid projects to put on the resume. This is very crucial, as you are 
interviewing for a technical role. So, the interviewer would be more interested to 
check out your current knowledge and gauge your understanding of various tech 
stacks, to know whether you’ll be able to handle any project given to you during the 
internship 
3. Core concepts that you need to know – DSA,DBMS, OS, OOPS. This forms the main 
part of testing in any interview. 
4. Never panic if you don’t answer a couple of questions in the interview. The 
interviewer is just seeing how you would act in different situations. 
5. Prepare well beforehand for Tell me about yourself. Remember - what you say in 
this is going to drive the rest of your interview, so ensure that you highlight all your 
strengths, hobbies and achievements in your introduction. 
6. Maintain a good rapport with the interviewer. That is very crucial. Keep a smile on 
your face throughout. Show them that you are genuinely interested about the role. 
 
If you have any further questions, feel free to reach out to me

About the company: 
Appian Corporation is a leading provider of a low-code automation platform 
that enables organizations to rapidly design, build, and deploy enterprise 
applications with minimal hand-coding. Appian integrates process automation, 
case management, AI, robotic process automation (RPA), business rules, and 
data integration into a unified environment. Its platform empowers businesses 
to streamline workflows, improve operational efficiency, and accelerate digital 
transformation. Appian serves industries such as financial services, 
government, healthcare, and manufacturing, providing both cloud-based and 
on-premises deployment options. 
Online Assessment: 
The Appian online assessment consisted of two primary sections: 
1. Multiple-Choice Questions (MCQs) – These covered a range of topics, 
including: 
o Aptitude: Logical reasoning, numerical problem-solving, and 
puzzles. 
o Basic SQL: Basic use of keywords and retrieval of data from a 
database. 
2. Coding Challenge – This section assessed problem-solving and 
programming skills. The specific task involved writing a program to 
determine whether a given list of strings contained any duplicate values. 
The solution was simple - iterating through the array and returning an 
appropriate result indicating the presence or absence of duplicates. 
Interview: 
The interview began on a cordial note with the classic “Tell me about yourself” 
opener, giving me a chance to set the stage. Without much delay, the 
interviewer presented me with a simple DSA challenge: find the maximum 
element in an array. While this might sound like a warm-up problem, it was 
relevant to the analytical nature of the Quality Engineer role — after all, even 
testers need to think like problem-solvers. 
Next, we started discussing about my projects. One idea in particular caught 
the recruiter’s attention, and the conversation went from “Tell me more” to a 
full-on deep dive into the origin, functionality, and execution of the project. 
This set the tone for the next phase: testing knowledge. I explained the 
different types of testing I had implemented in my “Hospital Appointment 
System” Project, covering Unit Testing, Integration Testing, and Regression 
Testing. 
Then came the main stuff — questions about Artificial Intelligence and 
whether it could design all test cases. I stood my ground, explaining that while 
AI is powerful, it lacks the hands-on experience of a seasoned quality engineer 
and might hallucinate (yes, I used the word) or overlook tricky edge cases. 
When asked why AI couldn’t test a project it had developed, I explained that AI 
is trained on existing data, while a human tester uses real-world insight to 
design precise, context-aware test cases. The interviewer countered that AI 
could do it, but I held my stance — politely but firmly. 
We then shifted to Selenium, where I described how I used it to test a login 
page, walking the interviewer through the process from initiating a browser 
connection to gracefully shutting it down after testing. 
Before wrapping up, I asked about the secret behind Appian’s sustained 
success, and the interviewer’s answer was as flawless as their product pitch. 
Round Two (HR): This was a mix of introductions, “Why Appian?” and “Why 
this role?” — the standard HR essentials. 
Round Three (Final HR): A quick check on whether I was comfortable with the 
role and a subtle nudge to see if I preferred a developer position instead. I 
reaffirmed my interest in Quality Engineering and assured them I would align 
with the company’s principles. 
Tips: 
For a Quality Engineer Intern role, a solid understanding of the Software 
Testing Life Cycle (STLC), Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC), and core 
testing concepts is essential. Focus on fundamental DSA topics such as arrays, 
strings, hash maps, and sets, as these are typically sufficient for the coding 
portion. Familiarity with testing tools like Selenium is a valuable advantage. 
Having a basic understanding of CI/CD pipelines can also strengthen your 
profile. 
Be thoroughly prepared to discuss your projects in detail, as most technical 
questions will stem from your resume. Only list skills and topics in your resume 
that you are confident in, and ensure you can explain them clearly. Be clear in 
whatever you speak and BE CONFIDENT – your body language and attitude tells 
them a lot about you before you speak. Dress professionally, and ensure proper 
grooming to create a strong first impression. 
ALL THE BEST!!

ARCESIUM INTERNSHIP INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 2021 – 22 
Arcesium is a global financial technology and professional services firm, delivering 
solutions to some of the world's most sophisticated financial institutions, including hedge funds, 
banks, and institutional asset managers. 
There were 4 rounds of  selection process, 1 online test (23/08/2021) and 3 technical 
rounds (27/08/2021) . 
Online Test :  (80 mins) 
Platform : HackerRank 
Section 1 : Aptitude – 15 MCQs (20 mins) : 
Questions difficulty level ranged from medium to high. This section had negative marking. 
Section 2 : Technical MCQs – 15 MCQs (15 mins) :  
Questions were challenging and covered a variety of concepts. This section also had negative 
marking. Concepts covered :  
 Predict the output for C++ and java code snippets – based on pointer manipulation in C++, 
inheritance based questions, interfaces in java. 
 DBMS – SQL queries 
 OS – process, memory management, etc. 
Section 3 : Coding – 2 questions (45 mins) 
1. Naruto Clan Curse – It is an application of level-order traversal on binary tree, where we 
have to return the avg of all the unaffected node values from each level. Min value of the 
extreme nodes of each level is calculated. A node is affected when its value is less than the 
above min value.  
2. ATM Change problem – It is a dynamic programming problem. A req amount, preferred 
denomination and set of available denominations are given. We have to return the number 
of  ways to make change. 
Ex : Amount = 1200  Preferred denomination = 500  denominations = [ 100, 200, 500] 
1. 1200 = 500 + 500 + 100 + 100 
2. 1200 = 500 + 500 + 200 
Output : 2 
 
Solution : Modification of  https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/coin-change-dp-7/ 
 
Technical Round 1 : (1 hour) 
This round was a pair coding round. 
I was given a backtracking question. First asked to write the pseudo code and optimize it. At last, 
code it in the language of your choice. 
Question : Given n x n matrix filled with 1, 2, 3 . Find the path with max reward from src to dest. 
1 -> move one cell right 
2 -> move one cell down 
3 -> movie one step either right or down 
1 2 1 2 
3 3 1 2 
1 2 1 2 
3 1 1 2  
Src = (0, 0) 
Dest = (3, 3) 
Max reward path = 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 1 -> 2 -> 2 -> 2 = 13  
 
Technical Round 2 : ( 1 hour) 
This round assessed the technical skills and projects mentioned in the resume. 
1. Asked to explain the database design of the DBMS project mentioned in my resume. 
Discussed about the scalability and potential security issues of the project. 
2. Several concepts of DBMS such as – Indexing, SQL queries, views – were also discussed as 
a part of project discussion. 
3. Brief discussion on several OOPs concepts and Java, as I mentioned Java in my resume, 
on : 
 JVM and platform independency of java 
 Garbage collection in java 
 Exception handling  
 Finalize keyword 
 Virtual functions 
 Method overloading vs over-riding 
 Inheritance 
 Abstract , static in java 
4. Questions from the following OS Concepts were asked : 
 Interrupts 
 Paging & Page fault 
 Thrashing 
 Deadlocks and its avoidance ( Banker’s algorithm) 
 
 
 
Technical Round 3 : (1 hour) 
I was given few coding questions and come up with an algorithm with specified time and space 
complexities. 
Note : Discuss all possible edge cases and efficiency of the algorithm in each case. 
1. Given an array of n consecutive integers where one of the element is over-written by 
another element in the array. Now this array is shuffled and find the element being over-
written with minimum time as possible without any auxiliary space. 
Ex: A[] = [51, 52, 53, 54, 55] -> [51, 52, 53, 52, 55] -> [52, 55, 51, 53, 52] 
      Result = 54 , as 52 over-writes 54 
 
2. Given a sorted array where all elements except one element is repeated twice. Find the 
distinct element with minimum time and O(1) space. 
Ex : A[] = [1, 1, 2, 2, 3, 4, 4, 5, 5]  Result = 3 
 
3. Given an mathematical expression place the parenthesis such that value of the expression 
is maximum. 
Ex : 5*2-4/1+3 
Ref : https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/all-ways-to-add-parenthesis-for-evaluation/ 
 
Key takeaway and concepts to be covered : 
1. Prepare “Tell about yourself” as attractive and concise, covering the key points of your 
resume. Answer confidently and try the questions you don’t know, don’t leave them 
unanswered. They’ll test your knowledge on the skills you mentioned in the resume, so be 
clear on those concepts. 
2. When given a coding question, first explain your intuition and analyze it’s time and space 
complexities and try to optimize it as much as you can. Feel free to use a pen and paper to 
solve and also try to approach the problem mathematically if possible. 
3. DSA – Arrays, Trees, Sorting, Heaps, Backtracking and Dynamic programming – among 
others. 
4. DBMS – Database design and relation schema, Indexing and hashing, SQL, Views, Triggers. 
5. OS – Basics of OS, Process and synchronization, deadlocks, memory management 
(especially Paging) 
6. OOPS – Fundamentals, Inheritance types, Polymorphism types, Singleton class 
implementation (not asked to me, but worth learning). 
7. Revise some common interview questions on the above mentioned concepts. 
8. Company archives in GFG gives a prior idea on the online test and technical interviews. 
Also practice the coding questions asked in the past from GFG Practice.

Amazon Internship Interview Experience

- Dhanya Venkateswaran ( 2020115026 )

About the company
Amazon.com, Inc. is an American multinational technology company that focuses on
e-commerce, cloud computing, digital streaming, and artificial intelligence. Some of its famous
products/services include Alexa, Kindle, Amazon Web Services, Prime Video, etc.

Role details
Offered role: SDE intern (Software Development Engineer)
Tenure: 2 months

Candidate Profile
The candidate's profile is the first thing that an interviewer sees and thus, sets the topic of
discussion for the interview. So, building a strong resume is of utmost importance. Do not
hesitate to mention any kind of achievement/award you have received be it
technical/non-technical. Give a crisp but detailed description of the projects you have worked on
and internships (if any) and be honest about your work/contributions.

During the intern season, you will hear a lot about having a one-page resume and how
advantageous it is. I would say that as long as you have good points to put on your resume, you
need not worry about how long it is. But try your best to keep it crisp and do not add unnecessary
information.

Preparation Strategy
The time taken by a person to achieve the same level of coding proficiency as another varies a
lot. Thus, the first step would be to assess yourself and see where you stand. Generally, 2 to 3
months of consistent coding would be sufficient but if you think you would need more time to
become confident, please give yourself more time.

Along with data structure and algorithms, interviewers might also pose questions on subjects like
OS, DBMS, etc. So, it is advised to have an overview of some of the important concepts in these
subjects like fragmentation, critical section problem, ACID properties, etc. When focusing on
coding, it is important to learn why we are using certain functions as questions based on
important concepts like OOPs can be asked. Time complexity is one of the most important things
that is looked at in an interview so be thorough with it.

Another taboo that I would like to address is that companies prefer students who can code in
C++/C and coding in languages like python would not be suitable. I attended the online test and
interviews of about 6 companies and I coded in python in all of them. What the companies look

 for is if you are able to communicate with them and understand the question and come up with
an optimized solution within the given time. The language you use is not important as long as
you are able to write optimized code for any question.

Some useful books/youtube channels that you could refer to include:

● Cracking the coding interview (Book)
● Grokking algorithms (Book)
● Striver (Youtube Channel)
● CodeBasics (Youtube Channel)
● Jacob Sorber (Youtube Channel)
● Apna College (Youtube Channel)
● William Lin (Youtube Channel - mainly for students interested in competitive

programming)

Preliminary Round
The preliminary round was an online round that was conducted on amazon's own coding
platform. 2 coding questions were asked in this round and 1.5 hours were given to solve them. I
don't remember the exact questions but one of them was a variant of binary search and the other
was a DP problem. The questions were fairly easy and I had solved similar questions in the past
so I was able to finish the test in 15 minutes and it passed all test cases.

Note that you need to come up with an efficient solution as fast as possible to get shortlisted as
somebody who was able to solve the same in an hour may not get selected because of the time
they took as most companies have a set limit to the number of people they can qualify to the next
round.

After the coding test, we were asked to take a work-style assessment which took about 15
minutes to finish.

Technical Round(s)
Around 300 students attended the online test and out of that only 75 students got selected for the
next round. They scheduled one 45-minute coding interview for all of us.

My interview started off with a bunch of questions based on my resume. One of the questions
was to name the most interesting project I have worked on till now and why I found it
interesting. If you are posed with a similar question, I would suggest you talk about the project
where you learned the most and be honest about the difficulties you faced and how you
overcame them. Use any opportunity to highlight your strengths when such questions are posed.

 After this, the first question was presented to me. The question was to detect a cycle in a linked
list. This is a very common question so I was able to solve it in around 10 minutes and I was
asked a bunch of questions on how it would work against edge cases and the time and space
complexity.

Once the interviewer was convinced, he posed the next question which was to explore all
possible paths in a binary search tree and find and print the path at which the sum of the node
values is maximum. For this question, I was asked to come up with my edge cases to test the
code. I used DFS to solve the question. A follow-up question was asked where I was also asked
to print the level at which the maximum sum is encountered but I had run out of time before I
could finish coding it out fully.

The interview ended with me asking a couple of questions about the interviewer's area of work
and his experience working at amazon.

Result
The waiting period was grueling and terrifying as the internship season is very hard on your
mental health. If an interview doesn't work out, you would not have much time to get over it as
the interview for the next company would be days away. I got my result exactly a week after my
interview and I was over cloud nine (and still am 😉) when I got to know that I got the
internship.

Tips
Here are some things that I wish somebody told me before I went through this entire process:

● Go through internship experience blogs

On the day before your interview, read the internship experience blogs of people who attended
interviews at that company. If you are lucky, you might even get the same questions. But either
way, you would be able to understand what they look for in a candidate and how you are
expected to tackle situations.

● Have a good connection with your seniors

Seniors will be your best friends during this season as they would have faced very similar things
in the past. Have a good rapport with them and ask all your questions (dumb or not) to them and
be very clear about the decision you take.

● Manage your time well

In an interview, you are only given 45 to 60 minutes to impress a stranger that you have the skills
to work in their company. In the given time, around 10 to 15 minutes would be taken up in
giving the general introduction. Try to prepare a standard answer to the "Tell me about yourself"
question that covers all your strengths. Thus, try to keep the discussion short and crisp and give
yourself more time to code and showcase your coding skills.

● Understand the hiring process at CUIC

 CUIC is the placement/intern cell of anna university. To ensure all students get placed, they have
certain rules in place. One such is that if you get an internship at a company, you will not be
allowed to attend the tests for the companies coming henceforth. So you either have to accept the
offer or leave the hiring process. If you reject the offer, you will not be allowed to attend the tests
of other companies. The solution is to have a good mix of good companies and safe bets that you
attend the test of.

● It is okay to feel bad

It sucks to face rejections especially when you thought you would get it. Understand that it is
okay and sometimes even luck plays a role in the selection and you might be left off. When you
get rejected, you have to pull your morale up somehow and analyze what you think went wrong.
It is completely okay to rant but do not let it derail you from the process.

Please reach out to me via my phone number if you have any doubts: +91 9789002028.

I hope this was useful and wish you all the best for your interviews!

Amazon Experience

**About the Company**:

Amazon is a multinational technology and e-commerce company founded by Jeff Bezos on July 5, 1994. Originally launched as an online bookstore, Amazon has since expanded to become one of the largest and most diverse online retailers in the world, offering everything from electronics and clothing to groceries and digital content.

**About the Role**:

The Software Development Engineer (SDE) Intern role at Amazon offers students a unique opportunity to gain hands-on experience in software development within one of the world’s leading technology companies. As an SDE intern, you will work on real projects that directly impact Amazon’s customers, involving tasks such as designing, coding, testing, and deploying software solutions. You’ll collaborate with experienced engineers, participate in code reviews, and engage in design discussions, all while solving complex technical problems that require innovative approaches. Amazon provides a supportive environment with mentorship and access to internal resources to help you grow professionally. This internship not only allows you to apply your programming skills and problem-solving abilities but also gives you a chance to contribute to meaningful projects that can shape the future of Amazon’s services. For those who excel, the internship often serves as a pathway to a full-time SDE position at Amazon, making it a valuable stepping stone in your technology career.

**Overview of the Process:**

The overall process consisted of

* An Online coding contest in Hackerank platform
* Interview Round 1 (Only DSA & Problem Solving) – Online mode

**HackerRank Amazon Online Round Experience:**

In the Amazon online round conducted on HackerRank, I encountered two medium-difficulty coding problems. These problems primarily focused on topics such as arrays, strings, dynamic programming, graphs, prefix sums, and binary search.

One important aspect of the Amazon online test is the emphasis on behavioral questions, which are asked after the coding section. Even if you partially pass the test cases for both coding problems, performing well in the behavioral questions can significantly enhance your chances of getting shortlisted.

Out of more than 400 participants, 85 candidates were shortlisted for the next round.

**Interview Round 1 (DSA Round):**

**Must Do in Coding interview**

**Think Out Loud**: Verbalize your thoughts and reasoning throughout the problem-solving process to keep the interviewer engaged and informed about your approach.

**Ask Clarifying Questions**: Before diving into the solution, ensure you fully understand the problem by asking any necessary clarifying questions. This demonstrates your attention to detail and prevents misunderstandings.

**Start with a Brute-Force Approach**: Begin by discussing the brute-force solution, as it shows that you can identify a basic starting point. Then, work towards optimizing it, if possible.

**Use First Principles Thinking**: Break down the problem into its fundamental components and analyze them. Explain your observations and thought process to the interviewer as you work through the solution.

**Keep the Interviewer Engaged**: Avoid long periods of silence. Keep the conversation flowing by sharing your thoughts, asking for feedback, and explaining each step of your approach.

**Focus on Thought Process, Not Just the Optimal Solution**: Interviewers are often more interested in how you approach the problem than whether you arrive at the most optimal solution. Demonstrate a clear and logical thought process.

**General Advice While Preparing:**

Focus on **problem-solving** rather than just memorizing patterns from SDE sheets. While SDE sheets are a valuable tool for revising concepts, the best way to truly learn data structures and algorithms (DSA) is through active problem-solving. If you have ample time, consider starting with platforms like Codeforces, where you can work on ad-hoc and greedy problems. These problems help build strong problem-solving skills that will be invaluable when tackling standard LeetCode-type questions. Above all, be consistent in your practice, regardless of immediate results. Consistency is key to long-term success.

Amazon Internship Interview Experience

-Kabileshwaran SD ( 2021115046 )

About the company

Amazon is a global tech company that started as an online bookstore and grew into one of the
largest e-commerce platforms. It lead in cloud computing with AWS, innovate with devices
like Echo, and focus on customer obsession, operational excellence, and continuous
innovation.

Role details Offered role:

SDE Intern (Software Development Engineer)
Tenure: 2 months

Preliminary Round

The preliminary round was conducted online using Amazon's own coding platform. In this
round, there were 2 coding questions that needed to be solved within 1.5 hours. While I don't
recall the exact wording of the questions, the first question was like

You are given a list of product names as strings, representing the order in which customers
have interacted with them on Amazon's platform. You need to determine the order of these
product names based on their frequency of occurrence. If two product names have the same
frequency, the one that appears second or more times before the other should come first.
Write a function that returns a list of product names sorted by these criteria.

Input:

A list of strings where each string represents a product name.

Output:

A list of strings sorted by frequency of occurrence. If two product names have the same
frequency, the one that appears for the last time before the other in the input list should
appear first in the output list.

Example:

Input: ["Laptop", "Phone", "Tablet", "Laptop", "Phone", "Tablet", "Tablet"]
Output: ["Tablet", "Laptop", "Phone"]

Explanation:




"Tablet" appears 3 times.
"Laptop" appears 2 times.

 

"Phone" appears 2 times.

Since "Tablet" has the highest frequency, it appears first. "Laptop" and "Phone" have the
same frequency, but "Tablet" appears before "Phone" for the second time, so "Tablet" comes
before "Phone" in the output.

The second question was related to some mathematical operation with the string. I can’t
remember the exact question and it was a medium level question.

I managed to solve both coding problems in the preliminary round within 20-25 minutes.
After the coding test, there was a mandatory work-level assessment, consisting of around 60
questions focused on Amazon's Leadership Principles. Many candidates mentioned that they
completed both coding problems but chose to skip this work-level assessment, which resulted
in them not being shortlisted. Therefore, it is crucial to complete the work-level assessment to
proceed in the selection process.

For more information on Amazon's Leadership Principles, you can refer to this link.

Technical Interview:

Around 400 students attended the online test and out of that only 20 students got selected for
the next round.

We received a 1-hour time slot for an online interview the day before the interview, which
took place on Amazon Chime. At the start of the interview, the interviewer sent a code editor
link via email, where he pasted the question. I was required to write my solution directly in
that code editor.

The interviewer, an SDE-II from Amazon's Bangalore office, briefly introduced himself and
mentioned that he had reviewed my resume, so he did not need an introduction from me. The
interview began with a question related to a payment system project I had worked on. After
explaining my solution, he seemed satisfied. He then casually asked how my day was going,
to which I mentioned that I had a compiler exam that morning. This led to questions about the
usefulness of studying compilers, which I answered by explaining their relevance.

The interviewer then moved on to the coding question, which was a variation of the three-
sum problem. The task was to return all unique combinations, even if there were repetitive
elements. I first explained the brute-force approach and then moved on to the optimal
solution, detailing the time and space complexities for each. After the explanation, he asked
me to write the code in the code editor, and we conducted a dry run together. He also inquired
about the time complexities of operations like insertion, search, and delete in map and set.

During the interview, the interviewer briefly asked me to explain Binary Search Trees (BST).
He then gave me a BST-related question: I was asked to perform preprocessing on a given
BST so that after this preprocessing, a preorder traversal would return the values in
descending order. I explained and implemented a solution using the mirror algorithm.
Reference. The interviewer was surprised that I solved it within 5 minutes. He then asked
about the time complexity of this recursive solution, followed by questions about space
complexity, such as the number of stacks created, the worst-case conditions, and the number

 of stacks if the tree is skewed. We discussed further details related to both time and space
complexities.

At the end of the interview, he asked if I had any questions. I inquired about the work culture
at Amazon and asked him about his personal experience working there.

Result: The following afternoon, I was preparing for my Lab exam when, around 11 am, I
received news from CUIC that out of 20 candidates, only 2 had been shortlisted, and I was
one of them. I was so happy about the result that I almost forgot I had an exam that
afternoon! 😅

Tips:

  Always start by explaining the brute-force solution, even if you already know the

optimal approach as they will assess your thought process too.

  Stay engaged during the interview; avoid being silent.

Preparation Docs:

For my preparation, I personally followed Striver's A2Z DSA sheet, which you can find here.
This resource helped me understand and cover most of the patterns in DSA. After learning
each new topic, I also solved random questions from LeetCode and GeeksforGeeks to
reinforce my understanding. Additionally, I followed Abdul Bari's DSA Algorithms playlist,
which provided a solid foundation in various algorithms.

Instead of focusing on solving a large number of similar problems, I recommend trying to
solve a variety of different patterns. This approach not only prepares you for unexpected
questions but also builds a deeper understanding of DSA concepts.

All the very best! Feel free to reach out to me if you have any doubts.

About the company

Amazon is a global tech company that started as an online bookstore and grew into one of the
largest e-commerce platforms. It lead in cloud computing with AWS, innovate with devices
like Echo, and focus on customer obsession, operational excellence, and continuous
innovation.

Role details Offered role:

SDE Intern (Software Development Engineer)
Tenure: 6 months

Preliminary Round

The preliminary round was conducted online using Hackerank’s coding platform. In this
round, there were 2 coding questions that needed to be solved within 1.5 hours

Question 1: Finding a Common Regex Pattern

Problem Description: You are tasked with developing a service that finds a common regex
pattern from a given set of patterns. The regex patterns consist of lowercase English letters
and wildcard characters (?) which can match any single English letter. Two patterns are said
to intersect if you can replace the wildcard characters in both patterns to form the same string
from both patterns.

The goal is to find the minimum number of wildcard characters (?) required in a pattern that
intersects with all the given patterns.

Reference: https://www.glassdoor.ca/Interview/Amazon-is-developing-a-string-matching-
library-and-you-are-to-develop-a-service-that-finds-a-common-regex-pattern-from-the-
QTN_7518037.htm

Initially I tried to solve this in brute force only few tast cases has been passed. Then I solved
this using DP where all test cases has been passed.

Question 2: Minimum Network Lag

Problem Description: The second question was comparatively straightforward. It required
finding the minimum network lag between given source and destination servers. Specifically,
you are provided with two arrays: one representing data centers and the other representing
destination servers. The task is to compute the minimum network lag.

 Input: datacentres = [1, 2, 2], servers = [2, 4, 5]

Output: 6

Approach:

1.  Sorting:

o  To minimize the total network lag, I sorted both the datacentre and destination

servers arrays.

2.  Compute Lag:

o  After sorting, I calculated the lag by summing the absolute differences

between corresponding elements from the two sorted arrays.

In this example, after sorting:

  datacentres = [1, 2, 2]
servers = [2, 4, 5]


The minimum network lag is calculated as:



|1 - 2| + |2 - 4| + |2 - 5| = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6

I managed to solve both coding problems in the preliminary round within 25-30 minutes.
After the coding test, there was a mandatory work-level assessment, consisting of around 60
questions focused on Amazon's Leadership Principles. Many candidates mentioned that they
completed both coding problems but chose to skip this work-level assessment, which resulted
in them not being shortlisted. Therefore, it is crucial to complete the work-level assessment to
proceed in the selection process.

For more information on Amazon's Leadership Principles, you can refer to this link.

Technical Interview:

Around 400 students attended the online test, and out of those, 87 students were selected for
the next round. We received a 1-hour time slot for an online interview the day before, which
took place on Amazon Chime. At the start of the interview, the interviewer sent a code editor
link via email where the coding question was pasted. I was required to write my solution
directly in that code editor.

The interviewer, an SDE-II from Amazon's Bangalore office with 4.5 years of experience,
briefly introduced himself and asked for my introduction. After I introduced myself, the
interviewer moved on to the coding question.

Coding Question 1: Task Scheduling

The first question was about task scheduling. Given a number of tasks N and task
dependencies, where [T1, T2] means Task 2 needs to be completed before Task 1, and [T1,
T3] means Task 3 needs to be completed before Task 1, you were to find a valid order of

 tasks. This problem is essentially a topological sort. This question is similar to the "Course
Schedule" problem on LeetCode.

I explained Kahn's algorithm for topological sorting and demonstrated a dry run with a
provided test case. I also noted that the algorithm would fail if there were cyclic dependencies
among the tasks. After the dry run, the interviewer asked me to code the solution. Once I
completed the coding, we did another dry run together.

After coding, the interviewer asked about the space and time complexity of the solution. I
explained that the time complexity is O(V+E) where V is the number of vertices (tasks) and
E is the number of edges (dependencies). I elaborated on how this complexity is derived from
the algorithm's operations. The interviewer also inquired about optimizing the solution, and I
mentioned the possibility of executing tasks in parallel using threads.

Coding Question 2: Trapping Rain Water

The next question involved finding the amount of water that can be trapped between
buildings of different heights after a rain. This question is similar to the "Trapping Rain
Water" problem on LeetCode.

Initially, I provided a solution with space complexity of O(2n) and time complexity of O(n)
and then I wrote the code. The interviewer then asked me to optimize the space complexity to
O(1) I explained the two-pointer approach to achieve this and the interviewer was satisfied
with the explanation.

Conclusion

At the end of the interview, the interviewer asked if I had any questions. Since I had interned
at Amazon for two months, I was familiar with the work culture and related aspects, so I said
I had no questions. The interviewer wished me all the best and ended the meeting 5 minutes
before the scheduled end time.

Tips:

  Always start by explaining the brute-force solution, even if you already know the

optimal approach as they will assess your thought process too.

  Stay engaged during the interview; avoid being silent.

  Be clear while explaining your thought process/approach

Preparation Docs:

  To cover all patterns, I followed Striver's A2Z DSA Sheet. For example, when learning

graphs, start with BFS, DFS, and distance algorithms, then practice related problems on

LeetCode and GeeksforGeeks.

   Develop a strong understanding of time and space complexity.

  Learn various data structures and their standard problems.

  If you can't immediately solve a problem, discuss your approach and thought process.

  Focus on solving a diverse range of problems rather than just a high volume of similar

ones. This strategy prepares you for unexpected questions and enhances your

understanding of DSA concepts.

All the very best! Feel free to reach out to me if you have any doubts.

**AMAZON PLACEMENT PROCESS**

**ABOUT THE COMPANY:**

Amazon is a multinational technology and e-commerce company founded by Jeff Bezos on July 5, 1994. Initially launched as an online bookstore, Amazon has grown into one of the largest global retailers, offering a wide range of products including electronics, apparel, home goods, and digital content. In addition to its retail operations, Amazon is a leader in cloud computing through Amazon Web Services (AWS), which provides cloud-based solutions to businesses worldwide. The company is also known for innovations like the Kindle e-reader, Echo smart speakers, and its popular subscription service, Amazon Prime.

**ABOUT THE ROLE:**

The Software Development Engineer (SDE) role at Amazon is a highly technical position focused on designing, developing, and maintaining software systems that can scale to meet Amazon's massive customer demands. SDEs work on solving complex problems in areas like distributed systems, cloud computing, optimization, and machine learning. They collaborate closely with cross-functional teams, including product managers, designers, and other engineers, to build innovative, customer-centric solutions. SDEs are responsible for writing high-quality, efficient code, improving system architecture, and ensuring scalability and reliability of services. The role demands strong problem-solving skills, a deep understanding of data structures, algorithms, and system design, and proficiency in programming languages like Java, C++, or Python.

**OVERVIEW OF THE PROCESS:**

The Placement Process for Amazon India consists of the following stages:

1.**Online Test:** Conducted on the Hackerrank platform and lasts for 70 minutes. It consists of two coding questions:

1. **TwoSum Divisible by a Number**: Given an array and a number, you are required to return the count of two-sum pairs that are divisible by the given number. This problem tests your understanding of hash maps and efficient counting techniques. The **difficulty level** is **medium.**
2. **Regex Intersection**: You are given an array of regex patterns consisting of lowercase English letters and wildcard characters ('?'). Two patterns are said to intersect if the wildcard characters can be replaced to form the same string from both patterns. You are tasked with finding the minimum number of '?' characters in a pattern that intersects with all patterns. The **difficulty level** for this problem is **medium**, as it involves complex string manipulation and optimization with time complexity constraints (1 ≤ m\*n ≤ 1e5).

The test also includes a **behavioural round**. If you perform well in this round, even if you do not fully solve the coding problems, you still have a chance of being shortlisted, as Amazon emphasizes leadership principles and customer-centric problem-solving.

Out of 1100 participants, 85 participants were shortlisted for interview round.

**INTERVIEW ROUND 1:**

After starting with an introduction where the interviewer asked me to explain my project, then the interviewer moved on to coding challenges. The problems were:

1. **Find the Maximum Element in Each Subarray of Size K**: You are given an array, and the task is to find the maximum element in every continuous subarray of size K. For example, given [1,2,3,4,5] with K=3, the result would be [3, 4, 5]. Initially, a brute force solution can be presented, followed by an optimized solution using a deque (double-ended queue) to maintain the window of maximums efficiently. The **difficulty level** is **medium**.
2. **Cycle Detection in a Linked List**: The problem involves detecting if there is a cycle in a linked list. The optimal approach is to use Floyd’s Tortoise and Hare algorithm, which uses two pointers to detect cycles efficiently. The **difficulty level** is **medium**.

The interviewer also pays attention to your problem-solving approach, communication, and thought process during the interview.

**MUST DO IN CODING INTERVIEW:**

* **Understand the Problem**: Clarify the problem statement and ensure you fully grasp the requirements before starting. Ask questions to resolve any ambiguities.
* **Plan Your Approach:** Outline a clear strategy and discuss it with the interviewer. Explain your choice of algorithms and data structures before coding.
* **Write Clean, Efficient Code:** Write organized, readable code with meaningful variable names. Aim for optimal solutions in terms of time and space complexity.
* **Test Your Solution:** Validate your code with different test cases, including edge cases, to ensure correctness. Be thorough in checking for potential errors.
* **Communicate Clearly:** Verbally walk through your thought process and code. Explain your reasoning and be open to feedback, showing your problem-solving skills and adaptability.

Shruthi Balaji - Information Technology

Software Engineering Intern ( + Full Time Offer on Performance) - Amazon India, January 2025 - June 2025

## **About Amazon India:**

Amazon is a company guided by four principles - customer obsession rather than competitor focus, passion for invention, commitment to operational excellence and long term thinking. Amazon strives to be Earth’s most customer centric company, Earth’s best employer and Earth’s safest place to work. Customer reviews, 1-Click shopping, personalized recommendations, Prime, Fulfillment by Amazon, AWS, Kindle Direct Publishing, Kindle, Career Choice, Fire tablets, Fire TV, Amazon Echo, Alexa, Just Walk Out technology, Amazon Studios, and The Climate Pledge are some of the things pioneered by Amazon.

Amazon as a company strives to hire focussed and innovative individuals who fit their ideals of the ‘perfect employer’ as discussed in their Leadership Principles. These principles are what makes Amazon the company of its caliber.

Some of their Leadership Principles are titled as follows:

1. Customer Obsession
2. Ownership
3. Invent and Simplify
4. Are Right, A Lot (focussed on strong judgment and good instincts of a leader)
5. Learn and Be Curious
6. Hire and Develop the Best
7. Insist on the Highest Standards
8. Think Big
9. Bias for Action (perform calculated risk taking, speed matters in business)
10. Frugality
11. Earn Trust
12. Dive Deep
13. Have Backbone; Disagree and Commit
14. Deliver Results
15. Strive to be Earth’s Best Employer
16. Success and Scale Bring Broad Responsibility

Amazon started from a garage, but they are not there anymore. Their impact on the world and the environment is undeniable. Amazon begins each day with a determination to make better, do better and be better for the sake of their customers, their employers and their partners. They lead everyday knowing they can do better tomorrow.

**Revisiting landmark moments in Amazon India’s Journey**

2013 - the amazon.in journey begins

2014 - Amazon makes a long term financial commitment to India

2015 - Amazon’s Great Indian Festival goes live

2016 - Prime launches with free and fast deliveries

2017 - Empowers India’s micro-entrepreneurs and women sellers

2018 - Launches the Hindi shopping experience

2019 - Expands reach with Indian Railways

2020 - Amazon pledges to grow with India

2021- Makes a bet on Indian Startups

2022 - Doubles exports pledge to $20 billion

2023 - Amazon Air gets wings in India

## **Software Engineering at Amazon**

Amazon works on “Service Oriented Architecture,” which is a software design style in which different services interact with each other through different communication protocols. Software engineers at Amazon are responsible for designing, developing and maintaining various software systems and applications that power the company’s e-commerce platform, cloud computing services, and other digital products and services (which have been listed previously)

**Some key responsibilities of Software Engineers at Amazon include:**

* Developing and enhancing the core of Amazon’s websites, mobile apps and other customer-facing applications to improve the user experience
* Building and scaling the distributed systems and backend infrastructure that support Amazon’s high volume transaction processing, inventory management, fulfillment and logistics operations
* Designing, implementing and maintaining the software that runs Amazon’s web services including EC2, S3, DynamoDB, and other AWS offerings
* Creating and optimizing the algorithms and models that power Amazon’s recommendation engines, search functionality and other data-intensive features
* Integrating new technologies and implementing innovative solutions to address the evolving business requirements and enhance operational efficiency
* Participating in the full software development life cycle, including requirements gathering, design, coding, testing, deployment and maintenance
* Collaborating with cross functional teams, including product managers, data scientists, and other engineering disciplines to deliver high quality, customer-centric software.

## **Overview of the Process:**

1. Application to Amazon India in the CUIC Portal.
2. Pre-Placement Talk
3. Online Coding Round
4. Technical Interview
5. Offer Acceptance

## **Application to Amazon India in the CUIC Portal: Low Effort, But is it Really?**

A short process, often done with excitement and hope. It is preferred that the candidate applies to the job offer as early as possible, as this may be a factor that could account for a candidate’s selection to further rounds.

Normally, it is important to ensure that all the necessary documents have been collected and stored in an easily accessible location, on the personal system. This ensures that if the company (in Amazon’s case, this actually happened) requests the candidate to apply to the company’s job portal, last minute frustrations can be avoided.

The actual application process in the Amazon Job portal was standard, and simple. Not many documents are needed, except for the candidate’s resume.

Note: Although a company like Amazon is vast, and employees’ experiences in the company have been recorded for the aspiring candidate to review, it is very very essential for him or her to thoroughly research the company and the role before applying. “Thorough” research is preferred as many companies may not have enough resources and reviews to read, but this must not stop anyone from trying.

## **Pre-Placement Talk: The Overlooked Samaritan**

The importance of pre-placement talk has not been stressed upon students enough. It is merely described as a session where “people from the company just come and talk,” which in all technicalities, it is. However, these are not just any people. Most of the time, it is alumni from the college, who are now comfortable in the company, taking time out of their day to attend this session. Which essentially means that any and every input they give is valuable, and can be *the factor* that determines whether a candidate gets selected or not.

A company’s Pre- Placement talk is the best place to not only understand the role, but to also understand the work environment, work life balance, and the additional facilities and benefits that the company provides. It is a rare chance to interact with employees from the organization, and understand *what exactly they are looking for in a future employee*.

Amazon’s Pre-Placement talk lasted roughly an hour, and it involved two sessions - the interactive question and answer session, and an additional problem solving session. The interactive session involved two of Amazon’s employees, a Senior Manager at Amazon, and a Software Development Engineer (who also happened to have MIT as his alma mater). They explained various projects at Amazon that they have worked on, their growth trajectory in the company and some interesting insights on projects. They were also able to provide some clarity on what is Amazon’s expectation from a candidate, and stressed on the importance of *Amazon’s Leadership Principles*.

The problem solving session was particularly important, and was conducted by two Software Development Engineers, and involved a discussion around one problem (Link to a similar problem: <https://leetcode.com/discuss/interview-question/2061238/Google-or-Onsite>), where the students were asked to not only find the optimal solution but also code it out on a sheet of paper (The solution to the problem they specifically asked for was Breadth First Search, and it was the optimal solution that ran in linear time)\*

In essence, Pre-Placement talks are very essential to understand what the expectation for an employee is, and can help resolve a lot of internal conflicts on whether or not a company is the right fit for the candidate.

## **Online Coding Round: The Frustrating Part.**

The Online Coding Round, often referred to as “Online Assessment (OA)” is a very mentally taxing round for many candidates. This particular round involves hours of intense preparation to be able to get ahead of the “competition” per say, by solving questions quicker than the majority. It involves practicing Data Structures and Algorithms diligently from platforms like LeetCode, CodeForces, and Hackerrank.

Normally, the selection criteria for the online coding round is a black box. Most candidates are not aware of why exactly they were not selected for the forthcoming rounds, despite performing exceptionally well. For some companies, the criteria involves a combination of a candidate’s grade point average, along with his or her speed (essentially how quickly they finished the assessment). For others, the criteria involves how optimally the solution of the problem has been coded out.

The online assessment involves two components:

1. Coding Problems
2. Multiple Choice Problems (Technical or Behavioural)

Amazon’s open assessment involved two coding problems, and one behavioral section.

### **Coding problems:**

Difficult coding questions are the heart of Amazon’s online assessment. They are difficult not only in terms of execution but also optimization. Normally, the online assessment involves two coding problems, and can vary from candidate to candidate. They are more often than not Leetcode Medium or Hard problems, and in order to solve them quickly, the candidate must have solved a similar or the exact problem in the past. Brute Force solutions are looked down upon, and based on the programming language that the candidate selects, may not even run successfully. Thus, the only way to pass this round successfully is to be able to understand the algorithm behind the question and tailor it to handle all possible constraints of the problem.

Amazon cares more about the optimality of the solution than completeness. If a Leetcode Hard problem was solved successfully with the most optimum solution, then the chances of the candidate being selected are quite high, despite the performance in the subsequent questions. Amazon values not just correctness and completeness but also the quality of the code. One good quality solution is better than two mediocre ones.

Here is the question everyone asks: How can I get Better? The answer to that is simply practice. Practice, Practice, Practice, with spaced repetition, not just the complex algorithms but also the more fundamental ones. Every complicated problem has a simple solution behind it, the only difficult aspect is to get to the simplest possible solution. Once the solution or solving logic is obtained, coding it is merely about syntactic skills and modularity or reusability of the code (how clean the code is).

### **Behavioral Round:**

Amazon’s Behavioral round involved up to 60 questions to test a candidate’s leadership capability and people skills. It involves questions that are deeply concerned with the candidate’s personality and their outlook on various scenarios.

Each question involved two statements that dictate an outlook of a situation, on either end of a spectrum rated with numbers and a neutral ground. Although the statements may initially seem mutually exclusive and completely unrelated, on further reflection it can be understood that they represent two contradictory personalities.

This round must be taken just as seriously as the coding round, as Amazon takes capability and leadership skills to be just as important, if not more important, compared to technical skills

The aggregate of the two sections took two hours, give or take a few minutes. The entire process is quite tiring, but it is important to remain alert, vigilant and mentally present throughout the duration of the test to perform well.

## **4. Technical Interview: Almost There, but Not Quite.**

Getting selected for the interview is hard. Getting interviewed is even harder. Amazon is known for being particularly rigorous with their interviews, therefore more than technical capabilities, mental presence and strength is extremely essential.

Amazon only had one round of interviews, which was purely technical. The interviewers are normally working as Software Development Engineers themselves, thus making them very very technically competent. Interviewers are often very alert, and even when a candidate thinks they are not paying attention, they are.

The interview was *virtual*, and the candidates were mailed the meeting IDs and a link to Amazon’s LiveCode (an online text editor, where the interviewer can see the solution being typed out as the candidate is typing). The candidates are normally asked to share their screens.

It is important to note that fleeting eyes and looking down during the interview is discouraged. If the candidate requires a piece of paper to work things out in the middle of the virtual interview, he or she must gently request the interviewer if it is okay to use one. This establishes a sense of trust between the interviewer and the candidate, and lets the interviewer know that they are still in control of the situation.

The technical interview had two coding questions, which have been listed here:

1. Palindrome Linked List: This is a classic linked list problem on leetcode, (Link to the problem: <https://leetcode.com/problems/palindrome-linked-list/description/>), which involves- finding the middle of the linked list, reversing the linked list from the middle, and checking simultaneously (with pointers to the start and end of the list) if the condition for a list to be palindrome is satisfied.
   I had initially given a solution that runs in linear time and occupies constant space. However, despite this being an optimal solution, I was asked to further optimize it by reducing the number of loops from three to two. This involved reversing the links right from the beginning as I was trying to get to the middle of the list. There were a lot of redirects from the interviewer for me to be able to get to this solution, however it is important to note that *interviewers do not mind helping the candidate. There is a difference between receiving hints and receiving the solution.* An interviewer may redirect the candidate once or twice, but if the candidate is unable to start thinking on the right track, he may move onto another question. This is not a good sign. I was able to stay on the right track and get to the solution the interviewer had asked of me, thus finishing the first problem.
2. Rotting Oranges: This is another classic Breadth First Search problem (Link to the problem: <https://leetcode.com/problems/rotting-oranges/> ). I was able to give the optimal solution immediately, after explaining my understanding of the problem and elaborating on various edge cases. However, as I had mentioned Depth First Search cannot be applied to this problem (this being multisource BFS, and involved finding the minimal time), the interviewer continued to ask me of different data structures, additional storage or any other method that can make DFS optimal for this problem too. I was asked to optimize my solution to this problem at least 3 times further, before I was asked to code it out on the platform.

**Key takeaways:**

* Always explain the actual problem statement to the interviewer, despite knowing or understanding it. This helps the interviewer see if the candidate has actually read through every single constraint, and has visualized all possible edge cases or not.
* Ask the interviewer to list some additional test cases, if any. This helps clear up any confusions or misunderstanding between the candidate and the interviewer. Try to ask as many questions as possible, if the solution to the problem is not immediately apparent.
* The candidate is allowed to take time to think in a technical interview. He or she can request the interviewer to allow that. During this time, it is important to voice out the train of thoughts, so the interviewer is able to understand how the candidate thinks. If needed, the interviewer will redirect the candidate to the right track.
* Always explain the solution, step by step, before coding it out on the platform. Explaining the solution can help the interviewer understand what is to come. It helps them understand whether or not the candidate’s solution is optimal. While coding the problem, it is important to explain every line of code as it is being coded.
* Never begin with the most optimal solution. Always begin with brute force, and slowly optimize it as time progresses. This helps the interviewer understand that the candidate is capable of finding a solution, then fine tuning it to fit the constraints, and make it optimal in a systematic manner.

## **5. Offer Acceptance: It’s Finally Over.**

The mental war is finally over. The list of candidates have been released, and the only thing left to do is to send you acceptance mail to the required email address of the placement cell, and fill any feedback form if need be.

The entire process of placement is a rite of passage into the real world, and can be both taxing and exhilarating. Mental exhaustion and feeling hopeless is all a part of it, and it is important to understand that there is in fact a light at the end of the tunnel. Continuous practice, mock interviews, and a good support system are essential during this difficult period. Finally, I would like to thank Centre for University Industry Collaboration (CUIC, Anna University) for this life changing opportunity.

Amazon Interview Experience
