studied in OS. I listed a few topics (general introduction to OS and its functionalities, threads, 
process synchronization, deadlocks and virtual memory). The follow back questions on the 
topics were: 
• What is a thread? Difference between a process and a thread. 
• What are deadlocks? 
• What is the difference between mutex and semaphore?  
With this, we moved on to the coding pair of the interview. The HackerRank link was 
shared for the code-pair part. There were totally 4 questions in this part of the interview. 
• Consider the below pseudocode and predict the output if main function is called. 
public class abc { 
 
public void f() { 
 
 
f(); 
 
} 
 
public void main() { 
 
 
f(); 
 
} 
}; 
We had a long discussion on this question, wherein I was not able to come up with the 
correct answer the first time. But, with the hints given by the interviewer, I was able to 
answer it up to some point, after which we moved on to the second question. 
 
• The 
second 
question 
was 
“Detect 
loop 
in 
a 
linked 
list" 
- 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/detect-loop-in-a-linked-list/. The interviewer explained 
me the question well with 2 examples, and asked me to code. I explained a simple 
approach, which will work if the maximum limit of nodes is known and then explained the

approach and try to optimize it further). The interviewer told to just write the function

• What will be the output of the following cases? 
i. 
int a = 10; int b = 20; 
… 
if(a==11 && b==20) { … } 
else { … } 
Which part of the if-else statement will be executed and what are all the 
expressions that are evaluated (whether b==20 will be executed or not)? 
 
ii. 
int a = 10; int b = 20; 
… 
if(a=11 && b==20) { … } 
else { … } 
What will be output now and what happens here? 
 
iii. 
int a = 10; int b = 20; 
… 
if(a=11 & b==20) { … } 
else { … } 
What will be the final evaluation of condition given inside the if statement? 
All these questions were on the basics of C Programming and I answered these 
questions satisfactorily. 
• What will be printed if this program gets executed? 
void main() { 
 
int* b = f(); 
 
printf(“%d”,*b); 
} 
int* f() { 
 
int c = 10; 
 
return &c; 
    } 
For this question, I was not able to come up with the correct answer the first time. But 
the interviewer gave me hints to come up with the correct answer. After that, I was able 
to provide a correct answer, with which the interviewer seemed satisfied. 
With this the interview got over. Before leaving, he asked me if I had any questions to 
ask. I asked him two questions which I prepared beforehand. He answered my questions and 
took leave. The results were out by 10 pm the same night. And, finally 11 of us were selected 
for the summer internship programme. 
 
TOPICS TO FOCUS: 
• Data Structures and Algorithms 
• Operating Systems 
• Database Management Systems 
• Object Oriented Programming Concepts 
• Whatever you’ve mentioned in your resume 
 
RESOURCES THAT WERE USEFUL FOR PREPARATION: 
• “Must 
to 
do 
coding 
questions” 
available 
in 
GeeksforGeeks 
- 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/must-do-coding-questions-for-companies-like-
amazon-microsoft-adobe/ 
• Resources shared by ACM – Student Chapter of CEG through CODE Intern Preparation 
programme. 
 
KEY TAKEAWAYS: 
• Always be in touch with DSA. Keep solving coding questions on platforms like LeetCode, 
HackerRank, GeeksforGeeks and HackerEarth. Consistency in solving coding questions is 
an important factor. Do try to solve 2 to 3 problems everyday if possible. 
• Know to code from scratch as well. Some companies expect you to code from scratch in 
the online assessment. So, kindly make sure you are thorough with the header files and 
syntax of the programming language well. (If you are using STL in cpp, make sure you 
know the header files for each of the elements used in the program and the syntax for 
each of the functions used with the template). 
• Once you have been shortlisted for the interview round by any company, kindly go 
through the interview experiences of that particular company available online. 
“GeeksforGeeks Archives (or) Interview Experiences” helped me a lot to get a basic idea 
on how the interview will proceed. Not only for the interviews, this helps for the online 
assessment rounds as well. 
• Prepare for “Tell me about yourself” beforehand. Make sure it is short and crisp. 
• Read and re-read your resume. If questioned on your resume (projects as well), you 
should be able to answer it satisfactorily. So, be thorough with whatever you’ve 
mentioned in your resume. 
• Before attending any interview, do research on the company’s profile. This gives you a 
basic idea on how the company functions, it’s mission and goal as well. If the interviewer 
asks “What do you know about the company”, you should be able to answer it. 
• Be honest and don’t panic. Stay calm even if you don’t know an answer for a question. If 
struck at any point, don’t hesitate to say “I’m not sure about this, I will definitely look into 
it”.  The interviewer will help by giving hints as well. 
• Don’t lose hope during the interview, thinking that you have not done well so far. This 
may affect you throughout the rest of the interview and that is not what the interviewer 
expects from you. 
• During the code-pair round, if you are asked to code, explain the lines of code while you 
are coding. This gives the interviewer an idea that you are going with the flow of the 
approach and you have understood the approach well. Also, give sensible names for 
identifiers and functions during code-pair part of the interview. 
• Prepare well for “HR Questions” too. Along with the general HR questions, based on the 
soft skills mentioned on your resume, you might be expected to narrate an incident where 
you think you possessed the particular skill. So, be prepared with narrating such incidents 
as well. 
• Always stay motivated and optimistic throughout the period of preparation. Never get 
upset, if you don’t get through the process of any company. You will definitely get a better 
company than that.

About Athenahealth:

At athenahealth we’re using technology to transform the
way our friends and family get medical care and how doctors
treat patients. We bring cloud-based web and mobile software
to the healthcare industry to build an information backbone
with the power to transform health delivery.

      AthenaOne is athenahealth’s integrated EHR (Electronic
Health Records), medical billing & practice management, and
patient engagement solution that leverages AI to transform
your performance.

About the Role:

Role: Product Engineering

We’re looking for developers who want to work on challenging
technology problems to create products that medical
professionals and patients use every day.

We work with a mix of technologies, but don’t hire developers
based on particular coding language.

• We're more interested in folks with raw talent and a burning
desire to succeed. The most important requirement for

 applying is a passion to learn and to make a difference
developing enabling technologies.

• Ability to learn and adapt quickly

• Experience reading and writing code in a programming
language

• Experience working collaboratively towards project goals and
deadlines

• Computer Science (CS) or relevant technical degree program
is a plus, though we will consider non-traditional, non-CS
degreed applicants.

Selection Process:

Day 1:

1. Online Assessment (In Offline Mode)

2. Technical Round

3. Technical + Managerial Round

Day 2:

4. HR Round

Round 1: Online Assessment

[1 hr 30 mins]

Online Assessment was conducted at CUIC in hacker rank
under the monitoring of company staffs. The online assessment
had 3 parts

i)  MCQ question on OOPS – 2
ii)  MCQ question on Data Structure – 5
iii)  Coding Question – 2

1) Minimum Number of swaps required to make the
valid parenthesis.

 https://leetcode.com/problems/minimum-number-of-
swaps-to-make-the-string-balanced

2) Lexicographically Smallest Palindrome - LeetCode

From the online assessment, 34 of us has shortlisted

for interview. The online test was conducted in morning.
The Interview process started in the afternoon of the
same day.

If the online assessment was conducted in CUIC,

don’t wait for optimal approach. Try to pass all the
provided testcases with even brute-force approach and try
to finish faster.

Round 2: Technical Interview

[50 - 60 mins]

The interview started with introduction. Then he told me to
explain about the project I have mentioned in my resume.

Project (Tech Stack) based Questions:

1. What is use-Effect in React?

2. Which type of React components (Class component or
Function Component) is used in my project?

3. What is redux?

4. What is bi-LSTM?

DBMS:

1. What are the normalisation forms?

 2. What is indexing? What are the types of Indexing?

3. A SQL query: Return the book title and the number of times
each book is borrowed.
* Book (ID, title)

* Reader (ID, name)

* Borrow (BookId, ReaderId)

OOPS, DSA:

1. Explain the 4 pillars of OOPS in C++? (Since, I mentioned I
am using C++ for DSA in my intro)

2. Application of stack

3. What is linked list?

4. Simulation of Quick sort for the given array.

Coding:

1. Moving zeros to the end of array.

Eg: [4,0,2,0,8,5] -> [4,2,8,5,0,0]

2. Find the index where the left and right subarray product will
be equal.

Eg: [2,3,4,8,6,4].
Output: 3 (left: [2*3*4] = right: [6 * 4])

Then the interviewer asked, do you have any questions. I have
asked about his work experience in AthenaHealth. He explained
about his past project works and also the project and tech
stack that the company was currently working on.

 Round 3: Managerial Round

[45 - 50 mins]

This round was a little tricky one. In this round, the interview
was purely based on the resume. He almost asked questions
from each and every section in the resume. So, be clear about
the details you have mentioned in your resume. He also asked,

1. What do you know about the company?

I told the company project details explained by my previous
round interviewer.

2. What are the past interviews you have attended? Why

you are rejected in those interviews?

I have acknowledged the mistakes I have done during one
of my interviews and what I have done to correct my mistakes.
[You should not be harsh on company. Try to acknowledge your
mistakes and also explain how you overcome it, in a polite
way]

3. Which programming language you will prefer? Python

or Java?
      I have answered python.

       4. What is GenAI? [Since the python team is working on
GenAI and OCR based EHR product]

The interview lasted for nearly 50 minutes.

Round 4: HR Interview

 [20 mins]

1. Tell me about yourself
2. Explain any one of your projects
3. Scenario - based question: You are working in a project
with a team of 4 members. One of your teammates has
a health issue. What would you do to finish the project
within the deadline?
4. What is your strength?
5.  The works you have done in the clubs?
6.  Explain the situation you have worked as a leader in a

team.

7.  What is AthenaHealth?
8.  What are the past interviews attended and why they

have rejected you?
9. Family background
10.  Are you comfortable with relocation?

Finally, 6 of us were selected.

My Preparation Strategy:

For DSA preparation, Striver’s Sheet will be very useful.

C.O.D.E Series and the ISTA weekly contests have helped me.
Try to participate in many coding events and online contests to
get familiar in coding.

For SQL, try to solve the SQL 50 series in Leetcode in

order. This will help you understand the key SQL concepts for
interviews.

       For Core subjects, refer GeeksforGeeks and Neso academy

For Aptitude, refer IndiaBix: https://www.indiabix.com/

For Puzzles, refer GFG Puzzles.

 For HR questions, there is no exact answer for it. So,

prepare answers for the common HR interview questions in
your own unique way.

Previous Interviews list:

Internship: Microsoft, VeGrow, Citi

Placement: Tekion

Key Suggestions and Tips from My Experience:

Start the preparation before the semester holidays. Once

the placement drive starts, there won’t be much time for
preparation.

In the semester holidays, try to understand the things you

are not comfortable and start working on improving it.

For the coding interviews, start with brute force approach.

Don’t directly go with optimal approach. This was the mistake
which led to my VeGrow interview rejection. So, always start
solving with brute force and try to give many possible
approaches. Explain the time and space complexity for your
code and ask for edge cases. They will only check your
understanding, so syntax does not matter much.

For DSA preparation, try to solve coding problems in an
organized way. The random problem solving will not give the
conceptual understanding. Try to solve more coding problems
in each topic. Though the quantity varies, solve many problems
until you are clear about the ‘basic syntax and question format’
of that topic.

For the technical questions, try to explain the concepts in

details and also provide a simple real-time example. Try to
explain all the concepts you have known relevant to that
question.

Be clear with the tech stacks you have used in your

 projects. Be clear with all the terms you have mentioned in
your resume.

For the HR questions, Be honest and confident.

Do the homework about ‘the company and the products

they are working on’, before going to interview. Even this
homework might help for your result.

Even after the good preparations, the panel luck and

many things that are not in our control also matters.

Still the good preparation only will make you grab the

available opportunity when things work in your way. Trust the
Process.

I hope you will crack your very next interview.

Barclays 
Barclays is a British multinational banking and financial services company 
headquartered in London, with a rich history dating back to 1690. It operates 
globally, providing a wide range of banking, investment, and wealth 
management services to individuals, businesses, and institutions. With a 
significant presence in the United Kingdom and an international footprint, 
Barclays offers retail and corporate banking, investment banking, asset 
management, and credit card services. The bank has been involved in various 
philanthropic initiatives and is known for its commitment to sustainability and 
environmental practices. 
 
Hiring Role: HTD Technology Graduate Analyst 
The hiring process is divided into 2 Rounds. 
 Online Test 
 Technical + HR Round 
Online Test: 
Platform: HackerEarth 
Duration: 1hr 30mins 
It consists of 12 MCQs, 1 SQL Query, and 1 coding question. 
They were based on Java, C, and C++, and find the output of given pseudocode, 
DBMS, SQL, and OOPs. Having a strong knowledge of CS fundamentals and 
SQL will help you clear this round. Practice more technical aptitude and predict 
the output questions.  
The SQL query was straightforward. It was an easy one. It was just a simple 
question framed into a very big paragraph. 
The coding question:   
Lexicographically smallest string 
Reference: 
https://leetcode.com/discuss/interview-question/4006812/string-generation-oa-
question/2045987 
 
Technical + HR Round: 
Duration: 45 minutes 
 
The interviewer greeted me and he started to read the “About me” section in my 
resume. He didn’t ask me for self-introduction. 
He was impressed by my CGPA and 12th marks and asked more about it. 
He asked about my favorite subject in school and I told Maths and Computer 
Science. He further asked what did I like in Maths. I said a variety of topics like 
matrices, linear algebra, calculus, and probability. In probability, he asked about 
the difference between permutation and combination. I explained it with an 
example and he was satisfied. 
Then he asked about my favorite subject in college. I told OOPS, Data 
Structures, Computer Networks, and Operating Systems. He then asked: 
 Why OOPS is your favorite subject? 
 Write C++ code for all 4 pillars of OOPS? 
I explained the code clearly with simple examples and he was ok with it. 
He asked about what sorting algorithms I know. 
I was always waiting for this question in an interview because I know around 9 
sorting algorithms which include: 
o Bubble sort 
o Insertion sort 
o Selection sort 
o Quick sort 
o Merge sort 
o Heap sort 
o Count sort 
o Radix sort 
o Shell sort 
I was not asked to write the code. I explained every algorithm along with its 
time and space complexity. I even explained their use cases and limitations. He 
was greatly impressed by this. It is not mandatory to learn all these algorithms 
but this was the turning point in my interview which majorly helped me. But I 
recommend you to know of all these because it makes you stand out from the 
rest of the others.Next, he asked me how strong you are in SQL. I said I am 
pretty strong in SQL. He handed me a pen and paper and asked about the ACID 
properties in DBMS. 
I explained each property in brief with an example. 
He looked at my projects and asked why I used Mongo DB since I had 
mentioned two full-stack projects in my resume which included it. 
I explained it with an example relating to my project and also told him why I 
preferred Mongo DB over MYSQL in my project. 
He further asked about the difference between SQL and NoSQL. 
He asked whether I knew about graph databases. I said I had not used it but I 
had heard about it since I came across it in my previous semester course "Big 
Data Analytics" 
The HR questions asked were: 
o What do you know about Barclays? 
o Why did you choose CEG? 
o Why did you not opt for IIT and NIT? 
o If not CEG, what are the other colleges you would have chosen? 
The interview ended with him saying “It was nice talking to you!” I was so 
happy to hear that. He asked me if I wanted to ask him any questions. Always 
ask questions to show your interest and prepare questions beforehand. I asked 
him about how they incorporate new technologies into their services. He 
answered my question and again said "It was nice talking to you “. He shook my 
hand and wished me all the best.  
 
Tips: 
 First and Foremost, you have to know the Barclays values- RISES. 
 Refer to the company website to learn more about RISES. 
 In behavioral interview questions, you have to answer in such a way that 
your answer aligns with at least one of the values. Because they evaluate 
you based on that. 
 Never lie in your resume, because in Barclays every interviewer has at 
least 10+ years of experience and they can easily catch you in a lie. 
 Be strong on what you mention in your resume. Questions will only be 
asked based on your resume. 
 When asked about your favorite subject don't tell him any subject that 
you are not so confident about. Because questions will be asked only 
based on that. 
 Read the job description beforehand. Questions will also come from that. 
If the job description includes cloud, microservices, GUI. Questions will 
also be asked from that. You don't need to know every concept they have 
mentioned in the job description, just basic knowledge is enough. 
 
Aptitude Preparation: 
 
Refer Indiabix. They have a ton of aptitude questions. Practice regularly. 
Important concepts: Problems on trains, Time and Distance, Time and Work, 
Simple and Compound Interest, Profit and Loss, Partnerships, Permutation and 
Combination, Probability, Ratios, and Percentages, Family members relations 
and ages. 
Tech MCQs- mostly Code outputs and Debugging. Predict the output for C, 
C++, and Java code snippets – based on pointer manipulation in C++, 
Inheritance-based questions, interfaces in java. 
 
DSA: 
 
Refer to Striver Sheet. It is more than enough. You should understand the 
intuition, thought process, and the time and space complexity behind every 
problem. Practice coding daily on websites like leetcode, hackerrank, coding 
ninjas and GeeksforGeeks. Participate in contests to manage your time in 
solving coding problems. Prepare standard questions like sorting algorithms, 4 
pillars of OOPS implementation, Queue to stack, and stack to queue. 
 
Core subjects: 
o DBMS 
o OOPS 
o OS 
o CN 
o System Design 
You need to be thorough with these concepts. Refer to websites like geeks for 
geeks, interview bit, PrepInsta, JavatPoint, and your semester notes. 
 
Project: 
This is the backbone of your resume. You need to know everything in and out 
of your project. Standard questions are: 
o Why did you choose this project? 
o What are the security features? 
o What are the challenges faced in the project? 
o Why did you choose this particular tech stack for this project? 
If you have hosted your project, do mention it because it makes a good 
impression on you. Update your GitHub and LinkedIn profile accordingly. 
Because the interviewer may also check your profile. You can also mention 
your coding profiles. At last mention some extracurricular activities in your 
resume because your resume should not be too technical. You should show him 
that you can balance both academic and extracurricular.   
 
 
HR and behavioral: 
 
Refer to many websites for Barclay’s behavioral questions and answers 
so you will get an idea of how to approach these questions. 
Important: RISES 
 
Most importantly, Refer to GFG archives before you appear for an 
interview 
 
Hope this helps. 
Thank you and All the Best.

BNY Mellon Internship Interview Experience

About the Company

The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation, commonly known as BNY

Mellon, is an American multinational Investment banking services holding
company headquartered in New York City. It is by far the world's largest
custodian bank and asset servicing company BNY Mellon is an investment
company, which provides investment management, investment services and
wealth management helping institutions and individuals succeed in markets all
over the world.

Coding Round 1

Timing: 90mins.
Mode : Online

Alike all the companies, Coding round was BNY Mellon's first level of
selection process. There were 4 coding questions distributed across various
domains and rated under different difficulty levels. The platform used was
hackerrank. Out of these 4 questions, one was categorised as easy, two were
medium and one was hard. Around 25 candidates were shortlisted based on
the number of test cases passed and points, for the next round.

Round 2: Technical Interview 1

Timing: 40mins.
Mode : Offline

This round started with my self-introduction.The interviewer asked me to
solve an aptitude question which is to find the next number in a sequence then
he asked me to write the logic for the coding question - find all pairs of
anagrams in list of strings and he asked about the fundamentals of sql and he
asked to write the sql query for a easy question. He asked to explain the
projects which I mentioned in my resume and also asked me about the recent
technologies which inspired me the most and he asked some questions
related to that. We are asked to write the answers in pen-paper mode. After
answering all the questions he gave and he mentioned, to perform well in the
next round.

 Round 3: Technical + HR interview

Timing: 40mins.
Mode: Offline.

This round started with my self-introduction.The interviewer asked me
to solve a  maths question based on Pythagorean theorem . He tested my
knowledge of Oops, to explain the features of Oops with some real life
examples. After I had explained to him, he asked me to explain my projects
and he asked some questions related to that. He also asked me about
some basics of cyber security like sql injection, phishing , XSS etc. Since
my project was a team project, he asked about my ability to work as a team
and how I used to manage team members when their performance is low.
Then he asked me how I would keep myself up to date and what steps I
made to learn new technologies apart from my curriculum.

16 candidates were given the offer for summer internships.

Tips:

● Practice coding questions and attend contests on platforms like

Leetcode, GFG , Hackerrank and codeforces.

● Thorough with basics of OOPS and DBMS.
● Make sure you have 2-3 good projects. It would be better if you have
projects on Angular Js and Java (company may expects from you, not
compulsory)

● Most of the interview questions will be from the resume.

Data Analytics. I had a Winter Internship for a period of 6 months (during 8th semester).

Preparation Strategy : Understand concepts of DBMS, OOPs, Networks, OS(basics) and Data
Structures. Take notes,
revision. Practice CP ( Competitive
Programming) from 3rd year. Start with basic problems and later increase the difficulty gradually.
Make an Excel sheet and list out the topics to be covered and update your daily progress in
that. Practicing in a group of 2-3 with your friend/classmates will be advisable. Group Discussion
plays a major role in learning. It will help you get a new perspective in solving a problem and
improve yourself. It will make you solve more problems when your friends solve them too. Try
solving at least 2 problems per day as a routine. During placements preparation ( at the end of
3rd year) solve 5-10 prbs per day. When you feel exhausted with problem solving, cover theory
concepts too. Refer to cheat sheets, notes and general
interview questions. Prepare your
resume well before 2 months of the drive. You can refine your resume after each interview and
based on the company too. In your continuous preparation take breaks and refresh your mind.
Attend mock interviews or ask your placed friends to conduct such rounds to get familiar with
interview questions. Do not get stressed during your placement drive, focus only on your
preparation. Be ready to face peer pressure.

Placement Drive Experience: BNY drive came in the month of August. It had 1 online coding
round, 2 technical rounds(physical) and 1 HR round(physical). The online round was for a
duration of 90 minutes where 4 questions ( 1 Easy – array/string based, 2 Medium and 1 Hard)
were needed to be solved. I don’t remember the questions as I attended a series of other
company rounds too.
I was shortlisted for the next round and had 2 days for interview
preparation. On the day of the interview, I attended the 1st technical round by 10:30 am. The
interviewer introduced himself and asked me to introduce myself. I started with my personal
introduction, then about my semester projects ( explained like a one-liner with objective and
tech-stack used ) and external projects( with other organization/club), explained about my
internship , my coding skills , my strong subjects and ended with involvement in club activities.
He asked me if
I had a subject related to AI and ML. When I said yes, he started to ask
questions related to them. I told him that I have not done projects in ML, but I have basic
knowledge in them. Then, he asked a question related to File Handling. I actually answered that
question in terms of array ,then linked list and concluded it in terms of Database record

 management. He liked the kind of perspective that I had. He asked questions related to SQL
query, joins , primary and secondary key and other DBMS concepts. I was asked to write
pseudocode for an easy coding question. Then, the questions were related to my projects and
detailed explanations about them. I was asked to tell about the hardships faced during my
project
journey. The external project which I had contributed gave an upper hand in the
interview. The 1st round lasted for about 45 mins. At the end the interviewer hinted me to be
ready for the next round. The 2nd round was a bit easier than expected. The questions were
related to my projects, DBMS and OOPs concepts. This interviewer expected me to answer in
java. But my core language is C++, so I informed him about it. I said I am clear with the
concepts and I can answer in terms of C++. He was fine with that. This round was for about 30
mins. The shortlist for the final round was announced a bit late. The HR round took place
around 3:30 pm. This round was quite tricky and tougher than the 2nd round. I started with a
basic intro about myself and the projects. I was asked to explain in detail about my external
project. I had more questions related to Club activities and volunteering. The questions were
more managerial and tested our soft skills. I was given a puzzle to solve. It was tricky then, but I
had discussed it once with my friend before. It took some time to solve. I had another puzzle
related to lift. I didn’t solve it completely as other questions were switched. This round lasted for
45-50 mins. I asked for feedback at every round and in previous company rounds too.

Link to puzzle: https://www.mathsisfun.com/puzzles/weighing-9-balls-solution.html

which you are not strong in. All questions are completely asked from Resume. If you aren’t sure
about any answer, tell the interviewer without hesitation and tell them you are ready to acquire
in future if needed. Try explaining answers with paper-pen
the
(flowchart/diagrams) , if you get stuck with explanations. Ask questions if you don't get what
they are asking for. Highlight your unique skills (hackathon participation, external projects,

PLACEMENT DRIVE – BNY Mellon

Batch : ( 2023-24 )

      - Sourav Kumar N R S

About the Company

The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation, commonly known as BNY, is an American
multinational Investment banking services holding company headquartered in New York City.
It is by far the world's largest custodian bank and asset servicing company BNY Mellon is an
investment company, that provides investment management, investment services, and
wealth management helping institutions and individuals succeed in markets all over the
world.

About the Role:

We were offered a Full Stack Developer Role. The day-to-day job at BNY includes working
with Angular, Springboot, Python, Git, etc. This role requires us to understand the business
requirements and provide them with an efficient system that serves the client with the best
experience. They offered the option to do a 6-month semester internship with them too.

Preparation Strategy:

I would divide the preparation strategy into two

1.  Long Term preparation
2.  Short Term preparation

Long Term Preparation:

One should always start their preparation early and keep learning consistently. Join

the Headfirst, CODE program conducted by ACM and Placement/Internship training provided
by the seniors from ISTA. Consistently learn. Long-term preparation is when you have ample
of time for your placement or intern drive. Try to cover all the topics that are given in the
CODE program or ISTA’s program.  Leverage the free content available on YouTube for your
learning.

Short Term Preparation.

This is when you have companies fast approaching you. Once you know what company is
coming in your placement/intern drive. Prepare the previously asked and most frequently asked
questions from those companies for each round. Go through this company well and know its
leadership principles if you are through the online round.

Interview Experience

Online Round

In the online round, we were asked 4 coding questions. 1 easy, 2 Medium, and 1 Hard

question was asked. One of the questions asked was a variation of the knapsack problem (DP). The
test was conducted on the Hackerrank platform with the duration being 1 hour.

 Round 1

  The next three rounds were face-to-face (offline) interviews conducted in CUIC. In this

round, I was asked to write the code for inserting a node in the linked list in the A4 sheet. The
interviewer reviewed the code and questioned about the corner cases in the problem. Apart from
this, I was asked one question on each of the computer science topics like DBMS, OS, CN, Distributed
systems, OOPS, Java, etc. Apart from this, I was asked questions from my projects too. The prominent
question was how I implemented security features in the Chat application I developed.

Round 2

In this round, I was asked to write the code for finding the number of pairs in an array that

sum up to K. Following the coding questions, I was asked few technical questions, few resume-based
questions, and a few behavioral test questions.

Round 3

This was the final round. Here I was asked a slightly harder coding problem. I was asked to

write the logic on an A4 sheet. I wrote the better approach in the paper, but he was not satisfied
with that. Then after a few minutes of discussion, I came up with the optimal approach and
explained it to him. I didn’t write that on paper. After the coding questions, I was asked to draw the
architecture diagram for one of my projects and explain it. Following this I was asked a few questions
technical questions and the interview was over. Within 2 hours the results were announced and I got
selected.

Tips and Tricks

1.  Start the preparation early
2.  Cover as many topics as possible
3.  Learn to approximately estimate time complexity based on the constraints given in the

coding problem

4.  Take notes in the class for each subject and revise them before going for placements
5.  Have a great mindset with fighting spirit because the placement/Intern drive will be very

mentally tiring

Preparation Materials

Striver’s SDE Sheet – Top Coding Interview Problems (takeuforward.org)

Leetcode DSA sheet by Fraz - Google Sheets

(165) take U forward - YouTube

All the Best

 DSA problem related to: https://leetcode.com/problems/group-anagrams/

mentioned in your resume.

VISA INTERNSHIP OFFER 
  ABOUT VISA, 
Visa Inc. is an American Multinational payments technology company which aims in providing 
fast, reliable and secure digital payments all around the world. It is often misinterpreted as a bank or 
credit card company. But actually, it provides financial services and facilitates digital payments. 
There were 2 rounds of selection process. One was an Online test and another was a Technical 
cum Managerial interview. 
1. Online test: 
It was conducted on hackerRank platform for 70 minutes. There were 4 coding questions of 2 Easy level 
difficulty and 2 medium level difficulty. 
• 
First Question: Given an array of n integers, write a function that returns the index of the first 
element in the array such that the next two consecutive elements (i.e., the element at the 
current index and the next two elements) are strictly greater than a given threshold. If no such 
index exists, return -1. 
• 
Second Question: You are given an array numbers. Distribute its elements between two arrays, 
first and second, as follows: 
1. The first element goes to first, the second to second. 
2. For each subsequent element, if the number of elements strictly greater than the 
current element is higher in one array, place the element in the other. If both have the 
same count, place the element in the shorter array. If still tied, place it in first. 
3. Return the merged result of first followed by second. 
• 
Third Question: You are given an n x m grid and an array figures representing different Tetris-
like shapes (A, B, C, D, E). Starting with a matrix of zeros, your task is to draw each figure on 
the grid using its 1-based index from the array. The shapes are placed on the grid according to 
specific patterns. Return the resulting matrix after placing all the shapes. 
• 
Fourth Question: Given an array of integers arr, write a function to count how many 
contiguous subarrays form a sawtooth sequence, where each element alternates in parity 
(even or odd) from its neighbors. 
Since there were only 4 questions, we were expected to solve both. A total of 15 students were 
shortlisted for the next round. 
2. Technical cum Managerial interview: 
It was conducted in MS teams. The duration of the interview was around 1 hour. Initially he asked me to 
introduce myself. 
There were some usual HR questions like: 
• 
Can you share a specific experience where you faced a significant challenge? How did you 
overcome it, and what important lesson did you take away from the situation? 
• 
What do you consider to be your weakness? 
• 
Tell me a situation where you have been so stressful in your life and how did you overcome it? 
• 
How would you handle a project with limited resources and a tight deadline? 
• 
Can you describe a situation where you received no initial instructions and explain how you 
managed to successfully navigate it? 
Finally, he asked me whether I had any questions for him. I asked him a couple of questions. 
• 
What does the work culture at Visa look like? 
• 
What are some common challenges interns might encounter during their internship? 
• 
What is the primary responsibility of an intern during the internship process? 
• 
I inquired about accommodation options during the internship. 
After the interview concluded, the results were announced the next day. Out of 15 
candidates, 5 were selected, and I’m one of them 
Key takeaways from interview experience: 
• 
Read & re-read your Resume. Be very clear about every single line put in your Resume. 
• 
Do an intensive research on the company profile and try to understand how do they make 
money out of their business. 
• 
Joining the interview 5 mins prior to the scheduled time will be very helpful. 
• 
Also, we can have a small conversation with the interviewer to get connected well. 
• 
Interviewers are not here just to reject us and they will definitely help us when we get struck 
somewhere. 
• 
Don’t lose your hope in-between the interview, if you answer wrong to 1 or 2 questions. 
 
Topics to be focused: 
• 
Data Structures and Algorithms. 
• 
Database Management Systems. 
• 
OOPS. 
• 
OS. 
• 
whatever skills and projects mentioned.

Interview Experience – NatWest 
Hi everyone, I am Badrinath S (2022503017) and I am glad to share my NatWest 
interview experience. 
 
Round 1 – Online Assessment (On Campus) 
The initial screening test was conducted on SHL. It tested: 
• 
Basic English grammar and comprehension 
• 
Logical reasoning (including visual reasoning) 
• 
SQL (MCQs) 
• 
2 coding questions 
The first coding question was very easy: 
Print even numbers first and odd numbers next. 
Here, there was a small trick. If you use i % 2 == 1 for odd numbers, it will fail for 
negative integers. 
The second question was a medium-level greedy problem. I always use Java in coding 
interviews since many companies prefer it (as most backends are in Spring Boot). For 
this question, I created another class called Pair with three variables so that managing 
comparisons after using Collections.sort became easier. 
My tip for this round: 
• 
Do not waste time reading the entire long paragraph. Many companies add 
lengthy text to consume your time. Instead, first read the input format and 
required output. That will give you a clear idea of what the problem is about. 
• 
Once you understand the requirement, try to map the problem to a suitable data 
structure. 
• 
Be strong in basics, such as how to take input using the Scanner class. 
• 
If your code does not work immediately, don’t panic. Use console print 
statements to debug and trace the error. 
• 
Above all, stay honest throughout your placement season. That’s the best way to 
prepare better and shine. 
• 
Prepare aptitude through mock test in SHL portal. 
I got shortlisted along with 23 others for the next round. 
 
Round 2 – Technical + HR (Combined, 45 minutes) 
Both interviewers were kind and introduced themselves at the beginning. They started 
by asking about the Pre-Placement Talk that happened just before the interviews. They 
also asked me if I knew about NatWest earlier. I replied that, as a cricket fanatic, I knew 
that NatWest had once sponsored the England Cricket Team. 
Next, they moved on to my resume. They asked about my projects and were impressed 
that they solved real-world problems. They said they liked my approach and mentioned 
that it aligned well with the company’s vision. At that point, I was confident that I had a 
good chance of receiving the offer. 
Then came the DSA round. They gave me a simple array question involving a HashSet. I 
explained my approach, and the interviewer suggested storing the index instead of the 
element. I explained that even then, the time complexity would remain O(N). The 
interviewer agreed and was satisfied. 
After that, they asked a SQL query to find the third highest mark. Initially, I wrote a 
nested query. The interviewer pointed out that its complexity could be reduced. I then 
suggested using ORDER BY DESC and picking the third record (I forgot the word 
“OFFSET,” but the interviewer mentioned it himself and said my approach was correct). 
They also asked a few basic ML questions—such as What is supervised learning? and 
What is an activation function?—based on my resume, which mentioned ML. 
Behavioral Round 
Next, they moved toward behavioral questions. 
They asked me about my view on learning new technologies. I replied that I am always 
open to learning anything necessary to solve a client’s problem. However, I also added 
that I am not the type of person who upskills without a clear need, as I prefer to focus 
my efforts where they bring value. 
They then asked me about my integrity. One question was: “What would you do if an 
ethically wrong person asked you to do something in the bank that is profitable for both 
the company and the bank?” 
I answered that I value honesty above all, as it is crucial for long-term success, even if 
shortcuts may give temporary relief. I also mentioned that if I had to reject an unethical 
request, I would do so firmly but with kindness, ensuring I handle the situation with 
respect. 
Another question was about AI. They asked me how I would use it. I responded that I 
would not allow AI to hamper my own thinking capabilities. Instead, I would use it to 
perform tasks where I already know the logical approach, thereby improving speed and 
execution. 
The interviewers were impressed with my responses. At the end, they asked if I had any 
questions. I politely said I didn’t have any. They told me they enjoyed the conversation, 
wished me all the best, and I left the room with a sense of gratitude. 
 
My Tips 
1. Always include ML in your resume if you have worked on it. Interviewers value 
that highly. 
2. Do not panic; stay calm and honest. 
3. Never over-exaggerate your projects. Interviewers are more impressed when you 
explain your projects truthfully and confidently. Explain your projects in the 
perspective of how they would solve a real life problem. 
4. Resume is the starting point: The flow of the interview was driven by my 
resume. Be ready to explain every project you include and link it to solving real-
world problems. 
5. Explain your thought process: In coding questions, the interviewer often looks 
at how you approach the problem rather than just the final solution. 
6. SQL efficiency matters: Knowing both a straightforward and an optimized 
approach shows maturity. 
7. Behavioral questions test alignment with values: Integrity, honesty, and 
adaptability were tested more than technical brilliance. Companies want people 
they can trust in sensitive domains like banking. 
8. AI and emerging tech: Instead of exaggerating, give a balanced view. Show 
openness to use new technologies while maintaining human reasoning. 
9. Confidence > perfection: Even when I forgot the keyword OFFSET, I handled it 
calmly. That mattered more than knowing every detail. 
 
 
Finally I got the offer along with 8  other canditates.Felt very relieved and was at peace 
after so much grind.

SAP Labs Interview Experience                       
Role : Developer Associate                                                                       
Total Rounds: 5  
Date: 18th August 2025 
 
 
It all began on a nervous evening in front of my laptop, preparing for the 
SAP Labs interviews.  
I had read countless experiences from other candidates, and now it was 
finally my turn to create one of my own.  
From the very first online test to the final HR discussion, here’s a step-
by-step look at how my SAP Labs interview unfolded and the lessons I 
took away. 
 
 
About Company: 
About SAP Labs India 
SAP Labs India is a major research and development hub of SAP, 
located in Bengaluru, Gurgaon, Pune, Mumbai, and Hyderabad. The 
Bengaluru campus is the largest, focusing on AI solutions and 
enterprise technology with a strong, customer-centric approach. 
About SAP 
SAP (Systems, Applications, and Products in Data Processing) is a 
global leader in enterprise software, helping businesses manage 
operations like finance, supply chain, and data analytics. Headquartered 
in Germany, SAP has been driving innovation for over 50 years and 
works with companies in nearly every industry worldwide. 
 
[Learn the basic concepts of SAP ERP products and the SAP HANA 
database they use. They usually don’t ask direct questions from these 
topics, but having this knowledge can give you a slight edge over other 
candidates by showing genuine interest in the company.]  
 
 
Selection Process 
1.Online Assessment (OA) 
 Duration: 100 minutes (1 hour 40 minutes) 
 Platform: Codility 
 Proctoring: Both video and audio 
 Questions: 2 DSA problems – one easy and one medium level. 
 Note: Each candidate received a different set of questions. 
 
Questions Asked to me  
Question -1 
Problem Statement 
You are given an array A containing N integers, sorted in non-
decreasing order. 
Your goal is to transform this array by performing a series of moves. A 
"move" can be one of two operations: 
1. Remove an integer from the array. 
2. Insert an integer anywhere in the array. 
The objective is to reach a state where for every unique value X present 
in the final array, it appears exactly X times. 
You need to find the minimum total number of moves (removals + 
insertions) required to achieve this goal. Note that it's permissible to 
remove all occurrences of a number. 
 
 
Example 1 
 Input: A = [1, 1, 3, 4, 4, 4] 
  Expected Output: 3 
Explanation: The initial array has two 1s, one 3, and three 4s. You can 
perform the following 3 moves: 
1. Remove one 1. 
2. Remove the 3. 
3. Insert one 4. The resulting array is [1, 4, 4, 4, 4]. In this array, the 
value 1 appears once, and the value 4 appears four times, 
satisfying the condition. 
 
Example 2 
 Input: A = [1, 2, 2, 2 ,5, 5, 5, 8] 
 Expected Output: 4 
Explanation:  
1.Remove 8 
     2.Remove 2 once 
3.Add two times 5  
Total moves : 4 
 
Example 3 
 Input: A = []  
 Expected Output: 0 
 
 
 
 
Question 2 
Problem Statement 
In a city, there are N districts (numbered from 0 to N-1) connected by M 
streets. The street network is described by two arrays, A and B, where 
each pair (A[K], B[K]) represents a street between two districts. 
There are also L hospitals in the city, and their locations are given by an 
array H. 
When an ambulance is needed in a district, it is sent from the hospital 
that can reach the district via the shortest possible route. It takes 
exactly 1 minute to travel along any single street. 
The task is to find the longest time required for an ambulance to reach 
any district in the city. This means you must: 
1. For every district, find the shortest possible travel time from its 
closest hospital. 
2. From all these shortest times, find the maximum value. 
If any district cannot be reached by an ambulance (i.e., it's not 
connected to any district with a hospital), the function should return -1.
 
I don’t remember the example for this question 
Similalar question : https://www.chegg.com/homework-help/questions-
and-answers/city-n-districts-numbered-0-n-1-connected-m-streets-
connections-described-two-arrays-b-len-q92788342 
 
I solved both questions  
Result of this Round : 
A total of 89 students were shortlisted for the interview. 
The results came out just one day before, in the afternoon, so I had only 
that evening and the next morning to prepare. 
 
My advice: if you’ve solved both problems, don’t wait for the results—
start preparing for the interview right away. 
If you get shortlisted, you’re ready. If not, that preparation will still help 
for the next company. 
 
This way, even if the shortlist is announced at the last minute, you’ll 
already feel confident and ready. 
 
 
Technical Interview – 1  
 
Duration: For me, this round lasted about 1 to 1 hour 15 minutes. 
1. Introduction & Project Discussion 
The interview started with the classic question: “Introduce yourself.” 
Next, the interviewer asked me to explain my second project listed on 
my resume. 
I began explaining my project, and he seemed very satisfied with my 
explanation. 
Later, he moved on to the technical part and asked, “Are you good in 
Java?” 
I confidently replied, “Yes, I’m good at it.” 
 
Questions asked related to java  
 
1.Exception Handling 
2.Whether try block cant exist with/without catch and finally 
3.checked exception and unchecked exception with examples for them 
4.Throw vs throws  
5.finally keyword 
6.Oops concept in java  
My Advice: 
Don’t just memorize OOP theory from sites like GFG. Instead, focus on 
understanding the concepts clearly and, if possible, relate them to your 
project. Explaining OOP concepts with examples from your own project 
makes it easier for the interviewer to understand and shows practical 
knowledge. 
 
 7.Polymorphism one example code question 
class Calculator { 
    int sum(int a, int b) { 
        return a + b; 
    } 
    long sum(long a, int b) {  
        return a + b; 
    } 
} 
Calculator.sum(10,5) 
Which method will be called  
I said sum(int a ,int b) 
Why long sum() isn’t called he asked  
Then what happens if only we have long sum() 
I said if we have only long sum() then long sum will be called  
He asked me to explain the reason 
Java looks for exact type matches first. 
If no exact match, it considers widening conversions (int → long, float 
→ double, etc.). 
8.Wrapper class 
 
we moved on to the DSA part. I was given four standard problems: 
1. Binary Search 
2. Set Matrix Zeros 
3. Find all permutations of an integer array 
4. Merge Sort 
I was asked to write code for all four problems and also explain each 
solution with a dry run. 
I managed to solve all the problems, but I struggled a bit with the dry 
run of the recursion in the permutation problem, even though it’s a 
standard question from Striver. 
The good part was that the interviewer helped me a lot throughout the 
session, guiding me when needed, which made the experience much 
smoother. 
Then he asked me whether you have any questions 
At the end of the interview, the classic question comes up: “Do you have 
any questions for me?” 
Instead of leaving it blank, use this moment to demonstrate curiosity 
and genuine interest.  
Ask about the team, the role, growth opportunities, or the projects you 
might work on. Thoughtful questions at this stage leave a strong, 
positive impression on the interviewer. 
 
My Advice: 
Avoid asking the usual, generic questions that everyone asks. Instead, 
try to ask a unique and thoughtful question—one that stands out and 
makes the interviewer remember you. 
I asked about the Agentic AI and Joule AI that SAP is currently using in 
development.  
 
 
 
NOTE: 
My interviewer didn’t introduce himself at the start, so at the end I 
asked, “Sir, could you please tell me about yourself and your role?” 
Always remember who interviewed you and what their role is—this 
information can be very helpful for future rounds.

GET(Graduate Engineering Trainee)

**Overview of the Process:**

The overall process consisted of

* An Video Interview
* Interview Round 1
* Interview Round 2

**Online coding Round**:

**1)Introduction:**

* The first question was a self-introduction where I was given 3 minutes to speak about myself.
* I talked about my educational background, skills, projects, and interests relevant to the position.

**2)Programming Question:**

* The next part was a programming round where I was asked a question related to regular expressions (regex).
* After solving the question, there was a follow-up video question where I had to explain my approach to solving the problem.

**3)Fitment Question:**

* The final question was a behavioral one, asking, "Why are you a good fit for Bank of America?"
* I elaborated on my skills, work ethic, and how my goals align with the values and opportunities at Bank of America.

**TIPS for Online Test:**

For video interviews, don’t panic and stay calm. Between questions, especially after programming tasks, take a moment to breathe, sip water, and refocus. Utilize the brief gap wisely to prepare for the next question. Manage your time well, as video responses are often timed. Stay composed, and ensure your technical setup (camera, mic, internet) is reliable.

**Interview Experience:**

In this round, being a mass recruitment process, the schedule was delightfully unpredictable, and no one knew when they'd be called. We were all asked to assemble at 8.30 AM, but my turn for Round 1 finally came at 4.00 AM -a casual 7.5-hour wait! After wrapping up Round 1, I was graced with a Massive 5 minute gap before called me into Round 2.

**Interview Round 1 (Technical):**

In this round, I was asked the classic **"Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters"** question. I explained both the **brute force** and **optimal solution** in detail. After that, the interviewer moved on to my projects. I described the projects I’ve worked on, highlighting the specific impact my contributions had, especially if it was a team project. I made sure to emphasize how my part drove the project's success.

Next, **Aptitude Question: *"****You have an infinite number of bags and lots of apples. All the bags are filled with an equal number of apples. If 8 apples are filled in all the bags, 7 apples remain. If 7 apples are filled, 6 apples remain, and so on until 2 apples are filled, leaving 1 apple. What is the total number of apples?"*I worked through the problem, explained my approach, but was unable to find the exact solution. However, I moved on and the round ended.

**Interview Round 2 (Technical+HR):**

In this round, I was first asked about my projects, specifically why I chose the **MERN stack** for my project. I explained its benefits and how it stands out from other development stacks. The interviewer then asked about my knowledge of **Git** and **Docker**, including some basic commands.

Next, I was given a set of **recursive programs** and was tasked with finding the solutions for each. Finally, I was asked to implement the **Tower of Hanoi** problem. After solving the problem, the round concluded

**PREPARATION TRICKS:**

**Practice Core Topics:**

* Focus on solving all the Striver questions on key topics like arrays, linked lists, strings, backtracking, and SQL. This will give you a solid foundation for tackling a wide range of questions.

**Review the Quick 75:**

* Before the interview, quickly go through Striver’s Quick 75 to refresh your memory on essential problems and solutions. This is a great way to get a final review of important concepts.

**Solve SQL Questions:**

* Practice solving SQL queries to ensure you're comfortable with data manipulation and querying during the interview.

**"Don't lose hope, take a breath, forge ahead, and give it all you've got."**

**Online Coding Contest on HackerRank Platform**

The recruitment process began with an online coding contest hosted on the HackerRank platform. This stage was designed to test candidates’ problem-solving and programming skills. Participants were required to solve a set of problems within a specified time limit. The questions covered a range of topics including algorithms, data structures, and logical reasoning. This stage was highly competitive and acted as a filtering round to shortlist candidates for the interview phase. Accuracy, efficiency, and coding style were key evaluation criteria in this round.

**Interview Round 1 (Technical/HR) – in CUIC**

The first interview round took place at the **CUIC (Centre for University Industry Collaboration)** and was a combination of technical and HR discussions. During the technical portion, candidates were assessed on their knowledge of core computer science concepts such as data structures, algorithms, databases, and programming languages. Practical problem-solving abilities and coding skills were also tested through live coding exercises or theoretical questions.

The HR segment of this round focused on understanding the candidate’s personality, strengths, weaknesses, and motivation for joining the company. Typical questions included discussing past projects, challenges faced during academic or internship experiences, and how the candidate aligns with the company’s values.

**Interview Round 2 (Technical/HR) – in CUIC**

It was too simple , the interviewer asked me to explain any one of the module on my project .It was just 5 to 6 minutes.

**Tricks That I Followed:**

**1. Completed the Online Test as Quickly as Possible**

Time management played a crucial role during the online coding test. I focused on solving the easier questions first to ensure I secured maximum points before tackling more complex problems. By maintaining a calm and methodical approach, I avoided spending excessive time on a single question. Practicing coding problems on platforms like HackerRank and LeetCode prior to the test helped me develop speed and accuracy. This strategy not only improved my confidence but also ensured that I completed the test within the allotted time frame, leaving no question unanswered.

**2. Thoroughly Read the Job Description to Tailor Responses During Interviews**

Before the interviews, I carefully analysed the job description to understand the role’s responsibilities, required skills, and expectations. This allowed me to tailor my responses to highlight how my technical expertise and past experiences aligned with the company’s needs. For example, if the job emphasized problem-solving and analytical skills, I prepared examples from my projects or internships that demonstrated these abilities. I also made sure to use relevant keywords from the job description in my answers to reinforce my understanding of the role.

**3. Asked Relevant and Thoughtful Questions in Each Round to Show Interest and Enthusiasm**

In every interview round, I prepared a list of thoughtful questions to ask the interviewers. These questions were focused on understanding the company’s culture, growth opportunities, and technical challenges associated with the role. For example, I asked about the technologies the team frequently uses, the nature of projects I might work on, and how the company supports employee learning and development. By doing so, I demonstrated genuine interest in the role and enthusiasm for contributing to the company. Asking insightful questions also helped create a positive impression and made the interaction more engaging.

**4. Recalled Details from the Company’s Presentations to Incorporate Into Answers**

I paid close attention to the company’s presentations and used that information strategically during my interviews. For example, I mentioned the company’s focus on innovation or specific initiatives they highlighted, and connected them to my skills or aspirations. This not only showed that I was well-prepared but also conveyed my dedication to understanding the company’s goals. Referencing specific points from the presentation helped me stand out as someone who took the time to research and connect with the company’s vision.

**Preparation Strategy:**

1.**Understanding Job Requirements**: I thoroughly reviewed the job description to identify key skills and areas of focus essential for the role.

2.**Technical Skill Enhancement**: Practiced coding daily on platforms like HackerRank and LeetCode, concentrating on algorithms, data structures, and problem-solving techniques.

3.**Concept Revisions**: Revisited computer science fundamentals such as databases, OOP, and networking to ensure a strong grasp of core concepts.

4.**Mock Tests**: Took timed mock tests to simulate the online test environment, improving accuracy and performance under pressure.

5.**Company Research**: Conducted in-depth research on Bank of America, understanding their business model, values, and recent innovations to tailor my interview responses effectively.

6.**Behavioural Question Practice**: Used the STAR method to prepare for behavioural questions, ensuring examples from projects and leadership experiences were ready to demonstrate my skills.

7.**Mock Interviews**: Practiced with friends and mentors, refining answers, improving delivery, and building confidence for actual interviews.

8.**Resume Preparation**: Revisited all academic projects and internships listed on my resume, ensuring I could clearly articulate my contributions and learning.

9.**Consistent Daily Schedule**: Balanced technical preparation, company research, and interview practice through a disciplined daily routine.

10.**Confidence Building**: Focused on holistic preparation, combining technical knowledge, communication skills, and company understanding to excel in every stage of the process.

GET (Graduate Engineer Trainee) roles. Bank Of America has its offices in Mumbai, Chennai,

ONLINE TEST

 Tell how you are the best fit for the role.

INTERVIEW

Round 1 (around 30 mins):

This was a face-to-face interview round held at CUIC. This was a technical + HR round.

Introduction

•
•  OOPS concepts with code (Objects, classes, polymorphism, overriding, constructors).
•  DBMS (SQL queries- group by, joins, count function).
•  Coding (Finding max, min of given numbers, binary tree lowest common ancestor)-Brute,

better, optimal, time and space complexity.

•  HR - What would you do if you face any conflicts or difference of opinions in your team? How

do you resolve it?

•  Extra curricular from Resume (NSS in my case) - What all have you done at NSS?
•  Five languages mentioned in Resume (just short friendly talk on how I learnt them).
•  Asked me if I have any questions for them to answer.

Round 2 (25 - 30 mins):

This was also a face-to-face interview round held at CUIC. I was called immediately in five mins after
the first round. This was also a technical + HR round.

Completely on tech stack mentioned in Resume (both in skills and projects section from Resume):

•  First they asked me to give brief explanation of all the tech stacks I know.
•  API ( How did u fetch data from it , write code)
•  XML, HTML, CSS, React ( All basic questions with code, both from projects as well as general

questions on them).

 After these questions, I was asked the following:

•  Questions on SQL again (All types of joins and for the given qn why do we use that particular

join).

•  DBMS - SQL - Why do we say it’s a relational DB?
•  With my project, since tech stacks like React or XML (JSX), or API cannot have relational

domains then how do u integrate with relational domain like SQL?

•  The interviewer gave two tables and asked why it was necessary to split them into two

instead of a single table.

I was then asked HR questions:

•  What would you do if a member of your team doesn't respond well or complete their work?
•  What would you do and how would you learn if a new tech is given to you in a project?

Then asked me if I have any questions for them to answer.

TOPICS TO BE FOCUSSED

Good knowledge in Coding, OOPS, DSA, OS, Reactjs, Nodejs, SQL. An in-depth understanding about
the projects listed on the resume and related concepts.

TAKEAWAY

The interviews went smoothly, and the interviewers were very supportive. Having a solid
understanding of all the projects listed on my resume and their related concepts proved invaluable.
Clear communication of my ideas played a crucial role, especially during the technical rounds when I
didn’t have precise answers. Be confident, interactive and share your thoughts throughout.

Best of Luck!

**Bank Of America Placement Experience**

**About the Company:**

Bank of America is a leading global financial institution committed to improving financial lives through responsible growth. Guided by its core values of integrity, inclusion, and purpose, the company serves clients, communities, and shareholders with a focus on innovation and sustainability. In India, it operates through BA Continuum India Private Limited (BACI), its wholly-owned non-banking subsidiary, which supports the bank’s Global Business Services (GBS). BACI delivers integrated technology and operations support across banking, trading, and risk management functions worldwide. Renowned for its strong execution, risk management, and commitment to employee well-being, Bank of America fosters a culture of diversity, flexibility, and innovation to drive excellence on a global scale.

**Overview of the interview process:**

1. Video Interview round
2. Technical Interview 1 (at CUIC)
3. Technical + HR Interview 2 (at CUIC)

**Video interview/coding round (90 mins):**

* This online round consisted of 5 questions, 3 Hr Questions and 2 coding questions.
* The coding questions were based on Arrays and strings , Hr questions had to be recorded with your camera and microphone switched on and the questions were “Explain the logic behind your code” , “Introduce yourself” and “Why do u think you are fit for the role at Bofa”.

**Technical Interview 1 (40 mins)**

The interview started with a simple introduction, where I talked about myself, my academic background, and my key projects. After that, they jumped straight into my projects. I was asked to explain each one in detail. They also asked if I had used Object-Oriented Programming (OOPs) in any of them, and when I said yes, they asked me to write a quick code snippet to show how I used it.

For my web development project, they were curious about how I connected to the database and wanted me to walk them through the entire workflow of the project. I explained the tools and technologies I used, how I handled the backend, and the overall functionality.

Next, they asked about my machine learning project. They tested me on some basic machine learning concepts and asked how I implemented the project , like what dataset and algorithms I used.

Then they asked 2 coding questions , the first one was to reverse the words in a sentence and the second question was to find the longest subarray with a sum equal to k .After coding, they asked me to solve the classic "bag of coins" puzzle from GeeksforGeeks. I shared my logical approach and solved it.

They followed this up with a quick SQL query to find the second largest salary in a table. Towards the end, they asked me a few basic questions on C++ and Python, covering things like memory management, data types in C++, Python libraries etc.

**Technical + HR Interview 2 (35 mins):**

This round was a mix of technical and HR questions.

On the technical side, the focus was mainly on SQL and Python.

* For SQL, they asked about all kinds of joins—inner join, left join, right join, self join, equi join, etc. I had to explain them and write examples.
* They also gave me a simple subquery question to solve and asked if I had worked with triggers and procedures. I explained the basics and wrote down the syntax for both.
* Write the code for detecting a loop or cycle in a linked list.

For Python, they asked:

* The difference between lists and tuples.
* Some common dictionary operations and how dictionaries are used.
* To write a simple palindrome-checking program.
* List 10 built-in Python functions and explain their use cases.

On the HR side, they asked questions like:

* How I would handle a conflict with a team member.
* What are my future aspirations and where I see myself in five years.
* To talk about my achievements and a few other personal questions.

One thing to note is that they repeatedly asked Python-related questions, even though I mentioned my core programming language is C++. So, my advice would be to brush up on Python if you’ve included it in your resume, as they might focus on it regardless of your primary language.

**Preparation Tips:**

* Be prepared to answer questions about anything you’ve mentioned in your resume. If you’ve listed a skill or a technology, make sure you know it in depth. Interviewers often test your understanding by asking detailed questions.
* Practice explaining your projects step by step, including their purpose, your role, the technologies used, and any challenges you faced. Be ready to dive into technical details if asked.
* Even if you’re unsure about something, stay calm and try to explain your thought process. If you don’t know the answer to a question, it’s okay to admit it rather than trying to guess or bluff.
* Revise core concepts related to your primary programming language , OOPs, DBMS, OS , CN etc.

**Preparation Resources:**

**Coding Practice:** Regularly solve coding problems on platforms like LeetCode to strengthen your problem-solving skills.

**GeeksforGeeks:** Use it to understand specific types of problems and practice puzzles, especially the **Top 100 puzzles** list. Their **Last Minute Notes** are great for subjects like Computer Networks (CN) and Operating Systems (OS).

**Love Babbar Cheatsheets:** For a quick yet thorough revision, refer to these cheatsheets:

* **OOPs:** [Love Babbar OOPs Cheatsheet](https://whimsical.com/object-oriented-programming-cheatsheet-by-love-babbar-YbSgLatbWQ4R5paV7EgqFw)
* **DBMS:** [Love Babbar DBMS Roadmap](https://whimsical.com/dbms-roadmap-by-love-babbar-FmUi8ffVop33t3MmpVxPCo)

Information Technology

FTE at Bank of America

**About the Company:**

Bank of America is one of the world's leading financial institutions, serving individual consumers, small and middle-market businesses, and large corporations with a full range of banking, investing, asset management, and other financial and risk management products and services. With a strong global presence, the bank is committed to connecting people and businesses to the resources they need to succeed. Its technology and operations teams play a crucial role in driving innovation, ensuring security, and optimizing operations to provide seamless and efficient services to its clients worldwide.

**Overview of the Process:**

The overall process consisted of:

- An Online Coding Contest on HackerRank platform

- Interview Round 1(Technical/HR) – in CUIC

- Interview Round 2(Technical/HR) – in CUIC

**Online Coding Contest on HackerRank Platform**

The recruitment process began with an online coding contest hosted on the HackerRank platform. This stage was designed to test candidates’ problem-solving and programming skills. Participants were required to solve a set of problems within a specified time limit. The questions covered a range of topics including algorithms, data structures, and logical reasoning. This stage was highly competitive and acted as a filtering round to shortlist candidates for the interview phase. Accuracy, efficiency, and coding style were key evaluation criteria in this round.

**Interview Round 1 (Technical/HR) – in CUIC**

The first interview round took place at the **CUIC (Centre for University Industry Collaboration)** and was a combination of technical and HR discussions. During the technical portion, candidates were assessed on their knowledge of core computer science concepts such as data structures, algorithms, databases, and programming languages. Practical problem-solving abilities and coding skills were also tested through live coding exercises or theoretical questions.

The HR segment of this round focused on understanding the candidate’s personality, strengths, weaknesses, and motivation for joining the company. Typical questions included discussing past projects, challenges faced during academic or internship experiences, and how the candidate aligns with the company’s values.

**Interview Round 2 (Technical/HR) – in CUIC**

The second and final interview round, also conducted at CUIC, was more in-depth and focused on both technical expertise and behavioural skills. This stage delved deeper into problem-solving capabilities, with candidates often presented with real-world scenarios to analyse and solve. Questions about system design, advanced algorithms, or case studies might also be included, depending on the role.

The HR portion aimed to assess the candidate’s cultural fit and soft skills. Topics such as teamwork, leadership experiences, conflict resolution, and long-term career goals were explored. This round provided the final opportunity for the candidates to make a strong impression on the interviewers.

**Tricks That I Followed:**

**1. Completed the Online Test as Quickly as Possible**

Time management played a crucial role during the online coding test. I focused on solving the easier questions first to ensure I secured maximum points before tackling more complex problems. By maintaining a calm and methodical approach, I avoided spending excessive time on a single question. Practicing coding problems on platforms like HackerRank and LeetCode prior to the test helped me develop speed and accuracy. This strategy not only improved my confidence but also ensured that I completed the test within the allotted time frame, leaving no question unanswered.

**2. Thoroughly Read the Job Description to Tailor Responses During Interviews**

Before the interviews, I carefully analysed the job description to understand the role’s responsibilities, required skills, and expectations. This allowed me to tailor my responses to highlight how my technical expertise and past experiences aligned with the company’s needs. For example, if the job emphasized problem-solving and analytical skills, I prepared examples from my projects or internships that demonstrated these abilities. I also made sure to use relevant keywords from the job description in my answers to reinforce my understanding of the role.

**3. Asked Relevant and Thoughtful Questions in Each Round to Show Interest and Enthusiasm**

In every interview round, I prepared a list of thoughtful questions to ask the interviewers. These questions were focused on understanding the company’s culture, growth opportunities, and technical challenges associated with the role. For example, I asked about the technologies the team frequently uses, the nature of projects I might work on, and how the company supports employee learning and development. By doing so, I demonstrated genuine interest in the role and enthusiasm for contributing to the company. Asking insightful questions also helped create a positive impression and made the interaction more engaging.

**4. Recalled Details from the Company’s Presentations to Incorporate Into Answers**

I paid close attention to the company’s presentations and used that information strategically during my interviews. For example, I mentioned the company’s focus on innovation or specific initiatives they highlighted, and connected them to my skills or aspirations. This not only showed that I was well-prepared but also conveyed my dedication to understanding the company’s goals. Referencing specific points from the presentation helped me stand out as someone who took the time to research and connect with the company’s vision.

**Preparation Strategy:**

1.**Understanding Job Requirements**: I thoroughly reviewed the job description to identify key skills and areas of focus essential for the role.

2.**Technical Skill Enhancement**: Practiced coding daily on platforms like HackerRank and LeetCode, concentrating on algorithms, data structures, and problem-solving techniques.

3.**Concept Revisions**: Revisited computer science fundamentals such as databases, OOP, and networking to ensure a strong grasp of core concepts.

4.**Mock Tests**: Took timed mock tests to simulate the online test environment, improving accuracy and performance under pressure.

5.**Company Research**: Conducted in-depth research on Bank of America, understanding their business model, values, and recent innovations to tailor my interview responses effectively.

6.**Behavioural Question Practice**: Used the STAR method to prepare for behavioural questions, ensuring examples from projects and leadership experiences were ready to demonstrate my skills.

7.**Mock Interviews**: Practiced with friends and mentors, refining answers, improving delivery, and building confidence for actual interviews.

8.**Resume Preparation**: Revisited all academic projects and internships listed on my resume, ensuring I could clearly articulate my contributions and learning.

9.**Consistent Daily Schedule**: Balanced technical preparation, company research, and interview practice through a disciplined daily routine.

10.**Confidence Building**: Focused on holistic preparation, combining technical knowledge, communication skills, and company understanding to excel in every stage of the process.

**BANK OF AMERICA (RANJITH KUMAR C)**

**SELECTION PROCESS:**

1) The company conducted three rounds. The first round was an online coding round with a requirement to post a video solution. The next two rounds were technical interviews.

2) In the online coding round, they asked two standard problems:

1. String problem

2. Balanced parenthesis checker

After solving both problems, candidates were required to record a video solution for any one problem. The video also included general questions asked by the company and had to be uploaded on the platform.

3) Technical Interview 1:

* They asked basic questions on core concepts like OOPS and DBMS.
* They also asked the logic and code for “Reversing a Linked List”.

4) Technical Interview 2:

* In the second round, they discussed my projects and asked me to write code for specific features of one of my projects.

**MY PREPARATION STRATEGY:**

* I started solving easy problems randomly on LeetCode during my second year. After getting comfortable with easy problems, I switched to medium-level problems.
* After solving problems randomly, I realized that the quantity of problems you solve doesn't matter as much as the quality. So, I referred to the Striver SDE Sheet and began solving the problems listed in it.
* In my third year, I started learning the MERN stack using Angela Yu’s course on Udemy. However, I did not focus much on projects, which turned out to be a major mistake.
* I started preparing for core concepts during the semester break after my 6th Semester.
