BANK OF NEW YORK MELLON 
FTE INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
 
ABOUT THE COMPANY: 
 
The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation, commonly known as BNY Mellon, is an American 
multinational investment banking services holding company headquartered in New York City. It is by 
far the world's largest custodian bank and asset servicing company. It provides investment 
management, investment services and wealth management helping institutions and individuals 
succeed in markets all over the world. BNY Mellon Technology Private Limited, established in 2000, is a 
group company of BNY Mellon operating in India, providing technology solutions to the bank. The 
Technology division has over 13,000 professionals, working across nine innovation centres worldwide, 
to deliver innovative technology solutions powering BNY Mellon business. 
 
      You can know more about the company at this website:       https://www.bnymellon.com/in/en.html 
 
 
Number of Rounds: 4 
 
1. Online Coding Assessment (90 mins) 
2. Technical Interview I (60 mins) 
3. Technical Interview II (45 mins) 
4. HR Interview (60 mins) 
 
Round 1: Online Coding Assessment: (90 mins) 
The online coding round consisted of 4 programming problems to be solved in 1 hour 30 mins. 
The test was conducted on Hackerrank platform. The problems were of different difficulty levels(1 easy, 
2 medium and 1 hard). The questions were as follows: 
Easy problem: (Distinct digit numbers) 
Print the numbers with unique digits in a given range (x, y) x and y inclusive. Time 
complexity was a factor to note while solving this. I solved it partially (8/12 testcases passed). I 
wasn’t able to optimize my solution and that’s why the 4 testcases got time limit exceeded. 
 
Medium problem 1: (Slot machine 2.0) 
 
I solved this completely. 
 
Medium problem 2: 
Given 3 coordinates (a, b, c) in a plane, check whether they form a triangle (It’s a 
triangle if the following conditions hold...|ab + bc| > |ac|, |ac + ab| > |bc|, |bc + ac| > |ab|). 
Check whether the points P and Q lie on the triangle formed. Return a number as per the 
below mentioned conditions: 
0 - If the points form a triangle and P, Q doesn't lie on the triangle 
1 - If only P lies on the triangle 
2 - If only Q lies on the triangle 
4 - If both P and Q lies on the triangle 
5 - If the triangle cannot be formed by (a,b,c) 
 
 
I solved this completely. 
 
 
Hard problem: (Bit profit) 
 
I was not able to solve this but I identified an edge case and returned the 
corresponding value for the same (2/15 testcases passed). 
 
Candidates who solved at least 2.5 questions were shortlisted for the next round. (22 were shortlisted 
for the next round) 
All the interview rounds were Face to Face and held at CUIC. 
  
Round 2: Technical Interview I (60 mins) 
 
The interview started with my self-introduction. And then the interviewer tested the 
knowledge of my fundamental data structures and OOPs. Concepts from arrays, linked lists, stack, 
queues and strings were asked. Then, the interviewer asked me to solve an easy level coding question 
both iteratively and recursively. Then, the interviewer asked fundamental questions from DBMS. 
Questions like, how APIs work and how Google search works were asked. The interview ended with 
questions based upon projects listed in my resume. 
 
Round 3: Technical Interview II (45 mins) 
The interview started with my self-introduction as before. The interviewer tested the 
knowledge of my fundamental data structure and OOPs. Then, the interviewer asked fundamental 
concepts from DBMS and some queries for a given table. He gave me two tables and asked me to 
write queries for the scenarios given. He asked me to explain my projects which I had put in my 
resume and also asked me some questions about the projects. He asked to explain how I normalised 
the tables of the database at each step in my project with diagrams. Couple of easy coding questions 
were asked and I solved both.  
 
 
Round 4: HR Interview (60 mins) 
 
The interview started with questions based upon my skills listed in the resume. He asked what 
skills I acquired as part of the curriculum and what I learnt at my own interest. He also asked how did I 
learnt the skills which I listed as learnt at my own interest. Following this, typical HR questions were 
asked. Some of them are: 
 What challenges you faced in past 6 months and how did you overcome them? 
 Did any conflict/misunderstanding arised between you and your teammate while doing the 
project and how it was resolved? 
 Are you praised by your surrounding people for your qualities? If so, what are those? 
 What did you learn apart from your college curriculum and what made you to learn it? 
 What do you do in your free time? 
 What do you know about BNY and why BNY? 
 
A behavioural question was asked after this. A hypothetical situation was given and he tested how 
would I react for the same. The situation is described as, I am a fresher who is part of a team comprising 
of 10-12 members. The team is so unresponsive that I wasn’t able to clear my doubts. None of them 
listens to my grievances and if some listened, no proper response was coming from them. 
After this a puzzle was asked to test my lateral thinking. The link to the puzzle is: 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/puzzle-18-torch-and-bridge/ 
 
 
12 people were given the FTE offer. 
 
 
 
General Tips: 
 Practice regularly on any of the coding platforms. 
 Try to complete Must Do Coding questions from GFG. 
 Familiarise with data structures and algorithms. 
 Concentrate on DBMS and OS too. 
 Have 2-3 solid projects. Self-Interest projects might be more helpful than subject related 
projects. 
 
Be ready to answer any type of questions based on your resume. 
 
Explain your thoughts while solving problems in technical interviews. 
 Try to give real life incidents as an example to the questions asked in HR interviews. 
 
Do not over-familiarize yourself with standard problems. 
 
 
 
This is Barathraj T from CSE, CEG (Batch 2023). If you have any queries, you can ping 
me at https://www.linkedin.com/in/barathraj-t.

**About the Company**:

Barclays is a major multinational bank and financial services company headquartered in London, United Kingdom. Founded in 1690, it is one of the oldest banks in the world with a rich history in the financial sector. Barclays operates in over 40 countries and provides a wide range of financial services, including retail banking, commercial banking, investment banking, and wealth management. They are actually the first company to introduce credit card in UK

Barclays Global Service Centre, India is a global capability center providing best-in-class Technology, Operations and Functions services to Barclays business(es) and customers globally.

**About the Role**:

During the internship I was put into a business area called “Functions Technology”. Usually Companies will allocate interns to various Business Areas(BA) according to their requirement. The domain was actually good. And yeah I should admit only the struggle is till you get an internship in a company but when you start doing your intern and when you start to experience the office life, it will be very nice. So try going to office everyday though you get work from home opportunity (This suggestion is only based on my intern period). I was supposed to work in an on-going project. So basically I was into Data quality framework and did data quality check for the most part of my intern and here and there I also involved myself in learning about how unstructured data is stored and that introduced me to vector db ,Pyspark.

**Overview of the Process:**

The overall process consisted of

* An Online coding contest in SHL platform
* Only 1 round of interview( Tech + Hr)

**Online coding contest in SHL platform**:

Usually if the platform is SHL, then the questions will be comparatively easy and less complex.
There were 2 sections-25 questions totally- 1hour time(not very sure)

* + 1st section – (20 questions)

MCQs(Aptitude, reasoning, concept related especially backend, db related)

Difficulty level: Medium

* + 2nd section – (5 questions)

No DSA questions. All the five questions were SQL query- related

So basically they will give the scenario and table and you just have to write a appropriate query alone for the given requirement.

Difficulty level: Medium to little hard

**Interview Round (Tech + HR):**

My Panel number was very chill and considerate. He made sure the candidate is not panicking and nervous. The interview went for almost an hour.

Technical:

Very first it started as usual I introduced myself ( after this he enquired about my native )and he asked me to walk him through my resume and yeah as I started with my Hackathon participation and he started questioning from there about “What did you do?”, “ how did you do?” and moved to my projects and he started questioning about the implementation, technical questions related that particular tools and technologies. In between I had concept -related questions too

* + Related SQL joins – various types
  + Sorting algorithms ( almost all easy to complex algos with an example)
  + Conceptual qns related to trees and graphs like DFS, BFS – explained with an example
  + I was asked to write a code to find the prime factors of a number ( I wrote in python) and explained him

HR:

In between tech qns, I had hr questions too here and there

* Why CEG?
* What after CEG?
* Where will you see yourself in next five years?
* What do you know about Barclays?
* Barclays usually have “RISES-Respect, Integrity, Service, Excellence and Stewardship” principle and so more HR will be related to that like they might ask you how did you apply this RISES principle when you ‘re heading your project team kind of?
* When you come to know that the interview is not interrupting you while answering and trying to listen well, you must try to put yourself in best possible way at that momemt.

In the last part of the interview, I asked him a few qns since it’s a fintech company I asked him “How much might happen in a day in Barclays and how much revenue will the bank make out of it? And what is the best thing about Barclays that you feel? And How did you find RISES principle applied in your worklife?

**TRICKS THAT I FOLLOWED** :

* First of all, Have patience, prepare consistently and hold out hope.
* I used ACM materials and Striver’s lecture helped me a lot and indiabix website for aptitude and few other materials.
* But meanwhile, try to build your resume which is equally important when you get selected for an interview.
* At least be thorough in the basics and in Arrays, Strings, hash maps and better know the rest of DSA concepts if not how to code.
* Also prepare yourself to tackle any sort of questions from your any nook and corner of your resume
* Mostly in interviews, they will try to check your understanding about the concepts.
* While going for an interview, make sure you visit their website and have a decent knowledge about that company and get some suggestions from someone who already gone for the same interview the previous year or someone who worked there.
* And during the interview , try to give your 100% and don’t try to manipulate much.

 2.  Interview Round (Technical + HR)

employee?

 Interviews 1 & 2

Since both the interviews were mixed(Technical + HR), I will separate this section

Technical:
DBMS: Asked to write simple SQL queries like self-join, group by, and rank
function.

SE: Stages of Software development, Approaching each stage, Risk Management,

Others: Questions were asked based on skills mentioned in my resume like Git,

Behaviour:

How do you sort out a technical difficulty you’ve run into?

Tips on How to Prepare
Have a solid understanding of the basics of your subjects in the second year.

Have at least two good quality projects (and at least one group project) that you

Have a good grasp of SQL and DBMS concepts. Use SQL HackerRank or LeetCode

Develop a basic intuition for Software Engineering.

Throw aside negativity while attending your interview and focus only on your

Presentation and Communication matter while attending your interview

**Kamesh S – INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY
Barclays Intern**

**About the Company:**
 Barclays is a major multinational bank and financial services company headquartered in London, United Kingdom. Founded in 1690, it is one of the oldest banks in the world with a rich history in the financial sector. Barclays operates in over 40 countries and provides a wide range of financial services, including retail banking, commercial banking, investment banking, and wealth management. They are actually the first company to introduce credit cards in the UK. Barclays Global Service Centre, India is a global capability centre providing best-in-class Technology, Operations, and Functions services to Barclays businesses and customers globally.

**Overview of the process:**The overall process consists of:
 • Online round (MCQ questions, coding question, and SQL question) (Online)
 • Interview round -I (Technical & HR) (Offline)
 • Interview round -II (Based on resume) (Offline)

**Online Round:**
 • In this round, there were MCQs from the basics of OOPs, especially Java, and some questions in SQL.
 • One medium-level programming question, with no restriction on the programming language.
 • One basic SQL question, again with no restriction on the language used for coding.

**Technical Round:**
 • For me, this round lasted about 20 minutes.
 • They asked about the resume. Make sure to know every single point in your resume.
 • The questions were technical: What was your role in the project? What programming languages do you know? Explain each project. What languages have you studied on your own?
 • They also asked if I had experience with cloud technology and how I used it in my projects.
 • Since Barclays is a fintech company, they asked simple questions about stocks to test basic knowledge.

**HR Round:**
 • After 2 hours, they told me I had the HR round (sometimes there is only one round where the technical round includes HR).
 • This round lasted about 20+ minutes.
 • Before this round, make sure to know about Barclays' Values (RISES).
 • They asked me to introduce myself and posed a single question:
 • What would you do if your team member is not completing his job on time but is trying to do his work?
 • Here, they looked into how I would solve the problem in different ways and wanted multiple answers.

Finally, they announced the result: 14 of us were selected from 29 students.

**Tips:**
 • Don’t worry too much about the interview; make sure to have a strong answer for "Tell me about yourself?" since it is the first question, and it sets the tone.
 • Learn OOP concepts thoroughly and be able to connect them to real-world examples.
 • Know basic SQL queries, especially if you've claimed knowledge of databases. Be prepared to discuss normalization, procedures, triggers, and possibly code.
 • Be familiar with basic OS concepts like booting, boot loader, page table, IPC, etc.
 • If you've used any cloud-based database like Firebase, make sure to know the cloud basics.
 • Finally, know every part of your resume. If you've mentioned that you are good at explaining concepts, they might ask you to teach them. If you've mentioned that you write poetry, they might ask you to write a poem!
 • If you feel tense during the interview, take a deep breath.

If you have any doubt in the questions, make sure to ask with the interviewer.

Technical Round

Around 45 students were selected for the technical interview, and I was among them. My

interviewer is working as a Vice President. When I introduced myself, mentioning my role

as the NSS General Secretary, the interviewer seemed intrigued and asked me to share a

 problem I solved in that role. After that, he shifted to technical questions and wanted me to

explain one of my projects. I chose to explain the more complex one.

The interviewer was keen to know more about my project, including the tech stack we used

and the purpose behind it. Since it was a departmental project and I only handled a part of

some follow-ups.

Next up were coding questions,

including two about binary trees. The ﬁrst was about

checking if a binary tree is balanced, and the second was about ﬁnding the Least Common

Ancestor. For both, I had to write out the code. He also asked me to explain a question from

a contest where I had won a prize.

To wrap things up, he asked a few questions about Barclays, such as why I wanted to work

there and what my plans for higher studies were. These were the types of HR questions

which were asked.

The company conducted only one technical interview round. 14 students were ﬁnally

BNY, I have encountered questions on topics such as multithreading in Java, dynamic

programming, recursive SQL queries and procedures, designing database schema,

system design, and operating systems (like LFU cache implementation).

**Barclays Interview Experience: OOP, DBMS, DSA, DP**

1. **OOP Concepts**: Questions revolved around basic OOP principles like inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction, and encapsulation. I was asked to explain with examples and how they are implemented in real-life applications. One scenario-based question involved designing a class hierarchy for a banking system.
2. **DBMS**: Key focus was on SQL queries, normalization, indexing, and transactions. I was given a problem to design a database schema for an e-commerce platform, and write complex SQL queries involving joins, aggregations, and subqueries.
3. **DSA**: This section had coding questions based on arrays, linked lists, and trees. I was asked to solve a problem on finding the longest increasing subsequence and another problem on implementing a LRU (Least Recently Used) cache.
4. **Dynamic Programming (DP)**: I had to solve a DP-based problem where I was asked to implement a solution for the "Knapsack Problem." They tested my understanding of both top-down and bottom-up approaches.

focusing  on  basic  DSA  concepts.  These  questions  were  not  too  challenging  and  did  not  involve

range of subjects, including DBMS, networks, and SDLC. The preparation I had done prior to the test

Interview: After passing the online test, I participated in an online interview with a senior colleague
based in Pune. The interview followed a standard format, beginning with an introduction. I was asked
a series of questions on DBMS, which were fairly basic. When asked about my  favourite subject, I
mentioned Operating Systems (OS), leading to a few questions on that topic as well.

The interviewer also inquired about my projects, specifically asking how I contributed to them and
their current status. Since the interview was conducted online, I was able to demonstrate my work by
showing project demos, which I had prepared in advance. Additionally, I was asked a question related
to computer networks. The interview concluded with a question about why I wanted to join Barclays,
which provided an opportunity to express my interest in the company. Overall, the placement process
was  straightforward,  with  only  two  rounds—the  online  test  and  the  combined  technical  and  HR
interview.

 SAISATHISH KARTHIKEYAN

TIPS AND TRICKS

1.  Strengthen Core Subjects and Fundamentals

Revisiting and mastering the fundamentals of your core subjects is crucial. Whether it’s DBMS,
Operating Systems, or Networks, having a solid understanding of these topics will set you up for
success, especially during technical interviews.
2.  Focus on Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA)

DSA concepts are instrumental in clearing the first round—typically the online test—of most
placement processes. This round is often the most challenging, so regular practice of DSA
problems is essential. Use platforms like HackerRank or LeetCode to hone your skills.

3.  Build a Strong Portfolio with Project Demos

A well-rounded portfolio with diverse projects is a great way to showcase your skills. Whenever
possible, have demos ready for your projects, as this can significantly enhance your profile
during interviews. Working on various projects across different tech stacks not only broadens
your knowledge but also boosts your confidence.

4.  Craft a Simple and Clear Resume

Your resume should be concise and focused, ideally fitting on a single page. It should clearly
outline your skill set, projects, experience, and education. Don’t forget to include any
certifications, technical events, or competitions you’ve participated in.

5.  Participate in Technical Events and Hackathons

Engaging in intra- and inter-college technical events, as well as hackathons, is highly beneficial.
These experiences not only help you learn new concepts but also enhance your resume. Plus, they
provide valuable experience working under pressure and in team settings.

6.  Be Honest and Confident in Interviews

During interviews, confidence is key. Speak clearly and be honest about what you know. If you
don’t know the answer to a question, it’s better to admit it than to try and bluff your way through.
Authenticity goes a long way in making a positive impression.

7.  Research the Company

Before any interview or even the online test round, take the time to research the company. You’ll
likely be asked why you want to join the company, so being well-prepared with knowledge about
their mission, values, and recent projects will help you answer confidently and convincingly.

CITI SUMMER INTERNSHIP INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
Company & Role Overview 
• 
Company: Citi (Citigroup) is a leading global financial services company 
headquartered in New York. Founded in 1812, it offers a wide range of services 
including banking, investment banking, and wealth management to individuals, 
corporations, and governments in over 100 countries. 
• 
Role Offered: International Clients Group (ICG) Technology 
• 
Other Role: Personal Banking & Wealth Management Technology (PBWMT) 
• 
Stipend: ₹75,000 per month 
• 
Internship Duration: 8 weeks 
Selection Process 
1. Online Assessment 
2. Technical Interview 
3. HR Interview 
Round 1: Online Assessment (September 27, 2024) 
The online test was 90 minutes long and consisted of four sections. 
Important Note: It was not possible to switch between sections; they had to be completed in 
the order they were presented. 
Test Structure: 
• 
Coding: 2 questions (45 minutes) 
• 
SQL MCQs: 12 questions (15 minutes) 
• 
React MCQs: 12 questions (15 minutes) 
• 
Angular MCQs: 12 questions (15 minutes) 
Section Details: 
• 
Coding Questions - The questions were considered easy and were based on arrays. 
• 
SQL Questions 
o The difficulty level was easy to medium. 
• 
React & Angular JS Questions 
o These multiple-choice sections included questions about finding missing terms 
and identifying correct code statements. 
Following the online assessment, 28 candidates were shortlisted for the next round of 
interviews for the ICG Technology role. 
Round 2: Technical Interview (Duration: 1 hour) 
The interview began with my introduction. I structured it to highlight my favourite subjects 
and fields of interest, specifically mentioning my proficiency in Database Management 
Systems (DBMS). This led the interviewer to start with questions about my DBMS project. 
Project 1: DBMS & Backend 
• 
Project Explanation: I spent about 10 minutes clearly explaining my project. Since I 
mentioned handling the backend and database, the interviewer asked for more details. 
I gave him an overview of the backend operations, including how my PHP scripts 
worked and interacted with the database. 
• 
Database Schema: He then asked me to explain my database schema. I detailed the 
structure of each table and explained the reasoning behind my normalization choices. 
This was followed by a question about the foreign key relationships I had 
implemented. 
• 
On-the-Spot Database Design: The interviewer then proposed a new project idea 
and asked me to design a database for it. 
o I started by asking clarifying questions to understand the project's 
requirements and finalize the necessary tables. 
o I designed each table with relevant columns and constraints, periodically 
checking in with the interviewer to ensure my understanding was correct. 
o After structuring the tables, he asked me to normalize the design. I explained 
my normalization strategy and justified my decisions. 
o Finally, he asked me to add foreign key relationships where needed, and I 
identified and included one. 
• 
SQL Queries: To conclude this section, he asked me to write several SQL queries for 
the database we had just designed. The queries primarily involved the use of JOINs 
and subqueries. 
Frontend & Other Technologies 
• 
React/Angular: The interviewer asked if I knew React or Angular. I was honest and 
stated that I was not very familiar with them. 
• 
HTML/CSS Task: He then roughly sketched a webpage design and asked me to 
write the basic HTML and CSS for it, which I did. 
Project 2: Python (DSA) 
• 
The discussion moved to my second project, which was written in Python and 
involved data structures and algorithms. 
• 
Shortest Path Algorithm: The project used a shortest-path algorithm. He asked 
which one I implemented, and I replied that I used Dijkstra's algorithm. 
• 
He followed up by asking why I chose Dijkstra's over other algorithms. I explained 
my reasoning and then named other shortest-path algorithms I knew, such as 
Bellman-Ford, explaining each one briefly. 
General CS Fundamentals 
• 
Java: He asked about my experience with Java. I explained that I was currently 
learning it as part of my semester coursework but was not yet proficient. 
• 
Sorting Algorithms: He then asked if I knew sorting algorithms. I said yes, and he 
specifically asked me to explain Radix Sort. I told him I was more familiar with 
Merge Sort and Quick Sort. He then asked me to explain Quick Sort, which I did with 
a diagram and an example. 
• 
Data Structures: The interviewer presented a scenario and asked which data 
structure I would use to solve it. I suggested a Queue, explained my reasoning, and 
compared it with other data structures to justify why it was the most suitable choice. 
• 
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP): Finally, he asked if I knew OOP concepts. I 
had prepared a concise, 10-minute explanation with a strong example, which I 
delivered clearly. He was impressed and appreciated how well I explained it. 
Conclusion & Feedback 
At the end of the interview, he asked if I had any questions for him. I had prepared two in 
advance: 
1. "How did I perform in the interview?" 
o He responded, "You are strong in what you know, and you know your 
strengths." 
2. "What areas should I improve on?" 
o He answered, "There is nothing you should improve; you are good. However, 
it would be helpful for your career in the industry to get to know more 
technologies like React and Angular." 
Round 3: HR Interview 
This was the final stage. Although it felt like a casual conversation, it was also an elimination 
round. The interview lasted for about 15-20 minutes. 
To my surprise, I was called in 9th out of the 11 candidates, even though I was second on the 
shortlist. When I entered, the HR manager mentioned he did this intentionally to see if 
waiting would make me nervous. 
Interview Discussion & Questions 
The conversation covered a mix of standard HR questions and personal topics: 
• 
Knowledge about Citi: He asked what I knew about the company. I shared 
information I had gathered from a senior who currently works there. He then asked if 
I had attended the Pre-Placement Talk (PPT). I answered honestly that while I had 
joined the session, I was traveling and couldn't pay full attention. 
• 
Motivation: He asked the classic question, "Why do you want to work at Citi?" 
• 
Future Aspirations: He asked, "Where do you see yourself in the future?" 
• 
Personal Interests: My resume mentioned an award in football. As a fellow football 
enthusiast, the HR manager started a friendly discussion about the sport, which helped 
build a good rapport. 
Conclusion & Feedback 
As the interview concluded, the HR manager gave me two key pieces of feedback: 
1. He advised that I should research a company more thoroughly before an interview. 
2. He shared that my technical interviewer had given "great feedback" on my 
performance. I asked him to pass on my thanks. 
I left the interview a little worried that my answer about the company wasn't strong enough. 
However, the results were announced an hour later. 
Final Result 
Out of the 11 candidates who made it to the final round, 7 were selected for the internship, 
including me.  
General Tips 
1. Be strong in DSA, DBMS, OOP, and OS. 
2. Create a clear resume and know every detail on it. 
3. Prepare explanations for your projects and a short pitch for each skill. 
4. Be honest and show them you are a capable candidate. 
 
All the very best!!

APPIAN INTERN INTERVIEW EXPERINCE 
 
Appian (pronounced App-ian) Corporation is an American cloud computing and enterprise 
software company headquartered in McLean, Virginia, part of the Dulles Technology Corridor. 
The company sells a platform as a service (PaaS) for building enterprise software applications. 
It is focused on lowcode development, process mining, business process management, and 
case management markets in North America, Europe, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia. 
Appian offers a lowcode platform with a visual interface and pre-built development modules. As 
with other low-code platforms, it enables businesses to create apps and automations using little 
or no code. 
ONLINE ASSESSMENT: 
The online assessment was held in glider.ai virtually and we had to solve 3 questions in 1 hr. 
Question 1: 
You will be given a set of paths where each path consists of a set of strings representing a path 
to a particular page starting from the ‘Main’ (homepage) element. You need to construct a tree 
representing every path given as input. The root node will be the ‘Main’ element. Finally print it 
out as per the required format. (Breadcrumbs problem) 
Sample input: 
 arr = [[‘Main’, ‘Dashboard’, ‘Settings’], [‘Main’, ‘Profile’, ‘Uploads’], [‘Main’, ‘Dashboard’, 
‘Products’], [‘Main’, ‘Profile’, ‘Remove], [‘Main’, ‘Manager, ‘Work’], [‘Main’, ‘Manager, ‘Review’]] 
Expected Output: 
Main 
  -Dashboard 
    -Settings 
    -Products 
  -Profile 
    -Uploads 
    -Remove 
  -Manager 
    -Work 
    -Review 
Question 2: 
Tickets are sold to a concert in a particular order. The number of tickets, the step size, and the 
offset will be given. Number of Tickets: This is the total count of tickets available for the concert. 
Step Size: This is the number of tickets to skip before selecting the next ticket. For example, if 
the step size is 3, every fourth ticket will be chosen. (It can also be a negative number.) Offset: 
This is the starting position from which the ticket selection begins. It can be thought of as the 
initial position in the sequence of tickets where the process starts. The challenge is to generate 
a sequence of ticket numbers based on these parameters, reflecting the order in which tickets 
are sold.  
It is a variant of the famous Josephus problem but with negative step. 
Question 3: 
You are provided with a list of group names and a list of people belonging to each group. 
Additionally, you are given an array of strings representing individuals standing in a line. The 
task is to reorder the array such that:  
1. If a person from a group is in the line, all other subsequent members of the same 
group should move to stand next to each other in the order they appear. 
 2. Once all members of a group are adjacent, replace their names with the group name. 
 
I was able to solve all 3 questions within 1hr. 22 people were selected for interview. 
INTERVIEW: 
Technical Round 1:  
This was a pair programming kind of round that happened for all students from CEG, but it was 
skipped for some students from MIT including me. 
 
Technical Round 2:  
This was a mix of technical and behavioral. As I had mentioned aspiring web developer in my 
resume, I was asked about what happens when an URL is typed in the browser. Then to name 
some data structures and their real-life examples completely away from computers. Then I was 
asked what my favorite subject was in general and in computer science and why. I explained 
why math was my favorite and why theory of computation was my favorite. Then, my favorite 
programming language and why. I said Scala and I was asked to list out its characteristics and 
about functional programming. Then, I was presented with a list of problems  
●​ identify the odd ball among eight balls, where seven balls were of the same weight, and 
one was either heavier or lighter. The challenge was to find the odd ball using a balance 
scale with just two weighings.  
●​ Pseudocode to distribute a total value according to weights in an array 
●​ Bubble sort and its complexity 
●​ Paranthesis matching 
Following that I was asked about OOPS concepts and if I had used them in any of my projects. 
And then I was asked to explain what was the favorite project I did and why I chose Appian. 
Finally, I was given an opportunity to ask questions in general or about Appian. 
Behavioral Round:  
This round was with two interviewers. I was asked some basic questions regarding my 
academics and personal background. Here too I was asked about favorite subject, programming 
language and why. Here it was more in detail with multiple follow-ups to my responses. I was 
then asked to compare Python and Scala. Then, I was asked what was my favorite project 
(Turing Machine simulator) out of those I put in my resume and the motivation behind it. Then, I 
was asked why I chose Appian. They remarked that I seemed more research-oriented and why I 
wanted to get into software development. Finally, I was given an opportunity to ask questions. 
 
In the end, 5 of us (4 of us from CEG and 1 from MIT) got selected for the position of Software 
Engineer Intern. 
 Tips: 
●​  Enhance your problem solving skills so that when faced with any problem in the wild, 
you can solve it without asking what species it is. 
●​ Be sincere and honest. Don’t express interest in a topic either when asked or in the 
resume because it would sound good. Mention what you really like so that you can 
speak more confidently. 
●​ Stay true to your interests. If your projects are aligned with your interests, it would give 
the impression of genuine interest. 
●​ In subjective questions like project related or favorite subjects, there is no right or wrong. 
Be confident. 
●​ Listen to the pre-placement talk carefully and try to learn as much as possible about the 
company. This can help you answer why you chose the company.

The interview panel, consisting of two members from Citi's ICG Tech Business Unit, began
by asking questions related to my resume. We first discussed my skills, with a particular

Following this, they provided me with a sequence of numbers and asked me to insert them
into AVL and Splay trees, as well as to perform deletions of certain numbers. Subsequently,
they presented a scenario involving airways and requested that I createtables for itanddraw
an ERdiagram.
In the data structures portion, they presented a scenario involving a train with various
compartments, where General and AC compartments are connectedsequentially. Theyasked
me to insert a Catering compartment between these compartments and to specifywhichdata
structure would be appropriate for this setup. I proposed a solution using Linked List and
provideda pseudocodeforthe requirement.This roundlastedapproximately 45minutes.

and hobbies. I was asked about my interest in pursuing a Master's, to which I responded
negatively. We also talked about Bollywood and Hollywood, exchanging opinions on their
film industries. The interview concluded with a brief overview of my family. This round
lasted around10minutes.

4.3 HR Interview
This round was short for most candidates due to time constraints, but I extended mine by
preparing well:
• The interviewer asked if there was anything I had not mentioned in my resume. I shared
real-world problem-solving projects, which were not part of the coursework projects listed
on my resume.
• I discussed my experience with a project aimed at helping the homeless, pitched during
a 7-day camp organized by Thozhan. The interviewer appreciated my initiative and
motivation.

what I would be doing if there was no engineering domain, and I answered ”service.”

| 5 Tips and      | Tricks    |         |     |
• CITI usually does not focus heavily on Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA) from
my experience. Instead, ensure you’re strong in database management (PostgreSQL,
MySQL), object-oriented programming (C++ or Java), and basic aptitude puzzles.
• At the same time, have a good grasp of data structures like arrays, linked lists, stacks,
| and queues | to stay in | a safe zone. |     |

•
Do not include skills or projects in your resume if you’re not confident about them.
• Generate possible questions from your resume and projects, and be prepared to code or
| explain | anything listed | there. |     |
| ------- | --------------- | ------ | --- |
•
During interviews, even if you’re unsure about something, handle it calmly and politely.
| Be confident | while speaking. |     |     |
| ------------ | --------------- | --- | --- |
• GPA is not everything—if you have a lower GPA, make up for it by shining with extra
| skills and | eagerness | to learn. |     |

**Gautham S – 2024 B.tech IT – Citi Corp**

**About the company:** Citi India is a leading global financial services firm, offering comprehensive banking solutions, including personal and commercial banking, investment services, and wealth management. Citi India provides innovative financial products and services designed to meet the diverse needs of individuals and businesses.

**About the role:** Project Manager/Scrum Master – I oversee project execution, ensuring timely delivery and adherence to scope, budget, and quality standards. I facilitate agile processes, coordinate cross-functional teams, and manage stakeholder communications to drive project success and align with organizational goals.

**Preparation strategy:**

1. Note down the different types of problems listed in “Striver’s SDE Sheet” ( Covers all the topics)
2. Take a week or 2 to UNDERSTAND and learn the topic, type of problem has a general skeleton, once you’ve learnt that your life gets a lot better.
3. Head to Leetcode and filter out and solve the easy questions first. Trust me it’ll help you to stay motivated. Most of the questions fall under the types that I spoke about before.
4. Don’t shy away from looking for solutions initially, whats important at this stage is learning to fit the types to the problem asked.
5. Move on to the Medium questions on Leetcode.
6. Start solving Striver’s SDE sheet. If you cant solve a question, watch the YT tutorial but come back and code it yourself without looking at the code.
7. If you’ve completed the sheet go for Leetcode Hard.
8. Pray all the gods to get all the luck you can. LUCK plays a major role sadly.
9. Make sure you know all the project you listed on your resume in ur fingertips.

**Placement Drive experience:**

Round 1: Online Test ( 5 Coding Questions & Personality Assessment)

Round 2: Face to Face at CUIC

1. General Intro
2. Questions about the project
3. Topic specific questions ( I used trees in my project so I was asked to do insert, delete and update an AVL Tree ,question on Linked list )
4. DBMS Questions
5. OS Questions
6. Leetcode Medium like coding question
7. 1 easy coding question.

Round 3: HR Interview at CUIC

**Tips and Tricks:**

1. Stay Motivated
2. Track you progress
3. See how you went wrong and why you went wrong
4. Note down questions that you think are good and do it later to keep in touch
5. Try not to be too rigid at the interview
6. DO NOT LIE ON YOUR RESUME
7. Striver’s SDE sheet is your bible
8. Do leetcode!
9. Don’t buy any paid courses, give it to me.

**Preparation docs or playlists:** Striver’s SDE Sheet/ TakeUforward . Just google it.

Feel free to reach out for any queries. Cheers and good luck.

Citi Interview Experience for Internship (On-Campus) 2022

Sanjay Pranao B G
2020115072
Information Technology

About the company:

Citi Solutions Center (CSC) is a global hub for innovation and operations within

Citigroup, providing comprehensive solutions and support across various functions like

technology, operations, finance, and risk management.

Overview of the Selection process:

The selection process included

1.  Online aptitude and coding contest

2.  Technical interview - Round 1

3.  HR - Final Round

Domains offered:

Although the specific roles were not explicitly mentioned, the candidates who got

selected in Round 1 were divided into two groups for offline interviews.

1.  Institutional Clients Group

2.  Enterprise Operations and Technology

Online aptitude and coding contest:

The coding contest was conducted on the SHL platform. It included 5 sections:

1.  Aptitude

2.  Logical Reasoning

3.  Verbal Ability

4.  Technical

5.  Coding contest

 Each section had a set time limit, and the next section could only be accessed after

completing the previous one. Each section had a time limit of approximately 20 minutes

with MCQs except the coding contest which had a time limit of 1 hour.

Questions asked in Coding contest:

1.  Rat in a Maze

2.  Not exactly sure, but a question based on tree traversal

Offline Interview:

A total of two rounds: Technical in offline mode and HR in online mode were held at

CUIC.

Technical Interview:

The interview panel consisted of two members from the ICG Business Unit of Citi. They

looked at my resume first and decided to ask questions based on that. I had done a

project in Java, so the first asked me to give the implementation of the project at a high

level.

Later, they asked me questions related to arrays, queues and linked list. And finally,

they gave me a scenario for a shopping website and asked me to create an entity

relationship diagram for the tables needed for the website.

HR Round:

After clearing the technical round, I was assigned to a final round with an HR panel. Due

to some time constraints, the HR round was done as an online round, with the

interviewer from the Citi HR team. The HR was very friendly, and we began with a brief

introduction, discussing my interests and hobbies. He encouraged my passion for

gaming, which is quite unusual to hear in an interview. We also talked about Bollywood

and Kollywood, exchanging opinions on their film industries. The interview concluded

with a brief overview of my family. This round lasted around 10 minutes.

Interview Experience

Name: Balasubramaniam M                                                     Reg No: 2018115018

Email: balasubramaniam082000@gmail.com

Company: Caterpillar India Private Limited

Caterpillar’s recruitment process contains four rounds. Eligible students

are allowed to attend round1 and round2. Based on round1 and round2
performance, students were shortlisted for Group Discussion. Based on
performance in GD, interview will be held.

Round 1: Aptitude

It contains quantitative aptitude, verbal and logical reasoning and

technical aptitudes. This round was conducted in AMCAT platform.

Round 2: Coding

Two coding questions were asked. Both the questions belong to easy

category. The questions I got was based on arrays. This round was also
conducted in AMCAT platform.

Round 3: Group Discussion

The shortlisted students were invited for Group Discussion (GD). GD was

conducted in Microsoft Teams. The shortlisted students were divided into
groups of 8 or 10. There were around 10 students participating in my group
and 2 to 3 panel members. Our group was given 2 topics to discuss. Some
other groups got only 1 topic. The 2 topics given to our group were “Is Gold a
good investment” and “Strategy or Execution”.

Round 4: Technical Interview

Students who got shortlisted in GD got a call/mail for the interview

process. There were 3 interviewers who interviewed me. Interview was
conducted in Microsoft Teams.

Initially I was asked to introduce myself. Then, I was asked about the
areas that I am interested in and the Internship that I have done. Then the
following questions were asked,

1.  What are the memories that are available in JVM?

 2.  State examples, where Heap and Stack memory are used.
3.  Can a static member function of a base class be overridden?
4.  If access specifiers are not mentioned to the members of base class, is it

possible to use it in the derived class?

5.  What is a final method?
6.  Name some of the collections in Java.
7.  In Collections, the data get stored in the order they are inserted or in a

random manner.

8.  How does hashmap work? How data get stored in hashmap?
9.  What is Hash Collision and how to eliminate it?
10. What are exceptions and name some exceptions
11. Are Exceptions different from Errors? Give an example for Error.
12. A Coding question: Reverse a given string using recursion.

INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE

Name:  SANJAYRAM R

Reg no: 2018115095

Email: sanjayramramesh@gmail.com

Company: Caterpillar India Private Limited

    The recruitment process consists of 4 rounds. 1st round is aptitude round, after finishing the
1st round eligible students appeared for 2nd round which is a coding round, few more students
were filtered in this round and the selected students appeared for 3rd round which is Group
Discussion, again the selected students appeared for the final round which is a one to one
interview HR + Technical.

ROUND 1:

    This is a aptitude round contains, quantitative aptitude, verbal - logical reasoning and technical
aptitudes conducted in AMCAT platform. This is not a major eliminator round, students who
answered reasonably well were selected for the round 2. Round 1 was held on 6.00 pm and the
selected candidates received link for round 2 by 9.00pm on the same day.

ROUND 2:

    This round was held on the same day of round 1 from 9.30pm - 10.30 pm. This is a coding
round, 2 questions were given one from array and other from string both were easy. I answered
both the questions and all testcases were passed. This is also in AMCAT platform.

ROUND 3:

    From round 2 shortlisted students were asked to appear for Group Discussion, this is round 3
which is a major eliminator round. This round was held few days after the round 2 . The
shortlisted students were grouped into a team of 6 where both CS and IT department from both
CEG and MIT were there. This round was conducted in MS TEAMS and we were given 2 topics
1. Globalization is a boon or bane - 15mins 2. Bit coin - 15 mins. Out of 6 students, 2 students
who spoke well were selected for the next round from each team.

ROUND 4:

    Round 4 is a HR + Technical Interview , which was held on the same day, selected candidates
received MS TEAMS meeting link. There were 4 interviewers, 2 handled HR and other 2
handled Technical part, this round went more than 45 mins for me.

    Initially they asked me introduce my self, I spoke all about my school education, my projects,
my Intern, my area of interest, my hobbies and my achievements. Then They asked me to
completely explain 2 of my projects, after explaining the 4 interviewers questioned me about all
the technical stacks in my project. This was the hectic part of the interview because they went
very deep inside the project, such that only if we had sincerely done the project we could answer
their questions. Since I did my project in firebase they also completely questioned me about
firebase. Questions about my project went more than 30 mins. Then they asked me a coding
question, There is an array of 0s and +ve integers, we need to sort the array such that all the
0s comes first followed by other elements, but in the same order as if they appear in the
original array, i.e)only 0s should be sorted other non 0s should not be sorted. 1st I gave a
brute force approach, but they asked about the time and space complexity and asked me reduce
it, then i gave an optimized approach and they accepted it.

   Then at last they asked whether I have any questions to ask them, I asked what will be my role
in Caterpillar and how was my performance and what are the areas I should improve. I
personally recommend to ask questions back to the interviewer it will always increase the
probability of getting selected.

NOTE:

    1. Always be confident while speaking to the interviewer, even though u give wrong answer
don't lose your confidence your confidence plays a major role.

2. There are n buildings with no. of floors specified in an array. There are k builders.

We have to allot the buildings to builders such that they construct only consecutive

buildings. Each builder takes one month to complete one floor. We have a budget of

(Rs.) b initially. We spend (Rs.) 5 in each month. You have to find maximum profit

amount (budget b – amount spent).

E.g.: Consider there are 5 buildings with floors [5,10,35,10,15], there are 3 builders

and we have an initial budget of Rs. 200.

If we split as Builder 1 – 5,10; Builder 2 – 35; Builder 3 – 10,15; we will have a max.

month of 35 so profit = 200 – 5\*35 = 25.

3. There was a music concert planned with given reserved seats in form of

coordinates as (x,y). There are speakers which covers only 90 degrees around it (in

any one of the quadrants). We can place speakers at any of these reserved seats.

Your task is to find minimum number of speakers needed such that it covers all the

audience (reserved seats).

E.g.: The coordinates are (-2, -2), (-1, -1), (0, 0), (1, 1), (2, 2). Placing 1 speaker (-2, -2)

can cover all remaining coordinates.

4. On a particular day, an employee can be ‘A’, ‘L’, ‘P’ where ‘A’ – Absent, ‘L’ – Late, ‘P’ –

Present. An employee can be rewarded with a bonus only if he is

Not ‘L’ for two days consecutively.

bonus?

**8** **students** were shortlisted for the physical interview held at CUIC.

**Technical Round 1:**

This round focused entirely on two DSA questions and lasted for about an hour. The session began with a brief introduction, after which the interviewer proceeded to the coding challenges:

1. **First DSA Question**: The problem was a variation of the Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS). The challenge was to solve a story-based problem built on top of the LIS concept.
2. **Second DSA Question**: The task was to determine if one string is a substring of another, with the additional twist that the string could be traversed in a circular manner.

After the DSA questions, the discussion shifted to my backend project, where I had used Redis. The interviewer asked about caching strategies, Time-To-Live (TTL) in caching, and some basic system design principles. He also inquired about how I would design an application like Spotify, and what data structures I would use to support its functionality.

**Technical Round 2:**

I was asked to give a brief description of the project and after that they went to the coding

part.

1. Given an array, we have to find the second largest element.

First the interview asked me to list out all the approaches that I came up with. After that he

asked me about the time complexities of each approach. At last, he asked me to write code for

any one of the approaches. He gave me an output and asked for the cases where will I get the

specified output.

2. Given a number, we have to find the next largest number using the digits of the given

number.

E.g.: Given number 52394, The next greatest number will be 52439.

This is similar to the question https://leetcode.com/problems/next-permutation/

Then he made a slight modification to the problem (to find the next smallest

element). He asked me to approach alone and not to code.

3. Given an array to find whether it is a rotated sorted array or not.

E.g.: [3,4,5,1,2] is a rotated sorted array (rotated in clockwise direction thrice).

He asked me to give the approach and to write the code in paper.

**HR ROUND:**

Firstly, the interviewer asked me to introduce myself, my family background and my interests.

Next, he asked me many questions like

1. Where I see myself in next five years?

2. What are the three important things to be followed and three things to be avoided to pursue

success in career?

3. What are my strengths and weaknesses?

The round went for nearly 45 minutes and completely full of friendly HR questions.

Out of 8, 2 got shortlisted.

**Must Do in Coding interview**

**Think Out Loud**: Verbalize your thoughts and reasoning throughout the problem-solving process to keep the interviewer engaged and informed about your approach.

**Ask Clarifying Questions**: Before diving into the solution, ensure you fully understand the problem by asking any necessary clarifying questions. This demonstrates your attention to detail and prevents misunderstandings.

**Start with a Brute-Force Approach**: Begin by discussing the brute-force solution, as it shows that you can identify a basic starting point. Then, work towards optimizing it, if possible.

**Use First Principles Thinking**: Break down the problem into its fundamental components and analyze them. Explain your observations and thought process to the interviewer as you work through the solution.

**Keep the Interviewer Engaged**: Avoid long periods of silence. Keep the conversation flowing by sharing your thoughts, asking for feedback, and explaining each step of your approach.

**Focus on Thought Process, Not Just the Optimal Solution**: Interviewers are often more interested in how you approach the problem than whether you arrive at the most optimal solution. Demonstrate a clear and logical thought process.

**General Advice While Preparing:**

Focus on **problem-solving** rather than just memorizing patterns from SDE sheets. While SDE sheets are a valuable tool for revising concepts, the best way to truly learn data structures and algorithms (DSA) is through active problem-solving. If you have ample time, consider starting with platforms like Codeforces, where you can work on ad-hoc and greedy problems. These problems help build strong problem-solving skills that will be invaluable when tackling standard LeetCode-type questions. Above all, be consistent in your practice, regardless of immediate results. Consistency is key to long-term success.

ANANYA K A

Company Name : Citi
Role: EO&T Intern

About Citi and the Role:

Citi Solutions Center (CSC) is a global hub for innovation and operations within Citigroup,
providing comprehensive solutions and support across various functions like technology,
operations, finance, and risk management. They contribute to the bank's ability to deliver
seamless financial services, supporting Citi's mission to be a trusted partner to its clients
globally.

Enterprise Operations and Technology (EO&T) within Citi Solutions Center (CSC) is the backbone
of Citigroup's global operations, responsible for delivering essential technology and operational
services that support the bank's vast network. EO&T at CSC focuses on developing and
maintaining the infrastructure that powers Citi's banking services, ensuring security, scalability,
and efficiency. This includes everything from managing data centers, cybersecurity, and cloud
computing to optimizing financial transactions and risk management systems. By leveraging
advanced technologies and global talent, EO&T within CSC ensures that Citi can operate
smoothly,
innovate continuously, and provide reliable, high-quality services to clients
worldwide.

Overview of the Hiring Process:

There were a total of 3 rounds of interviews which happened in CUIC and a coding test that took
place in the SHL platform.

There were a total of 3 roles offered to us from which we had to choose any one of our choice
before the coding test. The tests were curated specific to the roles so make sure you thoroughly
read the docs the company provides before opting for the role.

Online Coding Test

The online coding round was conducted in the SHL platform and had multiple sections,precisely
3. There was a time duration of 2 hours to complete the test.

 First Section: This was a purely aptitude-based section where speed was the key. The questions
were MCQs and were of easy level but needed to be solved faster in order to gain an edge. Try
to go for option elimination here instead of going for the hard way of finding every answer as
that saves a lot of time.

Second Section: This was also a MCQ-based section where all the questions were tech-themed.
The main topics covered here were DSA, OS and DBMS. Just get your foundations thorough on
these topics and you’re done. Again, speed plays a major role here too, so find your own
strategies to get to the answer fast and look at various types of problems in your freetime.

Coding Section: This was of easy-medium level and there was ample time to solve the 2
questions given to us. But here’s a catch too..since the questions were of easy-medium level,
optimizing them could gain you extra points and yeah,the time matters too. So, I gave the best
approaches to solve the problems and was done with the test before time. Sometimes, we tend
to ignore the edge cases in order to finish fast, so ensure that all edge cases are covered while
thinking of a feasible solution.

14 of us were shortlisted for the interview process that happened at CUIC.

Interview Process

Round 1 - Technical Round: This round consisted of questions mostly from the Resume and
other general questions such as hobbies, projects, co-curriculars, motive behind the projects,
social relevance of the projects and some language and tech-stack-specific questions that were
used in the project. In this round,they mostly check for the validity of your Resume and the
experiences that you state ,so be very truthful about them. Also,make sure that you talk in
detail about all the projects or internships that you have done to a point where they can
visualize what your projects are about or their motto is. Try to have meaningful projects in your
resume in order to rule this round. Remember one thing, the interviewers want to help you, so
when they ask you any question, try to bring out your approach completely, just start off with it
and you will progress as you delve further and further into it! The first question for every round
is ‘Introduce yourself’ and the last question is ‘Any questions for us’. Try to keep a clear idea of
the answers for these questions beforehand.

Round 2 - Technical Round:
This round was a purely technical round where I was asked to
write code for a couple of questions of medium to hard level in the language of my choice.
Questions on projects were also asked from the Resume and this time it was much more in
detail, questioning the validity and functioning of the projects. Whenever they ask coding
questions, make sure you explain your approach in detail to them before writing and then start.

 And always start with the brute-force approach and code your way to optimization. Toward the
end of this round, I was also asked a puzzle which involved aptitude and math that tested my
presence of mind.Focus on the logic to solve any problem and clearly define your approaches.

Round 3 - HR Round:
This round is the no-code round where your communication-skills,
teamwork, collaboration and conflict-resolution skills are tested. The HR asked me about my
projects here, but from a soft-skills perspective. All your knowledge about the company and role
is tested here.

A total of 4 of us were selected for the EO&T role .

General tips from my side:

Always keep your basics strong and keep revising them on a regular basis.
Read the complete role description thoroughly before applying for any company.
Ensure that you provide an optimized approach to your code and do it quickly.
Try to cover all edge cases that are possible for the given scenario as they eliminate the
issue of partial test cases getting done.
Pay attention to the PPT closely as they give you tons of points to talk about in the HR
round.
Complete the online test as soon as you can and don’t stare at the screen simply.
Before going for any interview, try texting the people working there or past people who
have worked there or people who have gone through the hiring process through
LinkedIn in order to get a complete picture of the process and what you can expect.
Try to focus on basics such as OOPS concepts, DBMS , DSA and OS , which means not
only the code but also the concepts involved behind that code.
Have 2 strong languages and keep the others as a standby. You don’t need to know many
languages,the depth of your understanding matters.
Never underestimate any question, it may sound simple but there is always a catch
attached to it.
Try looking for the company archives in geeksforgeeks etc before your interview.
Striver’s DSA datasheet and ACM’s CODE are good resources that can be used for
end-to-end preparation for all coding tests.

**SANJAY R - INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY
ICG INTERN AT CITI CSC**

**ABOUT THE COMPANY:**

Citi Solutions Centre (CSC) is a cornerstone of Citigroup's global operations, serving as a hub for innovation and excellence in technology, finance, risk management, and operations. With centres strategically located across the world, CSCs are designed to leverage diverse talent pools and deliver high-quality, centralized services. These centres are integral to Citi's strategy of enhancing operational efficiency, optimizing processes, and driving technological advancements. By supporting Citi's global network, CSCs ensure the bank remains at the forefront of the financial industry, delivering seamless and reliable services to clients worldwide.

**ABOUT THE ROLE:**

**Institutional Clients Group (ICG) within Citi Solutions Centre (CSC)** is pivotal in supporting Citigroup's global operations, focusing on delivering comprehensive technology and operational services for institutional clients. ICG at CSC is dedicated to developing and maintaining the infrastructure that powers Citi's extensive range of financial services for corporations, governments, and institutional investors. This includes managing complex systems for capital markets, transaction services, investment banking, and risk management. By utilizing cutting-edge technologies and a global pool of talent, ICG within CSC ensures Citi can offer seamless, secure, and efficient financial solutions. This enables Citi to meet the diverse needs of its institutional clients while driving innovation and maintaining a competitive edge in the global financial landscape.

**OVERVIEW OF THE PROCESS:**

The overall process consisted of

* An Online coding contest in SHL platform
* Interview Round 1 (Technical) – in CUIC
* HR Round

Domains offered for Intern:

* EO&T – Enterprise Operations and Technology
* ICG – Institutional Clients Group
* PBWMT – Product Development

**ONLINE CODING CONTEST IN SHL PLATFORM:**

* The coding contest was conducted on the SHL platform. Typically, online tests on SHL are easier compared to platforms like HackerRank, HackerEarth, or a company's proprietary platform, so don't be too concerned about the difficulty level. The contest comprised three sections: two aptitude sections and one coding section.
* The aptitude sections included straightforward questions, such as basic aptitude problems, technical multiple-choice questions, and topics like microservices and Apache Kafka. A unique aspect of SHL is that you cannot move to the next question without answering the current one, and once you move on, you cannot return to a previous question. On the bright side, there are no negative markings.
* The third section was focused on developing a simple app using MEAN stack:

1. We were asked to develop a simple front-end using AngularJS to get some details in a HTML form and send it the back-end.
2. ExpressJS is used as backend framework and using NodeJS to connect to MongoDB and store the data.

* In total, around 60 students were shortlisted for the interview process for the ICG intern role.

**INTERVIEW ROUND 1 (TECHNICAL):**

This round went on for 1 hour 15 minutes.

* I started off with my introduction.
* They asked me which language I am comfortable with and why.
* I said C++ and they asked me deep OOPS concepts in C++.
* They also questioned me OOPS in Java since I had mentioned that I know Java too.
* Then they asked me questions on OS in areas of synchronization, monitors, etc.
* In DBMS they asked about Normal Forms and Joins.
* They asked me a simple DSA question using Linked List. To find the minimum element in a linked list and asked me to optimize the approach.
* Then they gave me a puzzle to solve:

Consider there are 100 doors. And I will pass all the 100 doors 100 times and toggle the doors (Open if closed/Close if open). Initially all the doors are closed and in the 1st pass I will toggle all the doors. In the 2nd pass, we should toggle the doors in multiples of 2 (Door No: 2, 4, 6,…). Similarly in the subsequent passes we should toggle the doors in multiples of N in the Nth pass. The question is, “How many doors will be open after the 100th pass?”

* They didn’t need the exact answer. They only looked into my approach for solving the problem and my thinking ability.

**HR ROUND:**

It went for around 15 – 20 mins. The HR introduced himself to me and it was like a casual talk.

* Tell me about yourself, hobby, projects, family, etc.
* Do any of your parents are in govt job, had been working in Citi, etc
* Why do you choose Citi, etc.
* Any questions for him

She was satisfied with my answer and yeah… 11 candidates got ICG intern and I’m one among them.

**MY PREPARATION STRATEGY:**

* I didn’t prepare in my 4th semester holidays in a daily basis. I had spent my holidays in my summer project, a bit on intern preparation and in leisure. But as soon as the schedule of internships and placements are given by the CUIC, I did rigorous preparation. When I mean rigorous, I map it to its exact meaning without a cheat day. I was able to complete my intern training within 2 weeks in addition to the college work. I made sure to complete the preparation before the first company arrives. The reason why I mention this is, it’s not the duration of your preparation matters, covering all the topics, practising the needed matters the most.
* I used ACM CODE documents for my preparation
* Before every online test/ interview, I would surf the net and read the past internship experiences of that particular company. This gives an idea of how difficult the test would be, what are their expectations, etc
* And in addition, I approached my seniors who got intern in the concerned company and asked tips if there are any.  Interaction with seniors literally helped me to know the evaluation pattern, what do they expect, etc
* If I don’t know answers for the questions asked in a test of a company, I made sure to know that before the test of next company.
* Rejections do affect my confidence. But when you fall down, make sure that you are getting up from that.
* I had a casual attitude when I got used to the interview process after attending the tests of 4 – 5 companies. That really helped me to recover when I fail.
* I’m sure that the internship season would be stressful. You’ll have continuous tests, interviews, you have to manage your academics as well, lot of club stuffs and lot more. It was really difficult to manage all these, till I learnt how to do so. Every time when I feel stressed or down, I’ll let out my thoughts to my parents and my friends. Always only when you keep the stress with yourself, it hangs with you so let it go.

About the Company:

Citi Solutions Center (CSC) is a global hub for innovation and operations within Citigroup, providing
comprehensive solutions and support across various functions like technology, operations, finance,
and risk management. These centers are strategically located worldwide, enabling Citi to leverage
diverse talent and drive efficiency through centralized services. CSCs play a crucial role in enhancing

ensuring compliance with regulatory standards. They contribute to the bank's ability to deliver
seamless financial services, supporting Citi's mission to be a trusted partner to its clients globally.

About the Role:

Enterprise Operations and Technology (EO&T) within Citi Solutions Center (CSC) is the backbone of
Citigroup's global operations, responsible for delivering essential technology and operational services
that support the bank's vast network. EO&T at CSC focuses on developing and maintaining the
infrastructure that powers Citi's banking services, ensuring security, scalability, and efficiency. This
includes everything from managing data centers, cybersecurity, and cloud computing to optimizing
financial transactions and risk management systems. By leveraging advanced technologies and global
talent, EO&T within CSC ensures that Citi can operate smoothly, innovate continuously, and provide
reliable, high-quality services to clients worldwide.

Overview of the Process:

The overall process consisted of

● An Online coding contest in SHL platform
● Interview Round 1 (Based on Resume) – in CUIC
● Interview Round 2 (Technical) – in CUIC
● Interview Round 3 (Technical) – in CUIC
● HR Round

Domains offered for Intern:

EO&T – Enterprise Operations and Technology

ICG

PBWMT – Product Development

 Online coding contest in SHL platform:

The coding contest was conducted in SHL platform. Normally, if a online test is conducted in
SHL platform, it would be easier than Hackerrank, Hackerearth or any other platform of the
company itself. So don’t worry about the difficulty of the contest. It consisted of 3 sections
namely 2 aptitude sections and a coding section.

The aptitude section consisted of simple aptitude questions, technical mcqs, etc. But the
thing in SHL is you can’t move to the next question without answering the current question.
And once you have moved from a question, you can’t move to that question. No negative
markings.

The 3rd section consisted of 2 coding questions; both were simple questions.

1) Reversing a number
2) Given an array of numbers. Return the sum of 2 numbers whose product is

maximum. The numbers can be positive or negative.

14 students were shortlisted for interview process of EO&T intern.

TIPS for Online Test:

Always remember that if a test is easy, you have to race with the time. If more
people solve the given questions, then obviously the deciding criteria would be how
fast you did than others. Whether you do in brute force or in an efficient way, have
an eye on timing. I did the 2nd question in brute force but passed all the test cases.
The moment it passed all the test cases, I didn’t optimise my code. I just finished the
test before 30 – 45 minutes which greatly helped me get selected for the next round.

Interview Round 1 (Based on Resume):

This round was purely based on our Resume. The questions were general. The panel
consisted of 2 members and there were several panels. Questions and their complexity
depend on the panel. Some panel members even asked DSA questions.

● They had asked us explain our projects, the tech stacks used in it, etc.
● One of the panel members asked my favourite language and favourite project.

I said Python and I did a project with deep learning which was my favourite. He
asked me why did you choose that project, various steps involved in that project, etc
● Then they asked about the subjects which I study in my 5th semester and which is

favourite in it.
I said compiler engineering and Unix internals.

● Since I said Unix internals, he asked what do you study in that and asked How to
terminate a process that took all the memory while other processes are waiting for
memory.

● What’s your ambition?
● Any questions for him?

● They started with my introduction and stuff.

Fees paid. They had asked me to return the students who study Java course and not

● Write a code to find the missing number in the given range.

to write a procedure to sum up all the fees and pending fees.

another table table2. If the table 2 is full and trigger fails will it be updated in table

● Since I mentioned about compiler engineering as one of my favourite subjects, they
asked me about which compiler does java use? Do we need to change the compiler
for java if we change the OS? Which compiler does C uses? What's GCC? Why are so
many compilers present for C?

● And few small small questions here and there.
● Any questions for him

They weren’t bothered about the syntax. I mentioned in various places that I’m not sure
about the syntax and they were fine with that.

For few questions on Java and Compiler engineering, I said that we didn’t come to that level,
we are just in the basics and they understood my point of view.

For few questions, I got confused and confidently answered the wrong answer. But luckily,
they selected me for the next round.

Mostly, their first question was beyond the syllabus, operating on CSV files, etc.

Other questions like connect Python with postgres, Write code for insertion and deletion in
AVL trees in C, etc were also asked for other candidates.

 5 students were selected for the next round.

Interview Round 3 (Technical):

and a manager. D and E will report to him.

They asked me to write a query to list out the details of the manager and the count of
employees who are reporting to him.
I understood that I have to use INNER JOIN in the query but anyways I wrote a wrong query.

They were happy that I figured out that I have to use INNER JOIN and they explained the
correct query.

No shortlisting happened after this round. All the 5 candidates attended the HR round.

HR Round:

It went for around 15 – 20 mins. The HR introduced himself to me and it was like a casual talk.

● Tell me about yourself, hobby, projects, family, etc.
● Do any of your parents are in govt job, had been working in citi, etc
● Why do you choose citi, etc.
● Any questions for him

He was satisfied with my answer and yeah… 4 candidates got EO&T intern and I’m one
among them.

TRICKS THAT I FOLLOWED:

● As said earlier, I completed the online test as soon as possible
● The moment I came to know that I was selected for the interview, I patiently read the job
description. They had mentioned that “We are looking for persons who are eager to learn,
etc” and hence accordingly I framed the answers for “Tell me about yourself”
● In every round, they’ll ask “Do you have any questions for us?” as the last question.

Do ask a lot of reasonable questions,
about the company,

 tech stacks used,
kind of projects that we do in intern,
location of work,
Could you please tell me your experience in Citi,
The best thing in Citi according to you
Work culture in Citi, etc
Your questions will determine your eagerness to work in the company.

● At the same time, asking more questions seemed uncomfortable for me because that
seemed to be prepared and hence after asking 2 or 3 questions to the interviewers, I said, “I
do have a lot of questions but because of time being, I’m limiting myself”

● While asking questions,

I mentioned the full form of EO&T (Enterprise Operations &
few times which denotes that you have made few searches, read the

Technology)
documentation which again says your eagerness to work in the company.

● I listened to the PPT which actually helped me to get a view of what they do in Citi. This is my
advice, if you are selected for the interview, do gather info about the company and recall
whatever told in the PPT. Also while asking questions or answering the question “Why citi?”, I
mentioned “In the PPT, they said that…” which again added to my interest to work in Citi.

MY PREPARATION STRATEGY:

● I didn’t prepare in my 4th semester holidays in a daily basis. I had spent my holidays
in my summer project, a bit on intern preparation and in leisure. But as soon as the
schedule of internships and placements are given by the CUIC, I did rigorous
preparation. When I mean rigorous, I map it to its exact meaning without a cheat
day. I was able to complete my intern training within 2 weeks in addition to the
college work. I made sure to complete the preparation before the first company
arrives. The reason why I mention this is, it’s not the duration of your preparation
matters, covering all the topics, practising the needed matters the most.

● I used ACM CODE documents for my preparation
● Before every online test/ interview, I would surf the net and read the past internship
experiences of that particular company. This gives an idea of how difficult the test
would be, what are their expectations, etc

● And in addition, I approached my seniors who got intern in the concerned company
Interaction with seniors literally helped me to know

and asked tips if there are any.
the evaluation pattern, what do they expect, etc

● If I don’t know answers for the questions asked in a test of a company, I made sure to

know that before the test of next company.

● Rejections do affect my confidence. But when you fall down, make sure that you are

getting up from that.

● I had a casual attitude when I got used to the interview process after attending the

tests of 4 – 5 companies. That really helped me to recover when I fail.

● I’m sure that the internship season would be stressful. You’ll have continuous tests,
interviews, you have to manage your academics as well, lot of club stuffs and lot
more. It was really difficult to manage all these, till I learnt how to do so. Every time
when I feel stressed or down, I’ll let out my thoughts to my parents and my friends.
Always only when you keep the stress with yourself, it hangs with you so let it go.
