Interview Experience

Name :  A. Murugan

Reg. No : 2018115059

Company :  Lister Technologies

Month & Year : OCT 2021

Lister Technologies Recruitment process contains four rounds and they offer
two packages:

1). Samurai Programmer – 9 LPA

2). Power Programmer – 7 LPA

Round 1 : (Online Test)

It contains general aptitude questions and two programming questions.

We can crack this round if we knew all the basic concepts in aptitude and
practice it.

They asked 2 coding questions. One is easy and one is moderate level question.
We can solve these questions if we practice programming regularly/daily in
GFG/Leetcode/HackerRank/HackerEarth.

The candidates shortlisted from Round 1 attended the PPT. 59 students
shortlisted to attend PPT.

Round 2: ( Behavioural Assessment)

It is a non-elimination round.

Round 3 : ( Techno-Managerial Round)

This round went for like 90 minutes.

Interview started with introduce yourself.

Then he asked to elaborate the project which I mentioned in introduce yourself.

 I explained the project for 15 minutes and he asked questions from that.

Then he asked about my Final Year Project which we completed only less than
15% at that time. I explained about the Final Year Project and he asked some
questions and also give suggestions to improve it a bit. This discussion went for
like 15 minutes.

Then he asked me about how I am managing my time because I mentioned I am
part of 3 to 4 clubs and having some role to play in more than 2 clubs.

After that he started asking questions from Data Structures, Algorithms, DBMS,
Operating Systems, Computer Networks, Object Oriented Programming,
Software Engineering, React Js, Next Js, Ajax and some technologies I
mentioned that I have hands-on working experience.

In OS, he asked questions from Semaphore, Process Scheduling and Memory
Management.

In Software Engineering, he asked questions from Agile Methodology and some
basics.

He asked me to list various data structures and its applications.

He also asked SQL queries and some basic concepts from DBMS.

Finally Interview was over and he asked whether I have any questions.

 I asked him 3 questions at the end.

Be prepared or have some scripted answers for questions like introduce
yourself, some most common HR/Managerial questions asked in most of
the interviews ( Refer Google and Youtube), etc.

Have clear knowledge about your projects.

You must ask questions to the interviewers if you get a chance, because it
should be like two way conversation.

Round 4: (HR)

We made a Powerpoint to introduce ourself. So first she asked me to present the
PPT to introduce myself.

She asked questions about my family, strengths and weaknesses and other
typical HR questions. (Be prepared to answer all the HR interview questions
before the interview itself)

 She asked some situation based questions.

I got full marks in online test so she didn’t asked me to explain the answers. She
asked how I prepared to answer all the questions correctly.

They have our answers as PDFs and they asked questions from that. Some of
my friends are asked to explain the submitted answers.

At the end, she asked whether I have any questions to ask and I asked some
questions to her from the Pre-placement Talk. Make sure you listen to the PPT
well.

Finally, I got selected and got offer for Samurai Programmer.

Note :

•  Don’t give-up and keep practicing code and aptitude.
•  Be prepared.
•  We should be at the right place at the right time to get selected so don’t
get frustrated and stop revising/preparing in the mid of the placements.

All the best and All is well !!!

Mini Gnana Sekaran- Information Technology –6 months intern at Logitech

About the Company: Logitech is a globally recognized technology company specializing in
designing and manufacturing computer peripherals and software. The company is known for its
innovative approach to product development, delivering high-quality solutions in areas such as
gaming, video collaboration, and AI-driven advancements. With a strong focus on user
experience and technological innovation, Logitech continuously evolves to meet the needs of
both consumers and businesses.

About the Role:

Logitech offered two job roles for this internship:

1.  Frontend Development
2.  Generative AI

Overview of the Process:

The selection process included three rounds:

1.  Written Test (Technical Aptitude + Domain-Specific)
2.  Technical Interview (DSA, OOP, OS, Resume-Based)
3.  HR Interview (Behavioral, Leadership, and Project Discussion)

Interview Process

Round 1: Written Test

●  The written test consisted of two sections:

○  C++ Programming Questions (Mandatory for all candidates)
○  Specialized Test: Candidates could choose between a JavaScript test (for the

Frontend role) or a Generative AI test.

●  The test evaluated problem-solving skills, coding proficiency, and understanding of

role-specific concepts.

Round 2: Technical Interview

●  Data Structures and Algorithms: I was tested on various DSA topics, requiring efficient

implementations in C++.

●  Object-Oriented Programming: Questions focused on core OOP principles, including

inheritance, polymorphism, and design patterns.

●  Operating Systems: The interview covered key OS concepts such as memory

management, process scheduling, and concurrency.

●  Resume-Based Questions: The interviewer asked in-depth questions about my

projects, the tech stack used, and my problem-solving approach.

 ●  Leadership & Teamwork: I was asked about my leadership experiences, team projects,

and how I handled challenges.

Round 3: HR Interview

●  Resume Discussion: The HR round focused on my previous experiences, including

internships, projects, and extracurricular leadership roles.

●  Project Discussion: I explained the project I would be working on at Logitech, detailing

the technologies involved and expected contributions.

●  Behavioral Questions: Questions included how I handle conflicts, work in a team, and

adapt to new technologies.

Preparation Strategy

●  DSA & Problem Solving: Practiced coding problems, focusing on commonly asked

DSA topics.

●  OOP & OS Concepts: Revised core principles, ensuring a strong understanding of

system design and OS fundamentals.

●  Domain-Specific Knowledge: Prepared for the JavaScript and GenAI test by revisiting

frameworks, tools, and recent advancements in AI.

●  Company Research: Studied Logitech’s latest innovations, products, and company

culture to align my answers with their goals.

Tips & Tricks

●  Choose the Right Track: Pick a test paper (JavaScript or GenAI) based on your

strengths and interests.

●  Master the Basics: Focus on strong foundational knowledge in DSA, OOP, and OS.
●  Showcase Leadership & Problem-Solving: Be ready to explain how you led teams,

tackled challenges, and optimized solutions.

●  Ask Meaningful Questions: Engage with the interviewer by asking about the work

environment, projects, and growth opportunities.

NatWest Interview Experience 
NatWest Group is a prominent UK-based banking and financial services company, serving over 19 
million customers across retail, commercial, and private banking markets. As the group's Global 
Capability Centre, NatWest Group India plays a vital role in driving innovation and providing world-
class services and solutions to support the bank's global customer base. 
Role Offered: Software Engineer 
Sequence of Selection: 
1. Online Test 
2. Technical + HR Interview (Offline) 
 
Online Test 
The online assessment consisted of four sections: 
• 
Coding: This section contained two easy-level questions based on arrays and strings. 
o 
Problem 1: A problem related to finding the sum of elements at alternate indexes in 
an array. 
o 
Problem 2: Given a vehicle number (can be a positive or negative integer), 
determine its "track number." The track number is the smallest numerical 
permutation of the vehicle number's digits, which should not start with a leading 
zero. 
• 
SQL: This section contained 15 easy multiple-choice questions. No query writing was 
required. 
• 
Aptitude: This section focused on business aptitude and visual reasoning, including 
questions on scheduling tasks, counting figures, interpreting pie charts, and identifying 
missing figures in a pattern. 
• 
English Proficiency: This section covered general English skills, including grammar, 
vocabulary, and reading comprehension. 
 
Interview Round (Offline - 1 Hour) 
The interview was conducted in-person with a panel of two interviewers and lasted for 
approximately one hour. The discussion flowed as follows: 
• 
Resume Walkthrough & Project Discussion: The interviewers started by asking me to give a 
brief about my resume. They then asked me to explain one of my projects in detail. 
o 
Project: I explained my E-Commerce Recommendation System project. 
o 
Follow-up Questions: They asked about the choice of tech stack and the biggest 
challenge I faced. They also noted the absence of a Machine Learning project on my 
resume and asked if I was not interested in ML. I explained that I had completed an 
ML project but chose to feature other real-world projects on my resume, and then I 
briefly explained the ML project as well. 
• 
Positional Experience (CSEA): The interviewers asked about my role as the Logistics Office 
Bearer for the Computer Science Engineering Association (CSEA). They asked me to describe 
the association and my specific contributions to it. 
• 
Data Structures Problem (Strings): 
o 
Problem: "Print all characters that appear only once in a given string." 
o 
My Approach: I initially suggested a solution using a HashMap in Java. However, the 
interviewers added a constraint not to use any inbuilt collection classes. I then 
pivoted to a solution using two loops and a frequency counter array to keep track of 
repeated characters. They asked if this was the most optimized solution under the 
given constraint, to which I replied that it was the best I could think of at the 
moment. 
• 
SQL Query: Before the question, they asked me to rate my skills in SQL. 
o 
Problem: Given a single table with columns (emp_id, date, status), write a query to 
display the details of all employees who had a "present" status for at least three 
consecutive days. 
o 
My Approach: I explained and wrote a query using a self-join on the same table 
twice to solve the problem. 
• 
Behavioral & Situational Questions: The interviewers then asked a few HR-style questions: 
o 
"Describe yourself." 
o 
"Explain a situation which made your friend move in a completely different 
direction." 
o 
"Explain a situation where you had to take responsibility for a team." 
• 
OOP Concepts: The final technical discussion was on Object-Oriented Programming, where I 
was asked to explain the four pillars of OOP (Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, and 
Polymorphism) using real-world examples. 
At the end of the interview, I asked the interviewers about the tech stack used at the company and 
the potential work location. 
 
Results 
The results were announced on the same day. 9 students were selected for a direct full-time offer. 
 
Takeaways 
• 
Strong knowledge in DSA, OS, CN, DBMS, and OOPS is required. 
• 
Prepare your self-introduction well. Try to include your interests, the tech stacks you know, 
the projects you have done, and any achievements you have. 
• 
For DSA questions, don’t jump directly to the optimal solution. First, explain the brute-force 
solution along with its time and space complexity, then move to the optimal solution, 
highlighting why it is better. Even if you don’t know the exact solution, share your thought 
process. 
• 
Be well-prepared on your projects—know them in detail, including challenges faced and 
how you solved them. 
• 
Always prepare a few questions to ask the interviewer at the end of the round. 
• 
Be confident and be prepared. 
 
Resources I Followed 
• 
DSA: Striver’s SDE Sheet and Striver’s YouTube videos. 
• 
OS, DBMS, OOPS, CN: GeeksForGeeks, InterviewBit, and similar online platforms. 
• 
Language Specific: If Java is your coding language, have in-depth knowledge and prepare it 
from GeeksForGeeks and other platforms as well. 
 
A Personal Note: 
This was my fourth interview in total (2 during internships and 2 during placements, and I didn't get 
an internship offer either). So, don't lose hope. Give your best, as better things are coming for you at 
the right time. Keep learning, preparing, and making yourself better than you were yesterday. 
 
Best of Luck! 
 
Feel free to contact me; I will be more than happy to help.

CEG – CSE’ 26,

Mobile: +91 7358010926

APPIAN INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
 
Appian Corporation is an American cloud computing and enterprise 
software company headquartered in McLean, Virginia, part of the Dulles 
Technology Corridor. The company sells a platform as a service (PaaS) for 
building enterprise software applications. It is focused on low-code 
development, process 
mining, business 
process 
management, and case 
management markets 
in North 
America, Europe, 
the Middle 
East, and Southeast Asia. Appian offers a low-code platform with a visual 
interface and pre-built development modules. As with other low-code platforms, 
it enables businesses to create apps and automations using little or no code. 
There were four rounds of interviews. 
1. Coding round 
2. Technical Round 1 
3. Technical Round 2 
4. HR round 
Coding Round: 
Candidates were sent a link to attend the online coding round, which was 
held on the Glider platform. We had to solve three questions in an hour. 
Qn1:  
There is a line consisting of people from different groups. We need to 
rearrange the line so that people from the same group can stand together. If all 
the persons who belong to the same group stand in line, then the names of the 
persons can be replaced with the group name. The order of the groups in the 
line and the order of persons within the group in the line should not change. 
Qn2:  
Tickets are sold to a concert in a particular order. The number of tickets, 
the step size, and the offset will be given. 
Number of Tickets: This is the total count of tickets available for the concert. 
Step Size: This is the number of tickets to skip before selecting the next ticket. 
For example, if the step size is 3, every fourth ticket will be chosen. (It can also 
be a negative number.) 
Offset: This is the starting position from which the ticket selection begins. It can 
be thought of as the initial position in the sequence of tickets where the process 
starts. 
The challenge is to generate a sequence of ticket numbers based on these 
parameters, reflecting the order in which tickets are sold. 
Qn3:  
You will be given a set of paths where each path consists of a set of strings 
representing a path to a particular page starting from the ‘Main’ (homepage) 
element. You need to construct a tree representing every path given as input. 
The root node will be the ‘Main’ element (Breadcrumbs problem). 
Focus on your problem-solving approach to provide an optimal solution. 
22 of us got selected for the next round. 
Technical Round 1: 
I was asked to code on the Glider platform again (pair programming 
interview). I need to find the Fibonacci numbers from a stream of numbers. My 
interviewer helped me optimize the code. I was also asked to explain the logic to 
him. Later, he asked me about my projects, the difficulties I faced in building 
them, and the way I solved those problems. At last, he asked me if I had any 
questions to ask him. This round mainly focused on my problem-solving skills. 
Technical Round 2: 
This round revolved primarily around my resume. I was asked about the 
subjects that I like. Since I expressed my interest in DSA, the interviewer asked 
me about the data structures that I have worked with and also asked me about 
how to choose the right data structure for a problem. I was asked about 
asymptotic notations, and he wanted to find the worst-case time complexity of 
quick sort. He asked me whether I had taken part in any coding contests (CP 
contests). At last, he also gave me a chance to ask him a question. I was asked 
technical questions in this round, which weren’t too tough to answer. Just 
brushing through the DSA concepts that we have learnt in our earlier semesters 
is enough. 
HR Round: 
Even in this round, I was asked a few technical questions related to DBMS. 
I was asked about the many-to-many relation and the number of tables required 
to 1on RDBMS. I was asked personal background questions. Even in this round, I 
was given a chance to ask the interviewer a question. Make sure that your 
question is impressive. 
Finally, 5 of us (4 of us from CEG and 1 from MIT) got selected for the 
position of Software Engineer Intern. 
Key takeaways  
1. Try to provide optimal solutions 
2. Time management (you need to code for 3 problems in an hour) 
3. Focus on DSA 
4. Answer the questions confidently 
5. Questions are based on what you have provided in the resume. So have a 
clear understanding of concepts mentioned in your resume. 
6. CGPA plays a crucial role here. Try to maintain a CGPA of 8.5 and above. 
Hope this helps  
All the best

NatWest Group Interview Experience (Internship)  
 
About NatWest Group 
NatWest Group, formerly known as RBS Group is a British banking and insurance 
holding company based in Edinburgh, Scotland. It is considered one of the “Big Four” banks in 
the UK. It is also known as a bank of brands offering personal and business banking, insurance 
and corporate finance.  
 
Eligibility Criteria 
●​ 6.0 CGPA from 10th onwards  
●​ No current arrears 
 
Selection Process 
1.​ Online Assessment (Offline at CUIC) 
2.​ Pre-Placement Talk (Offline at CUIC) 
3.​ Technical + HR Interview (Offline at CUIC) 
 
Preparation Overview 
The coding questions were mostly array based and on the easier side. Try to solve 
questions on Leetcode, Hackerrank, GeeksForGeeks to improve your problem solving skills so 
you can easily solve the questions. Find a study sheet or plan you like and try to fully solve it to 
help cover all the fundamentals and give you direction. Make sure you understand the solutions 
instead of trying to memorize them. This understanding will also help you clearly explain your 
solution if you are asked a DSA problem in the interview as well as to improve your chances in 
online assessments.  
Since the aptitude questions required careful consideration and time management, try to 
solve aptitude questions on pattern completion, graphs and chart analysis, logical reasoning, and 
English aptitude on IndiaBIX or other platforms to get a better idea.  
 Go through common interview questions and study DSA, OOPS, DBMS, OS, Networks, 
and SDLC concepts,  as well as the fundamentals of at least one programming language.  
Be confident in everything you include in your resume. Anything you mention in your 
resume is fair game to be asked and expected to be answered with clarity. If you are not 
confident, exclude it. Have a good understanding of your projects and be ready to explain them 
and answer follow up questions.  
 
Online Assessment  
It was conducted on the SHL Platform at CUIC. It consisted of 4 sections that could be 
completed in any order.  
 
1.​ English Aptitude (15 minutes, 12 questions, no free navigation) 
●​ Reading comprehension, detecting what is wrong with the sentence, finding the 
synonym/antonym, finding the correct sequence of sentences,  etc.  
●​ IndiaBIX English aptitude has many similar questions.  
 
2.​ General Aptitude (36 minutes, 24 questions, no free navigation) 
●​ This section was totally different from other aptitude questions I’ve seen online and in 
other online assessments.  
●​ The questions required basic arithmetic and logical reasoning, but what made it difficult 
was the totally different format, time constraint and lack of free navigation, making time 
management crucial.  
●​ It was interactive and had questions that required us to select dates, schedule meetings, 
assign rooms based on a given criteria, adjust bar graphs and pie charts to reflect correct 
percentage values which are to be calculated based on given information, as well as to 
determine the next pattern given a sequence.  
●​ There was an option to attend a practice test, and if this option is available, please opt fto 
complete it as it helped me get acquainted with the format and helped me to avoid 
wasting time during the actual test  
 
3.​ Coding (45 minutes, 2 questions, free navigation) 
●​ The driver code was provided and only the required function needed to be implemented, 
though this is not the case for many companies where you need to code from scratch.  
●​ However if you require any data structure or algorithm that is not already included, you 
will have to explicitly add the header or import it yourself  
●​ The questions were both easy level array-based questions  
●​ Each participant randomly received a different set of questions.  
 
Question 1:  Return the odd-indexed elements of an array after sorting it in ascending order  
Input : [3, 1, 6, 6, 5, 7] 
Output : [ 3, 6, 7] 
 
Question 2:   Return the maximum perfect square within a given range. The question was 
straightforward, but not so explicitly given 
Input : [3, 26] 
Output : 25 
 
4.​ SQL (15 minutes, 10 questions, free navigation) 
●​ Query based questions were asked where we were supposed to determine the output, 
guess the query based on output, as well as conceptual questions.  
 
It seemed that the aptitude section was the deciding factor in many cases in terms of who 
got shortlisted, but doing well on the coding and other sections will always improve your 
chances.  
23 students were shortlisted after this round. The shortlist was announced the same week 
the day before the interview.  
 
Technical + HR Interview 
It took place at CUIC following an offline Pre-Placement Talk (PPT). In general, there 
was only one round of interview for panels with two members, though some panels had only one 
member and those students had to go through two rounds of interviews.  
First, the interviewers introduced themselves and then I did the same. Try to prepare 
something short but memorable about yourself, your experiences or your goals. Though I was 
visibly nervous in the beginning, the conversational nature of the interview helped me feel less 
nervous. 
They asked me follow up questions based on my introduction and then asked me 
questions about one of my projects, my contributions, how I would handle certain situations 
relating to the project such as if the other contributors are not willing to implement my ideas.  
Then I was asked to write a simple SQL join query after which I was asked what 
language I felt was my strongest. Since I had answered Java, I was then asked general Java 
questions like what is garbage collection, error handling, Scanner vs BufferedReader, etc. 
The flow of the interview is very much guided by your responses and many questions 
were asked from terms I had mentioned in my answers, so don’t mention anything you can’t 
answer a follow up question for. For example, when talking about garbage collection I had 
off-handedly mentioned the finalize() method and the immediate follow up question was to 
explain what it was.  
I was then asked to write pseudocode for an array-based question. When asked to write 
pseudocode, always try to explain your train of thought including any bruteforce solution that 
comes to mind, and always remember to explain any edge cases that may be present.  
 
Question :  Given an array of characters, sequentially print the character and its frequency 
Input : [a, a, b, b, b, c, d, d, e] 
Output : a2 b3 c1 d2 e1 
 
I was finally asked if I had any questions for them. I asked how I could better equip 
myself for this internship if given the opportunity. This question turned into a series of HR 
questions where I was asked how I would learn new things, and what I think is required for 
effective team collaboration.  
Try to prepare a question beforehand based on something that you want to know, or 
something you learned from the company’s PPT etc. The interviewers were very kind and 
engaging making for an overall good experience. 
 
Finally, 9 students were shortlisted for the internship, and I am very grateful for the opportunity.   
 
General Advice 
I’m not sure how qualified I am to give advice :), but from personal experience, the main 
thing I would stress is to not lose faith in yourself if you are not shortlisted. The mental toll and 
seriousness of the whole process only sunk in for me when companies actually started coming 
despite previous warnings. This process has quite a bit of luck involved, but the only thing we 
can do to improve our chances is to keep practicing, which is hard to do when it feels like there 
is no end in sight. If you find yourself struggling with this, remember that you are not alone, and 
many before you have felt exactly the same. So, keep going and may you see the light at the end 
of the tunnel!  
 
Resources 
1.​ LeetCode, HackerRank, GeeksForGeeks, takeUForward, IndiaBIX 
2.​ https://leetcode.com/studyplan/leetcode-75/ 
3.​ https://leetcode.com/problem-list/oizxjoit/ 
4.​ https://takeuforward.org/interviews/must-do-questions-for-dbms-cn-os-interviews-sde-co
re-sheet/ 
5.​ https://takeuforward.org/interviews/strivers-sde-sheet-top-coding-interview-problems 
6.​ C.O.D.E series conducted by ACM-CEG

Amazon Interview Experience 
 
About the company: 
Amazon is a leading American multinational technology company with a primary focus on e-
commerce, cloud computing, online advertising, digital streaming, and artificial intelligence. It is 
recognized as one of the "Big Five" tech giants in the U.S. As a product-driven company, Amazon 
is also part of the well-known MAANG group, representing the most influential companies in the 
tech industry. 
• Role Offered: SDE-2m Intern. 
• Stipend: 1,10,000 INR + 400 USD per month. 
• Selection Process: PPT, Online Assessment, Technical Interview. 
Online Test: 
The assessment included two coding questions and a work personality test. The online 
Assessment was conducted in HackerRank platform. 
• Coding Questions: 2 (Total time: 70 minutes) 
• Personality Test: No time limit (Mandatory to complete) 
 
Coding Question 1: 
Given an integer x and a vector cost of integers. Your task is to count the number of pairs (i, j) 
where i < j and the sum of cost[i] + cost[j] is divisible by x. 
 
Approach:  
I used modulo arithmetic to find pairs whose sum is divisible by a given integer x. By calculating 
the remainder of each element in the array and using a hash map to track the frequency of these 
remainders, we can efficiently find complement remainders that, when added to the current 
element's remainder, result in a multiple of x. Then I counted the valid pairs by incrementing 
based on the frequency of the complements. 
Coding question 2: 
Given a list of strings patterns, each consisting of characters and wildcard symbols (?), determine 
the minimum number of columns where a character conflict occurs. A character conflict in a 
column happens when different non-wildcard characters are found in that column. Wildcard 
symbols are ignored in conflict checks. Return the count of such conflicting columns. 
Approach: 
For each column, we shall track characters seen so far, ignoring wildcard characters (?). If two 
non-wildcard characters differ within the same column, it indicates a conflict, and we increment 
the count of such conflicts. 
Based on this Online Assessment, 52 people were shortlisted for the next round. 
Technical Interview: 
The interview took place on Amazon's Chime platform, with a link provided to join the 
meeting. Two recruiters participated: one as the primary interviewer and the other as an 
observer. Each of us introduced ourselves, and it was noted that there would be no separate 
HR round. So make sure that  you showcase your technical strengths and relevant projects 
during your introduction. Following this, you’ll be asked to share your screen and use 
LiveCode to tackle the coding questions.  
The interviewer will post the coding question on the platform. Be sure to read and 
understand the question thoroughly. It’s a good idea to take a few minutes to consider your 
approach before starting. If you have any questions about the problem, don’t hesitate to ask 
the interviewer for clarification. 
Begin by describing the brute force approach before moving on to the optimized solution. 
Ensure you engage with the interviewer during the coding process and avoid being silent. 
When discussing time and space complexities, provide a thorough explanation. 
Coding Question 1: 
Given a 2D array where each index represents a bomb with a specific detonation radius. 
When a bomb detonates, it can set off other bombs within its radius. The task is to find the 
maximum number of bombs that can be detonated starting from any single bomb, 
considering the cascading effect of detonation. 
Problem Link: https://leetcode.com/problems/detonate-the-maximum-
bombs/description/ 
First, the interviewer asked me to explain the brute force approach, which I did using DFS. 
Then, she asked about the time and space complexities. After 5 to 10 minutes, she asked 
me to code the function. She also wanted to know if there were any possible optimizations. 
I thought about it for a few minutes, and then she moved on to the next question. 
 
Coding Question 2: 
Given an array of integers where each integer represents a ball of a specific color: 0 for white, 
1 for red, and 2 for blue. The task is to sort this array in-place such that all balls of the same 
color are grouped together. This problem is often referred to as the "Dutch National Flag 
Problem." 
Problem Link: https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/sort-an-array-of-0s-1s-and-2s/ 
She directly asked for the optimized approach. Since I was familiar with the three-pointer 
algorithm to solve the problem, I explained that method to her. I was then asked to code the 
solution and perform a dry run with the provided sample input. Finally, she inquired about 
the time and space complexities of the code. 
The interview was scheduled to last exactly one hour. You are likely to be selected if you 
solve both questions within the time limit. At the end, the interviewer asked if I had any 
questions for her.  
Be confident and explain your approach clearly. The interviewers are mostly interested in your 
thought process rather than your code. 
All the very best!

INTERNSHIP EXPERIENCE 
 
ABOUT THE COMPANY 
 
Accenture is a multinational professional services company originating in the United 
States and headquartered in Dublin, Ireland, that specializes in information technology 
services and management consulting. Accenture provides a wide range of services and 
solutions in strategy, consulting, digital, technology, and operations. The company helps 
organizations to improve their performance and create sustainable value for their 
stakeholders. 
 
INTERVIEW PROCESS 
 
Total Rounds : 4 
 
Round 1 : Cognitive and Technical Assessment 
 
This is a 90 minutes assessment consisting of 90 MCQs covering the following sections: 
• English Ability 
• Critical Thinking 
• Abstract Reasoning 
• Common Application and MS Office 
• Pseudo Code 
• Fundamental of Networking, Security and Cloud 
This is an eliminatory round. Within 5 - 10 minutes of submitting the test you will get 
the results and candidates who have cleared the assessment will receive an email to proceed 
to the second round. 
 
Round 2 : Coding Assessment 
In this round there will be 2 coding questions that can be solved in your preferred 
programming language : C, C++, Java, Python, … within 45 minutes. The questions will range 
from easy to medium difficulty. This is also an elimination round and successful candidates 
will receive a mail regarding the next round. 
 
Round 3 : Communication Assessment 
 
After 3 days, you will receive a mail to participate in the communication assessment. 
This is a mandatory round but not eliminatory. This round is designed to evaluate your English 
pronunciation, speaking, and listening skills. This round will take around 30 minutes. Make 
sure to sit in a noise free environment. Consists of the following sections : 
• Reading 
• Listen and Repeat 
• Answering to Questions asked 
• Building a sentence from the given jumbled words 
• Story retelling 
• Answering Open ended Questions 
After completing this round you will receive a mail to choose the time slot for your 
interview. 
Round 4 : Interview 
 
The interview will be conducted after 3 days of your communication assessment in 
your preferred time slot. The interview will last around 10 – 15 minutes. It was a 1 on 1 
round and the interviewer asked me the following questions: 
1. Tell me about yourself. 
2. He asked me to explain about one of the projects mentioned in my resume. 
3. He asked me if it was an individual project or team project? 
4. How did we split the project among the team? 
5. Difficulties faced during the project 
6. Who evaluated your project and what was the feedback? 
7. Have you implemented the change provided by your evaluator? 
8. Asked me about my hobbies  
9. Do you have any questions for me? 
 Take Away 
 
Be honest with whatever you mentioned in your resume and have a clear knowledge 
of all your projects. Have a basic knowledge in all core concepts and practice DSA 
consistently on platforms like Leetcode. 
Hope this helps, thank you!

Round 3 : HR 
The HR round was very casual as compared to other rounds and it was for about 15-20 minutes.

1. Introduce yourself.

Take away:

 Make yourself comfortable with the interviewer and ask him to repeat the question if you 
don’t understand it rather than providing a wrong answer partially listening to the question. 
 Be confident, bold, such that interviewer should feel the positivity in you.

don’t rush to crack some other jokes(though they may be friendly).

don’t know how to answer a question, think for a while and discuss your ideas with them 
and have an interaction with them. Its not necessary that you should arrive at the answer. 
 If you don’t know about the topic they ask, don’t directly say you don’t know, try diverting 
them to the topic you are familiar about, like “I am not so strong in this version but I have 
worked in the lower version of this software” and bring them to the topic you know. This 
technique heavily impresses the Interviewer.

Amazon Interview Experience 
 
Amazon is an American multinational technology company focusing on e-
commerce, cloud computing, online advertising, digital streaming, and artificial 
intelligence. It is considered one of the Big Five American technology 
companies, alongside Alphabet (parent company of Google), Apple, Meta 
(parent company of Facebook) and Microsoft. It is a product-based company and 
one of the infamous MAANG companies. 
 
Role Offered: SDE-2m Intern 
Stipend: 1,10,000 INR + 400 USD per month 
Sequence of selection: PPT, Online Assessment, Technical Interview 
 
Online Assessment: 
 
It consisted of two coding questions and a work personality test. 
• Coding Question - 2(A total of 70 minutes) 
• Personality test – No time constraint (But compulsory to complete) 
The question pattern for the online assessment would change every year. This 
year it was only coding round whereas previously they would ask coding 
questions along with some technical questions. So, prepare yourself for both 
coding questions and MCQ’s. 
The online Assessment was conducted in HackerRank platform. 
 
Coding Question 1: 
Given an array of strings which consists of the names of the websites that a user 
visits. The task is to return an array of strings in the order such that the websites 
that are recently visited should appear first and also the array must only contain 
distinct elements. 
 
Sample Testcase: 
Str = [“Amazon”, “Facebook”, “YouTube”, “Twitter”, “Facebook”, “Amazon”, 
“YouTube”] 
Output:  
Ans = [“YouTube”, “Amazon”, “Facebook”, “Twitter”] 
Each got a different set of questions and mostly everything was easy. 
 
Coding Question 2: 
Given a binary string where some positions are marked as x other than 1’s and 
0’s. And the defect rate is the number of positions in which the two consecutive 
characters are different. The task is to minimize the defect rate by filling the 
positions which are marked as x by either 0 or 1 and return the defect rate. 
 
Sample Testcase: 
Str = “0100x011x10xx” 
Output: 4 
Explanation: 
Replace the first x by 0, the second x by 1 and the remaining x’s by 0 so you will 
get “0100001111000” and the number of positions in which the two consecutive 
characters are different are 4. 
 
Then a personality test was conducted which mostly had questions which was 
asked to test our thinking ability and how we approach a problem. I don’t think it 
was included in the marking scheme. 
 
Based on this Online Assessment, 20 people were shortlisted for the next round. 
 
Technical Interview: (1 hour) 
The interview was conducted in Amazon’s chime platform. We were provided 
an amazon chime link for the meeting. 
In the interview, we had an introduction where each of us took a turn introducing 
ourselves. In this interview, you wouldn’t have any HR round to talk about your 
strengths. So, when you introduce yourself, tell them your technical strengths and 
what projects you have worked on. Present yourself in a pleasing way. Then you 
would be asked to share your screen and use LiveCode to code your questions.

me to code the same. Then they enquired me about the time and space 
complexities.

they enquired me about the time and space complexities.

Tips:

practice 
is 
important.

questions 
in

• I followed C.O.D.E. resources and takeUForward YouTube channel for 
my preparation on topics like Trees, Graphs, DP and Recursion. 
• If you are not confident with DBMS, OS and OOPS, then prepare for them 
simultaneously because you won’t be having much time between the online 
test and the interview. 
• Aptitude is as important as DSA. At least start learning aptitude two weeks 
before your online test. 
• Even though I was not asked about my projects, in every interview they 
will ask about your projects, and they might ask you about the database 
structure and all. So be prepared to answer any questions related to your 
projects. 
• For any DSA question give them the brute force approach and don’t jump 
to the optimized answer directly. The interviewers are mostly interested in 
your thought process rather than your code. 
In the end, after all the preparation go through the 14 leadership requirements 
from Amazon (just in case, interviewer may check) and STAR approach. 
 
All the very best!!!

**Mphasis interview Experience**

**About Mphasis:**

Mphasis Limited is a global information technology services and consulting company headquartered in Bengaluru, India. Founded in 2000, the company provides end-to-end technology solutions including application development, cloud computing, artificial intelligence, data analytics, cybersecurity, and business process services. Mphasis primarily serves clients in banking, financial services, insurance, healthcare, and logistics sectors, with a strong focus on digital transformation through its Front2Back™ approach. The company operates across multiple countries and works with several Fortune 500 clients, making it a well-known IT services provider in India and globally.

**About the Role:**

**Software Engineer ( Intern + Full Time )**

**Selection Process:**

1. **Online Test**
2. **Technical Interview**
3. **HR interview**

**Online Test:**

The online test was conducted on the HirePro platform. It consisted of 55 multiple-choice questions covering Aptitude, English, and Computer Programming, along with one programming (coding) question. Around 96 candidates were shortlisted.

**Round 1 - Technical Interview [40 Mintues] :**

The interview began with my self-introduction, followed by questions about my project. I explained my projects, and the interviewer asked several related questions, which I answered. Later, I was asked two questions on Data Structures, followed by questions on Operating Systems and Database Management Systems (DBMS) AND OOPS. The interviewer also asked about the programming languages I am confident in, specifically Python and Java.

**Round 2- HR Interview: [10 Mintues]**

The HR interview focused on my willingness to relocate and my certifications. I also clarified details regarding my package and joining date. Finally, 60 candidates were shortlisted, and I was one of them.

**My preparation Strategy:**

I practiced problem-solving on LeetCode using the Striver A–Z Sheet. During my initial coding practice, I worked on problems from the ACM problem sheet. I revised core computer science concepts using GeeksforGeeks and InterviewBit, covering DBMS, Operating Systems, OOPS, Computer Networks, and System Design.

In my placement journey, during the initial period, I was not able to clear the online tests. However, after about one and a half months of consistent preparation, I cleared my first online test. That first interview gave me confidence in the placement process. I gained valuable experience and confidence, as I had no prior interview experience except for mock interviews.

Through virtual interviews, I learned the importance of checking camera, microphone, and internet connectivity beforehand to avoid technical issues.

I attended four interviews, and Mphasis was the fifth company where I attended an interview. I performed well, but I was not selected for the next round. During that time, I experienced a lot of stress and self-doubt. However, I learned an important lesson: trust the process. Do not lose hope. Prepare consistently and never give up. Patience is key in the placement journey—never lose hope and trust the process.

D E SHAW INTERNSHIP INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
 
About the company- D. E. Shaw is a multinational investment management 
firm founded in 1988 by David E. Shaw and based in New York City. The 
company is known for developing complicated mathematical models and 
computer programs to exploit anomalies in financial markets. 
Role Offered- SDET Intern (For the Quality and Test Engineering team based in 
Hyderabad) 
Stipend- 1,50,000 INR per month 
Selection process- 
1. Online Assessment 
2. Technical Interview 1 
3. HR Interview 
4. Technical Interview 2 
 
Online Assessment 
The Online Assessment was conducted on Hackerrank with a total duration of 95 
minutes. The online assessment had 3 sections- 
• Coding Questions (1st Question - 20 minutes & 2nd Question - 30 minutes) 
• Technical MCQs (12 Questions - 17 minutes) 
• Aptitude MCQs (14 Questions - 18 minutes) 
Since each section had a separate time limit, it was necessary to complete the 
test sequentially and the extra time would not be carried forward for the 
remaining sections. The MCQs also had negative marks for incorrect answers. 
 
Coding Question 1 
Given an array of integers of size n, consider subarrays of a given size k. For 
every possible subarray of size k within the given array, calculate the sum of the 
absolute differences of all pairs of elements in that subarray and return the 
maximum sum in that array. 
Example input –  
Array - [1,2,3,5] 
k - 3 
Output- 
8 
I solved this question with a Brute Force approach after finding all possible 
subarrays of size k and finding sum of absolute differences for each subarray to 
find maximum sum of absolute differences. However, I was able to pass all 
example testcases without exceeding the time limit. 
 
Coding Question 2 
Given a string s and two empty strings pre and post, find the lexicographically 
smallest post string that can be formed by using all characters of string s by 
performing either of the two operations – removing an element from the 
beginning of s and adding it to the end of pre or removing an element from the 
end of pre and adding it to post. 
Example input –  
s – ‘cab’ 
Output –  
‘abc’ 
I used a stack approach for moving characters in and out of the pre string and I 
used the Greedy approach to ensure that the lexicographically smallest string is 
the result. I was able to pass all example testcases with my approach. 
 
Technical MCQs 
The Technical MCQs were simple questions mostly based on DSA, DBMS, CN, 
finding errors in programs, predicting the output of a program and completing a 
portion of a given code. 
 
Aptitude MCQs 
The Aptitude related MCQs were a bit complex and included complex math 
questions too with the time limit making it harder to answer. 
 
Based on the results of the Online Assessment, 17 people were shortlisted for 
the Interview. 
 
Technical Interview Round 1 (1 hour) 
The interview took place at CUIC. The interview started off with the panel having 
a look at my resume. Since I had completed a few projects related to DBMS and 
Data Science, they asked questions based on DBMS and to test my aptitude, 
problem solving skills. 
1) If we were to create a database system for the Indian voting system, what 
tables should be included and with what form of normalization? 
I was asked to write the relational schema of the tables on paper. After I gave 
them my answer, they asked me to consider adding an element of security to 
the database system. I told them about having an admin to control access. 
However, they were not satisfied with my answer and asked for a more 
secure database system. We moved on as I didn’t know the answer. 
 
2) What is the need for normalization and what are the different forms of 
normalization? 
I told them the answer and gave them an example of when normalization 
would be required. They also questioned me about the normalization of the 
database I used in one of my projects. 
 
3) You are tasked with using a water dispenser that can dispense cold, warm, 
and hot water to fill cups. Each type of water (cold, warm, hot) corresponds 
to an array position (amount[0], amount[1], and amount[2] respectively) 
indicating how many cups of each type you need to fill. The machine has two 
modes of operation-  
• In one second, it can fill two cups of different types of water.  
• In one second, it can fill one cup of any type of water.  
The goal is to find the minimum number of seconds required to fill all the 
cups as per the amount array. 
I explained my approach of filling two maximum types at a point of time and 
moving on to the next type after finishing off with atleast one type. They 
were satisfied with my approach and I was asked to write the code on paper. 
 
4) Given an increase in the number of IPL teams, it was decided to divide the 
total number of teams in two groups and the matches played by each team 
would be based on the following conditions- 
• 1 match with every other team in the same group 
• 2 matches with other teams in the other group 
Given n number of teams, find the total number of matches conducted. 
It was more of a mathematical aptitude question as they asked me to find 
the answer for 10 teams. I gave the wrong answer at first but I was able to 
correct myself later. I was asked to find a general formula for it considering n 
number of teams and write the code for a function to implement the same 
on paper. 
 
5) Given a table with Employee name, ID, Job name, Job ID, State Code, State 
with several repetitive values in all the attributes, I was asked to find a 
suitable primary key, possible functional dependencies and the normal form. 
I was also asked to normalize the table and write relational schemas of the 
final normalized forms on paper. 
 
We also had general discussions about the internship during the interview. At 
the end of the interview, they asked me if I had any questions for them. I asked 
them for any suggestions to improve my skills. They advised me to look for more 
creative and unexpected solutions for coding questions instead of the usual 
solutions. 
 
HR Interview (15 minutes) 
The interview was more of a friendly discussion with the HR. I was asked 
questions like- 
1) Why do you prefer an internship related to testing instead of developing? 
2) What made you choose D. E. Shaw for an internship? 
3) If given a better opportunity in another company, would you prefer to stay or 
leave? 
4) What are your plans for higher studies? 
5) What do you know about D. E. Shaw and what did you get to know from the 
Pre Placement Talk? 
6) Would you prefer to work in a new environment far away from your friends 
and family? 
I told the HR about why I was interested in a FinTech related internship. I 
explained how I believed testing and developing would require equal levels of 
creativity and so it doesn’t matter whether it’s testing or developing. I also 
told her that a Software related internship in a company would be a dream 
come true and factors like a new work environment wouldn’t affect me 
much. I also said that better opportunities, higher studies are all a part of a 
future plan but I would like to gain some hands-on experience before making 
any important life decisions. 
 
After the first technical interview and the HR interview, few of us were asked 
to attend the second technical interview in the evening. 
 
Technical Interview Round 2 (45 minutes) 
The interview started off with the panel asking me to introduce myself. After 
the introduction, they moved on to the DSA questions. 
1) Given a list of library books and a list of students with each student having 
read a few books, find the minimal set of students that must have read 
maximum/almost all the library books together. 
I gave the wrong answer at first as I misunderstood the question. However, 
the interviewer was able to correct me and I was able to give the right 
answer eventually. I explained my logic of using the sliding window approach 
considering the student roll numbers were in order and books read by each 
student was stored in a dictionary with their roll numbers as keys. I also 
explained another approach using Kadane’s algorithm as the interviewer was 
looking for an optimized approach. I was questioned about the time and 
space complexity of my approach and asked for a more optimized answer. 
We moved on as I couldn’t think of a more optimized answer. 
 
2) There are 3 repositories in a github account. Each repository has around 50 
files and testing a file would require an enormous amount of time. 
Considering the problem that raises an error involving a combination of one 
file in each of the repository due to a bug in one of the files in the 3 
repositories, suggest an approach to find the file with the bug without taking 
much time. 
I explained my approach of using binary search considering the fact that a 
bug in a particular file will also affect the working of the subsequent files. I 
was asked about the time complexity of my approach and they were satisfied 
with my approach. 
3) Given a m X n matrix with integers, find a path providing the maximum sum 
from the top left corner to the bottom right corner by moving diagonally up 
or diagonally down or towards the right and without returning to the same 
spot more than once. 
I gave them a backtracking approach considering movements in the 
diagonally up, diagonally down and right directions. I was asked about the 
time and space complexity. I had to write the code on paper and they were 
satisfied with my approach. 
 
4) The question was about ball collision similar to the asteroid collision available 
on leetcode. 
https://leetcode.com/problems/asteroid-collision/description/ 
I explained a stack approach for it and wrote the code on paper. They were 
satisfied with my answer after I explained the time and space complexities. 
 
After the DSA questions, they had a look at my resume and inquired generally 
about my achievements. As I had mentioned C++ and Python as my skills, 
they asked me about the difference between the two languages. I explained 
about the differences of the compiled and interpreted language. They also 
asked me which language would be better in different situations involving 
programs with time as an important factor, programs that need not be 
precompiled again and again and so on. 
 
At the end of the interview, they asked me about why I chose to dive deep 
into a few specific languages instead of exploring more. They also advised me 
to improve myself with newer technological skills. 
 
The results were announced later at night and 2 of us from CEG were 
selected for the internship. 
 
General Tips- 
It’s completely fine if you are not a great coder in the start. You always have 
time to improve your skills. 
• Practice coding in websites like Leetcode, Hackerrank as it helps improve 
problem solving skills. 
• Have a look at questions in Leetcode Premium for the company. 
• Practice ACM CODE questions for an understanding of every single topic 
in DSA. 
• Learn from the experiences of seniors. 
• Try practicing aptitude questions also as it has great importance in the 
online assessment. 
• Improve your speaking skills. The interview will be more of a discussion 
rather than an oral test and the interviewer also notices your ability to 
interact easily. 
• The interviewer is more interested to know your creative answers to 
their coding questions. So let your creativity flow while finding optimized 
solutions. 
• Keep revising old concepts while learning new ones. 
Last but not the least…. Stay calm! 
 
All the best! 
 
MuthuVaishnavi Anand 
CEG CSE 26’

**Saravana Kumar P - Information Technology**
**Intern + FTE at NCR Voyix**

**Company Overview:**

NCR Voyix is a leading provider of next-generation technology solutions, specializing in industries such as banking, retail, hospitality, and telecommunications. With a global presence, NCR Voyix offers innovative software and services that help businesses improve operational efficiency and customer engagement. The company is known for its cutting-edge technologies in automation and digital transformation, making it a prominent player in the tech industry.

**Online Assessment Round:**

**Coding Questions:**

1. **3Sum Problem:** The task was to find all unique triplets in an array that sum up to zero. I was required to implement an efficient approach to solve this problem, avoiding duplicate triplets while maintaining optimal time complexity.

**MCQs:**

There were 30 multiple-choice questions covering topics such as:

* Object-Oriented Programming (OOPS)
* Database Management Systems (DBMS)
* Operating Systems (OS)
* Docker
* Kubernetes

These questions tested my theoretical understanding of core concepts in these subjects. The coding round was conducted on HackerRank.

**Technical + Managerial Interview Round:**

**Project Discussion:**

I was asked to explain my projects in detail. I discussed:

1. **File Compressor** – A web-based file compression tool using JavaScript that features an intuitive UI and uses the Huffman coding algorithm for compressing and decompressing files, enabling efficient storage and reliable data retrieval.
2. **Chat Website** – A MERN stack-based project with Socket.io integration, allowing real-time communication through one-to-one and group chats. It also featured secure authentication using bcrypt and JWT.

I explained the goals, challenges, technologies used, and outcomes of these projects. The interviewer showed interest in understanding the real-world application of my work.

**Coding Questions:**

1. **3Sum Problem:** The interviewer asked me to walk through both brute-force and optimized approaches to solve the 3Sum problem that I encountered during the online assessment round. I explained the brute-force solution first, discussing its time complexity, followed by the optimized approach using two-pointer and sorting techniques to improve efficiency.

**SQL Questions:**

During the interview, I was given several scenario-based SQL problems. I was asked to:

* Write SQL queries to address given scenarios.
* Explain how normalization works and how it helps reduce redundancy and improve data integrity.
* Discuss the importance of indexing in databases and how it can optimize query performance.
* Solve problems involving joins, set operations, and nested subqueries, which tested my ability to work with complex database operations.

**HR Interview Round:**

**Personal Questions:**

In the HR interview, I was asked about my family background and future plans, including whether I plan to pursue a master's degree. There were questions about my interest in NCR Voyix and where I see myself in five years. Additionally, the interviewer asked how I handle conflicts within a team and my involvement in extracurricular activities.

**Company-Specific Questions:**

* Why did I choose NCR Voyix?
* What attracts me to this role and the company's culture?

I also asked about the roles and responsibilities for the position and the technologies used within the company.

**Preparation Strategy & Advice:**

1. **Start Early:** Although I started preparing towards the end of my third year, I recommend starting earlier to have more time for practice.
2. **Focus on Fundamentals:** It is crucial to have a strong understanding of DBMS, OOPS, and DSA concepts. I found Striver Sheet useful for practicing DSA.
3. **Problem-Solving Approach:**
   * Start with a brute-force solution.
   * Progress to more optimized approaches with explanations on time and space complexity.
4. **Theory Topics:** Use resources like InterviewBit and GeeksforGeeks for theory preparation on topics like OOPS, DBMS, OS, and Cloud technologies.
5. **Soft Skills:** Communication is key. Practice explaining your problem-solving process and your experiences clearly.
6. **Behavioral Questions:** For behavioral questions, I used examples from my projects, internships, and extracurricular activities to demonstrate my skills and experience.
7. **Learn from Seniors:** Speaking with seniors who’ve gone through the interview process gave me valuable insights into the company’s recruitment patterns.
8. **Stay Calm:** It is important to remain calm and methodical throughout the interview process. Keeping a clear head helps in approaching problems effectively.

Overall, my interview experience at NCR Voyix was highly engaging and tested my technical, problem-solving, and communication skills thoroughly.

About the Company:

NCR Voyix (operating in India through Terafina Software Solutions Pvt. Ltd.) is a global

technology company that powers everyday consumer transactions across industries such as
banking, retail, hospitality, and financial services. With a presence in over 35 countries, the
company delivers innovative software platforms, cloud solutions, and digital transformation
services that help enterprises create seamless and secure customer experiences.

NCR Voyix’s solutions are widely used for self-service systems, payment processing,

and enterprise software, supporting millions of interactions worldwide. The company
emphasizes cutting-edge engineering, global collaboration, and delivering technology that
simplifies and enhances consumer interactions.

About the Role (Software Intern):

I have been selected for a Software Intern position at NCR Voyix, where the internship

includes a structured 6-month training period. During this time, I will be working under the
guidance of senior engineers to gain hands-on experience in software development, debugging,
testing, and understanding enterprise-level workflows.

This internship is performance-based, meaning that full-time employment is not
guaranteed but may be considered depending on my performance during the training period.
The role provides exposure to real-world projects, modern technologies, and professional
development practices, allowing me to build industry-ready skills within a global product-based
company.

Overview of the selection process:

Our Online Assessment And Interviews all happened at CUIC . We had four rounds:

1)  Online coding round in Hackerank (40 Got shortlisted)

2) Technical Interview (20 got shortlisted)

3) Managerial Interview  (10 got shortlisted)

4) HR Interview (All 10 were selected for the internship)

 Round 1 : Online coding round:

[1 hr ]

The online coding test was conducted at CUIC through the HackerRank platform. We were
given two coding problems—one easy and one hard. The harder problem was challenging for
most candidates, so it’s completely normal even if you couldn’t solve it fully.

Question 1:
 Link: https://leetcode.com/discuss/post/7366207/atlassian-question-how-to-solve-this-by-rjuij/
 Tip: I initially attempted a graph-traversal + factorial-based approach, which gives the correct
logic but exceeds the time limit due to constraints. The optimal solution involves using
combinatorics, which is more efficient and runs in roughly O(n log n) time.

Question 2:
 You are given a set of points on a Cartesian plane. These points collectively form multiple
squares, and in a few squares one point is missing. The task is to find and return the missing
point for incomplete squares.
 Tip: This can be solved cleanly using an XOR-based method or by using set operations to
identify the missing coordinate.

Round 2 : Technical Interview(Offline):

[ 1 hr ]

I had a panel of two interviewers. The discussion began with my self-introduction, after

which I walked them through a few of my projects. They were genuinely interested, and I
explained one of the projects in depth. Most of the questions at this stage were focused on the
work I had done, the technical decisions I had taken, and the outcomes.

Next, the interview moved into DSA. They referred to the online assessment questions,

and I was asked to pick one and explain it. I chose the hard problem, described my initial
approach, clearly justified why it failed due to time complexity, and then walked them through
the optimal approach. They also asked for the time complexity, and I explained that along with
writing the pseudo-code on paper.

There was a whiteboard with partially erased Python code written on it. I was asked to

interpret the code and explain what it might be doing. The twist was that even they did not know
the original code (it was pre-written); the intention was simply to test how I approach an
unfamiliar or incomplete task.

Next came a system/design question: “Consider you are designing a WhatsApp-like

application. Design the database.”
 I identified the stakeholders, listed the entities, and drew an ER diagram. They further asked
how the user messages table would look, so I designed the schema, taking into account both
user-to-user and user-to-group messaging. I ensured my design satisfied 3NF.

We also briefly discussed end-to-end encryption and how messages remain secure. This

led to a conversation on JWT, which I explained in detail.

 Round 3 : Managerial Round(Offline)

[ 45min ]

The managerial round was informal and conversational, mainly focused on

understanding my motivation and cultural fit. I was asked questions such as why I chose NCR
Voyix, what I liked about the company, and what I knew about its corporate culture. The
discussion also included topics like work from home vs. work from office, plans for higher
studies, family background, and a groundbreaking technology I recently came across. There
was also a brief conversation about toxic workplace culture—what I think contributes to it, how
to avoid it, and how I would handle such situations professionally. Overall, the round assessed
my communication skills, mindset, and ability to work well in a healthy team environment. (I may
not remember every question, but these were the key themes.)

Round 4 : HR Interview(Offline)

[ 10min ]

Around ten candidates were shortlisted for the HR round. The interview was brief and

focused on basic HR-oriented questions such as why I chose NCR Voyix, my family
background, and my willingness to relocate or work from cities like Bengaluru or Chennai. This
was the quickest round of the day, and all ten candidates were selected. The entire recruitment
process started at around 8:30 AM and went on until 11:00 PM, making it one of the longest
interview days ever conducted at CUIC.

Preparation Strategy:

For DSA, I mainly followed Striver’s SDE Sheet and the NeetCode 150 list. I also made it

a habit to solve daily LeetCode problems and regularly participate in LeetCode contests. (I
personally wouldn’t recommend Codeforces for interview preparation as it is more
competitive-programming–oriented and not fully aligned with typical interview patterns.)

For DBMS, I practiced the LeetCode SQL 50 problems and HackerRank SQL
challenges. I focused strongly on topics like joins, subqueries, CASE/IF, window functions,
normalization, and database design, as these commonly appear in interviews.

For the core CS subjects, I didn’t follow separate dedicated material. Instead, I revised
important concepts from Operating Systems and Computer Networks using quick summaries
and explanations from ChatGPT.

On a final note, I had also attended several other interviews, including companies like

Nokia and Netradyne. I genuinely gave my best in both, and those experiences taught me a lot
about handling pressure, adapting to different interview styles, and staying confident even when
the questions get tough. These interviews also made me realize an important truth: along with
preparation and performance, a bit of luck and timing also play a role in the selection process.
Every interview, whether successful or not, added to my learning, improved my thought process,
and helped me handle the NCR Voyix interview with more maturity and clarity. Looking back, the
entire journey shaped me far more than a single result could.

**Preetham Vijayandhran- Information Technology –**

**Intern + FTE at NCR Voyix**

**Company Overview:**

NCR Voyix is a leading provider of next-generation technology solutions, specializing in industries such as banking, retail, hospitality, and telecommunications. With a global presence, NCR Voyix offers innovative software and services that help businesses improve operational efficiency and customer engagement. The company is known for its cutting-edge technologies in automation and digital transformation, making it a prominent player in the tech industry.

**Online Assessment Round:**

**Coding Questions:**

1. **Balancing Teams:** In this problem, a sports coach needs to balance the skill levels of two teams, A and B, by filling empty positions (indicated by 0 in the skill array). The challenge was to ensure that the sum of the skills for both teams becomes equal after filling the empty positions. If balancing isn't possible, the output should be -1.
2. **Maximal Substring:** Given a binary string dataStream of even length, consisting of '0's and '1's, the task was to swap characters at two distinct even indices and their adjacent characters in order to find the longest non-decreasing substring. The problem required efficient handling of swaps and string manipulation.

**MCQs:**

There were 30 multiple-choice questions covering topics such as:

* Object-Oriented Programming (OOPS)
* Database Management Systems (DBMS)
* Operating Systems (OS)
* Docker
* Kubernetes

These questions tested my theoretical understanding of core concepts in these subjects. The coding round was conducted on HackerRank.

**Technical + Managerial Interview Round:**

**Project Discussion:**

I was asked to explain my projects in detail, where I discussed the goals, challenges, technologies used, and the outcomes of the projects I had worked on. This was an opportunity to demonstrate my problem-solving and technical skills, and the interviewer showed interest in understanding the real-world application of my work.

**Coding Questions:**

1. **Balancing Teams:** The interviewer asked me to walk through both brute-force and optimized approaches to solve the Balancing Teams problem that I encountered during the online assessment round. I explained the brute-force solution first, discussing its time complexity, followed by the optimized approach, where I focused on how we can reduce time complexity while maintaining accuracy.
2. **Maximal Substring:** Similarly, I was asked to explain my solution for the Maximal Substring problem. I started by outlining the brute-force approach and then moved to the optimized version. The interviewer was particularly interested in how to efficiently handle the swaps and ensure the longest non-decreasing substring was found with optimal time complexity.

**SQL Questions:**

During the interview, I was given several scenario-based SQL problems. I was asked to:

* Write SQL queries to address given scenarios.
* Explain how normalization works and how it helps reduce redundancy and improve data integrity.
* Discuss the importance of indexing in databases and how it can optimize query performance.
* Solve problems involving joins, set operations, and nested subqueries, which tested my ability to work with complex database operations.

**HR Interview Round:**

**Personal Questions:**

In the HR interview, I was asked about my family background and future plans, including whether I plan to pursue a master's degree. There were questions about my interest in NCR Voyix and where I see myself in five years. Additionally, the interviewer asked how I handle conflicts within a team and my involvement in extracurricular activities.

**Company-Specific Questions:**

* Why did I choose NCR Voyix?
* What attracts me to this role and the company's culture?

I also asked about the roles and responsibilities for the position, and the technologies used within the company.

**Preparation Strategy & Advice:**

1. **Start Early**: Although I started preparing towards the end of my third year, I recommend starting earlier to have more time for practice.
2. **Focus on Fundamentals:** It is crucial to have a strong understanding of DBMS, OOPS, and DSA concepts. I found Striver Sheet useful for practicing DSA.
3. **Problem-Solving Approach:**
   * Start with a brute-force solution.
   * Progress to more optimized approaches with explanations on time and space complexity.
4. **Theory Topics:** Use resources like InterviewBit and GeeksforGeeks for theory preparation on topics like OOPS, DBMS, OS, and Cloud technologies.
5. **Soft Skills:** Communication is key. Practice explaining your problem-solving process and your experiences clearly.
6. **Behavioral Questions:** For behavioral questions, I used examples from my projects, internships, and extracurricular activities to demonstrate my skills and experience.
7. **Learn from Seniors:** Speaking with seniors who’ve gone through the interview process gave me valuable insights into the company’s recruitment patterns.
8. **Stay Calm:** It is important to remain calm and methodical throughout the interview process. Keeping a clear head helps in approaching problems effectively.

BNY SUMMER INTERNSHIP INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
Nanda Kumar 
2022503558 
CT batch of 2026, MIT 
 
No of Rounds: 3 (Online Test + Technical interview + Technical & HR interview) 
Stipend: 75,000 INR per month  
Duration of internship: 8-10 weeks 
 
Round 1: Online Assesment 
 
 
There were 4 questions totally. 1 easy, 2 medium and 1 hard. 24 students 
from CEG and MIT students were selected for the Technical round.  
 
Round 2 : Technical Round 
 
 
The Technical round primarily focused on networking, databases, and 
computer fundamentals, along with some personal discussion. The interviewer 
began with general questions about my college  whether MIT comes under Anna 
University, where it’s located, and how many companies had visited the campus. 
 
The discussion became conversational  we spoke for nearly 15 minutes about 
football, one of my interests, and about movies with good direction and 
cinematography. The interviewer and I connected well over that.  
 
Then, we moved on to core questions in Computer Networks such as: 
 
• How routing works and what the Internet is. 
• What happens on the backend when a query is typed into Google. 
• How local and global area networks are connected using ISPs and bridges. 
 
In DBMS, I was asked to write a query using UNION. I admitted I wasn’t sure 
and asked the interviewer to explain it  which he did. He asked me few questions 
on Big data as he was working on that currently. He asked me few questions on 
API , though I didn’t know the answer at first I was sure that I would be able to 
deduce it, so I asked the interviewer some time to think and answered it. 
Throughout the interview, I maintained openness to learning even when my 
answers were incorrect, I was eager to understand the correct ones. The 
interviewer appreciated that attitude. 
 
In DSA, I was asked to write a program to reverse a string in any language on 
paper (I used C, though the interviewer was familiar with Python).  
 
Other questions included real-life examples of queues and stacks. He later 
mentioned that my basics were strong and communication was very good, and 
that I had great learning potential. Half of the interview was personal and the 
remaining half was technical.  
 
This round lasted over 40 minutes and 10 of us were selected to the second 
round.  
 
Round 3: HR Round 
 
In the HR round, I was asked to explain my projects in detail. I described 
both my software and hardware based projects thoroughly, which impressed the 
interviewer. Many questions were asked over my projects and I answered most 
of it. When asked why I worked on a hardware oriented project while others 
focused on web development, I explained how hardware projects also help 
computer science students expand their technical understanding and my interest 
over hardware based projects.  
 
He then asked me what an Operating System is and why we need it. My 
explanation caught his attention, and he even introduced me to the head of the 
Pershing branch at BNY to appreciate the way I had explained it. That moment 
emphasized how clarity in basics can make a strong impact. 
 
Finally, among the few behavioural questions the interviewer asked “Why 
BNY?”, I connected my interest in finance with the company’s domain. I shared 
my personal experience with hedge funds, forex markets, stocks, trading firms, 
clearance, investments, bonds, futures and commodities  even mentioning that I 
had done a few trades myself. He encouraged me to add that experience to my 
resume, saying, “You know what kind of company we are right ,why don’t you 
include this in your resume. This gives you a strong edge.” 
 
Finally 6 of us were offered internships.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Key takeaway’s :  
 
• Be thorough with your resume and projects. Don’t mention anything you 
don’t know in resume. 
• Be strong with your basic DSA, DBMS, OOPS and OS.   
• Stay calm and confident even when you don’t know something. The way 
you handle unknown questions matters more than having every answer 
right. It’s okay to ask for some time to think or say you don’t know. Show 
them you’re willing to learn. 
• Learn about the company and try to speak if you have any personal interests 
over the company’s domain (Finance).  
• Try to connect with the interviewer, both professionally and personally. Try 
to have a conversation with them instead of an interview.  
 
ALL THE BEST

My Journey to Getting Selected at Amazon 
 
[There is always going to be someone who does things the easy way but that should not stop you from doing 
things the right way. It is your journey. 
-a wise person] 
Introduction 
Landing a software development role at Amazon has been one of the most rewarding experiences of my life. The 
journey was challenging, but with determination, preparation, and the right mindset, I was able to secure the 
position. In this article, I’ll share my experience, preparation strategy, and the lessons I learned along the way. 
1. Preparation Phase 
I began my preparation months before the interview. Since I already had a solid foundation in programming 
languages such as Python, C, HTML, CSS, JavaScript, PHP, and SQL, I focused on sharpening my problem-solving skills. 
LeetCode was my go-to resources for solving coding problems but Geeks for geeks will be a greater choice. By the 
time the interview came around, I had solved around 190 problems on LeetCode, which significantly boosted my 
confidence 
2. The Interview Process 
The Amazon interview process was thorough, consisting of several stages, each testing a different aspect of my 
skillset. 
Online Coding Test: The first step was an online coding test that featured algorithmic problems. This stage was crucial 
as it tested my problem-solving approach, coding efficiency, and understanding of data structures. 
Questions asked: 
1. Find the minimum distance between two aws stations (something like that) 
Example: 
Server_stations=[2,5,1,3]  //positions of the server stations 
Receiver_stations=[1,5,6,2] //positions of the receiver stations 
Approach: 
 
Sort the two arrays and sum the absolute difference 
2. Write a function that matches a regular expression (with * as a special character) to a given text and returns 
the length of the longest matching substring. 
You can find this question on leetcode: 
https://leetcode.com/problems/wildcard-matching/description/ 
Approach: 
We have a loop traversing the string. If we find a matching character, the same or a " ? " we move both 
indices forward. 
If we find an " * " we keep the index we found it on and move this and the track of the second index.  Lastly 
we have a loop for the edge case where the " * " is the last char of the string. 
It is bit easier to clear this round, most of my solved the two problems (questions asked for everyone were different.). 
 
Code Pair interview: The next phase involved technical interviews where I had to solve problems live while explaining 
my thought process. This was similar to the coding pair interviews I had practiced for. 
Mode: Online 
Timings: 1 hour (You should able to solve both the questions within 1 hour) 
Questions asked: 
1. You are given a two strings and it contains hash (‘#’) which is a backspace character. If you encounter this 
special character while traversing the string you should delete the previous character. Finally you should 
return true if both strings are equal. 
Example: 
1. S=”abbb###c” 
 
    T=”ac” 
 
    Output: True 
 
2. S=”abbb##c” 
T=”abc” 
Output: False 
 
 
Similar question: 
 
 
https://leetcode.com/problems/removing-stars-from-a-string/ 
 
 
The answers I provide: 
First I told the brute force approach (using stacks) ,but the interviewer asked to optimize it. But I was 
clueless at the time but the interviewer gave me a hint , then I told the optimized approach ,the 
interviewer satisfied with my answer and asked me to dry run it and finally asked me to code with that 
optimized approach. 
2. Find the next greatest element in a Binary search tree. This is very basic question on BST , no need to explain 
further. 
 
Finally they asked me about the time and space complexity .  
Tips: 
Prepare on topics such as 
- 
1D and multi dimensional Dynamic programming 
- 
Graph 
- 
Trees 
- 
Linked List 
If you solve two questions on time ,you will mostly be selected. 
Preparation guide: 
 
Neetcode and Greg Hogg at youtube. 
 
 
 
All the best.
