FIDELITY INVESTMENTS 
About the company: 
 
Fidelity Investments, commonly referred to as Fidelity, earlier as Fidelity Management 
& Research or FMR, is an American multinational financial services corporation based in 
Boston. The company, founded in 1946, is one of the prominent FinTech company which 
offers a wide range of financial services to customers including investors, large scale 
companies, etc… 
Number of rounds: 3 
Roles: Software development and data science internships 
Online Assessment:  
 
The online assessment is conducted on HirePro platform for nearly 1 hour 30 minutes. 
The assessment consists of 4 sections. 
 
1. The first section comprises of English vocabulary aptitude questions. The questions 
are straightforward and easy to answer. 
 
2. The second section comprises of technical aptitude questions where questions were 
like guessing the output, debugging and MCQs related to Operating Systems, OOPS, DBMS 
queries, computer network. The questions were in easy to medium difficulty range. Stick to 
one programming language and get familiar with it (C++, Java or Python).  
 
3. The third section consists of two coding questions. First question is ‘Given an array 
of integers. Find the sum of largest digit in each integer of the array’.  
 
Second question is ‘Given a number with some blanks in it. Find the largest possible 
perfect square number formed by filling all the blanks. E.g.: 6 2 _. The output will be 625’. 
 
Both questions are in easy and medium difficulty. I was able to solve all the testcases 
in both the testcases. Use brute force method in online assessments if you can’t think of 
optimized approach. 
 
4. In fourth section, we have to write just the algorithm for two questions related to 
Dynamic Programming. I finished the first question completely with proper algorithm. But I 
was not able to finish the second question on time. 
 
24 students got shortlisted for the interview round. I was one of them.0 
Note:  
 
Be strong in aptitude and try to clear as many testcases as possible on coding round.  
Time constraint is also a factor in selection process. Don’t forget to submit your answers! 
Technical Interview: 
 
The interview was held at CUIC and on 8:30 AM, we had a small presentation regarding 
the company’s internship drive. Then the interview started. 
 
The interviewer asked me to introduce myself. After that she reviewed my resume and 
gave some comments regarding my schoolings and CGPA. She was very friendly and I was able 
to answer the comments with ease. The she asked me an experience which prompted me to 
learn web development (Since I mentioned it as my foremost skill in my resume). I told her 
about how YouTube intrigued me to learn the behind the scenes of client and server 
communication. Then I explained about the Tech stacks I am specialized in, which is MERN. 
She was satisfied with my answer and asked whether I prefer Angular or React. I explained 
the use cases of both the frameworks and told her my personal opinion as well as a 
generalized opinion.  
 
Then she asked whether am I familiar with DBMS. I said yes. She asked some basic 
concepts like normalization, ACID properties, foreign key. Then she asked me to write a query 
for finding second largest salary in Employee table. I wrote it down and explained it to her. 
She was satisfied and asked what type of DB is used in Facebook, Twitter. Then she asked me 
whether I know the DB used by Google. Since I was familiar with the tech trends, I answered 
the questions correctly. Then she asked me which sorting is better: Merge Sort or Quicksort. 
I told her both has its pros and cons and explained the situation where each sort would be 
better. She was impressed by it.  
 
She then started asking about the projects mentioned in my resume. I was able to 
answer all the questions easily. She then asked how I implemented AI use case in my DBMS 
project. I told her about OpenAI API and how I implemented it. She asked a follow up question 
for it: Difference between AI and ML. I was not able to answer the question correctly. But she 
smiled and explained its difference to me. 
 
Then the interview moved on to CTF and the projects I have done there. I have 
explained about the projects I was working on. She was greatly interested in it and asked 
many questions such as Tech Stacks, Payment portal regarding the project. I was happy to 
answer all the questions with ease. 
 
Then she asked me some fun questions to lighten the mood. She asked me about my 
hobbies, which book genre I was interested in, which book character I preferred the most and 
she asked me whether I can talk in Hindi. I told her I can only write and read in Hindi. She 
smiled and asked me whether I have any questions. I asked her about the Tech Stacks used in 
Fidelity and the work culture in the company  
 
Overall, the interview went for 40 minutes. Keep the interviewer engaged and don’t 
give one-line answers. Explain every answer in detail and be honest in your resume. 
 
Within 15 minutes, I got selected for HR round. 
HR Round: 
 
The interviewer asked about myself. She then asked me some typical questions like: 
Why engineering, Why CEG. Then she asked why did I choose Fidelity. Always do a little 
research about the company before interview and include facts about the company subtly 
during HR interview.  
 
She then asked me whether I have worked in teams. I told her about CTF club and the 
projects I have worked on. She then asked about the conflicts I have faced in a team, 
drawbacks I have faced in a team, what I did to overcome those setbacks.  
 
She then asked me whether I have any questions. I asked her about the working 
conditions for interns after they received PPO and working preference for interns (On site or 
WFH). With that interview finished. It lasted for about 20 minutes. Keep the interview as a 
conversation and don’t hesitate to ask questions politely when you want. 
 
In the end, 6 students got internship. I was happy to be one of them.  
 
 
Key Points to note: 
• Solve competitive questions from Leetcode, Hackerrank, CodeChef, etc… 
• Be strong in DSA, DBMS, OS, OOPS. 
• Always mention projects in your resume and be strong in the tech stack you are using. 
• Be honest in your resume. 
• Keep the interview engaging.

Wells Fargo (Intern Drive) 
 
Name     :  T Nathin Kishore  
Batch      :  2022 – 2026 
Contact  :  7339362808 
 
Online test : 
        The online test was conducted in the SHL platform.  It was a proctored test and we were 
asked to attend  the test in IST dept labs. The test consisted of four sections. The first section 
consisted of verbal questions. It had twelve questions which had to be completed within 15 
minutes which could be answered pretty easily if you had basic English knowledge. You can’t 
move between sections. 
         The second section consisted of 14 business aptitude questions with a time limit of 25 
minutes. Questions were based on stocks and shares, data interpretations based on pie 
charts, bar charts, graphs. You should be able to solve those easily with basic maths. Just 
don’t spend too much time on a question and try to complete all the questions. There is no 
negative marking and you can’t navigate between questions of a section.   
        The third round consisted of two easy to medium level coding questions with a time limit 
of 40 minutes. In this section you can navigate between questions. Driver code will be given. 
The first question is  https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/area-of-intersection-of-two-circles/ . 
You have to return the answer rounded up to six decimals.  
The second question was simple. You have to return a lexicographically sorted list of repeated 
words in a given string. You could see how many test cases you code has passed. It was clearly 
stated that they are expecting a solution which passes all the test cases rather than an 
efficient code which is partially passing the test cases. 
         The fourth section consisted of one question which has to be solved in 10 minutes. The 
question was based on königsberg seven bridges. I could solve 2 of the 3 coding questions. 
More weightage was given to aptitude and verbal than coding questions. You can solve the 
coding problems in the language of your choice. 
22 students got selected for the interview. 
 
Technical round 1 : 
         The round started with standard tell me about yourself question. This round revolved 
mostly around oops, dbms and os for me while others had questions which included sql 
queries, garbage collection etc..,. They asked us to write any sorting of your choice. It is better 
to know quick, merge, heap sorts. It’s okay if you don’t know answers for some questions. At 
last, the interviewer asked me if I had any questions for him. Be prepared with some 
questions. Some candidates were eliminated after this round. 
Round 2: 
         My experience of this round will be different from my peers. They had technical and  
questions based on their resume. This round was completely me asking questions to him. He 
mentioned in his intro that he had run a startup before joining Wells Fargo. So I asked mostly 
about his startup like where he went wrong etc..,. In this round he was just observing how 
curious I am.  
 
Round 3: 
         This was a typical HR round. The interviewer asked me questions like why do want to 
intern at Wells Fargo, what are the challenges faced while doing my dbms project as a team, 
how I handled it etc.., You just needed to let them know that you are a good team player. 
After this, 12 candidates were selected out of the initial 22 for internship. 
 
TIPS: 
• Be thorough with basics of core subjects like os, dbms, oops  
• Commmunicate your thoughts with confidence. There were observing how well you 
are communicating your thoughts. 
• With regard to online test try to solve almost all the aptitude and verbal questions

First Question (Easy): 
Given an array, find the efficiency of an array. To find the efficiency choose a pair such that all pair 
sum should be equal and then eff+=pair.first+pair.second. Efficiency should be maximum.  
Test case 1: 
Input: 
N=6 
A=[4,3,2,3,1,5] 
Output: 
22 
Explanation: 
Eff=1*5+2*4+3*3 (Here 1+5==2+4==3+3) 
Test case 2: 
Input: 
N=[1,2,2,7,2,4] 
Output 
-1 
Explanation: 
No pairs can be found such that sum is equal in all pairs. 
Topics: Array, Sorting. 
Second question (Medium): 
Given two strings such that convert one string to another string with only two operations given 
applying any number of times. 
Return 1 if possible else 0. 
Two operations: 
1) You can swap str[i],str[i+1] 
2) Substring of k (k>1) size with same elements (ASCII Value=K) in that substring can be 
converted to next elements (ASCII Value=K+1). Example: “aaadcd” to “bbbdcd”. Here k=3 
and with ‘a’ same element in that substring is converted to next element ‘b’. Note: ‘z’ can’t 
be converted to next element. 
Test case 1: 
Input: 
”caa” 
“bbc” 
Output: 
1 
Explanation: 
Operations: 
Swap s1[0],s1[1] => “aca”  
Swap s1[1],s1[2] => “aac” 
Then k=2 from 0-1 index with a element to next b element => “bbc” ==str2 
So output 1: 
Test case 2: 
Input: 
”abc” 
“bcd” 
Output: 
0 
Explanation: 
We can’t convert str1 to str2 with those two operations. 
Topics: Strings, HashMap. 
Third Question (Medium): 
Given a 2D matrix with 0 and 1 values in it. 0 -> you can go and 1 -> you can’t go. K value given. 
K -> you can jump at most k steps in horizontal and vertical direction. We have to find minimum no 
of steps required to go from (0,0) to (N-1, M-1). If not possible return -1. 
Test case 1: 
Input: 
0 0 1 
0 0 0 
N=2 
M=3 
K=3 
Output: 
2 
Explanation: 
(0,0)[k=1]->(1,0)[k=2]->(3,0). There is no obstacle in the path. 
Test case 2: 
Input: 
0 1 0 
1 0 0 
N=2 
M=3 
K=3 
Output: 
-1 
Explanation: 
We can’t move to any cell from (0,0). 
Topics: DFS, DP, Backtracking. 
Forth Question (Hard Problem): 
Given an array. You have to apply operations in it 
Operations:  
1) 1 v w => change vth element to w 
2) 2 v v => change every element in that array which are less than v to v. 
These two operations are given in sequences. You have to apply and return the modified array. 
Test case 1:   
Input: 
[7,2,5] 
Operations: 
2 6 6 
1 3 9 
2 8 8 
Output: 
[8,8,9] 
Explanation: 
2 6 6 => [7,6,6] 
1 3 9 => [7,6,9] 
2 8 8 => [8,8,9] 
Topics: Array, 2D Array, Sorting, Searching, Priority Queue.

APPIAN INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
Role: Software Engineer 
The interview process consisted of four rounds. 
Online Coding Round: 
Three coding questions were asked. The questions were same for everyone, including those in 
the summer internship drive as well. 
1. Breadcrumbs in a website – Given a list of lists of string of paths to webpages in a 
website, generate the navigation tree and return the root node.   
2. There is a line consisting of people from different groups. We need to rearrange the line 
so that people from the same group can stand together. If all the persons who belong to 
the same group stand in line, then the names of the persons can be replaced with the 
group name. The order of the groups in the line and the order of persons within the 
group in the line should not change. 
3. There are n people waiting in line to buy tickets to a Taylor Swift concert. The ticket 
manager starts at an offset position m and distributes the first ticket. Then, the manager 
jumps k steps in a cyclic fashion to give the next ticket, and this process continues until 
all tickets are distributed. The task is to determine the order in which tickets are given 
out.  
This sum is derived from the Josephus sum. 
Most of the students who solved all three questions got shortlisted for the interviews. 
 
Technical Interview – 1: 
The interview started with a brief introduction about myself. Then the interviewer asked about 
the basics of OOPS and had me explain how data structures like stacks, queues, and trees have 
real-world applications. I gave a few examples, such as a management hierarchy for a tree. 
After that, he asked me this puzzle. He then asked me to solve the Two Sum problem and run 
the code for a few test cases. Finally, he asked me about how a web server and DNS work. The 
interview ended with a few questions from my side. 
 
Technical Interview – 2: 
After a brief introduction about myself, the interviewer read through my resume and asked 
some questions about my skills. He then asked me to explain one of my projects. Once I had 
given him the explanation, he asked a few follow-up questions and wanted me to describe some 
challenges I faced during that project. He also asked about my summer internship experience 
and the work I did there. Then he asked whether I would join their company even if I received 
a PPO from my internship company. I replied that, as per CUIC policy, I should accept the first 
offer I receive. Finally, the interviewer gave me a chance to ask him a few questions. 
Interview – 3: 
It was more of a casual conversation. He asked about my HSC and college scores and how I 
learned the things I mentioned in my resume. He also asked about some YouTube channels I 
had followed to learn those topics. He asked similar questions about my summer internship, 
just like in my previous interview. Toward the end, I asked him a few questions, and the 
interview lasted for around 15 minutes. 
Key Takeaways: 
• The coding questions were mostly based on arrays. 
• Follow C.O.D.E resources. It will help you to get an idea about various types of 
problems in each topic. And I followed takeUForward YouTube channel for the topics 
like Binary Search, Graphs, Trees and DP. 
• Go through commonly asked puzzles available on GeeksforGeeks. 
• Be thorough with your resume and the projects you have included. 
• Be prepared with the basics of DBMS, OS, OOPS, and Networks. 
• Initially, for any DSA question, you can give the brute force approach and then tell how 
you can optimize that. While coding, ensure you effectively communicate your thought 
process to the interviewer. 
• Learn about the company before the interview, and ask questions when you can. 
 
All the very best.

D.E. Shaw Interview Experience 
 
D.E. Shaw & Co., also known as the D. E. Shaw Group, is a prominent global 
investment and technology development firm. Founded in 1988 by David E. Shaw, a former 
Columbia University computer science professor, the firm has established itself as a leader in 
quantitative investing, proprietary trading, and technology-driven financial strategies. D.E 
Shaw India was founded in 1996 and it has offices at Hyderabad, Bengaluru and Gurugram. 
Role Offered: SDET Intern 
Stipend: 1,50,000 INR per month 
Sequence of Selection: PPT, Online Assessment, Technical Interview, HR Interview 
Online Assessment: 
It consisted of three sections: 
• Coding Question – 2 (20 mins + 30 mins) 
• Technical MCQs – 12 (17 mins)  
• General Aptitude – 14 (28 mins) 
The test lasted for 95 mins and an important point to note is that you can’t go back to previous 
section once you have submitted it, and even if you submit one of the sections earlier than the 
stipulated time, the remaining time will not be carried forward.  
Coding Question 1: 
It was a slight variation of  Print all possible words from phone digits  problem. Instead of 
printing all possible words, there will be a set of words and a sequence of digits, we need to 
find the number of words out of the given set that can be formed by pressing the given sequence 
of digits. 
Sample Testcase: 
contacts = [“ehcv”, “fibo”, “dgat”] 
pattern = 3428 
Output: 2 
Solution: 
3 – ‘d’, ‘e’, ‘f’ 
 
 
4 – ‘g’, ‘h’, ‘i’ 
 
 
2 – ‘a’, ‘b’, ‘c’ 
 
 
8 – ‘t’, ‘u’, ‘v’ 
Only two contacts (“ehcv” and “dgat”) can be formed by pressing 3428 buttons. So, the answer 
is 2. 
 
 
Coding Question 2: 
Given an array of weights and profits. You can remove as many weights as possible to make 
the weights array as non-decreasing. You need to make the weights array non-decreasing such 
that the profit is maximum. 
Sample Testcase: 
weights = [1,3,2,4,2] 
profit = [5,6,8,7,9] 
Output: 22 
Explanation: Removing weights[1] and weights[3] makes the weights array non-decreasing 
and maximizes the profit. 
Technical MCQs: 
Technical MCQs were easy to medium level, and the questions were based on predicting output, 
identifying the line where logical error occurs, DSA and DBMS. 
General Aptitude: 
I couldn’t remember many of the questions but there was a logical reasoning kind of question 
with 1 statement and 4 questions based on that, which was little bit similar to Logical Reasoning 
(but more complex than this) and was difficult. Except those 4 questions, others were easy to 
medium level. 
Marking Scheme for MCQs: +3/-1 
 
Based on this online assessment, 21 people were shortlisted for the next round. 
 
Technical Interview – 1: (1 hour) 
 
The interviews were conducted at CUIC. The interview started off, and I was asked 
about my strong subject. I told them I am equally strong in both DBMS and OS. Then the 
interviewer asked about the advantages of DBMS over file system and he said that even DBMS 
stores data in files then how it is advantageous? I was not able to answer the way the interviewer 
wanted to be. Then they started to check my problem-solving skills. 
1. Given an array of integers, find the number of triplets with even product. 
Initially, I gave a wrong answer but the interviewer corrected me and I was able to give 
the correct solution. 
 
2. Given an array of integers, where each index represents an animal and arr[index] 
represents its predator. Find the minimum number of cages required so that none of the 
animals will be killed by others. (Transitive relation is also possible (i.e) if 2 is predator 
of 3 and 1 is predator of 2, then 1 is also a predator of 3) 
I told them that we can solve using graphs but the interviewer wanted me solve in O(1) 
space complexity and he asked me what kinds of input might be edge cases that we 
need to consider and I was asked to write the code on paper. 
 
3. Given two strings s1 and s2, find minimum length substring in s1 which contains all 
characters of s2 in it. 
I was able to give the solution and write the code, but the interviewer asked me to reduce 
its time complexity but I was not able to do that. 
 
Then the interviewer checked my OS knowledge. They asked, In IDEs like VSCode, 
how code editor and terminals run at the same time and how terminal executes background 
processes and all. I was not sure about the answer and I explained to them about processes, 
threads and all but I was not able to give the accurate answer. 
Then the interviewer asked me to convert given postfix to prefix directly but I couldn’t 
answer that. Then the interviewer asked about OOPS like virtual function, abstract methods, 
run-time polymorphism and all and asked me to write code related to that. 
 Then, finally I was given a question related to binary search. Given an 2D matrix sorted 
in spiral order, write algorithm to find an element. I gave them the brute force O(n2) solution 
but the interviewer asked me to optimize it and asked me to tell what came to mind, and I told 
them we could use binary search but I was not able to tell the correct logic. 
Finally, the interviewer asked whether I had any questions for them, and I asked about 
the challenges that an intern might face during their internship. 
 
There wasn’t any specific shortlisting, and the students who performed well were asked 
to attend another round of interviews. I was the last one to be called for another round of 
interview at 8:15pm. 
 
Technical Interview – 2: (45 mins) 
 
The interview started off with self-introduction (in my first round of interview they 
haven’t even asked to introduce myself). Since I have mentioned that I am good at React and 
Node in my resume, the interviewer asked the following questions: 
 
The interviewer first asked why Node was preferred by most of the programmers, even 
though there are a lot of options available for the backend. Then he asked that there is a web 
application where Node from backend pushes data each 1 sec and front end is developed using 
React whose state is maintained by Redux. Is it possible to check whether node pushes data 
every one second? If so, how? I was not sure about this question, so we moved on. 
 
Then the interview asked some basic questions about process in OS and how process 
data are maintained. Then they asked about various scheduling algorithms and how processes 
undergo execution. Then they asked which scheduling algorithm you would use to maximize 
resource utilization. 
 
Then they started off with DSA questions.  
1. Given an array in which all elements except one occur an even number of times. 
I gave them the efficient XOR approach. XOR of same numbers is 0 so all the numbers 
which occur even number of times get cancelled out by taking XOR for all numbers. 
Then immediately I was given another question related to XOR. 
 
2. Given an array of elements and integer k, we need to remove k elements to maximize 
the XOR of all elements. 
I was only able to give the brute force approach to this question. 
 
3. Given an array of integers and an integer k, find all triplets with sum less than or equal 
to k. 
I first told them the brute force method and, then gave them the three-pointer approach. 
 
4. Given an array of pages and intelligence and an integer k, where pages represent no of 
pages in each book and intelligence represent amount of knowledge you gain by reading 
that book, k represents maximum pages you can read in a day, find the maximum 
intelligence you can gain in a day. 
It was similar to 0/1 knapsack problem. I was asked to write the code on paper once I 
gave them the solution. 
 
5. Given a 2D grid of characters and a word, return true if the word exits in the grid. From 
one cell you can move to any of the four neighbouring cells. 
I gave them backtracking solution and wrote the code and the interviewer was happy 
with that. 
 
Then the interviewer wrote some code and asked me to find the time complexity, guided 
me to the correct answer and asked about the order in which constructor and destructor get 
executed in inherited sub classes. With that the interview got over. 
I had no HR interview. 
The result was announced the next day, and I was the only one who got the offer. 
General Tips: 
• Regular practice is important. Solve problems in Leetcode, GeeksForGeeks and 
Hackerrank. A lot of people don’t prefer Hackerrank, but at least get familiar with it 
because the online assessment will be conducted on Hackerrank. 
 
• Follow C.O.D.E resources. It will help you to get an idea about various types of 
problems in each topic. And I followed takeUForward YouTube channel for the topics 
like Binary Search, Graphs, Trees and DP. 
 
• There is no common method or strategy to prepare for interviews. You need to find your 
own strategy which makes you feel confident in solving problems. 
• If you are not confident with DBMS, OS and OOPS, then prepare for them 
simultaneously because you won’t be having much time between the online test and the 
interview.  
 
• Aptitude is as important as DSA. At least start learning aptitude two weeks before your 
online test. 
 
• Even though I was not asked about my projects, in every interview they will ask about 
your projects, and they might ask you about the database structure and all. So be 
prepared to answer any questions related to your projects. 
 
• A day before your online test, refer to previous year questions asked by the company 
that might get repeated. 
 
• Explain to the interviewer about the logic and how you came up with this solution. Most 
of them are interested in your thought process, not the code. 
 
• Initially, for any DSA question, first give the brute force approach and then tell how 
you can optimize that.  
 
• Whenever you learn a new concept and if you feel you might forget it, please make a 
note of it (either handwritten or a word doc) and maintain it throughout your preparation 
which will help you during your last-minute revision. 
 
 
All the very best.

BNY MELLON – INTERNSHIP INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
 
BNY Mellon (The Bank of New York Mellon Corporation) is a global financial 
services company headquartered in New York, formed in 2007 through the 
merger of The Bank of New York and Mellon Financial Corporation. The firm 
focuses on asset servicing, investment management, and treasury services, 
supporting clients in more than 100 markets worldwide. With trillions of 
dollars in assets under custody and management, BNY Mellon is a trusted 
name in the financial industry. The company employs thousands of 
professionals across the globe, with strong technology and operations hubs 
in Pune and Chennai, where much of its innovation and digital 
transformation is driven. 
The Interview process consisted of 3 rounds: 
1. Online Assessment 
2. Technical Interview 
3. Technical + HR Interview 
 
Round-1: Online Assessment 
The test was conducted in Hackerrank platform. There was a total of 4 
questions and we were given 2hrs 45mins to solve them. 
1. Maximizing the Redundancy 
You are given n servers represented as a binary string, where 1 means 
operational and 0 means not operational. At most one operation can be 
performed: choose a consecutive segment and flip all values (1 → 0, 0 → 1). 
The task is to find the maximum redundancy, defined as the number of 
unique possible counts of operational servers after performing the 
operation. 
Sample Input: 0011 
Sample Output: 4 
 
2. Server Clusters 
You are given an array serverProp representing the properties of n servers. 
Two servers at indexes i and j are considered connected if the greatest 
common divisor (GCD) of their properties, serverProp[i] and serverProp[j], is 
greater than 1. Connected servers form clusters in the network. 
You need to return an array of integers where the i-th value represents the 
size of the cluster to which the i-th server belongs. 
Sample Input: n = 3, serverProp = [1, 2, 4] 
Sample Output: [1, 2, 2] 
 
3. Minimum Cost 
There are n points on the x-axis (1, 2, …, n), where each point i has a cost 
associated with it cost[i]. 
Starting from position 0, you can jump forward up to k steps at a time. 
Whenever you land on a point, you incur its cost. The goal is to find the 
minimum cost required to reach point n. 
Sample Input: n = 5, cost = [4, 3, 9, 3, 1], k = 2 
Sample Output: 7 
 
4.Largest Subset 
In a scheduling system, there are n events represented by two arrays, start 
and finish, each of size n. 
 The i-th event begins at start[i] and ends at finish[i]. 
 A subset of these events is considered high-priority if there exists at 
least one event in the subset that overlaps or intersects with every 
other event in the subset. 
Your task is to determine the maximum size of a high-priority subset of 
events. 
Sample Input: n = 4, start = [1, 2, 3, 4], finish = [2, 3, 5, 5]   
Sample Output: 3 
My performance: 
1. 15/15 test cases ✅ 
2. 14/15 test cases ✅ 
3. 9/15 test cases ✅ 
4. 10/15 test cases ✅ 
 
Round-2: Technical Interview 
The second round took place at CUIC. A total of 26 candidates were 
shortlisted from the OA and we were split across 6 panels with an 
interviewer in each.  
The interviewer in my panel was very friendly and chill and it helped me 
initially as I was nervous about the Interview. The interview went for about 
30-40 mins. After settling in, the interviewer started browsing through my 
resume and asked me to give an introduction. The interviewer then moved 
on to the technical questions and asked me to choose any of the core 
concepts I had mentioned in my resume for which I chose Object Oriented 
Programming (OOP)  and he started asking some questions about OOP: 
 What are the 4 pillars of OOP? 
 Explain about any one of them with a real life example. 
 Some questions related to relationships in Inheritance. 
Then, he moved on to my projects and asked me to explain about the Real 
Time Chat Application project which I had done using MERN stack. I 
explained my part of the project which I worked with. After that he asked 
me to explain about any one of the projects I had done as per my 
preference, I explained about the Java project (Spaceship Game) in great 
detail (use case, functionalities, etc.). Then he tested my knowledge in DSA 
with a problem: 
 I don’t remember the exact question but it went like this. How would 
you design a parking lot by checking if a slot is free for the car to park 
or not and assign them the free slot. 
Initially, I was not really sure whether it was a coding question so I asked the 
doubts I had and told him my approach and wrote it down in a paper. Even 
though I made few mistakes in my solution, he corrected me and was trying 
to test my thinking skills. Then he asked me to explain about any one sorting 
technique of my choice. 
Finally, he asked me if I had any doubts for which I asked “What kind of 
work we would be assigned to during the Internship?” and “How well did I 
perform in the interview?” for which he pointed out my strengths and 
weaknesses of what he had seen of me in the Interview. I asked some other 
questions as well. 
I was one of the 14 candidates who got shortlisted for the next round. 
 
Round-3: Technical + HR Interview 
It took place sometime after the second round got over. I had a long 
conversation for about an hour with the interviewer. The interview started 
with self-introduction. During the very initial stages, the interviewer pointed 
out and asked why I had mentioned programming languages in a random 
order as he assumed it was in the order of my preference and told me most 
of the people would read it in that way, I was told this in the previous round 
also so avoid this mistake. 
Then, the interviewer asked me various doubts regarding my MERN stack 
project and asked various hypothetical questions while developing a project 
and how I would handle the situation. To give a basic idea, the questions 
were like: 
 How would you deal with a customer when you have time constraints 
on meeting the deadline of a project? 
 How would you deal with a situation when your project members are 
not available? 
I told how I would handle the situations and he further complicated the 
situation of the question and it went on like that. Then, he asked me to 
name the aggregate functions and some inbuilt functions from DBMS. 
Moving on to DSA he asked me 2 questions: 
 4 friends go to a restaurant and pay the bill in a disoriented ratio, how 
would the split be between each of them and he added further more 
which I don’t really remember much. 
 A question related to obtaining a substring from a string. 
For the first question, I wrote my approach in a paper logically explaining 
him and he asked me write the code as well in a language of my preference 
and I wrote in C++. Although my answer was not perfect, I gave the logical 
idea with coding approach and he helped me further and taught me how to 
solve the missing part. For the second question, I gave him a sliding window 
approach for which he agreed with.  
For major part of the interview, I was asked about my projects and many 
hypothetical questions. The interviewer also asked me some practical 
questions related to Software Engineering Concepts like SDLC. He also asked 
me how well do I know Spanish as I had mentioned it in languages in my 
resume. 
Finally he asked me if I had any questions for him, and I questioned with 
various questions like “How did he handle the situation when he was at my 
place (candidate)?” and so on. It was a friendly and casual conversation 
which went on for an extended duration. In the end, he mentioned to me 
that he had a great time conversing with me and the interview ended. 
 
The results were announced after a prolonged wait, and 6 of us were 
selected for the Internship ( 4, 2 from MIT, CEG respectively). 
Tips: 
 Be 100% thorough with all things mentioned in your resume. 
 Try not to be anxious and be as confident as possible during the 
interviews. 
 Don’t panic and stutter when you don’t know the answer for a 
question, such questions are asked to test your decision making under 
pressure and rather reply with similar things relevant to the question 
(“I am not sure on that but I know about this”, “I will look into it”, etc.) 
think for sometime and answer I DON’T KNOW only if you have no 
idea about the question. 
 Be honest with the interviewer. 
 Respond with creative and thoughtful answers which come to your 
mind at that moment. 
 Present yourself showing your genuine interest for the job 
opportunity. 
 Be curious and ask the interviewer various questions when asked to 
making the conversation interactive. 
 Be thorough with the core concepts you have learned up until at the 
time of interview especially DSA, DBMS, OOP. 
 Be prepared on what you tell for self-introduction, this is crucial as the 
interviewer gets keen and curious about the strengths, hobbies and 
other things you mention. 
 Create a habit of practicing DSA problems regularly in coding 
platforms like LeetCode to become more familiar with DSA concepts. 
 
Don’t get vexed if you are not hired, try your best and the good luck, results 
will eventually find you in the future. 
 
Best of luck! Please do feel free to reach out to me if you have any further 
doubts,

Global Analytics 
 
 
Global Analytics aka GAIN credit is a fin-tech company which develops solutions to provide 
short-term loans to people with low credit score. 
 
Interview process consisted of 3 rounds after the online test: 
 
 
Round 1: Programming 
 
This round seemed to test whether you know how to approach a problem, code the solution, and  
optimize. They will do so giving you the code that you did in the online test and ask you to explain 
the logic and see if you can do something to optimize your solution. Then you will be given some 
coding questions and ask you to code. I was given the 4 questions below: 
1. Count the number of submatrices that can be formed by 1s in a 2d boolean matrix. 
(Optimized solution of the problem from the online test) 
2. Count the number of islands formed by 1s in a 2d matrix of 0s(water) and 1s(land). 
3. Given two strings with some characters and ‘#’ denoting a backspace…check if they both 
result in the same string after the backspaces are done accordingly(solve with and without 
extra space) 
4. Given an array of positive and negative integers. You are allowed to jump from an index i 
to: i+1,i-1, and k in one jump where k is any other index in array where arr[i]==arr[k]. Find 
the minimum number of jumps needed to reach the end of the array of you start from the 
first index. 
 
Round 2: Technical discussion (Projects, programming, technical questions) 
1. Explain your machine learning project. 
2. How does Linear regression work? 
3. What is Mean Squared Error? 
4. What is gradient descent? 
5. Explain your website’s login module code. 
6. How will you use sessions and cookies?

9. ACID properties in RDBMS

11. Write a code to find the height of BST. 
 
Round 3: (Talk with Director, Technical + HR)  
 
Given a C program based on pointers, asked to find the output, explain what happens in each line. 
The program was something like this: 
 
#include<stdio.h>  
#include<stdlib.h>  
#include<string.h>  
void func(char *str)  
{  
 
str=(char*)malloc(500);  
 
strcpy(str,"global analytics");  
}  
int main(void) 
{  
 
char *str=(char *)malloc(50);  
 
strcpy(str,"hello");  
 
func(str);  
 
printf("%s -- %d",str,(int)strlen(str));  
 
return 0; } 
 
1. Some in-depth questions about the memory allocation and the pointers in the program like 
where are the variables stored actually and how they are stored. 
2. What is the biggest number a char can hold? 
3. What will be inside stdio.h? 
4. What is the use of python’s dictionary? 
5. Explain any one project. 
6. He also picked another project from my GitHub and asked me to explain the code. 
 
Topics to be focussed:

◆ Thorough knowledge of projects mentioned in resume 
◆ Core subjects like DBMS, C, data structures.

AMAZON SDE 2 MONTH INTERNSHIP EXPERIENCE
2022
About the company:
Amazon is a product based company. It has a lot of divisions like e-commerce,
cloud computing (AWS), digital streaming, AI (Alexa), ECHO and so on. It’s
one of the MAANG companies.
Experience:
Amazon isn’t a regular visit to our college. They approached our college for
internship 2 years back and left without picking any interns. But in recent
trends, Amazon is taking a good count of interns from all the colleges around
India.
They held two rounds for selection. The first one was online assessment.
They asked 2 coding questions and then a work personality test. The
questions were very easy, it was more of practical and implementation
oriented. Most of us were able to attend both of them. The personality test
was kept to check your work ethics and whether you are a fit for their work
culture. It is almost like other usual personality test where you would be
provided with a situation and you must either agree or disagree. We still don’t
know whether it is taken as a metrics for evaluation.
OA: (HackerRank)
The question pattern for the online assessment would change for every year.
This year it was only coding round whereas previously they would ask coding
questions along with code debugging (i.e semantic and syntax errors) and
some technical questions sections. So prepare yourself for both coding and
also technical questions.
Interview:
Then for the interview, they selected around 74 students (both MIT and CEG).
The interview is one of the crucial part in selection for Amazon.
Interview Format: Code pair
“I’d rather interview 50 people and not hire anyone than hire the wrong
person” - Jeff Bezos.
In the interview, we had an introduction where each of us took a turn
introducing ourselves. In this interview, you wouldn’t have any HR round to
talk about your strengths. So when you introduce yourself, tell them your
technical strengths and what projects you have worked on. Present yourself in
a pleasing way. Then you would be asked to share your screen and use
LiveCode to code your questions.
You will be provided with a question. Even if you already know the answer,
start with brute force because the entire interview will be testing your
communication with the interviewer and your approach to problems.
When providing your approach, go in hand with the time and space
complexity and also why you are going with it. And then, after the approach,
you will be asked to code. When doing so, explain the code when you are
typing and what that snippet would work as.
If time permits, the interview would go for another question. In some cases, 3
questions could be asked. It depends on the interviewer. In the end, you will
be allowed to ask a few questions. Ask appropriate questions that help them
understand how much you are interested in working for the company.
Round 1 - Online Assessment
The Online Assessment was split into two sections. One being the coding
round and other being the work-personality round.
The coding round was really easy. The question were like real life problems of
the basic question.
1. Optimize arranging the letters in a button cell phone.
For each string given as test case, we have to find the optimized way to
arrange the letters in the 9 button such that minimum number of clicks should
be performed to type the text.
Sol: use Priority Queue
2. Find the grayest area in a given screen ( matrix ) .
Each cell in a matrix/ screen may be black which is represented as -1 and
white as 1. To calculate grayness for each cell you would have to find the
difference between no of black area and no of white areas in the cell’s
identified row and col. The one with maximum value (absolute) is the answer.
Sol: Calculate row_sum and col_sum for each row and col. Then using that,
find the maximum absolute sum of row_sum[i] and col_sum[i].
Round 2 - Code pair interview
1. Run-length-encoding
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/run-length-encoding/
The task is to convert a string “aaabbcccc” to “a3b2c4”
Questions asked
- Condition in which a string could be “aaaabbaaa”
- Reduce space complexity
- Does the length of the solution string is always less than original
2. Happy Strings
https://leetcode.com/problems/longest-happy-string/
The task is you will be given 3 characters ‘a’, ‘b’, ‘c’ that has a, b, c
occurrences. The substring of the resultant string generated shouldn’t have
three consecutive same characters (like “aaa”). Generate the longest string
Question asked
- The same question when instead of three characters infinite number of
numbers would be provided with any number of occurrences.
Tips:
Prepare well on these topics
- Strings
- Linked List
- Trees
- Arrays
The online assessment needs practice on coding. Prepare using LeetCode,
GeeksForGeeks. The interview shortlist process might need a bit of luck. But
when you enter the interview, you should be able to tackle the trick questions
posed by the interviewer. In the end, after all the preparation go through the
14 leadership requirements from Amazon (just in case, interviewer may check)
and STAR approach.

Gen Digital (Norton Lifelock) Interview Experience

-Kabileshwaran SD (2021115046)

About the company

Gen Digital, formerly known as NortonLifeLock, is a cybersecurity company that provides a
range of products designed to protect users' online identities, devices, and data. Their flagship
product, Norton 360, offers comprehensive antivirus protection, a VPN for secure browsing, and
tools for online privacy. The company also offers Norton Password Manager, which helps users
securely manage and generate passwords, and Norton Safe Search, ensuring safe browsing
experiences.

Additionally, Gen Digital provides solutions like LifeLock, which monitors personal information
and alerts users to potential identity theft. Their products cater to individuals and families,
focusing on safeguarding digital lives through advanced security features, identity protection,
and real-time alerts, all aimed at providing peace of mind in an increasingly digital world.

Role details Offered role:

Final Semester - 6 months Internship cum Placement - based upon performance during internship
period

Preliminary Round

The preliminary round was conducted online using the Deselect coding platform. In this round,
participants were required to solve three coding questions within a time limit of two hours:

1.  Coin Change Problem: The first question was similar to the classic coin change

problem, where candidates needed to determine the number of ways to make a certain
amount using a given set of coin denominations.

2.  Password Validation: The second question involved checking a password against

specific constraints to determine its validity. Candidates were required to implement logic
to assess whether the provided string met the defined password criteria.

3.  Longest Substring Divisible by 3: The third question presented a string sss of length
mmm. The task was to find the length of the longest substring whose sum of ASCII
values is divisible by 3. If no such substring existed, the candidate was to return 0.

Technical Interview-1:

 Around 250 students attended the online test, and from that group, 85 students were selected for
the next round at CUIC. The interviewer began with his introduction, followed by mine. During
my introduction, I mentioned my strong proficiency in data structures and algorithms (DSA).
After that, he quickly moved on to DSA questions.

The first question presented was about an array of product prices and the number of coupons, K,
that could be applied to reduce the prices such that it should be minimum among all the
possibilities. For example, given the product prices of 100, 50, and 10, and K equal to 2, we can
apply the first coupon to the price of 100, reducing it to 50. We can then apply the second
coupon to the price of 50, further reducing it to 25. The new prices would then be 50, 25, and 10,
leading to a final sum of 85. So here we need to apply coupon for the products whose price was
high among all. I initially explained a brute-force approach using sorting, followed by an optimal
solution utilizing a priority queue(max heap). The interviewer seemed satisfied with my
explanation.

He then presented me with a medium-level problem from LeetCode, which involved evaluating a
mathematical expression provided in string format (Basic Calculator II)
https://leetcode.com/problems/basic-calculator-ii/solutions/1734060/c-solution-evaluation-of-
infix-expression/.

I explained my approach using a stack, wrote the code, and conducted a dry run with a sample
test case, which he found satisfactory.

Next, he inquired about my knowledge of SQL and PostgreSQL. He provided three tables and
asked me to write queries that involved joins, CASE statements, GROUP BY, ORDER BY, and
LIKE clauses.

The interviewer then shifted to operating systems topics, asking about multiprocessing, threads,
context switching, shared memory, mutexes, and semaphores. For each topic, I attempted to
clarify my explanations with diagrams.

He also asked me about the projects I had completed during my internship. He showed interest in
the technology stack I utilized and posed questions to better understand my projects. I illustrated
the architecture of my internship project to provide clarity.

Following this, he asked me several questions related to object-oriented programming (OOP),
including topics such as inheritance, abstract classes, and interfaces.

Finally, he asked if I had any questions for him. I inquired about the technology stack used at
Gen, the work culture, and the types of projects interns would be involved in. After our
discussion, he provided positive feedback, stating that I demonstrated a strong understanding of
my projects. He also mentioned that I performed very well in the interview and congratulated me
on progressing to the next round and advised me to ask more testcases during solving DSA.

This round lasted approximately 30 to 40 minutes, while some students spent around an hour,
particularly those who focused heavily on solving the DSA questions.

 Technical Interview-2:

The interviewer shook my hand and introduced himself. He was a senior-level manager with 15
years of experience at Gen Digital (Norton). He reviewed my technical interview papers for
about five minutes, where I had documented my answers for coding, SQL, project architecture,
and a separate scoring sheet that included remarks on various topics such as DSA, DBMS,
System Design, and Operating Systems.

After this initial review, he asked me to rate my proficiency in DSA on a scale of 1 to 5. I rated
myself a 4 out of 5. He then posed a question about finding the angle between the hour and
minute hands of an analog clock based on their positions. I clarified the question by asking a few
questions to ensure my understanding. Through calculations, I determined that if the hour hand is
at 3 and the minute hand is at 6 (30 minutes), each hour represents 36012=30\frac{360}{12} =
3012360=30 degrees, and each minute represents 36060=6\frac{360}{60} = 660360=6 degrees.
Therefore, the angle can be calculated as the absolute difference between hour×30\text{hour}
\times 30hour×30 and minute×6\text{minute} \times 6minute×6. He confirmed this was correct
but noted that it would not yield accurate results in all cases. He provided a counterexample with
the time set to 3 hours and 30 minutes, explaining that the hour hand would actually be between
3 and 4. I acknowledged this and adjusted my calculations accordingly, which satisfied him. (For
reference, this question is similar to LeetCode's Angle Between Hands of a Clock problem.)

The next DSA question involved determining if string B is a substring of string A. I suggested
using the substr function in C++, but he asked me to explain how it works and the underlying
algorithm. I mentioned the KMP algorithm. He then inquired if I was familiar with the trie data
structure. I admitted that I only knew the basics, and he mentioned that the substr function
utilizes a trie. Although he decided to move on to the next question due to my lack of familiarity
with tries, he subtly asked how a Google search engine functions, specifically how it
recommends searches based on a single letter input. I explained that this also involves a trie data
structure and illustrated how it could be represented in a tree-like format.

Next, he presented the course schedule problem, which is well-known in graph theory and
typically involves topological sorting. I explained my approach and he asked me to write the
code. After I completed the coding and dry ran it with various sample inputs, he expressed his
satisfaction.

He then asked me, “What is the worst challenge you have faced in Computer Science, and how
did you overcome it?” Since I mentioned AWS in my resume, he inquired about the projects I
completed during my internship, specifically any challenges I faced while working at Amazon. I
explained the use of Amazon SQS in my project and discussed how it supports parallel
processing. When he asked what happens if data sent to one of the parallel processing units fails,
I initially mentioned that the data would be lost. He corrected me, explaining the concept of
visibility timeout in SQS.

 He also asked me to differentiate between RDBMS and NoSQL, explaining why horizontal
scaling is a challenge for RDBMS. I discussed the benefits of NoSQL and sharding, explaining
how data is managed when adding additional units to the system. He seemed pleased with my
responses.

Finally, he asked if I had any questions. I inquired about the technology stack his team uses and
requested feedback on my interview performance. He remarked that I demonstrated strong skills
in DSA and expressed confidence in my ability to advance to the next round.

This round lasted approximately one hour. However, when I spoke with friends who attended the
same round, I learned that their interviews primarily focused on projects, OOP, DBMS, and a
single DSA question related to arrays, stacks, or strings. This indicated that the nature of
questions can vary based on the interview panel and the introductory statements made at the
beginning of the interview.

Technical Interview – 3 – (Techno Mangerial round):

The interviewer began by asking for my introduction and then introduced himself, mentioning
that he previously worked at Samsung Research Institute in Bangalore. However, he relocated to
Gen Digital (Norton) in Chennai for family reasons and he was a senior-level manager with 10
years of experience at Gen Digital (Norton). He then reviewed the score sheets from my first and
second rounds, which had been filled out by previous interviewers. They noted that I was "strong
in DSA" and scored me a perfect 10 out of 10. He inquired about the level of DSA questions
asked during those rounds, and I responded that they covered topics up to graphs and tries. He
remarked that I demonstrated a strong understanding of DSA and indicated that he would not be
asking any further questions from DSA.

Instead, he transitioned to asking about React, specifically the virtual DOM and hooks, and I
answered those questions confidently. He then asked why I was interested in Gen (Norton) and
what I knew about the company. I discussed the cybersecurity products that Gen develops,
prompting him to ask for my definition of cybersecurity. I explained the concept and outlined
various types of cyber attacks, including malware, ransomware, phishing, SQL injection, DDoS
attacks, and man-in-the-middle attacks. He expressed surprise at my knowledge of man-in-the-
middle and DDoS attacks and asked me to explain them or move on to the next question. I chose
to explain both concepts in detail.

Next, he inquired about my experience with cloud-level projects and whether I would be
comfortable working on client-level projects. Unfamiliar with the term "client-level projects," I
asked for clarification and confirmed that I would be open to such work.

He then posed a few HR-related questions, such as how I would manage multiple tasks if I
realized I couldn't complete them all within the given deadline. I provided my responses to those
scenarios.

 The interview concluded on a positive note, with him advising me to speak louder during online
meetings, as lower volume can hinder listeners' ability to hear clearly. I also asked him questions
regarding the transition from intern to full-time employment and related topics.

This round went for around 15 minutes.

Conclusion:

I am pleased to share that I was selected for the position. Having previously attended interviews
at Amazon and Apple, I found the interview process at Gen Digital (Norton) to be of particularly
high standards and expectations. In the Amazon interview, candidates are typically asked only
two DSA questions; successfully answering them is often sufficient for selection. At Apple, there
are two rounds that include basic questions about projects, fundamental computer science
concepts, managerial inquiries, and one DSA question.

In contrast, the process at Gen Digital consisted of three rounds, in addition to the online coding
round. In the first two rounds, I encountered six DSA questions, which ranged from medium to
hard difficulty on LeetCode, along with in-depth inquiries about distributed systems and
database management systems (DBMS). This rigorous process underscored the emphasis Gen
places on strong technical knowledge and problem-solving abilities.

Tips:

•  Always start by explaining the brute-force solution, even if you already know the optimal

approach as they will assess your thought process too.

•  Stay engaged during the interview; avoid being silent.

•  Be clear while explaining your thought process/approach

Preparation Docs:

❖  To cover all patterns, I followed Striver's A2Z DSA Sheet. For example, when learning

graphs, start with BFS, DFS, and distance algorithms, then practice related problems on

LeetCode and GeeksforGeeks.

❖  Develop a strong understanding of time and space complexity.

❖  Learn various data structures and their standard problems.

❖  If you can't immediately solve a problem, discuss your approach and thought process.

 ❖  Focus on solving a diverse range of problems rather than just a high volume of similar ones.

This strategy prepares you for unexpected questions and enhances your understanding of

DSA concepts.

All the very best! Feel free to reach out to me if you have any doubts.

SWATHI K – INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY

-TDPA FTE IN OPTUM

**About the Company:**

Optum, part of the UnitedHealth Group family of businesses, is a leading information and technology enabled health services company serving more than 132 million consumers, delivering the right care when and how it’s needed. We are dedicated to evolving health care so everyone can have the opportunity to live their healthiest life.

**About the Role:**

The Technology Development Program (TDP) at Optum is an entry point into the world of health technology, providing recent graduates the opportunity to gain hands-on experience in developing, implementing, and supporting advanced technological solutions that improve healthcare services. As a Software Engineering Associate within TDP, you’ll contribute to projects across multiple domains, including artificial intelligence, data analytics, and application development, all while supporting the modernization of healthcare.

**Overview of the Process:**

The overall process consists of

* An online test
* Technical Interview – in CUIC
* HR Round – in CUIC

**Online coding contest:**

* Solved a problem to find the largest square of true values in a 2D boolean matrix that appears at least twice, using dynamic programming to calculate square sizes and count occurrences efficiently.
* Encountered questions on various programming topics:
  + **OOPS:** Inheritance, Polymorphism.
  + **Java:** Exception handling.
  + **SQL:** Writing queries to filter data.
  + **Artificial Intelligence:** Approaches such as Machine Learning, Knowledge Representation, and Searching.

Overall, the test covered a wide range of technical concepts, requiring both problem-solving skills and theoretical knowledge.

**HR :**

The HR interview started with a casual introduction. It was around 15–20 minutes, covering the basics and my interest in Optum.

* **Tell me about yourself**
* **What do you know about Optum?**
* **Project discussion:** She asked me to describe one project in detail—covering the problem statement, what it does, and challenges I faced. She wanted it to be clear and concise.
* **Relocation:** Am I open to relocation?
* **Why Optum:** Why do I think Optum is a good fit for me?

My interview started with HR, followed by the Technical round, while others had it in reverse. She seemed satisfied with my responses, and I was selected for the next interview round.

**Interview (Technical):**

Duration: ~ 1 hour 45 mins

1. **Introduction** and resume review.
2. **DSA Questions:**
   * Asked about my DSA learning and practice experience.
   * Given a question on a right-rotated sorted array to find the rotation count:
   * Suggested brute-force solution (O(n)), then optimized to O(log n) with hints provided.
3. **Project Discussion:**
   * Discussed two projects (one Full Stack, one Python based):
   * Explained which were collaborative vs. solo.
   * He asked for a deeper understanding of how the code works internally.
   * Questions on collaboration, time management, and project deployment.
   * Asked why I hadn’t deployed the project for public use if it’s for people’s benefit.
4. **Technical Concepts:**
   * ReactJS: Why it evolved beyond HTML, JS, CSS—focused on Virtual DOM.
   * DBMS Normalization: When to use normalized vs. denormalized tables and the reasons.
5. **Wrap-Up:**

He shared insights on IT life—advice on adapting, managing workload, and what freshers can expect in the work environment.

**Tips and Tricks:**

* Focus on DSA: Know both brute-force and optimal solutions.
* Cover a range of topics: Arrays, Linked Lists, Stacks, Queues, Trees, Graphs, DP (even a week of prep can help!).
* Master core theory: OOP, DBMS, SQL, OS, CN.
* Stay calm: Answer confidently, keep a subtle smile.
* It’s okay to miss a question or two—stay positive!

**Preparation Journey:**

* I consistently prepared during my 6th-semester holidays.
* I realized that it’s not about how long you prepare, but rather covering all necessary topics and practicing effectively.
* For resources, I used ACM CODE documents and researched past experiences of candidates who interviewed at specific companies. This helped me gauge test difficulty and expectations.
* While rejections impacted my confidence, I learned to bounce back and developed a more relaxed attitude after attending multiple interviews, which helped me cope with failures.
* I acknowledge that the placement season is challenging, with continuous assessments alongside academics and FYP. It was tough to manage everything until I figured out a balance.
* Whenever I felt overwhelmed, I shared my feelings with friends and family, which relieved some stress. Keeping it all bottled up only adds to the burden.

ABINAYA S – INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY

ORACLE PLACEMENT PROCESS EXPERIENCE:

ABOUT THE COMPANY:

Oracle Corporation is a leading global technology company specializing in
database software, cloud services, and enterprise software solutions. Known for
its flagship Oracle Database, the company also provides a comprehensive suite
of cloud computing services, including infrastructure, applications, and security
solutions. Oracle's significant acquisitions, such as PeopleSoft, Sun
Microsystems, and NetSuite, have expanded its product portfolio, making it a
key player in various industries. Additionally, Oracle owns the Java
programming language and integrates AI and machine learning into its offerings
to drive innovation and automation.

OVERVIEW OF THE PROCESS:

The placement selection process consisted of several stages, starting with an
online test, followed by technical interviews, and finally an HR round.

1. Online Test

The first stage was an online test, which was a mix of technical and aptitude-
based questions. It included:

- MCQs related to Operating Systems (OS), Database Management Systems
(DBMS), Computer Networks(CN) and Aptitude.

- Comprehension questions to evaluate reading and understanding abilities.

- 1 coding question titled "Efficient Harvest." The question involved calculating
the maximum profit by selecting segments of data and optimizing it using a
sliding window technique.

2. Technical Interview-1

The first technical interview began with questions about my resume and a few
questions like "Tell me about yourself." They focused on checking my

 understanding of Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) concepts. The
interviewer asked me to explain the following with real-time examples:

- Polymorphism

- Abstraction

- Encapsulation

- Inheritance (with a discussion on types of inheritance)

The questions were asked to evaluate not just theoretical knowledge but also the
logic behind OOP concepts. Additionally, I was asked to compare Array vs.
Linked List, and there were discussions on the use of `this` pointer and
`super()`.

For the coding part, I was asked to:

- Find the first duplicated element in an array, for which I had to provide
brute-force, better, and optimal solutions while discussing their time and
space complexities.

3. Technical Interview-2

The second technical interview focused on SQL queries and coding. The
interviewer gave me two database tables and asked me to write several SQL
queries for like 10-15 scenarios:

- Basic SELECT queries

- ORDER BY, GROUP BY, JOINS, and sub-queries

I was then given an application format and was asked to write HTML and CSS
code according to specific requirements.

For coding part, the problem was to find the sum of composite numbers from
1 to `n`. I was again expected to provide brute-force, better, and optimal
solutions while discussing the efficiency of each approach.

 The interviewer also asked me about my project domain (NLP) and the use of
APIs in my project.

4. HR Interview

The HR interview focused on assessing my communication skills and overall
personality. The questions included:

- Tell me about yourself

- Strengths and weaknesses

- Details about my project

- Hobbies: I mentioned that I enjoy reading, and I was asked to name a few
books I had read recently, the author, reasons for choosing those books, and a
summary of each book.

- My experience with learning the French language was also discussed,
specifically why I chose to learn it.

The HR interview was mostly conversational to know how well I could express
myself and my thought process.

TIPS AND TRICKS:

- Platforms like LeetCode, HackerRank, and Codeforces are great for practicing
coding problems.

- Start with basic data structures like arrays, linked lists, stacks, and queues to
strengthen your foundation.

- Gradually proceed to complex ones like trees, graphs, heaps, and tries.

- Always analyze the time and space complexity of every problem you work on.

- Develop the habit of solving problems using brute force first, then move to
better and optimal solutions.

- Maintain a separate notebook to note down key concepts, approaches, and
solutions.

-I used resources like Striver's A-Z DSA sheets, SDE sheets, and placement
materials from seniors for preparation.

 -Read technical subjects such as OS, DBMS, CN, and OOP, and make note of
key points for last-minute revision.

- Once shortlisted for an interview, research the company and look for interview
experiences online or from seniors.

- Prepare for common HR questions and practice speaking to avoid hesitation.

- Always think of optimal solutions (in terms of time and space complexity)
when solving coding questions.

- Be able to explain OOP concepts like inheritance, polymorphism, abstraction,
and encapsulation with real-world examples.

- Know how to solve common Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA) problems
like sorting, searching, and traversing arrays, trees, and linked lists. Be prepared
to explain the time and space complexity of your solution.

- Practice SQL queries on various scenarios, including joins, subqueries, and
grouping functions.

- Explain your approach clearly and logically when writing code on a paper.

- Know your project inside-out. Be able to explain your role, technologies used,
and how you solved specific problems. Be prepared for detailed technical
discussions on APIs and the domain.

-Practice your self-introduction with your academic background, projects, skills,
and interests.

-Prepare yourself for question like your strengths and weakness beforehand and
don’t think of a new answer at the time of interview.

- HR rounds mostly test our communication skills. Stay calm, and answer
questions about hobbies, books, and languages fluently.

In the technical interview, which took place in the afternoon, I covered a lot
about my projects and technical skills. I started with a quick introduction about
myself and my background before diving into my project experience.

Project Discussion:
● First Project: I talked about a summer project we worked on during my
2nd year. I explained the tech stack we used, some challenges we faced,
and the collaborative side of working as a team. We used MongoDB for
the project, and I shared why we chose it over PostgreSQL, especially
emphasizing MongoDB's sharding feature, which allows better data
distribution.
● Next Project: Then, we discussed another project from my resume. I
walked the interviewer through the algorithms and features we
implemented, using diagrams to give a clear idea of the implementation.
He seemed interested in the details, which made it easy to explain our
approach and the reasons behind our design choices.
Technical Questions:
● Authentication vs. Authorization: I gave an overview of both concepts,
breaking down how each one works in a typical application.
● Favorite Subject (Computer Networks): Since I picked networks as my
favorite, he asked about specific layers. I explained the network and data
link layers and how IP and MAC addresses operate within them.
● SQL Problem: He gave me a query to solve, which I initially approached
using subqueries. Then, I used joins to make it more efficient.
● DSA Problem: For the DSA question, I outlined my approach and coded
the solution on paper, explaining its space and time complexity. I also
pointed out an additional optimization, which he appreciated.
Learning New Tech Stacks: The interviewer was curious about how I learn
new tech stacks. I shared that I usually combine team insights with my own
research. For example, when I learned React, I took advice from teammates and
also did my own deep dive before reaching a conclusion.
Why Optum?: Finally, we discussed why I was interested in Optum. I
mentioned the points from their presentation that stood out to me—like their
work in health technology and how their projects impact real people.
We wrapped up with a quick chat about the role of new technologies, including
ChatGPT, in improving workflows. It was a positive way to end, and we both
agreed on the power of tech in making life easier.

Outcome:
In total, 15 students were selected for the TDP Associate role at Optum.
My favourite part of the process was how they focused on understanding the

T E C H N O L O G Y   D E V E L O P M E N T   P R O G R A M   A S S O C I A T E   A T   O P T U M

ROLE:

Full Stack Engineer

ABOUT THE COMPANY:
Optum, part of the UnitedHealth Group family of businesses, is a leading
information and technology enabled health services company serving more
than 132 million consumers, delivering the right care when and how it’s
needed. We are dedicated to evolving health care so everyone can have the
opportunity to live their healthiest life.

ABOUT  THE ROLE:
The Technology Development Program (TDP) at Optum begins with a 4-
week bootcamp, followed by a 12 month development experience that
includes two 6-month rotations and concludes with a grad placement. As a
TDP Associate, you’ll gain invaluable experience across a variety of domains,
benefit from mentorship by technology leaders, and engage in networking
and collaboration opportunities across the technology organization. The
program’s cohort-based structure fosters a more personalized employment
experience, helping to build community and drive engagement beyond that
of a traditional hire.

OVERVIEW OF THE PROCESS:
The overall process consists of:

An online test
HR Round – in CUIC
Technical Interview – in CUIC

 ONLINE TEST:

This assessment consisted of a single question (full-stack based), and the most
important aspect was that you were allowed to use your preferred tech stack
from a predefined list of stacks. The question had multiple tiers of
requirements and was graded based on your ability to attempt all the
subsections. The highest-scoring attempt was considered as the final score.

Points were awarded even for partial solutions, so candidates were
encouraged to submit their code even if they were unable to complete
everything.

This was an AI co-piloted assessment, similar to the Copilot version
(Cosmo), which was provided to help you complete tasks more efficiently.
You were expected to make use of the AI co-pilot as much as needed.

Note: *This assessment was unlike those of the previous years.

*Make sure you use check the mails sent from the company regarding

the assessments.

HR ROUND : (30 mins approx.)

This round was so easy to clear as the HR who interviewed me was so chill
and only a normal  convo – like questions were asked. It was to make sure
your English communication is good.

Introduce yourself
Asked about native , and how are you adapted to Chennai
Hobbies and interests
Apart from that , what do you do?
Clubs and participations

 How do you manage along with your studies
Projects

  Why optum? If it is your fav, why?

Any questions for her?

Note: *Communicate well. Don’t panic.

TECHNICAL INTERVIEW : (1:45 hrs)

My interviewer is a backend developer at Optum (one of our IT department
alumni), a very knowledgeable and experienced person.
He asked to introduce myself and proceeded on with the tech questions.
OOPS, DBMS, SQL, and DSA was concentrated. Networks wasn’t a part but
doesn’t mean we can neglect it. Some companies ask networks in deep too. Be
very clear in your projects.
OOPS:
           The regular questions like abstract class, interface, polymorphism and
how is it in java,c++ and python. Refer gfg and your textbooks.

DBMS SQL:

•  Two simple queries were asked.
•  Normalization, joins, indexing, and some more.,
•  Explain- SAAS, PAAS, IAAS

Since it is a full stack role , I brushed up the concepts regarding the web dev
part also.
Every question he asked was through my resume. He kept on striking out the
part that were done asking for. Eg questions:
•  Differentiate html and React
•  Why MongoDB is mostly used in your works?
•  How do you secure API?
•  Why does a backend infrastructure is required?
•  Why a page suddenly logs out if unused for some time?
•  What are APIs and why do you need that?

 •  What happens when we build a project? What does the build folder

contain?

•  How many HTML files are there in react, how does this html file work

in react?

There were more built on questions from the answers I gave.

DSA:
      Second smallest number in the array.
      Generate all the subarrays given an array.
Questions like why optum? Any questions for me? were asked.

*Use brute force too but I forgot it at the moment yet the interviewer was
happy.
*One advice from the interviewer: Learn everything in depth.

TIPS:

•  I documented materials used for studying and revised periodically.
•  Used strivers A-Z sheet for DSA.
•  Solved SQL 50 in leetcode.
•  Learnt core concepts (OOPS, SQL, DBMS, CN, System Design.,)
•  Have good projects in hand .. would be a great hit in interviews. My
projects mostly aligned with the company’s domain and was of great
help.

•  Sharing ideas and learning with friends is important.
•  After  attending  each  online  tests,  if you  were  not able  to  solve  the

question, try it aftermath the coding test.

•  Use your 6th sem holidays efficiently.
•  Be genuine. If you don’t know the answer accept it. Believe me,my

interviewer taught me those.

•  It doesn’t matter if you fail;make sure to fail better the next time and

eventually you will pass.

 Maybe you are waiting for a bedtime story; meanwhile, the universe is
writing a fairytale for you.
All the best for your placements and feel free to contact me!

Interview Experience

Optum, Gurgaon / Hyderabad - Optum_Campus Hiring

Role: Full Stack Engineer

Round 1 :

Online test:

Online test took about 1.5hrs. It consists of 4 sections each focusing on Aptitude, Cognitive thinking,
English Grammar and one Coding Question. The first 3 sections are of easy to intermediate level. The last
One coding question was of Medium level (as of Leetcode). The question is like to

“The game: there are N players, and Player P starts with the ball. The goal: Pass the ball around but
make sure after X passes, it’s back in Player P's hands.

One rule: you can’t pass the ball back to the same person you just got it from. Keep it moving and mix
it up”

Note: A game is considered as "complete" if the ball ends up with the player
who started it.

input1: An integer value N, representing the number of
players,
input2: An integer value P, representing the player who starts
the game.
input3: An integer value X, representing the maximum number of moves allowed to pass the ball.
Output Specification:
Return an integer value representing the number of possible ways to complete the game.
input1: 3
input2: 2
input3: 4

Output: 3

Solution: I did used the brute force that involves starting from one person and then implemented DFS
algorithm starting from the player P as they mentioned and explored all the possible ways.

Around 59 students got shortlisted(including myself)

 Round 2 : HR Interview

The HR interview is pretty easier, it was conducted in cuic (but virtual), cuic provided everything for
attending the interview all you need is to prepare only. The interviewer asked to introduce myself and
asked basic managerial questions related to the projects like

“How you do manage conflicts with your teammates?”

“What approach you used for learning?”

“How do you face failure?”

Tips: The interviewer expects you to be honest and he/she doesn’t expect any fake stories. So be honest.

Round 3: Technical Interview

The technical interview takes off with casual discussion on academics and some basic questions like

“why you choose IT?”
“why you choose optum?”

“Optum is a product or Service company?”(listen to their preplacement talk properly)

Then the actual tech questions begin. He asked about the tech stacks I used to make the projects

I used mern, flutter with dart and etc,. He asked me more and more on their fundamentals and
differentiating between tech stacks.

Then he asked the following:
“Buy and sell stocks (second level ) available in leetcode. It is find the no of ways to attain the max
profit).”

I told him the  dynamic programming approach but he was satisfied with recursion code itself.

“Print the output and tell the no of tuples in output after joining the two tables with all types of joins”

“Suppose if you have server that is accessed by multiple clients simultaneously, how will you ensure
the efficieny?”

I believe I have answered the above question correctly. At last he was satisfied with me .

Tips : Be prepared with some questions that you can ask back to the interviewer at last. Then prepare for
the core concepts and wire your brain to solve problems by practicing regularly.

16 got selected(including myself)
