**Standard Chartered Interview Experience**

**About Standard Chartered:**

**Standard Chartered** is a leading international banking and financial services company headquartered in London. It operates in more than 50 countries, with a strong presence across Asia, Africa, and the Middle East. The bank offers services in retail banking, corporate banking, wealth management, and investment banking. Founded in 1969 through the merger of the Standard Bank and Chartered Bank, it focuses heavily on emerging markets and global trade.

**About the Role:**

Standard Chartered visited our campus offering the **Development Engineer** role. The position focuses on developing and maintaining software applications for digital banking systems, writing efficient code, and collaborating with teams to build secure and scalable solutions.

**Selection Process:**

• **Round 1**: Pymetrics Assessment (16 Games – Behavioral and Cognitive Evaluation)
• **Round 2**: Value-Based Assessment (Video-based Situational Judgement Questions)
• **Round 3**: Online Coding Test (2 Coding Questions – Array and Sliding Window Based Problems)
• **Round 4**: Technical Interview 1 (Resume Discussion, Projects, OOPS Concepts, Basic DSA Problems)
• **Round 5**: Technical Managerial Interview (Detailed Discussion on Internship, Projects, and Real-life Scenarios)
• **Round 6**: HR Interview (Background Check, Career Plans, and General HR Questions)

**Round 1: Pymetrics Assessment** [No Time Limit]

This round consisted of **16 games** that evaluated skills like attention, decision making, learning pattern, and risk-taking. It was a **major elimination round**, where nearly **two-thirds of the candidates were filtered out**. The main focus was on **consistency in decision making across different games**.

**Round 2: Value-Based Assessment** [No Time Limit]

This round included **12 video-based workplace scenarios** with five response options each. Candidates had to **drag and rank the responses between “Effective” and “Non-Effective.”** The round evaluated **decision making, teamwork, professional behaviour, and alignment with company values**.

**Round 3: Online Coding Test** [90 mins.]

This round consisted of **two easy to medium level coding questions**.

**Question 1: Minimum Number of Partitions After Rearranging Data**
Candidates were given n storage partitions where each partition had used[i] amount of data and a maximum capacity[i]. Data could be moved and split across multiple partitions without exceeding the capacity. The objective was to rearrange the data such that it occupies the **minimum number of partitions**.

**Approach:**

* Calculate the **total used data** across all partitions.
* **Sort the capacity array in descending order**.
* Select partitions with the **largest capacities first**.
* Keep adding capacities until the **total capacity becomes greater than or equal to the total used data**.

The number of selected partitions gives the minimum number required. This greedy approach works because using larger capacities first reduces the total partitions needed.

**Question 2: Maximum of Minimum Disk Space in Segments**
An array space[] representing disk space of computers and an integer x representing segment length were given. For each contiguous segment of size x, the **minimum disk space** had to be determined. Finally, the **maximum value among all these minimums** had to be returned.

**Approach:**
A **Sliding Window technique with a Monotonic Deque** was used:

* Maintain a deque storing indices in **increasing order of values**.
* Remove elements from the back that are **greater than the current element**.
* Remove elements from the front that fall **outside the current window**.
* The **front of the deque always gives the minimum element of the window**.
* Track the **maximum among these minimum values** as the window slides.

This approach ensures efficient computation with **O(n) time complexity**.

**Round 4: Technical Interview** [Approx. 25–30 mins]

This round mainly focused on **resume discussion, projects, and basic programming concepts**. The interviewer asked detailed questions about the **projects mentioned in the resume**, including implementation details, technologies used, and challenges faced.

Topics covered included:

* **Object-Oriented Programming concepts** (Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction)
* Difference between **OOP and procedural programming**
* Questions related to **internship or project work**
* Discussion on **data structures used in the project**

Coding questions included:

* **Check whether a string is a palindrome**
* **Convert a sentence into proper casing using Regex**
* Some **basic string manipulation problems**

The interviewer mainly evaluated **problem solving approach and clarity of explanation**.

**Round 5: Technical Managerial Interview**

[Approx. 30 mins]

This round was more discussion-oriented and focused on **internship experience, project details, and real-life problem solving situations**.

Some questions asked:

* Explain your **internship project in detail**.
* What **technologies and tools** did you use?
* What was the **most challenging problem** you faced?
* How did you **solve that problem**?
* How do you **handle pressure situations**?
* Describe a **real scenario in college where you handled pressure or tight deadlines**.
* How do you **prioritize tasks when multiple deadlines exist**?
* If a **team member is not contributing**, how would you handle it?

This round mainly evaluated **communication skills, teamwork ability, and practical thinking**.

**Round 6: HR Interview** [Approx. 10 mins]

The HR round was mainly focused on understanding the candidate’s **background, career plans, and stability**.

Some questions asked included:

* Tell me about yourself.
* Why do you want to join **Standard Chartered**?
* Are you planning to pursue **higher studies**?
* Do you have any idea about **start-ups**?
* Do you have any **interest in politics**?
* Is anyone in your **family involved in politics or business**?
* Why not try **government exams**?
* Are you willing to **relocate if required**?

This round mainly checks whether the candidate is **serious about the role and willing to work with the organization long term**.

* **Finally 9 candidates were selected, and I was one among them.**

.

**My Preparation Strategy:**

• I consistently practiced **DSA problems**, covering topics like **arrays, strings, dynamic programming, backtracking, and graphs**, although most interview questions mainly focused on **arrays and string manipulation**.
• I revised **core computer science concepts** such as **OOPS, DBMS, Operating Systems, and Computer Networks** before the interview rounds.
• I made sure to **clearly understand and explain my projects and internship work**, since most technical questions were based on resume discussions.
• I practiced solving **coding problems regularly** on platforms like LeetCode and HackerRank to strengthen problem-solving skills.
• I discussed technical concepts and interview questions with my **friends and peers**, which helped in clarifying doubts quickly.
• I also prepared for **managerial and HR questions** by thinking about real-life situations where I handled pressure or worked in a team.
• I reviewed **previous interview experiences** to understand the pattern of questions asked in technical and HR rounds.

**Previous Interviews list** :

* Amazon
* HSBC
* Applied Materials
* Ramco
* Tekion

**Key Suggestions and Tips from My Experience:**

* Be **consistent during the Pymetrics games**, as the platform tracks behavior patterns.
* During coding rounds, focus on **writing clean and correct logic rather than overly complex solutions**.
* Be **thorough with your resume and projects**, since interviewers ask many questions from them.
* Practice **basic DSA problems, especially arrays and strings**.
* During interviews, **explain your thought process clearly while solving problems**.
* Prepare examples from your experience to answer **situational and managerial questions** confidently.

General Tips: 
• 
Stay confident and calm – don’t put unnecessary pressure on yourself. 
• 
Communicate naturally – speak as if you are talking to a friend; this helps reduce nervousness.

Maintain composure – don’t be overly afraid; show a balanced level of confidence and positive 
attitude.

offline rounds). 
• 
Answer smartly – if you don’t know a question, try to relate it to relevant topics you are familiar 
with instead of staying silent. 
• 
First impression matters – give a clear, confident, and engaging self-introduction 
• 
Real life examples-provide real life examples whenever possible

About NatWest Group

Online Assessment 
Online assessment was conducted on 25th August 2025 on the SHL Platform at CUIC. 
It consisted of four sections which could be attempted in any order.

The questions were array-based and of easy difficulty level.

A driver code was provided; we only had to implement the required function. 
• 
Each participant was randomly assigned a different set of questions. 
 
Question 1: 
Given an array of integers and two values, start and end, return a new array containing only 
the elements that lie between start and end (inclusive), while preserving their original order. 
 
Input:  Array: [12, 76, 34, 98, 11, 54, 63, 45] 
Start: 30 
End: 60 
Output: 
Array: [34, 54, 45]  
 
Question 2: 
Given an n-digit number return the smallest possible n-digit number that can be formed using 
its digits. 
Input:  
Number: 90018 
Output:  
Number: 10089 
Although the questions were presented with additional filler context, the core expectation was 
exactly as written here. 
 
2. SQL (15 minutes, 10-12 questions, free navigation) 
• The questions involving selecting the correct option that produced the given output, 
determining the output of queries, and answering a few conceptual questions. 
• This section was relatively easy and could be solved with basic SQL knowledge. 
• It is recommended to be acquainted with SQL fundamentals and practice solving SQL 
queries beforehand. 
 
3. General Aptitude (36 minutes, 24 questions, no free navigation) 
• 
An optional practice test was available before the actual test. It is recommended to 
attend it, as the questions were quite different from those in other OAs. Attending this 
test also helps in becoming familiar with the platform. 
• 
The section was interactive, and the questions required scheduling meetings, adjusting 
bar graphs/pie charts to reflect correct percentage values (calculated from the given 
information), completing graphs based on node values, selecting dates, and assigning 
rooms based on given criteria. 
• 
Overall, the questions were of medium difficulty but time-consuming. 
 
4. 
English Aptitude (15 minutes, 12 questions, no free navigation) 
• 
This section comprised reading comprehension, synonyms and antonyms, error 
spotting, identifying the correct sequence of sentences. 
 
23 Students were shortlisted after this round. The shortlist was announced a day before the 
interview. 
 
Technical Interview + HR Interview (25 to 30 minutes) 
  
A PPT session was held on 29th August 2025 at CUIC. Generally, there was only one round 
of interview with two panel members. The interview for interns started at around 2.00 pm. The 
entire interview was based on my Resume and the answers I provided. 
Introduction 
I was asked to introduce myself. It is important to keep the introduction short and crisp, and 
be clear in advance about what you will share, as the panel may ask follow-up questions based 
on your introduction. 
 
Projects 
I was asked to explain all the projects I had listed in my resume. I described: 
• What the project was about, 
• Why I chose to do it, 
• The tech stack I used and the reasons for choosing it, and 
• The impact of the project. 
  
SQL query 
I was asked an SQL query related to one of my projects, It was an easy question involving 
JOINs and multiple WHERE clauses, they began with a basic query and then added a few 
follow-ups, and finally I was asked to explain my approach. I also provided an alternative 
solution. 
It is important to clearly explain your thought process and approach to the interviewers. 
 
Technical Section: 
• 
What is the difference between map and hashmap? 
• 
I was asked about my preferred programming language, the reason for choosing it and 
was also asked why I had learned four languages. 
• 
How strong am I in DSA? 
• 
What are the applications of linked list? 
• 
Since I had mentioned SDLC in my resume under coursework, they asked me to explain 
what I knew about SDLC. 
• 
I was then asked to explain about Normalization in DBMS. 
• 
They asked why I had used Google Colab (as mentioned in my resume). I explained that 
I had used it as part of my Gen AI coursework. 
 
HR Section: 
I was given a chance to ask questions—don’t miss this opportunity! 
 
I asked the panel about their journey at NatWest, and they were quite invested in 
sharing their experiences. I then asked about the involvement of AI at NatWest, their 
expectations from a fresher, and the kind of work I would be doing there. Finally, I 
asked for their suggestions on my resume. 
 
Finally, 9 students were shortlisted for the internship, and I am glad to be one among them.

My Personal Experience 
 
I started my preparation during the summer vacation of my second year. I used to solve 
2–3 DSA problems each day on LeetCode and GeeksforGeeks (though I had solved problems 
on LeetCode earlier, I wasn’t consistent back then). 
I didn’t really feel the heat of the competition until I started receiving notifications about 
internships and updates on selected candidates in WhatsApp groups. That’s when I sped up my 
preparation. 
For the OA, I brushed up on SQL concepts, revised general aptitude from IndiaBix, and 
practiced a few problems on LeetCode. I also went through placement experiences and solved 
problems similar to those. 
Note: Don’t forget to bring your calculator. 
For the interview, I would advise studying OS, DBMS, OOPS, and CN concepts in advance. 
Don’t postpone this until the last moment (which is what I did). Even though I wasn’t asked 
about OS, CN, and OOPS, many companies do ask them, so it’s better to be prepared. 
Note: Be thorough with your resume, I was asked about SDLC because I had listed it. Make 
sure you can explain your projects clearly: why you chose this project, its outcome, highlighted 
features, the tech stack you used, why you used it, and be ready for follow-up questions.  
 
General Advice 
•   Maintain proper posture during the interview. 
•   Communication is the key. 
•   Start your preparation as early as possible, but remember it’s never too late to start. 
•   Intern drives can be overwhelming but keep improving your skills and the results 
will follow.

Coding Question 2: 
The task is to write a program that determines and returns the current state of a tennis game 
based on the points scored by two players. 
 Tennis Scoring Rules Recap: 
• 
A tennis game is scored in points: 
o 1st point → 15 
o 2nd point → 30 
o 3rd point → 40 
• 
After both players reach at least 40 points, three special conditions apply: 
1. 
Deuce: 
▪ 
When both players have scored the same number of points (and at least 
3 points each). 
▪ 
Example:   5–5. 
2. 
Advantage: 
▪ 
When both have at least 3 points, but one player has 1 point more than 
the other. 
▪ 
Example: 6–5 (Advantage Player 1) or 5–6 (Advantage Player 2). 
 
3. 
Win: 
▪ 
A player wins if they have at least 4 points and at least 2 points more 
than the opponent. 
▪ 
Example: 8–4 → Player 1 wins. 
Example: 
nameP1 = "Alice" 
nameP2 = "Bob" 
winner = {"Alice", "Bob", "Alice", "Alice"} 
Alice scores: 3 points (15 → 30 → 40)  Bob scores: 1 point (15) 
 Output: "40 - 15" 
{If more points are played and it becomes 4–4: 
 Output: "Deuce" 
If it becomes 5–4: 
 Output: "Advantage Alice" 
If it becomes 6–4: 
 Output: "Alice wins"} 
➔Even though the tennis game rules look lengthy, the implementation logic is actually 
very simple. The whole problem boils down to a few clear if–else conditions based on the 
scores of the two players. 
 
TECHNICAL INTERVIEW ROUND  1 (45 mins): 
The technical interview was conducted virtually. Initially, the interviewer asked me to 
introduce myself, followed by a few questions about my projects — particularly focusing on 
the unique contributions I had made to them. After that, the session moved to a pair coding 
round. The problem given was the "Restrictive Candy Crush" problem GFG. The interviewer 
clearly explained the problem statement and asked me to first describe my approach with a 
dry run of the test cases. 
I explained the stack-based approach, where each number and its count are stored and 
updated as we process the input. She then asked me to write the pseudocode and explain it 
step-by-step, followed by an analysis of the time and space complexity. She also asked me 
to suggest the possible test cases that could be designed for this problem to ensure 
correctness and handle edge scenarios. This part of the interview focused more on how 
interactively I was solving the problem and thinking through different situations. The 
interviewer was satisfied with my approach and explanation. 
Next, the discussion shifted to concepts related to Locks and Semaphores. I was asked to 
explain read-write locks, two-phase locking (with an example), and provide an overview of 
locks, semaphores, and concurrency. I explained each concept with suitable examples, and 
the interviewer was satisfied. 
 
TECHNO MANAGERIAL INTERVIEW (R2): 
 
The  interview began with a self-introduction ,where I briefly talked about my background 
and interests. This  was followed by a discussion of my projects, where I explained  few 
projects and the reason for choosing the particular tech stack. The interviewer also asked for 
my opinion on Python versus Java and why I prefer one over the other,and asked me to list 
out the cases where Python can be preferred and then about which tech stacks I am familiar 
with and my level of experience with each and about the Software Development Life Cycle 
(SDLC) and its different phases in the design process. This was followed by, behavioral and 
Situational Questions: 
1.Conflict Management: “How would you manage a conflict between your teammates?” 
2. Tech Stack Issue Midway: “You realize halfway through the deadline that the chosen tech stack 
won’t work. What will you do?” 
3. New Technology with Limited Time: “If you are given a new tech stack you’re not familiar with 
and have only 2 days to complete the work while your teammates are busy, what will you do?” 
4. Team Preference:“Do you prefer working alone, in a team of two, or in a larger team?” 
 
The interviewer then returned to discussing my project work and asked how long it took me 
to adapt to a new skill, how I managed the learning process, and how I prioritized my tasks 
during the project. I utilized the last 10 minutes of the interview to ask the interviewer about 
Apple’s work culture and his journey of getting into Apple, which helped me gain valuable 
insights and left a positive impression. The interview lasted around 45 minutes. 
 
[The online assessment was conducted on 4th September, and the results were announced 
on the same day. My first interview round was held on 23rd September, followed by the 
second round on 24th September. This gave me around 20 days to prepare effectively.] 
 
 
 
 
KEY TAKEAWAYS: 
1.  Give a strong and impressive self-introduction – it’s the most important part of the 
interview as it reflects who you are and sets the tone for the rest of the discussion. 
2. Know your project work thoroughly – interviewers are highly experienced, so you 
must be confident and well-prepared to discuss your contributions, challenges faced, 
and solutions implemented. 
3. Practice coding consistently on platforms like LeetCode and GFG, and focus deeply 
on Operating Systems, DBMS, OOPs, Computer Networks, and System Design – not 
just theoretically but also with practical applications, as interviewers often expect 
real-world examples. 
 Pitch yourself effectively – most of the questions will be based on what you say, so 
be thoughtful and strategic while presenting your skills and experiences. 
4. Research the company thoroughly before the interview – understanding its culture, 
values, products, and work environment helps you align your answers better. 
 
                                                               ALL THE BEST!!! 
Keep practicing consistently — remember, every interview is a learning process that helps 
you grow and improve.

WELLS FARGO (PLACEMENTS)  
Role: Program Associate 
Batch: 2020 – 2024 
Mode: Offline 
Number of Rounds: 4 
About the Company: 
Wells Fargo is a prominent multinational financial services company based 
in San Francisco, USA. They offer a wide range of services including banking, 
investments, credit cards, mortgages, as well as consumer and commercial 
finance. In India, they have offices in Chennai, Hyderabad, and Bangalore. 
Round 1: Online Assessment 
The online test was conducted on the SHL AMCAT platform and 
consisted of three sections: 
1. Verbal Aptitude 
2. Business Aptitude 
3. Coding Section 
This format is typical for Wells Fargo's online assessments. The verbal 
aptitude section tested vocabulary and comprehension. The business 
aptitude section focused on data interpretation from pie charts, bar charts, 
graphs, and stocks & exchanges. While these sections weren't particularly 
difficult, the time constraints made quick reading and analysis essential. 
The coding section featured three problems. The first two were quite 
easy, and most candidates could pass all test cases. The third problem was 
of medium difficulty, but the challenge was compounded by the use of an 
older C++ version, which didn’t support vectors. It was a graph-based 
problem with only two test cases. My code passed one of them, which 
turned out to be sufficient. 
Out of all candidates, 59 were selected for the next round, with 22 being 
added just an hour before the interviews through an additional shortlist. 
Tip:  
The aptitude section seemed to carry more weight than the coding 
part. I recommend starting to practice aptitude questions on platforms like 
IndiaBix and PrepInsta about a month before placements begin. For coding, 
LeetCode and GeeksforGeeks should be sufficient, but it's best to start 
learning DSA at least three months in advance, as online tests are becoming 
increasingly challenging. 
 
Round 2: Technical Interview 1 
In this round, the interviewer was friendly and helped me feel at ease, 
knowing it was my first interview. He started with a real-life problem that 
required sorting as a solution. I explained Merge Sort in detail, as it’s a good 
algorithm for in-depth discussion. He asked a few follow-up questions as 
well. 
We then discussed the differences between C++ and Java, and the 
interviewer dug deeper into this topic. Next, he moved on to system design 
and posed the problem statement: "Consider you're storing data about all 
Indians in a database. How would you design such a database? What factors 
would you consider?" I suggested a few solutions to improve access time, 
reduce latency, and enhance scalability. 
I felt I didn’t do well on the system design part, but the interviewer gave 
a positive response. 
 
Round 3: Technical Interview 2 
This round began with a basic question: "What is your favorite data 
structure?" I chose graphs and explained traversal algorithms. The 
interviewer asked several real-life questions, and I answered them using 
concepts like cycle detection, shortest path, and weighted graphs. 
The conversation then shifted to my projects. I walked him through the 
entire process, and he asked about alternatives to the stack I used and the 
innovations I implemented. We also covered some HR questions, like where 
I see myself in 3-5 years, handling conflicts within a team, and task 
assignments. 
Overall, this round went smoothly. The interviewer seemed pleased, 
and his feedback was positive. 
Tip:  
To succeed in technical interviews, it’s important to clearly explain 
your ideas and solutions. Don’t let anxiety or nerves get the best of you; 
mock interviews can help build confidence. Be thoroughly familiar with 
everything on your resume, as most questions will be asked from it. 
 
Round 4: Behavioral + HR Round 
This round was more like a conversation than a formal interview. I was 
expecting the usual HR questions, but the interviewer seemed genuinely 
interested in getting to know me. She asked about my passions and how my 
college experience has shaped my character. Since she was also involved 
in the pre-placement talk, I asked her a few questions based on that, and 
she seemed impressed with my attentiveness. 
The HR round lasted about 20 minutes and ended on a positive note. 
In total, 20 students were selected. 
 
 
 
Key topics to focus on include: 
o DSA (for Online Assessments and Interviews) 
o Aptitude 
o Core Subjects 
o Projects 
o System Design 
o Puzzles

Citi CSC Intern Interview Experience ‘23 
 
About the company: 
Citigroup Inc., commonly known as Citi, is indeed an American multinational investment bank and 
financial services corporation. Its headquarters are in New York City. Citi operates globally, offering a 
wide range of financial products and services to individuals, corporations, governments, and 
institutions. 
 
Citi also has a presence in India and operates under the name Citi India. In India, Citi has established 
Citi Solution Centres, which are specialized centres focused on technology, processing, analytics, and 
financial processes. These solution centres play a crucial role in supporting Citi's operations and 
providing various services to its customers. 
 
Citi's presence in India reflects its commitment to leveraging global talent and resources to deliver 
innovative solutions and efficient services to its clients worldwide. 
 
Number of rounds: 3 (After shortlisting of candidates above 8.5 CGPA by CUIC)  
1) Online Test  
2) Technical Interview 
3) HR Interview 
 
ONLINE ASSESSMENT: 
 
Platform: SHL AMCAT 
The test has few basic requirements: 
 
A Personal Computer / Laptop. 
 
A Webcam connected with the System (Not required in case of Laptop with functional in-
built Webcam) 
 
Smooth and uninterrupted Internet connectivity (throughout the testing session) 
 
Duration of Assessment: 2 Hrs 
 
The online assessment had 2 sections: 
 
APTITUDE SECTION 
 
CODING SECTION 
45 mins of Aptitude questions- 
In my experience, the aptitude questions in the assessment ranged from easy to medium difficulty. I 
found that calculating values and solving expressions took up a significant amount of time, making it 
a time-consuming process. What I noticed was that logical-based questions required careful 
attention because even though the question itself seemed easy; the answer options could be quite 
tricky. 
Do not spend too much time on a single question. It's crucial to manage time effectively and not get 
stuck on challenging questions. Instead, make it a point to move on to the next question (Note You 
can't come back to unmarked question again) 
 
What surprised me was that the aptitude round carried equal weightage as the coding round, 
highlighting its importance in the overall evaluation process. So, it's essential to prepare well for the 
aptitude section to maximize the chances of success. 
 
75 mins of coding questions- 
This section contained 2 questions. We could choose to code them in our preferred languages (Java / 
C++ is suggested) 
 
1. Program to Print the Trapezium Pattern: Given ‘num’ which indicates number of lines. The task is 
to print a trapezium pattern in num lines. (Program to Print the Trapezium Pattern - GeeksforGeeks) 
2. Couldn't recollect the question: something related to map.  
 
The questions were of easy difficulty, and I was able to pass all test cases in first question and 3-5 test 
cases in second question using brute force approach. 
 
TECHNICAL INTERVIEW: 
In my experience, this round was a face-to-face interview conducted physically at CUIC and lasted for 
30 minutes. From Anna University, a total of 48 students, including myself, were shortlisted for this 
round. We were divided into four groups based on our domains: FXLM, Risk, Support, and TTS. I was 
assigned to the FXLM domain interview. 
 
The interview commenced with the standard question of introducing myself. I understood that the 
answer I gave would create the first impression about me. Therefore, I decided to present my 
introduction in the form of a short story. I explained how I ended up pursuing computer science 
engineering at CEG after studying biology in school, why I chose this field, what I have learned from 
it, and shared my interests. 
 
During the interview, I was asked about how I managed to understand computer science concepts 
and subjects despite having no prior knowledge before joining college. I shared my experience of 
how my first year of college was quite challenging, and I had to find ways to grasp the concepts. I 
explained that I sought guidance from seniors and actively engaged in projects for each subject, 
which allowed me to gain a practical understanding of the topics. Implementing the concepts 
practically helped me comprehend them better. 
 
The interviewers also inquired about my projects, which I realized was an important question in the 
Citi intern interview. They were not solely interested in assessing my technical skills but also wanted 
to know about the innovation I brought to the projects and my knowledge of the languages, 
frameworks, and stack used. They emphasized how I collaborated with my teammates and 
showcased my leadership skills. I highlighted two high-quality projects that I could confidently 
present on my resume and to the interviewers. 
 
Additionally, I was asked about a few object-oriented programming concepts and database 
management system concepts, although I couldn't recall the exact questions. Lastly, they presented 
me with a puzzle to solve. The puzzle involved placing ten balls in five lines in a specific manner 
(referring to the link: https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/puzzle-10-balls-in-5-lines/). 
 
Towards the end of the interview, they gave me an opportunity to ask any questions I had, and then 
the interview concluded. 
 
HR ROUND: 
 
I am unable to explain about this round cause I had only 2 minutes of HR Round. The 
interviewer asked me to tell him about myself and asked me to leave : ). 
 
IMPORTANT TIPS: 
 
I have limited experience preparing for coding questions on coding platforms, and I consider 
myself fortunate to have received an easy question in my coding round. You can refer to the 
experiences of my other friends for more insights. 
 
 
When it comes to the aptitude round, it's important to practice time management by 
keeping track of the time taken to solve each question. Look for websites that offer aptitude 
questions and try to understand the underlying logic behind the solutions. 
 
 
Having a basic understanding of data structures, algorithms, computing complexities, and 
related concepts is beneficial. 
 
I recommend solving coding questions in Java or C++ rather than C or Python. It's often 
helpful to initially attempt a brute force method for coding questions, as it can satisfy most 
test cases. If you have no idea how to solve a problem, try hard coding specific solutions to 
satisfy the test cases. 
 
 
It's advantageous to have at least two solid projects that can enhance your resume. Ensure 
you have an in-depth understanding of your projects, including the problem statement and 
any novel aspects of your solutions. 
 
 
When preparing your resume, aim for a minimalistic approach. Include details that you are 
confident about and can answer during an interview. Order your projects and programming 
languages based on your level of confidence and knowledge. Avoid lying about any 
information on your resume and refrain from including things you are unsure about. 
Additionally, include any relevant extracurricular activities. 
(https://drive.google.com/file/d/1GxYBY-
Wbn63foJq2PDWRysVOUvxCQv8Q/view?usp=sharing) 
 
 
Before the interview, review last-minute preparation materials and frequently asked 
questions on object-oriented programming, database management systems, operating 
systems, data structures and algorithms, and popular puzzles. 
 
 
In Citi, the right attitude matters more than just technical skills. Be confident and 
demonstrate a willingness to learn and admit when you don't know something. Instead of 
simply saying "I don't know" to unknown questions in an interview, try to respond in a 
constructive manner. It's beneficial to have some knowledge about the company, the 
domain, and the role you are interviewing for at Citi. 
 
 
Lastly, it's worth noting that Citi is considered one of the easier companies to secure an 
internship with. They don't solely focus on technical skills. Stay confident and relaxed 
throughout the process. 
 
Don’t worry too much about the intern drive or give up in the middle. Sometimes luck plays a crucial 
role, and you will land up in a better place at right moment. All the best!!

Question 1 – Dataset Cleaning Cost You are given a string dataset of even length 
consisting of lowercase English letters. You can remove any two characters in one 
operation: 
If both are the same → cost = matchCost
If different → cost = mismatchCost 
Your goal is to remove all characters while minimizing the total cost.
Testcase 1:
dataset = "ouio", matchCost = 2, mismatchCost = 4
Here the matchCost is lower than mismatchcost so need to match as many chars as possible
'o' - 'o' got matched cost=2  'u','i' left in the string
'u' - 'i' got mistmatched so cost=cost+mismatchcost=2+4=6 string is now empty so return 6
Testcase 2:
s = "ouio", matchCost = 6, mismatchCost = 4 
Here the matchcost> mismatchCost so try to mismatch chars as possible to reduce the cost
'o'-'u' cost=4
'i'-'o' cost=cost+mistmatch=4+4=8 so return 8
Testcase 3:
s='ooouio' matchcost=6 mismatchcost=4
Here we have to match a 'o'-'o' no other way so ans would 14
From the question I figured out we are trying out different possiblities and choosing 
the minCost, So figured it as a Dp problem and coded a soln that passed 13/15 Testcases.
Question2 - You have a pipeline string and a failedService character.
Each second, every occurrence of failedService removes its preceding character (if any).
All removals happen simultaneously.
Continue until no more removals possible.
Output = number of seconds until stabilization.
TestCase 1:
pipeline = "database", failedService = 'a'
At second 1:'a' preceding chars are removed after that pipeline="aaase"
At second 2:'a' preceding chars are removed pipeline="ase"
now 'a' got no preceding so the string got stablished so return the no seconds which is 2
I coded a O(n*m) soln which eventually got time limit execeeded for the last testcase.
n - size of the string
m - no of seconds

The 2nd question was a LinkedList question were I was asked Reorder the List.
I gave away 3 different approaches:
  1. Creating a new list with the same copy of value and reversing it, then 
  merging one from the given list and another from the reversed list.
class Solution {
public:
    void reorderList(ListNode* head) {  //I Coded the soln in paper
        ListNode* temp=head;
        ListNode* revList=new ListNode(0);
        ListNode* temp1=revList;
        int n=0;
        while(temp){
            n++;
            temp1->next=new ListNode(temp->val);
            temp1=temp1->next;
            temp=temp->next;
        }
        revList=reverse(revList->next);
        ListNode* ans=new ListNode(0);
        temp=head;temp1=ans;
        for(int i=0;i<n;i++){
            if(i>=ceil((double)n/2)){
                break;
            }
            temp1->next=temp;
            temp=temp->next;
            temp1=temp1->next;
            if((n & 1 && i!=n/2) || (n%2==0)){
            temp1->next=revList;
            revList=revList->next;
            temp1=temp1->next;
            }
            
        }
        temp1->next=NULL;
        head=ans->next;
    }
    ListNode* reverse(ListNode* p){
        ListNode *q=nullptr,*r;
        while(p){
            r=q;
            q=p;
            p=p->next;
            q->next=r;
Amazon SDE Intern(6M) Experience
4
        }
        return q;
    }
};
Approach 2: Instead of creating a new list I gave an approach using stack which will 
store the list in reverse order which is also of time complexity of O(n) and 
space complexity of O(n).
Approach 3: I gave an In-place technique where I use constant space.
Using slow-fast pointer technique I will find the middle of the list and reverse the 
2nd part alone.
Then picking one from the start and another from the reversed list and merging it.
TC:O(n) SC:O(1)

Tekion FTE Interview Experience
Intro:
-> Tekion is a software development company that develops business
applications for automobile industries and dealers.
-> For our batch(2019 - 2023) Placements started by 2nd - 3rd week of August.
-> Tekion came as the 15th company for our Placements and I had one Online
Round and 3 technical rounds and one HM discussion and finally HR round.
Online Round: - hackerearth platform
We had 5 coding questions.
1) Auto Suggest - (Edit Distance ,Levenshtein Distance) - 50 marks
2) URL checking - 20 marks
3) How many patterns - 20 marks
You are given a tree with N nodes. Only one of the nodes In the
tree is painted black and the rest are painted white. You can paint any white node as
black if it has an edge connecting a black-painted node.
Determine the count of distinct painted trees we can obtain by painting some of
the white nodes as black following the condition. Two trees are considered painted
differently if there is at least one node painted black in one tree and white in the other.
4) Triplets Count with modification - 50 marks
5) A Remainder Problem - 100 marks
You are given a large number N in the form of a string. An integer K
and Q queries are also given.
For each query, you are required to determine whether such
subsequences of N exist and following the provided condition:
When the number formed by concatenating the digits of N
without changing relative ordering is divided by K then it leaves the remainder X(each
number in the queries array).
If you can choose a subsequence then the answer for that
query(X) is “Yes” .
Else the answer is “No” for that query(X).
Example:
Input : N = 25 ; K = 5 ; Q = 2 ; queries = {0,1}
Output : [“yes”, “no”]
There is a subsequence “5” when divided by 5 gives remainder as 0(X).
So, “Yes” for the 1st query.
There is no any subsequence when divided by 5 gives remainder as 2(X).
So, “No” for the 2nd query.
For this bruteforce solution i.e generating all subsequences and checking
won’t work since the maximum length of the given string ‘N’ is very very large.
Technical Interview Round - 1:  Face-to-Face @CUIC
35 students were shortlisted for the interview Round - 1.
-> This round was more like a discussion.
-> Firstly, the interviewer asked me to write a code for merge sort.
-> Then he asked me to reverse the given number without converting it to a string
or an array. ex) n = 34561   rev(n) = 16543.
-> Then he asked a problem in array, the problem statement goes like this,
Given an array of even length. We can add any number of pairs (n,n) at
any place in the array(but two n’s should be added continuously) such that the
array must be splitted into two equal parts(0 to n/2 and n/2 to n). We must find the
minimum number of such pairs, if it is not possible to do so then, return -1.
eg) arr = [1, 2, 2, 1]
Here we can add (1,1) at the 3rd position so that arr we become like,
[1, 2, 1, 1, 2, 1] => now first part (1 2 1)and second part(1 2 1) of the array
is equal, so our answer is 2
eg) arr = [3, 4, 2, 1] here it is not possible, so answer = -1.
-> Then he asked me about the balanced parenthesis problem.
-> Then he asked me about indexing in DBMS and asked how it is implemented?
I explained about indexing and B+ trees.
-> Then he asked what I did in my internship?
Technical Interview Round - 2:
20 students were shortlisted for the interview Round - 2.
-> This round was fully based on my projects.
-> I had put one DBMS and one ML and one Intern project in my resume.
-> I explained those 3 projects.
-> Then the interviewer asked what could I add more in the those projects and
how                             could it be implemented.
-> Then he asked many questions from backend and Database since he is from
the backend team.
-> How to overcome sql injection attacks?
-> How is the user authenticated and where are the authenticated details
stored?
-> How I stored the passwords in my Databases.
-> Then many follow up questions in backend and Databases.
Technical Interview Round - 3:(only few people had this round)
-> This round was very easy compared to the previous 2 rounds.
-> This round was a DSA + technical subjects discussion round.
-> He asked me to reverse a linked list using recursion and write pseudo code.
-> He asked what DFS and BFS were and asked me to perform inorder, preorder
and postorder traversals.
-> He asked me about the Round-Robin Scheduling algorithm.
-> He asked about different keys  present in the Database tables.
-> Then He asked whether I had some questions for him.
HM Discussion:
->This round was a discussion about what we will be doing in tekion.
-> Two people jointly attended this discussion.
-> If we had any doubts or questions about our role, we could ask them
to the interviewer( he was the head of the backend team).
-> This round was short and went for around 15 minutes.
HR Round:
-> The interviewer asked me to introduce myself.
-> Then she asked where my native place was located and then she
asked about my family background.
-> Then she asked about my strengths and weaknesses and why I call
them my strengths.
-> Then there was a one behaviour question like “You have an offer from 2
companies, one is from tekion and another from another company, Apart from money
what factors will you consider?”
-> Then she asked whether I had any questions for her.
Learnings and Takeaways:
-> Keep your expectations to zero.
-> Stick to your strengths and reduce your weaknesses.
-> Minimise your negatives and mistakes done in your previous
interviews and online rounds and try to solve those questions for which you did not find
the solution.
-> Have good knowledge in DSA, DBMS, OS,OOPS.
-> Before Any interview, revise all sorting algorithms(imp : merge sort ),
OOPS concepts, SQL queries, DBMS interview questions, OS interview questions.
-> Ask out for help from your seniors and friends when you get stuck( not
during the test :) ).
-> Interviewer will also help you when you get stuck.
-> Prepare answers for some standard HR questions and prepare some
questions to ask them to the interviewer.
Finally 12 people were placed in tekion including myself.

My SAP LABS Interview Experience: 
About SAP LABS: 
SAP (Systems, Applications, and Products in Data Processing) is a global leader 
in enterprise software, best known for its ERP solutions that help businesses 
integrate and manage core processes like finance, supply chain, HR, and 
customer service. Headquartered in Germany, SAP operates in over 180 
countries and enables organizations to run efficiently, sustainably, and 
intelligently 
Role:Developer Associate 
 
 
1st Round – Online Test 
The first round was an online coding assessment consisting of two questions: 
1. An array-based problem 
2. A tree-based problem 
Both were of easy to medium difficulty level. 
 
2nd Round – Technical Interview (Round 1) 
From the test, about 90 candidates were shortlisted for the interviews. 
My slot was late in the day, and I was called around 4:20 PM. The panel had 
two members. 
Flow of the Interview 
• Self-Introduction 
• Project Discussion 
o Explained my project. 
o Questions on which data structures I used and why. 
o Asked to normalize an ER diagram from my DBMS project. 
• DSA Questions: 
1. 
Merge point in a linked list 
2. 
Most frequent letter in a word 
• Puzzle: 8-ball puzzle 
• Club Activities: Questions about my involvement 
• SQL Queries: 
1. 
Using subquery 
2. 
Using joins 
• My Questions to Panel: 
o What are the skills an intern can learn at SAP? 
o How does a regular day look like at SAP? 
  Duration: ~50 minutes 
Takeaways: 
• Communicate with clarity and confidence. 
• Keep answers simple; don’t exaggerate (interviewers ask follow-ups 
based on your words). 
• Be thorough with your projects, as most questions revolve around them. 
 
3rd & 4th Rounds – Technical + Behavioral Combined 
Since my previous round ended late, my second technical and behavioral 
rounds were combined into one long session (~90 minutes). 
Panel 
• Senior Architect 
• Manager 
Flow of the Interview 
• Self-Introduction 
• Project Discussion 
o Explained workflow and security aspects. 
o Asked to draw and normalize an ER diagram for another project 
(not the one I had prepared). 
• Technical Questions: 
o API-related concepts 
o One SQL query 
o DoS attack 
o SQL Injection 
o OOPs concepts (4 pillars) 
o Java (multithreading, garbage collection) 
o Indexing 
o Deadlock 
• Behavioral Questions (by Manager): 
o “You say you’re a quick learner and adaptable—can you share a 
situation to prove this?” 
o “Tell me about your journey so far.” 
o Teamwork-related questions 
o Work-life balance questions 
Takeaways: 
• Technical: Strong grasp of core concepts (DBMS – especially 
normalization, CN, OOPs, SQL). 
• Behavioral: Be honest and always back answers with real-life examples. 
 
5th Round – HR Interview 
The final round was the HR round, which lasted about 20 minutes. 
Flow of the Interview 
• Self-introduction 
• Questions about extra-curricular activities 
• “Why SAP?” 
• “What is SAP?” 
• Discussion on package 
• “What will you do if we don’t select you?” 
• Since I mentioned reading books, he asked: “What was the last book you 
read?” 
The HR ended with a friendly note: “Let’s cross our fingers and hope for the 
best.” 
Takeaways: 
• Be genuine and positive in your responses. 
 
Final Result 
Out of 90 candidates, only 10 were selected—and I’m grateful to be one of 
them.   
 
Overall Key Learnings 
1. Confidence + Communication are as important as technical skills. 
2. Don’t overcomplicate or exaggerate—simple and clear answers work 
best. 
3. Be well-prepared with your projects and core subjects (DBMS, OOPs, 
SQL, CN, DSA). 
4. For behavioral rounds, answer honestly with examples from your 
experiences.

Tekion 
 
 
Tekion is a cloud-built platform focussed on transforming consumer experience in the 
automotive industry. The company uses advanced machine learning and AI capabilities for a

Selection Process consisted of: 
 
Round 1 - Online Test 
• MCQ section- Around 15 or 20 questions related to data structures, DBMS, loop outputs and 
other topics 
• Coding section consisted of one easy question, two medium and one hard. 
Below were my questions: 
➢ Easy- Given an array of nine stocks, return the largest number of stocks lesser than or 
equal to a number ‘n’. 
➢ Medium 1- Given an integer ‘n’ and two arrays, check how many elements of second 
array is present in first array. If number of common elements less than ‘n’, return -1, 
else return the maximum number of common elements. 
➢ Medium 2- Given two strings, how many characters needed to make a palindrome 
with the second string (substrings from the first string can be used to make 
palindrome)? 
➢ Hard- Given a number ‘n’ and two strings, if a value equal to or less than ‘n’ is added 
to each letter of the first string, is it possible to obtain the second string? 
I solved 7/10 test cases for the easy question and 2/10 test cases for the hard question. 
 
Round 2 - Technical Interview I 
• What is Polymorphism? 
• Difference between runtime polymorphism and compile time polymorphism. 
• Implement two stacks using a single array. 
• Implement three stacks using a single array. (I didn’t know the answer) 
• To code: 1. Implement stack 2. Given an integer n, find pairs of numbers in a sorted array 
equal to ‘n’ 
Language used:  C++. 
 
Round 3 - Technical Interview II 
• What is Paging? 
• What is Indexing? 
• What is Hashing? 
• What is an exception? What are the types of exceptions? 
• What is a Singleton Class? 
• To code: 1. Given linked list, point to nth last element. 2. Map data structure related question 
3. Singleton class 4. Exception example 
I coded the first two questions in C++. Interviewer asked if I was comfortable with Java. As 
I agreed, the interviewer asked me to code the next two questions using Java. 
 
Round 4: Managerial Interview 
This was an informal round and only consisted of questions related to my resume. 
The interviewer asked me about the projects mentioned in my resume, the programming languages I 
knew and about the certificates I obtained through online courses. 
  
 
Round 5: HR Interview 
This was also an informal round. Following questions were asked: 
• What are your strengths and weaknesses? 
• What was your favourite subject in school? 
• If not in this field, which profession would you enjoy to work in? 
• What are your hobbies? 
 
Note: All above interviews began with the question ‘Introduce yourself’. (Helpful tip: Prepare your 
introduction in advance. Try to mention points not already present in your resume like technical fields 
you are interested in and in case you have done an internship, what you learnt during that internship. 
Being well-prepared with your introduction will give you a huge confidence boost and will set a great 
tone for the rest of your interview.) 
 
Final Result: Selected :D 
 
Topics to be focussed: 
Data structures 
Object Oriented Programming 
Database Management Systems 
 
Java Basics 
 
What helped me through the placement process: 
• Keeping a positive attitude- Even if you don’t get shortlisted or don’t make it through the 
interviews, always remember these placement tests provide you invaluable experience in the 
form of type of questions asked in job interviews and knowledge of what companies are 
looking for. Know that the effort you are making is worth it and let this keep you motivated 
throughout the placement process.   
• Learning from mistakes- If there is a question you couldn’t answer in a test or interview, going 
back to it later and understanding where you went wrong ensures you don’t make the same 
mistake twice.  
 
Hope this helps  
 
 
All the Best!

About Tekion

Tekion  Corp  is  a  cloud  technology  company  founded  in  2016  by  former  Tesla  CIO  Jay
Vijayan.  The  company  is  headquartered  in  Pleasanton,  California,  with  a major technology
center in Chennai, India.

The  platform,  Automotive  Retail  Cloud  (ARC),  is  an  end-to-end,  cloud-native  solution
designed for automotive dealerships. ARC integrates multiple modules such as CRM, DMS,
digital  retail,  service  management,  analytics,  and  AI-powered  insights.  Tekion  also  offers
Automotive  Enterprise Cloud (AEC), which connects OEMs and dealerships through digital
tools like configurators, online payments, and advanced analytics.

My Interview Process and Experience

The  Tekion  recruitment  journey  was  a  well-structured  process  spread  across  multiple  rounds,  each
designed to evaluate not just my technical skills but also my ability to think critically, solve problems
efficiently, and stay composed under pressure.

Round 1: Online Coding Round

●  10 MCQs covering DSA, DBMS, and Operating Systems.
●  1 SQL query to test database knowledge.
●  1 medium-level DSA coding problem.

From this round, I was shortlisted for the next stage.

INTERVIEW DAY

The  big  day  started  with  a  surprise—two  consecutive  coding  rounds that turned out to be
elimination  stages.  These  rounds pushed us to prove our skills in both frontend design and
backend logic, highlighting our ability to think fast and stay focused.

 Round 2: Coding Round - 1 at CUIC

This was the first round on the interview day. It had three parts:

●  A frontend-based task.
●  One medium-level DSA coding question.
●  One hard-level DSA problem.

Around 20 candidates (including me) were shortlisted from this stage.

Round 3: Coding Round - 2 at CUIC

This round tested my ability to write efficient code and reduce time complexity. I solved:

●  A string-based DSA problem. Similar to leetcode problem:
https://leetcode.com/problems/word-break/description/

●  Another problem where my first solution used arrays, but I realized it was

inefficient. To optimize, I restructured my logic using a queue, which reduced the
overall time complexity and made the solution much faster.

From here, 10 candidates moved ahead, and I was one of them.

Round 4: Technical Interview

The technical round was conducted by two interviewers and focused on testing both my core
fundamentals as well as the depth of my project knowledge.

●  Frontend related questions.
●  Code snippet analysis – explain the output and logic.
●  C++ and Java concepts-based questions.
●  Projects  mentioned  in  my  resume  –  with  focus  on  APIs,  tech  stack,  and

implementation.

●  Questions about my certifications and learnings.

This  stage  was  very  interactive  and  practical.  Out  of  10,  5  candidates  including  me were
shortlisted for the final HR discussion.

Round 5: HR Interview

The  HR  discussion  was  more  about  my  personality,  career  goals,  and  motivation  to  join
Tekion. Questions included:

 ●  Self-introduction.
●  What do you know about Tekion?
●  Why do you want to join Tekion?
●  Behavioral questions like:

○  Importance of mentorship.
○  My perspective on teamwork and team spirit.

At  the  end  of  the  day,  the  selected  candidates  were  announced.  I  was  among  them  and
received the offer.

I received:

●  A 6-month internship.
●  Conversion to Full-Time Employment (FTE) upon successful completion.

Key Suggestions and Tips from My Experience

Preparation

●  Don’t lose hope – be confident in what you know.
●  Be the best in the areas you’re strong at and highlight them.
●  Practice LeetCode problems regularly.
●  Revise last-minute concepts from GeeksforGeeks and other online resources for a

quick brush-up.

●  Balance coding with subject knowledge – parallel preparation in core subjects like

DBMS, OS, and Computer Networks is equally important.

Interview Strategy

●  Be  very  clear  about  everything  you  include  in  your  resume  –  expect  questions  on

each point.

●  Answer in crisp, concise words.
●  Using the right technical keywords is more impactful than long explanations.
●  Stay confident and structured in your responses.

About Tekion

Tekion is a cutting-edge technology company that provides innovative solutions for the

1. Online Assessment (Round 1)

·
·

The online assessment was conducted on HackerRank.
It contained a total of 12 questions:

o 10 Technical MCQs.

o 1 SQL Query (easy-level).

o 1 DSA Problem (coding).

DSA Question Asked

Problem Statement: You are given an integer array arr[] of size n and an integer k. Your task is
to find the minimum length subarray of arr that contains all k consecutive integers (numbers
forming a continuous range of length k).

The subarray does not need to have elements in sorted order. If no such subarray exists, return
-1.

Example:

Input: arr = [7, 3, 5, 6, 4, 9]
k = 3

Explanation:

- Subarray [3,5,6,4] → contains {3,4,5,6} → includes {3,4,5} → length = 4
- Subarray [5,6,4] → contains {4,5,6} → ✅ valid, length = 3 (minimum so far)
- Subarray [7,3,5,6,4] → contains {3,4,5,6,7} → length = 5

Minimum length = 3

______________________________________________________________________________

 2. Interview at CUIC

______________________________________________________________________________

i) Offline Coding Round 1

Frontend Section: - Task: Create a dynamic form builder using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript.
- Focus: DOM manipulation, event handling.

Backend Section: - Two coding problems:

1. Buy and Sell Stock II (LeetCode): Find maximum profit with unlimited transactions

using a greedy approach.

2. User Activity Log Analysis: Two files (Day 1 and Day 2) with timestamped user visits.
Retrieve users visiting two different pages on both days. Initially solved with Python file
handling, then re-implemented using CSV format.

______________________________________________________________________________

ii) Offline Coding Round 2 (DSA Focused)

1. Word Break II (LeetCode) - Input: string s and wordDict. Return all sentences by inserting
spaces so every word exists in wordDict. Words can be reused. - Example: s = "catsanddog",
wordDict = ["cat","cats","and","sand","dog"] → Output: ["cats and dog","cat sand dog"] - Solved using
recursion with backtracking.

2. Longest String with Limited Character Repetition - Input: integers a, b, c for counts of ‘a’, ‘b’,
‘c’. Construct the longest string without three consecutive identical letters. - Example: a = 1, b =
1, c = 7 → Output: ccbccacc - Solved using a priority queue (max-heap) strategy.

 · Burn one rope from both ends. When it finishes, exactly half of the other rope

AMAZON – INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
About the Company: 
Amazon is one of the world’s largest and most innovative multinational 
technology companies. With a strong customer-first approach, Amazon is 
renowned for revolutionizing online shopping through services like fast delivery, 
Amazon Prime, and Alexa-enabled smart devices. Its diverse business operations 
and commitment to innovation make it a dream workplace for professionals 
worldwide, offering unparalleled learning opportunities and a dynamic work 
environment. 
Role: Support Engineer 
Stipend : 50,000 + 400 USD per month 
During the month of November, Amazon conducted a hiring drive for the role of 
Support Engineer. It was the second time after August that Amazon conducted a 
drive, having previously recruited candidates for the role of Software 
Development Engineer. 
Overall Process: 
Online Test, Technical Interview I, Technical Interview II 
Online Test: 
69 of us who applied were allowed to sit for the online test comprising 14 
sections covering DBMS, OOPS, Programming, Networking, Error 
Handling, Software Engineering, Data Visualization, and more. It was all in 
MCQ format. We had one DSA question to solve (easy-level question). 
Technical Interview I: 
17 of us made it to Round 1, i.e., Technical Interview I. 
The virtual interview happened on the AWS Chime platform, and I had two 
interviewers. 
 Initially, they asked me to introduce myself. 
 In my resume, I had projects in Machine Learning, Full Stack 
Development, and Front-End Development. 
Question 2: You have done projects in several domains. How did you manage 
this? 
I answered: 
“Yes, sir, of course, we developed projects in several domains. The thing is, we 
would focus on a specific domain during each semester. For example, during 
our second semester, we focused on front-end development. In our fourth 
semester, we focused on back-end development and the database part. So, it was 
like a cumulative process, building on everything we learned. Therefore, it 
wasn’t that hard.” 
 Question 3: Explain your Machine Learning project. 
(Note: In all three interviews I attended, the interviewers never skipped 
asking about the ML project. Since AI/ML is everywhere, be thorough with 
your ML project if you include one in your resume.) 
I explained my project, Android Malware Detection System, and the 
algorithm I used. 
 Question 4: What was the reason behind choosing this specific project? 
What was the problem statement you had in mind? 
I answered with a real-life example I had faced. 
They tested my knowledge in DBMS asking 
How will you apply any condition in a query ? 
What keyword will be used to fetch unique values from a column ? 
Next, they moved on to DSA. 
A live coding link was shared where the interviewer and I could view and edit on 
the same platform. 
 
DSA Question 1: How will you find the occurrence of a character in a given 
string? 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/program-count-occurrence-given-character-
string/ 
I explained the approach using a hash map. They then asked me to code it (the 
logic function alone). Afterward, I dry-ran the code with an example and tested it 
against a test case. 
Even though the time complexity was O(N), the interviewer asked me to reduce 
it to O(N/2). At that moment, I came up with the idea of using a two-pointer 
approach along with a hash map. The interviewer was satisfied. 
 Scenario-based Question: 
What if I want the same alphabet in upper or lower case to be considered 
the same (e.g., ‘a’ and ‘A’)? How will you modify your code? 
I suggested converting all the characters in the string to a specific case 
(either upper or lower) before counting occurrences. 
DSA Question 2: Given an array, place the odd-indexed elements first and the 
even-indexed elements next. 
I gave an approach with O(N) complexity, typed the logic, and dry-ran it. 
Finally, they asked me if I had any questions for them. I asked for feedback on 
my performance, and they provided some. The two interviewers were very 
encouraging and appreciated every answer I gave. The process was smooth, and 
the interview lasted around an hour. 
Key Takeaways: 
 Practice DSA. 
 While coding, explain your approach. 
 Take care of edge cases. 
 
Technical Interview II: 
9 of us made it to Round II (Technical Interview II). 
Like Round 1, this interview happened on AWS Chime with a live coding link. 
Introduction: 
I introduced myself, and the interviewers introduced themselves. 
DSA Questions: 
1. Find the missing number. 
https://leetcode.com/problems/missing-number/ 
2. Loop in a linked list. 
https://leetcode.com/problems/linked-list-cycle/ 
I was asked to code and dry-run these with a test case. I solved and explained 
both questions. 
Linux Commands: 
1. How will you find a specific word in a given text file? 
2. What is the command used to edit a text file? 
3. One more similar question. 
I couldn’t answer all three correctly, but I attempted to think of and try 
something. 
OS Questions: 
1. What is a deadlock? What are the necessary conditions for a deadlock? 
2. Explain CPU scheduling algorithms with examples. 
I took four processes, assumed their arrival time and burst time, and 
explained how SJF (Shortest Job First), SRJF (Shortest Remaining Job 
First), RR (Round Robin), PS (Priority Scheduling), and FCFS (First Come 
First Serve) would work. 
Project Questions: 
They asked me to explain a project close to my heart, the tech stack used, and 
where JavaScript was used in my project and why. 
The interview lasted just half an hour and happened on November 14th. 
Key Takeaways: 
Be confident. 
It’s not about answering all the questions right. Give what you can. 
As I said , Virtual Interview happened on November 14th, 
All my friends departed for our national industrial visit (IV). I stayed back in 
college to attend the virtual interview, while a friend of mine attended an in-
person interview on campus the same day. After completing the interview, four 
of us somehow caught up with our friends in Bangalore and managed to join the 
IV. 
On the last day of the IV (November 18th), I got the result: 
Three of us, two from Computer Science and one from Information Technology, 
got selected. 
I was one of the two Computer Science candidates. 
 
 
A Personal Note: 
I am not a pro coder or anything, and I come from a bio-math background in 
HSE. I joined college with no prior knowledge of what Computer Science 
actually was. Of course, I felt inferior at times, thinking, “Coding isn’t meant for 
us.” But I realized that Computer Science isn’t just about typing and printing 
something on the screen. It’s about understanding the things that happen behind 
the scenes. It’s not merely about coding; it’s about understanding the concepts. 
Best of Luck! 
Resources I Followed: 
 DSA: Striver’s SDE Sheet, Nikhil Lohia’s and Striver’s YouTube videos 
(Personal suggestion : Nikhil Lohia’s explanations are top-notch). 
https://takeuforward.org/interviews/strivers-sde-sheet-top-coding-interview-
problems/ 
https://www.youtube.com/@nikoo28/featured 
 OS, DBMS, OOPS, C++: Career Ride YouTube channel. Their 20–25 
minute videos cover every important topic. You can still explore those 
topics in-depth. https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=careerride 
 CN, Linux: GFG, Interview Bit, and similar online platforms. 
Feel Free to reach out if you have any queries 
Email : tharaniarumugam514@gmail.com 
Phone: 6382033914 
LinkedIn : https://www.linkedin.com/in/tharani-
arumugam?utm_source=share&utm_campaign=share_via&utm_content=profile
&utm_medium=android_app

text:
Walmart Global Tech Placement Interview Experience 
Role: SDE II 
Number of Rounds: 4 (1 online + 3 offline) 
Job Location: Chennai/Bengaluru 
Job Description: Walmart Global Tech is the technology wing of Walmart 
corporation. You will work on multiple domains like multichannel supply chain to 
complex retail systems in the point of sale, finance, logistics and transportation 
domains. You'll create game-changing solutions / applications using cutting-edge 
technology and streamline process through innovation. We're constantly reinventing 
how we save people's money in our retail stores, online and through their mobile 
devices, and we do it in an unexpected way. Whether you're analyzing data, 
simplifying the supply chain or creating solutions to make this planet more 
sustainable, what you'll be really doing is redefining the customer experience and 
making lives better. You get to work on a wide spectrum of technologies and 
heterogeneous platforms such as Java, Hadoop, Cassandra, ERP, Middleware tools, 
low latency messaging systems, machine learning and open-source frameworks 
 
Online Round:  
Platform: HirePro 
MCQs: 25 MCQs from OS, DBMS and OOPs 
Difficulty Level: Easy 
Coding Questions: 2 coding questions were asked. 
1. A string problem where given a sentence move vowels forward that are 
followed by a consonant. Don’t do anything for vowel followed by vowel, 
or consonant followed by consonant or consonant followed by vowel.  
And remove all spaces and special characters after moving of vowels. 
 
Sample input: "hello world! how are y#ou2" 
Sample output: hlleowrldohworaeyou2 
 
2. Given a list of integers nums, return a list of integers representing the no. 
of the factors of the each of the element in nums. 
Totally 14 candidates were selected for next round. 
 
Technical Round 1 (1hour): 
Difficulty Level: Medium 
I wasn’t asked any DSA questions in this round. The interviewer was very interested in 
my resume, and asked me questions about the skills I had mentioned in my resume. 
Since I had written Java as a skill, I was asked to write a simple API endpoint which 
fetches records from a DB. I was asked about my Internship experience, and what I 
had learnt. Java is not compulsory, but knowing it really helped me through this 
round, as I was asked lot of questions on Java. Answer honestly about your 
experiences as you will be tested on it.  
10 people were shortlisted from this round. 
 
Technical Round 2 (1hour): 
Difficult Level: Medium 
In this round I was asked to solve 3 DSA problems. 
1. Grouping Anagrams: https://leetcode.com/problems/group-
anagrams/description/ 
2. Given a big number, find the max product of k consecutive digits. Return the 
digits. 
Sample input: number = 567893 
 
 
 
K = 3 
Sample output: [7,8,9] 
3. Kth smallest element in a BST: https://leetcode.com/problems/kth-smallest-
element-in-a-bst/description/ 
 
Give the brute-force method first, then give optimized solution. It is done on pen and 
paper, so you just have to explain your solution as the interviewer wants to see how 
you solve problems. And be able to calculate time and space complexities. 
8 people were shortlisted after this round. 
 
HR Round (10-15 minutes): 
In this round I was asked about my projects, and why I chose to do these projects. 
Then I was asked what do I know about Walmart, and why do I want to work at 
Walmart. 
This round is your chance to show the interviewer that you are really passionate 
about technology and explain your projects and your achievements. 
Finally, 5 people were selected. 
 My advice: 
• Have a very good resume, use standard LaTex templates. Select your best 
projects. Include all the skills you have acquired. 
• Knowing Java is not compulsory but will be an advantage. I was asked lots of 
questions on Java, since I had mentioned it as my skill. 
• Be confident and comfortable in your answers. Don’t exaggerate your skills 
and be modest. 
• Showcase all your interests in technology. 
• Be good at DSA and calculating time and space complexities. Give all possible 
solutions you could think of during DSA round, even if its not optimized. You 
can discuss with interviewer about different approaches you could think of, 
and which you think is the best solution. 
Contact Info: 
Phone: 9030269853 
Email: rstharun11@gmail.com 
LinkedIn: https://www.linkedin.com/in/tharun-r-s-3b8985229/

EZHIL OVIYA C - Information Technology – TE Placement in Thirdwave corporation, japan

**About the Company**:

ThirdWave Corporation, based in Japan, is a renowned technology-driven company focused on delivering cutting-edge solutions in embedded systems, hardware-software co-design, and semiconductor technologies. The company emphasizes innovation, cross-cultural collaboration, and technical excellence. With a strong reputation in the tech space, ThirdWave offers a unique environment to work on real-world applications that drive global impact.

**About the Role**:

I got placed at ThirdWave Corporation, Japan through the college’s campus placements. The opportunity was open to students from the CS, IT, and MCA departments. I have been selected for the position of Technology Engineer, a role that involves working on innovative tech-driven solutions and participating in core engineering activities. ThirdWave are actually starting up their AI labs so it’s a fresh start. As a Technology Engineer, I will be expected to contribute to the development, testing, and optimization of high-performance systems, which may include areas like embedded systems, digital design, and hardware-software integration. The role is a perfect blend of research and development, technical problem-solving, and hands-on implementation, offering immense learning and growth potential in cutting-edge technology domains. It also opens up avenues to collaborate with global teams and be part of a culture that emphasizes precision, innovation, and continuous improvement.

**Overview of the Process:**

The overall process consisted of

* Resume Shortlisting
* Interview Round (Based on Resume and General HR) – in CUIC

Domains offered:

Technology Engineer – AI domain

**Interview Round (Based on Resume and General HR):**

This round was purely based on our Resume and General HR questions. The questions were general. The panel consisted of 3 members and a translator to translate between Japanese and English, and there were two panels. This round went for like 45 minutes to an hour, since there were only two panels and four of the candidates were interviewed at a time.

* Tell me about yourself.
* They had asked us explain our projects, the tech stacks used in it, etc.
* What challenges did you face while working on your projects?
* What role did you undertake in the projects?
* Why did you choose ThirdWave?
* Any questions for the panel about the company?

**TRICKS THAT I FOLLOWED**:

* Do Update your resume regularly according to the job description and always be thorough at what you’re mentioning in your resume.
* Don’t put anything that you don’t know in the resume.
* You should be in the position to be able to answer any questions based on your resume.
* Polish your communication skills well as most companies do prefer that sometimes more than our experience.
* I listened to the PPT which actually helped me to get a view of what they do in ThirdWave. This is my advice, if you are selected for the interview, do gather info about the company and recall whatever told in the PPT. Use that knowledge for answering questions about the company and why you’re interested in working there.
* In every round, they’ll ask “Do you have any questions for us?” as the last question.
  Do ask a lot of reasonable questions,
  about the company,
  tech stacks used,
  kind of projects that we do in intern,
  location of work,
  Could you please tell me your experience in ThirdWave,
  Work culture
  Your questions will determine your eagerness to work in the company.
* So, if they do ask, “Any questions to us?”, do ask atleast a question about the company or the role or whatever interests you.
* Be confident and honest while presenting your work. Even if your project isn't super complex, your clarity and involvement in it will show your dedication.
* Be prepared with a good answer to “Tell me about yourself”, tailored to what the company values. Make sure to show genuine curiosity about the company.
* Read up about their work, culture, and industry standing – it helps shape your answers better.
* Don’t worry if it feels casual – interviews like these often check how well you communicate and whether you're eager to learn and grow.

**MY PREPARATION STRATEGY:**

* I began my preparation journey during my 3rd year semester holidays. Initially, it wasn’t consistent—there were a lot of distractions, procrastination, and moments of self-doubt. It was especially discouraging when many of my friends got placed during the 7th semester, and I hadn’t. That phase hit hard.
* Balancing final year project work, academics, and placement preparation at the same time was no easy task. There were times when I felt overwhelmed and emotionally drained, but one thing I never allowed myself to do was give up.
* One major lesson I learned is: never stop working on your dreams. No matter how delayed it feels, continuous preparation, especially in programming and aptitude, is crucial. If we lose touch, it becomes really hard to bounce back.
* I finally got placed just one month before the end of my 8th semester, and looking back, I realize how much persistence paid off.
* I didn’t spend much time on coding platforms like HackerRank, but I was very thorough with the fundamentals — Data Structures, OOPs, DBMS, Operating Systems, and Computer Networks. These core subjects formed the foundation of my confidence during interviews.
* I started preparing for aptitude later in the process but made sure I understood the logic behind every concept rather than memorizing tricks.
* One piece of advice I’d give: pay attention to what you learn in college. Most of the interview questions I faced were closely related to what we study in our core subjects. So don’t underestimate them—they helped me immensely.
* It was a long journey filled with ups and downs, but if there’s one thing I’d say to anyone preparing—keep moving forward, no matter how slow it feels.
* Progress is progress.
