Placement Experience-Verizon

                      -Vishal Sanjeevi AS(2022115127)-IT Purple

About the Company:

Verizon  is  a  multinational  telecommunications  conglomerate  and  one  of  the  world's  leading
providers of wireless and wireline communication services. It's a key player in the global technology
and communication industry, consistently ranking high on the Fortune 500 list, currently at #30. They
focus  on  delivering  high-quality  products  and  services  and  actively  invest  in  AI,  ML,  and  next-
generation technologies.With its corporate headquarters in New York City, Verizon is recognized as
the world's second-largest telecommunications company by revenue.

About the Role:

The role is SDE 1  (Software  Development  Engineer 1).  While  the  exact  domain was not  specified,
candidates  are  expected  to  be  open  to  work  across  multiple  areas,  including  backend,  frontend,
AI/ML, and software development.

Overview of the Process:

The overall process consisted of

●  An Online coding contest in AON Platfrom(Virtual)
●  GD followed by AI Prompting Round-in CUIC
●  Technical Interview Round– in CUIC
●  HR Round-in CUIC

Recruitment Process Experience:

The placement drive was a multi-stage process that tested various skills, from technical knowledge to
soft skills.

1. Online Test

The online test consisted of four sections.

  Essay Typing: We were given a topic and had to write an essay of 190-240 words in 25 minutes.
This tested our writing and time management skills.Topic given to me was ”Are Electric cars
the future”.

   GenAI  MCQs:  This  section  had  30  multiple-choice  questions  focused  on  Generative  AI,

including scenario-based questions. The time limit was 30 minutes.

  Coding Round(40 Minutes): There were two coding questions. The first was to determine if a
number is "googly," which is true if the sum of its digits is a prime number. The second was a
Dijkstra-based problem about  finding the  minimum  time to collect  energy  while  traversing
cities. My understanding of graph algorithms was crucial here.

  English/Vocabulary Assessment: This section had a flexible number of questions and featured
negative marking. A unique aspect was the '?' option, which you could select if you weren't
sure of an answer to avoid negative marks. This section also included simple and enjoyable
puzzles. The more correct answers you gave, the higher your level and score.

The results for the online round were announced two days after the test. Out of 322 candidates, 80
were shortlisted for the interview round.

2. Group Discussion (GD) and AI Prompting Round

The next day, we had the GD and AI prompting rounds.

  Group  Discussion:  We  were  divided  into  teams  of  9,  and  the  discussion  lasted  about  25
minutes. The topic was "Are online ways of life (online education, hybrid work, e-commerce,
etc.) better or not?" The key here was the quality of your speech and your active participation.
The  panel  judged  us  on  how  well  we  involved  ourselves  in  the  discussion  and  contributed
meaningfully, rather than just stating a couple of points and staying silent. From my panel, 7
out of 9 were selected, while other panels had a higher rejection rate.

  AI  Prompting  Round:  This  was  a  non-eliminatory  round.  We  had  to  choose  a  problem
statement  from  a  given  list,  write  a  prompt  to  solve  it  using  an  AI  tool,  and  explain  the
prompting  technique  used.  We  also  had  to  mention  any  previous  AI/ML  projects  we  had
worked on. It's important to be fully knowledgeable about what you submit in this round, as
it formed the basis for many questions in the technical interview. Having AI/ML projects on
your resume definitely adds more value.

3. Technical Interview

The technical interview lasted for about an hour. After a brief self-introduction and introduction from
the interviewer, he delved into my projects, showing a particular interest in my final year project. We
had a deep, 20-minute discussion on it, and he seemed impressed.

Next,  the  interviewer  transitioned  to  my  responses  from  the  AI  Prompting  round.  I  had  chosen  a
problem statement related to cyber security, and he asked me basic questions on the topic to gauge
my foundational knowledge. He also asked me to explain in detail why I had framed the prompt the
way  I  did  and  to  walk  him  through  my  entire  approach.  This  part  of  the  interview  reinforced  the
importance of being completely aware of what you submit in the AI prompting round.

He then moved on to DSA, asking a basic string-based palindrome question. He also asked for a dry
run of Binary Search, its time complexity, and how that complexity is derived. The same was asked for
Merge Sort. The interview then shifted to SQL. I was asked to explain all the different types of JOINS
with proper examples and to differentiate between a LEFT JOIN and an OUTER JOIN. He then gave me
a medium-level SQL question involving two tables. I couldn't provide a complete solution, but I politely
explained that I was unable to come up with one, and he was fine with it. The interview concluded
with questions about my knowledge of Verizon and my motivation for wanting to join the company. I

 asked  two  sensible,  high-quality  questions,  which  is  always  a  good  practice.  Of  the  80  shortlisted
students, 32 were selected for the HR round.

4. HR Interview

The HR interview was straightforward and lasted about 15 minutes. He asked me to briefly explain my
projects,  inquired  about my  family background,  asked  the  typical “Where  do you see yourself in 5
years?” question, my location preference and again, asked why I wanted to join Verizon. A crucial tip
here is to be flexible about location. When asked for a location preference, it's best to say you are
open to any location based on the company's requirements, as rejections can happen due to this. All
32 students who made it to the HR round were selected, and I was one of them.

MY PREPARATION STRATEGY:

I began my preparation during my 6th-semester holidays, focusing on a strategic approach to cover
essential topics. My main focus was on Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA), particularly Arrays and
Strings, as well as Greedy algorithms and Dynamic Programming (DP). I also made sure to learn the
fundamentals of Trees and Graphs.

I highly recommend utilizing Striver's A to Z DSA sheet and his video tutorials from TakeYouForward.
This  resource  proved  to  be  extremely  helpful.  For  practice,  I  focused  on  the  LeetCode  150  and
LeetCode 75 lists, which helped me build a strong problem-solving foundation. In addition to DSA, I
extensively practiced SQL queries, solving easy and medium-level questions, which gave me a lot of
confidence in this area.I learnt all the core concepts from GeekForGeeks and InterviewBit.

One of the most important aspects of preparation is maintaining a  competitive spirit.  I found that
learning  and  practicing  with  friends  was  far more  effective  than  studying  alone.  This  collaborative
environment kept me motivated and helped me improve faster. It's also worth  noting that while a
good  CGPA  is  advisable  for  eligibility,  typically  an  8.0  and  above  is  a  safe  bet,  it's  the  skills  you
demonstrate that matter most in the end.

TIPS and TRICKS:

Focus on Fundamentals: A strong foundation in Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA),
particularly Arrays and Strings, is essential as most of the companies focus on these. Practice
problems on platforms like LeetCode,Hackerrank etc.

Be a Team Player: Learning and preparing with friends fosters a competitive and supportive
environment that makes the process more enjoyable and effective.

Be Honest: If you don't know the solution to a problem, it's better to be honest and polite rather
than trying to fake it.

Ask Quality Questions: Always prepare and ask good, sensible questions to the interviewer at
the end. It shows your genuine interest in the role and the company.

Master Your Communication Skills: Strong communication is crucial. Make sure you can articulate
your  thoughts  clearly  and  confidently.  Practice  explaining  your  projects  and  problem-solving
approaches to others.

 Showcase  Flagship  Projects: Instead of  listing  every project  you've  ever worked  on, focus on your
flagship projects. These are the projects where you made a significant contribution to the real world
and that provide genuine value. Be prepared to discuss these in detail, as they will be a focal point of
your interviews.

Hope this detailed breakdown of my experience helps you in your preparation.

All the best!

TEKION INTERVIEW EXPERIENCE 
About the Company: 
Tekion, founded in 2016 by former Tesla CIO Jay Vijayan, is a fast-growing cloud 
technology company revolutionizing the automotive retail industry. Headquartered in Pleasanton, 
California with major tech hubs in India, Tekion serves over 2,000 dealerships and 52 OEM brands 
worldwide through its flagship Automotive Retail Cloud (ARC) platform. 
 
Number of Rounds: 4 
1. Online Assessment 
2. Technical Interview - 1 
3. Technical Interview - 2 
4. HR Interview 
 
Online Assessment: 
MCQ - 20 Questions (DBMS, OS, OOPS, DSA) 
DSA: 
Problem 1: 
You are given two arrays, marie and annie, which represent the face cards of two players, and a 
string flip that contains either "Even" or "Odd". The game is played in rounds, where each index i 
of the arrays represents one round. 
• 
If flip = "Even", then the players calculate their scores as: 
o Marie’s score for this round = abs(marie[i] – annie[i]) 
o Annie’s score for this round = abs(annie[i] – marie[i]) 
• 
If flip = "Odd", both players skip their cards for that round (no score is added). 
After each round, the value of flip toggles (i.e., "Even" becomes "Odd", and "Odd" becomes 
"Even"). 
Your task is to find the final score of Marie and Annie after all rounds. 
 
Example: 
Input: 
marie = [4, 5, 6, 7]   
annie = [1, 2, 3, 4]   
flip = "Even" 
Process: 
• 
Round 1 (flip = "Even"): 
Marie = |4 – 1| = 3 
Annie = |1 – 4| = 3 
• 
Round 2 (flip = "Odd"): skipped 
• 
Round 3 (flip = "Even"): 
Marie = |6 – 3| = 3 
Annie = |3 – 6| = 3 
• 
Round 4 (flip = "Odd"): skipped 
Final Scores: 
Marie = 3 + 3 = 6   
Annie = 3 + 3 = 6 
 
Problem 2: Bandwidth Allocation (0/1 Knapsack Variant) 
You are given: 
• 
An array bandwidthNeeded[ ], where bandwidthNeeded[i] represents the amount of 
bandwidth required to process the i-th process. 
• 
An array requestsProcessed[ ], where requestsProcessed[i] represents the number of 
requests that can be processed if the i-th process is allocated its required bandwidth. 
• 
An integer totalBandwidth, which represents the maximum total bandwidth available. 
You need to allocate the available bandwidth to processes in such a way that: 
• 
A process is either fully allocated its required bandwidth or not allocated at all (no partial 
allocation). 
• 
The total allocated bandwidth does not exceed totalBandwidth. 
• 
The total number of requests processed is maximized. 
Your task is to determine the maximum number of requests that can be processed with the given 
total bandwidth. 
Example 
Input: 
bandwidthNeeded = [2, 3, 4, 5]   
requestsProcessed = [3, 4, 5, 6]   
totalBandwidth = 5 
Explanation: 
• 
If we choose processes 1 and 2: bandwidth used = 2 + 3 = 5 → requests processed = 3 + 4 
= 7 
• 
If we choose processes 3: bandwidth used = 4 → requests processed = 5 
• 
If we choose process 4: bandwidth used = 5 → requests processed = 6 
The optimal allocation is processes 1 and 2 → 7 requests. 
Output: 
Maximum requests processed = 7 
We took the online assessment on the HackerRank platform at CUIC. I managed to solve the first 
question completely and the second question partially, and I did well in the MCQ section. A total of 
30 students from CSE were shortlisted for the next round. 
 
Technical Interview 1 (50 minutes) 
This round mainly focused on DSA and Core subjects. 
• 
The interviewer first went through my resume and asked about my projects and the tech 
stacks I had worked with. 
• 
Since I had mentioned NSS under campus involvements, he asked about that as well. 
• 
He then asked me about my favorite subject. I answered DBMS, so he followed up with 
several DBMS-related questions. 
 
DBMS Questions 
1. What is replication, sharding, and partitioning? 
2. What is the CAP theorem? 
3. What is a transaction? 
4. What are the ACID properties? 
5. What are the different types of joins? 
6. What is functional dependency? 
7. What is a read/write conflict? 
8. What is copy-on-write? 
DSA Problems 
He then moved on to DSA and asked me three problems: 
1. Given an array and integer k, left shift the array by k times. 
2. Find the parent node of a given node in a binary tree. 
3. Find the intersection node of two linked lists. 
I provided both brute-force and optimal approaches for all the problems and wrote the pseudo-
code as requested. The interviewer was satisfied with my solutions. 
In the middle of the interview, I was also asked to introduce myself. 
OS Questions 
Later, the interviewer asked if I was familiar with OS concepts. Since I said yes, he asked the 
following questions: 
1. What are semaphores? 
2. What is a mutex? 
3. What is a process? 
4. What is a thread? 
5. What are paging and segmentation? 
6. What is a page fault? 
7. What is thrashing? 
8. What is a deadlock? 
Finally, the interviewer asked whether I had any questions. I asked two questions in return. 
 
Technical Interview 2 (45 minutes) 
For the first 10 minutes, the interviewer introduced himself and spoke about the company. After 
that, I was asked to introduce myself. 
This round was mainly focused on projects. 
• 
He asked about my favorite project, and I explained my CIP project. 
• 
He asked several follow-up questions, such as: 
o What tech stack did I use? 
o Why did I choose that particular stack? 
o Have I worked with AWS, and what services do I know? 
• 
He also gave me a few scenarios and asked how my application would behave in those 
cases. 
• 
I was then asked to design an algorithm for a scenario related to my project and explain its 
time complexity. 
The interviewer also asked a DSA problem: 
Problem: 
Given an array of strings, remove all the strings that have a permutation of themselves present in 
the array. 
I provided both a brute-force and an optimal approach and discussed the time complexity for each. 
Towards the end, he asked a few questions from Operating Systems (OS) as well. 
Finally, the interviewer asked whether I had any questions, and I asked a couple of questions in 
return. 
 
HR Interview (15 minutes) 
This round was more of a casual conversation. 
• 
The interviewer first introduced herself. 
• 
She then asked me to introduce myself. 
• 
I was asked whether I had attended the pre-placement talk. 
• 
She asked what I think about the company and what makes me a good fit for the role. 
• 
I was also asked about the key takeaways from my projects and to explain all of them 
briefly. 
• 
She asked me to share any conflicts I had faced with my teammates and how I managed 
them. 
• 
Finally, she asked whether I had any questions, and I asked a couple in return. 
At the end of the process, 9 students from CSE were selected, and I was one of them. 
 
Key Notes: 
• 
Revise your core subjects like DBMS, OS, Computer Networks, and DSA thoroughly before 
the interview. 
• 
Practice solving coding problems and be prepared to explain both brute force and optimal 
approaches. 
• 
Always mention the time and space complexity when explaining your solutions. 
• 
Be confident in your projects and clearly explain the technologies used, design choices, and 
outcomes. 
• 
Communicate your answers in a structured and confident manner. 
• 
Prepare for behavioral questions such as teamwork, conflict management, strengths, and 
weaknesses. 
• 
Stay calm during the interview and admit honestly if you don’t know something, while trying 
to reason it out. 
• 
Show genuine interest in the company by asking thoughtful questions at the end of the 
interview.

Barclays 
Barclays is a British multinational investment bank and financial services company. Barclays is 
organized into four core businesses: personal banking, corporate banking, wealth management, 
and investment management. Barclays Global Service Centre (BGSC), India is a global 
capability center, supporting Barclays businesses worldwide, delivering innovative end-to-end 
business solutions in Technology, Operations and Functions. 
Hiring Role: Analyst 
The hiring process is divided into 3 Rounds. 
 Online Test 
 Technical +Managerial Round 
 HR Round 
Online Test: 
Duration: 1hr 30mins 
It consists of 30Mcq and 2 Coding Questions. 
It was conducted on Cocubes Platform. The MCQs had most of the weightage and topics were 
on C, C++, OOPS, Database Management Systems, Computer Networks and Operating System. 
Mostly theory questions and some output prediction question. The questions were easy to 
moderate level. 
The 2 Coding questions are: 
1) Array Based: Given an array, for example arr = [“apple”, “orange”,” apple”, “orange”, 
“grapes”] we have to print “yes” for repeated elements and “No” for non-repeated 
elements in the array. 
2) String Based: Given a string, we have to count the numbers and characters in the string. 
Both the coding questions are straightforward and is not time-consuming. 
Most of them have solved both the coding question. So, the MCQ part was the decision maker. 
Nearly 200 students appeared for the online test and 30 got shortlisted for the next round.   
Technical + Managerial Round: 
Duration: 30 mins. 
First 15 minutes for technical question and last 15 minutes for managerial question. 
It was held on office premises. We were asked to report to the company for the interview 
process.  
The interviewer had my resume and asked me the typical “tell me something about yourself”. 
Then he started asking some technical questions: 
1) Questions related to my projects which I mentioned in my resume. He asked about the 
technologies I used in it and what are the challenges I faced during the project phase. 
2) Question related to data structures and algorithms. I was given a problem and asked to 
solve it using any of the data structure with less time and space complexity. 
Then he started asking some managerial questions: 
1) Scenario Based Questions: Given a scenario like you have a dispute with your project 
mate for an idea proposal. How will you convince him to accept your idea? 
2) Behavioral Based Questions:  Given a situation how will you think and react? 
HR Round: 
Duration: 15mins 
First and Foremost, you have to know the Barclays values- RISES. The other interview 
questions were: 
1) Tell me about yourself? 
2) Why Barclays? 
3) Give some example where will you use Barclays values in Real life? 
Tips from my side: 
 Do a good research about the company and its latest news. 
 Be honest with your resume and thorough with the projects you have mentioned. 
 Always have a question at the end to ask the interviewer. 
 Be prepared with some Basic HR questions beforehand.

About Me :

month before our 3rd year starts.I followed only strivers A-Z dsa sheet and solved problems on 
my own and at the time of interview I have completed around 75% of that sheet.I used C++ for 
solving problems. That’s all about me. 
About Online Assessment and Interview:  
Goldman Sachs was the first company that visited our campus and also the first to Keep 
online assessment. 
Online Assessment: 
About OA, it was conducted on HackerRank platform. The  topics that OA had are, Output 
guessing from oops both Java and C++ , and about sorting algorithms that is basically theory 
side of dsa, and had about 3 to 5 Aptitude Questions( just maths problems that we solved 
during our school days) and one Hr question was also asked, like what you will do in this 
situation, then finally three coding questions. 
Coding Question – 1: 
It was based on sorting.  
Given an array, e.g., [12, 13, 6], sort it in ascending order according to the number of factors of 
each element in the array. The output of the question is [13,6,12].  
Explanation:  
12 → Factors: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 12 → 6 factors 
13 → Factors: 1, 13 → 2 factors 
6 → Factors: 1, 2, 3, 6 → 4 factors 
Sorting them by the number of factors (ascending order): 
13 → 2 factors 
6 → 4 factors 
12 → 6 factors 
Hence, the output is [13, 6, 12]. 
Actually this was an very very simple question, our only task was to write optimal algorithm for 
finding the No. of factors of an element. I wrote that algorithm that finds the factors in O(root 
N) time where N is the  element for which factors are need to be found.  
After writing a function for finding the No. of factors, then I used In- build Sort function in C++, 
and just passes an input array, and an Custom Comparator where the element are compared 
according to the factors. I used that factors function in this custom comparator. 
Hence, within five minutes I was able to solve this problem and all the 15 test cases passed.  
Coding Question-2:  
I don’t exactly remember the question now, It was like N organic substances will be given, 
each organic substance will have two values like growth factor and additive factor. Our goal 
was to find the minimum absolute difference Between growth factor and additive factor 
across all possible combinations of organic substances.  How the growth factor and additive 
factor was calculated for combination of organic substances is the multiplication of all the 
growth factors in that combination is the new growth factor for that whole combination and 
similarly the addition of the  additive factors in that combination is the new additive factor for 
that combination. 
 
I solved that problem using Recursion first, like just finding all the possible subsequences  
While finding itself will multiply the growth factor and add additive factor and then finding the 
minimum once end index is reached. But that solution was not accepted for some test cases. 
Then later I solved this using power set algorithm which is used for finding subsequences. 
Both take same time c but space c differs since  power set doesn’t use recursion stack space 
just uses bit manipulation and loops.  
Then for this problem also all the 15 test cases was passed after using that power set algo. 
Actually I didn’t read this question, I directly went to sample input and output and I was able 
to understand what we have to do and find.  
Anyone who solved subset sum problem can solve this problem to.  
Coding Question – 3:  
In this question , we will be given with new employees and their work experience and skill 
score as string [][3] format  like, 
E 1 2  
like this will be given in string format, [x][0] will be either E or Q, E stands for employee.  
And [x][1] ,[x][2] represents score and work exp respectively for that employee. These 
employees can be assigned ids from 1 and goes on just have to assume like that. After that, Q 
stands for query, it will be also present in that string[][3] itself, like 
E 1 2 
E 2 3 
E 3 4 
Q 2  
E 5 6  
Q 3 
This Q 2 means that the employee 2 that E 2 3 needs  a mentor for him and our task is to find 
the employee among the employees given using the criteria like, skill and exp should be 
greater than the employee who needs a mentor. If multiple employees satisfy the condition, 
return the employee who has least diff of skill score and work exp between the employee who 
needs a mentor and the employee who can be assigned as a mentor. 
Was asked to return the ans in vector of strings.  
I coded this problem for nearly 20 min and was able to pass only 7 test cases.  
Then I just submitted and started solving MCQs and aptitude questions.  
 
After few the days, the results were announced, around 28 students were selected for 
interview and I was one among them. 
 
Interview Round- 1 : 
This is my first interview, and it was held at CUIC, and I was also very nervous. But the 
interviewer spoke well and was good. 
 
First, he asked me to give a brief introduction about myself. Then he started with a basic 
aptitude math question, like when two dice that have 6 faces are rolled, what is the probability 
of the sum of the faces being prime? I wrote the sample space on the paper, I was explaining 
the answer, and he was satisfied with that answer. Then the follow-up question was, if we roll 
dice with N faces for M times, what will be the probability of the sum of faces after the dice 
are rolled for M times  being prime? I was asked to solve it in a programmatic way and was 
also asked to write the pseudocode. I wrote the code and also explained my approach. I just 
solved that problem using recursion, that is brute force, just trying out all the possible ways, 
and while finding, I would just add the sum, and after we reach M+1 times, I would stop and 
check whether the sum is prime or not. And after counting, I would just divide that count by all 
the possible combinations, which is N^M, to find the probability. I just explained this and 
wrote the pseudo code. 
 
He was satisfied with my answer. And in the follow-up question, he asked the time and space 
complexity of the code I wrote and I also said that, and he was satisfied with those answers 
too. 
 
Then he asked me whether I had any questions for him. I just asked about the tech stack they 
use, since many of our seniors also asked this same question (I read it in the placement 
experiences PDF). That’s all about round 1. Overall It went well.  
Out of 28 people, nearly 18 were eliminated in this round itself. 
Interview Round -2:  
Immediately after I had lunch around 1 o clock, I was called for Round 2. Since I had already 
attended Round 1, I was a bit more confident. 
 
First, he asked me to give a brief introduction about myself, and then he directly started diving 
into my projects. He took one of my projects and asked me to explain what we did, etc. He 
asked me to write the tables that we created in the database for that project and to explain 
the difficulties we faced, as well as how I normalized that table. I explained everything. 
After that, from one of the tables I explained, I was asked to write a query. That query was of

given. I was asked to write the pseudocode and also explain the time and space complexities. 
I did that too, and overall, he was satisfied. And I was sure that will get selected for round 3 
and also started preparing for it.  
 
 
Interview Round – 3: 
After round 2, within 10minutes I was called for next round. In this round, one mam was there, 
actually this was also an technical round , I thought It would be an HR round.  I was asked to 
explain only about the projects I did. She asked all the possible questions that can be asked

and At last she asked me whether I have any questions and I asked what is the recent 
challenge that your team faced and how you solved them. She was answering to my question,

I was asked to wait till 6.15 and after 6.15, the students who were asked to wait were called

Preparation tips: 
• I referred Abdul Bari on Udemy for theory concepts of DSA.

Accolite  
 
It consists of 5 rounds. 
Round 1: Aptitude round 
 
There were aptitude questions based on Operating Systems, Computer 
Networks, Database Management Systems, Quantitative Aptitude, Logical reasoning. A 
mix up of all these aptitudes rounding to 30 questions. This test was held on EduThrill 
platform and the total time given was around 30-40 minutes. You really need to be very 
quick in solving those quantitative aptitude which will boost your time to analyze the 
technical aptitude questions. Most of the students except a few were selected for the 
next coding round. You need to answer a reasonably correct number of questions to get 
selected to the next round. 
 
 
Round 2: Coding round 
 
In this round, 2 coding questions were asked and both of them were very easy 
ones. With a little bit of practice one could solve them both in 5-10 minutes. I would 
highly recommend to practice coding in Hackerrank platform for beginners and Geeks 
for geeks and Code-chef platform for intermediates and advanced level coders. Try 
completing the must do interview questions available in Geeks for geeks well ahead of 
the placement season. The below are the questions asked 
1. Trapping rain water  
(https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/trapping-rain-water/) 
2. Happy arrays 
(https://codechef.com/problems/HAPPYARR (Not a same question but similar to this)) 
 
Mainly the Coding round was fully focused on solving real time problems and it 
was held on Codelyzer platform. Candidates can use any language of their choice for 
solving the coding questions. Since the questions were easy many were able to solve 
them both, hence the selection criteria were based on space and time complexity and 
also the time taken by the candidate to solve the problems. The candidates selected for 
the face to face interviews were announced 2 hours post the coding round. Around 80 
were shortlisted for the next round. All the face to face interviews were held over skype. 
 
 
Round 3: Technical interview 
The interview began with formal greetings. She then asked me to introduce myself and 
had a casual talk with me to make me feel comfortable. The following questions were 
asked 
 
1. The task is to divide an array into K non-empty subsets such that the sum of 
elements in every subset is same. All elements of this array should be part of 
exactly one partition. 
 
2. The task is to find if there is a triplet in an array whose sum is equal to the given 
value. 
 
3. The task is to split the given array into K subarrays (they must cover all the 
elements). The maximum subarray sum achievable out of K subarrays formed, 
must be minimum possible. Find that possible subarray sum. 
 
4. She then tested my knowledge on binary trees by asking about the different kind 
of traversals like inorder, postorder, preorder, level order and asked me the 
difference between then with an example. 
 
The below are some links which will help you to solve those questions. 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/partition-set-k-subsets-equal-sum/ 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/split-the-given-array-into-k-sub-arrays-such-that-
maximum-sum-of-all-sub-arrays-is-minimum/ 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/find-a-triplet-that-sum-to-a-given-value/ 
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/tree-traversals-inorder-preorder-and-postorder/ 
 
WHAT I SUGGEST TO YOU FROM THIS ROUND : 
 Give a strong introduction about yourself since that is the first impression about 
you to the interviewer. 
 Take some time to analyze the problem. Break the entire problems into sub 
problems and try solving the sub problems to arrive at your answers. 
 Discuss your approach with your interviewer and make them involved in your 
approach. 
 Try cracking those corner cases first to impress your interviewer. 
 
Many were unable to crack this round due to its level of hardness but luckily I was on 
the other side. The next interview was held after 15 minutes post completion of this 
round. 
 
Round 4: Technical interview 
 
The interviewer asked me to introduce myself and I was asked questions on my 
resume. We had a long chat on my summer research intern project and then the 
interview began. 
1. Given a Binary Tree, convert it to a Doubly Linked List. The left and right pointers 
in nodes are to be used as previous and next pointers respectively in converted 
DLL. The order of nodes in DLL must be same as inorder of the given Binary 
Tree. The first node of inorder traversal (left most node in BT) must be head 
node of the DLL. 
 
Note: To be very honest, I became nervous on seeing this question but I 
didn’t give up and started to suggest some approaches. The interviewer 
then found that I was not so comfortable with this question and gave me 
another question. 
2. Find Maximum Length of a sub-string without any repetitions. 
- 
For “ABDEFGABEF”, the longest substring are “BDEFGA” and 
“DEFGAB”, with length 6. 
- 
For “BBBB” the longest substring is “B”, with length 1. 
 
3. Given a string s1 and a string s2, write a snippet to say whether s2 is a rotation 
of s1? 
(eg given s1 = ABCD and s2 = CDAB, return true, given s1 = ABCD, and s2 = 
ACBD , return false) 
 
4. Reverse a linked list in groups of given sizes 
5. A puzzle to test my problem solving capability   
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/puzzle-1000-light-bulbs-switched-on-off-by-1000-
persons-passing-by/ (not the exact one but with some modification in the 
numbers)  
 
WHAT I SUGGEST TO YOU FROM THIS ROUND : 
 Be honest with the interviewer if you are asked whether you find it difficult or not , 
be honest with him but suggest some approaches to express your “never give 
up” attitude. 
 Always try to optimize your solutions if you are asked to. Have good knowledge 
of the time and space complexities. 
 Practice a lot of puzzles and riddles as solving them show off your problem 
solving skills.  
 Always discuss your approach with the interviewer as he/she may give some 
hints to optimize your approach, never keep your interviewer idle. 
At the end of this round, the interviewer was very much impressed with my performance 
and he connected the call to his manager immediately for my HR round. 
  
Round 5: HR interview 
 
The manager asked me to introduce myself and started testing my 
communication and thinking out-the-box skills. He asked me about the Covid-19 impact 
on Tamil Nadu and questioned me on the effectiveness of the measures taken by the 
Government during this pandemic and also asked me to suggest some measures if I 
were given the authority. Then we had a chat about Indian railways giving free Wifi 
access to its passengers (good/bad/what else could be done), climate in my area of 
residence and so on. 
 
Later he questioned me on aptitudes regarding Distance/Speed and 
Time/Clocks. He even gave me two problems to solve on finding the angle between 
minute and hour hand. Later he was very satisfied and appreciated me for my 
performance. 
Finally 11 of them were selected and I got my name on that list. :) 
 
 
WHAT I SUGGEST TO YOU FROM THIS ROUND : 
 Be prepared with quantitative aptitudes as it can even help you in your HR round 
as it did in my case. I would suggest websites like Indiabix, Freshers world etc. 
 
 Even though I was not asked any formal HR questions like 1. Why should I hire 
you? 2.What are your strengths and weakness , be prepared to answer them as 
well as banking and investment sectors which are on for placements would ask 
them for sure. 
 
Topics to be focused: 
1. Data structures & Algorithms. 
2. Competitive coding/Problem solving. 
3. Object Oriented Programming Language for implementation in the interview. 
(C++/JAVA is mostly preferred by the interviewers) 
4. Projects mentioned in your resume. 
5. Aptitude (Verbal/Reasoning/Quantitative) 
6. Communication (Be prepared with your answers for famous HR questions) 
 
ALL THE BEST!!!

Citi – Intern Interview Experience 
 
 
About the company 
Citi is a global bank that offers a variety of financial services, including 
banking, credit cards, and investment solutions. It operates in more than 160 
countries and values innovation, diversity, and teamwork, creating an inclusive 
workplace where employees can grow. Citi’s mission is to help people, businesses, 
and communities achieve their financial goals. 
Role: Application Developer (App Dev) 
Stipend: 75,000 INR per month 
Duration of internship: 8-10 weeks 
 
 
Selection Process : 
The Selection process had 3 rounds. 
1. Online test 
2. Technical Interview 
3. HR Interview 
 
1. Online test (12.10.25) 
This online test had coding basically. 
1st question - Temperature_Check 
2nd question - Shortest_Unique_Substring 
3rd question - Largest_Square 
 
The first question was very simple. We can solve it just by using a switch 
statement. The problem was like the user gives a temperature value , we have to 
determine the range of it and return the comments given as per the question. 
 
The second question was simple. It was similar to the problem 
Shortest_Unique_Substring. 
 
 
The third question was like creating  a algorithm for the given question 
which was in medium difficulty . I could not remember the question exactly . 
 
 
As the test was in online, completion time was one of the factor for getting 
shortlisted for the interview .
 
 
 
2. Technical Interview (05.11.2025) 
A total of 82 students from CEG and MIT was shortlisted for technical 
interview. Both HR and Technical interviews were in-person interviews which held 
at CUIC, CEG. 
The interviews were conducted in the Auditorium of CUIC, with students being 
randomly assigned to each panel. All panels had a two interviewers. Randomly, I was 
assigned to the most difficult panel. 
After greeting the interviewers, I handed them my resume. The interview 
began with the interviewers introducing themselves, followed by them asking me to 
introduce myself. 
Be prepared with the self-introduction as it makes the first impression. I 
started off with the self-intro as instructed. As I had mentioned about my extra-
curricular activites explicitly. We had a conversation about my cricketing journey for 
2-3 minutes. 
After reviewing my resume, they told me that they weren’t interested about 
the projects that I had put on my resume as all the people who attended the 
interview was having similar kind of projects using MERN stack, DBMS etc…. So, they 
told me that they are moving to the fundamentals of CS(OS, OOPS, DBMS, Networks 
and  Data Communication), and about programming languages  mainly JAVA. 
He first started asking questions about JAVA 
• 
What is JVM? 
• 
Difference between JVM and JRE. 
• 
How does the complier compile the .java file ? and he asked me to explain the 
flow of control. 
• 
What compiler is used in JAVA? 
Then he moved onto DBMS where the questions were 
• 
He gave me a description about the table schema and I had to create a table 
for the description which he gave and write it down on a paper. After writing 
he checked it. 
• 
He asked me to create an index for a column in that table and asked me to 
write it down. 
Then the interviewer moved onto OS 
• 
He asked me about the concepts of OS and I explained him about the concept. 
• 
He asked me whether I am familiar with Linux OS. 
• 
He asked me to explain some Linux commands using an example with its 
usage 
 
 
Then he moved onto OOPS 
• 
He asked me about Polymorphism . 
• 
I was asked to write the code for Static polymorphism and he checked it. 
• 
Then he asked me to explain Run-time polymorphism. 
• 
In inheritance he asked me a question stating that “Class A has a numerous 
methods. Class B is inherited from Class A. Now I have to access the class A’s 
method using the object of class B. Is it possible?”. And he asked me to write 
the code of that particular line. 
Then he moved onto Full Stack 
• 
What is frontend and backend? 
• 
How to frontend and backend communicate with each other? And I was asked 
to write the code on the paper and explain him. 
• 
He gave me a situation that, “The API from frontend transmit the data to the 
backend and the API that should catch it is down. What would happen?”. I 
replied him and the asked “What kind of error type and number ?“. I answered 
the question and he asked “Why not 404?”. I explained him and he gave many 
code asked me find out where it is used. 
The second interviewer was listening to me till then and he asked a 
question about the working of MySQL in the backend like how does the query  
“Select  * from “ takes the records from the table and what data structure is 
during the retrieval process of the query. He asked me to try and explain it. I told 
my approach using trees(B trees) and he asked me “why not graph ?”. So I 
changed my approach using the Graph using BFS and DFS. Then I was asked to 
explain about the BFS process. 
Finally, both the interviewers were satisfied my answers and they gave me 
a chance to ask them questions. 
The first round (Technical round) got over. It was around 1 hr. 
Everything depends upon the interviewer. Some interviewers asks less 
questions. While some of the interviewers go in depth about your project and the 
fundamentals. So be prepared for both. 
 
2. HR Interview (05.10.2024) 
From the 82 students who were qualified for technical round, 30 were 
selected for the next round (HR). 
It was a one-to-one interview. Since she had a copy of my resume on her 
hand, I didn’t hand over my resume. She began with her introduction, and then I 
followed with my own introduction. When I mentioned my extra-curricular 
activities, she was impressed with it and I also added my 12th mark which

VISA Interview Experience

-  Arunachalam M

About the company

Visa Inc. is an American multinational financial services corporation headquartered in San
Francisco, California. It facilitates electronic funds transfers throughout the world, most
commonly through Visa-branded credit cards, debit cards and prepaid cards.

Role details

The role offered by Visa was for a Software Engineer Intern.

Candidate Profile

I had made 2 projects each covering a different set of skills. The first one was a movie
booking project using XAMPP stack. The other project was finding the shortest path between
any 2 buildings in CEG campus which can implemented to any campus using Dijkstra.

My first project covered my DBMS and web development knowledge and my second one
covered my DSA skills. Try having a project like a web/app on a different topic unlike the
standard hotel reservation system etc as interviewers prefer different types of project
irrespective of the stack used. Also try to have at least 2 projects in your resume.

Mention the skills that you are confident in your resume and never lie in your resume as it
creates a bad impression and more chances of rejection are created.

Preparation Strategy

Preparing for interviews require consistency and dedication. With these 2 skills, you can
easily crack any coding interview.

First strengthen your language skills in C++,java or python. I would recommend C++ as its
fast, easy to learn and code. Try the basic HackerRank C++ sums to understand the nuances
of the language. Learning STL is a must for C++. For python or java their respective
Collections library.

Next step is to take one topic at a time and solve many sums on them. Take a topic for ex
arrays, practice lots of sums based on arrays from Geeksforgeeks and leetcode until you are
confident with arrays. Then move on to the next topic, strings linked list etc

Keep solving in gfg and leetcode regularly. Attend the biweekly and weekly contests of
leetcode and any programming contest hosted by gfg as they help you prepare on how to
solve sums in a limited time frame.

 Try solving sums from codeforces upto 1200 rating level as those sums are asked in the
online assessment round of many companies. These sums also help in improving your
problem solving skills a lot.

Another important thing I would like to mention is to document and store your solutions for
important sums. Don’t store for necessarily all the sums, store only the important sums that
uses a particular concept like two pointer, sliding window etc. I would recommend opening a
github repository as it is online and can be accessed anywhere from your phone or from any
device. Write your approach, intuition, logic, code and the time and space Complexity. This
helped me a lot to prepare for interviews

Instead of solving random sums, experienced youtubers have created sheets which can be
followed to move towards the right direction.

https://takeuforward.org/strivers-a2z-dsa-course/strivers-a2z-dsa-course-sheet-2/

The above link contains an amazing curated sheet of problems which covers all the topics.
This sheet has been curated by Striver who is currently working at Google

Other resources I had used were :

DP series :
https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLgUwDviBIf0qUlt5H_kiKYaNSqJ81PMMY

https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL_z_8CaSLPWekqhdCPmFohncHwz8TY2Go

Tree series :

https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLgUwDviBIf0q8Hkd7bK2Bpryj2xVJk8Vk

Graph series :

https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLgUwDviBIf0rGEWe64KWas0Nryn7SCRWw

Basically follow Striver’s channel as it has everything covered!!

Preliminary Round

The first round was an online assessment round which was conducted on HackerRank in
offline mode proctored by dept staff. Two questions were asked and the duration was 1 hour

The questions asked were :

1.  Find the maximum difference of the max and min element of every component in the

graph.

2.  You are given two teams of sizes a and b. The total number of participants are n. Find
the minimum number of teams that can be formed using all the n participants into
either teams of sizes a or of size b

Solution :

 1.  This is a standard graph problem which can be solved by DFS or BFS
2.  This is an adaptation of the Minimum number of Coins sum (Dynamic Programming)

I was able to solve both the sums within 1 hour and later I was shortlisted for the next round

Technical Round(s)

The 2nd round was a technical DSA round which was conducted in Microsoft teams at CUIC.

My interviewer joined on time and started a friendly conversation about my educational and
family background. Then he started to slowly moved towards my projects in my resume.

He asked why I did this project and what is the unique feature of your project that
distinguishes yours from the other ones out there. Then he asked few more technical
questions regarding the projects.

Next he asked me to join the HackerRank code-pair link and gave me a DSA question

Given a string and a number as input, rotate each and every character of the string

according to the number mentioned. Ex : string : abc, number : 1, then output is bcd

The number can also be negative and error messages should also be displayed for edge cases
like when there is an empty string, or an alphabet instead of a character etc.

I was able to solve the sum and the subsequent edge cases and follow up questions that were
asked.

My interview round lasted for 1 hour 10 minutes. After a few hours, the results were out and I
was selected.

About the company

VISA is a payment facilitator. It facilitates electronic funds transfers throughout the
world, most commonly through Visa-branded credit cards, debit cards and prepaid
cards. Contrary to public opinion, they aren’t a bank or a credit card company.

Role Details

Summer Internship – Software Engineering Intern

Preparation Strategy

•  Work through the problems of LeetCode and GFG.
•  Pick a youtuber of your choice and then refer their SDE sheet. Stick to one
sheet and aim to cover all the easy and moderate problems in their list.
•  Refer takeuforward on YT for various algorithms. The channel is entirely in

English.

•  Try mycodeschool for pointers. Your understanding of pointers should be rock
solid as it forms the base of almost all the other data structures. So take your
time with this.

•  Refer GFG for algorithms
•  Get the basics right: arrays, pointers, trees, linked lists, stack, queue, heap,

•

binary search, tree traversals, etc
In my opinion, you don’t need to know complex topics like DP, graphs,
segment tree for internship interviews. However, it helps if you know it as they
will be asked during placements.

•  Try Codechef, Codeforces and Codewars if you are interested in CP. This will
help you down the line. Regularly give contests on these platforms. Don’t
worry about your ratings. Pick up problem-solving skills

•  Try to get 6-stars for problem solving on hackerrank. This badge covers

almost all the important topics you’d need.

•  Learn CPP. Don’t use Python for coding as it quite slow. I wouldn’t

recommend Java either because it is verbose. CPP is the ideal language for
competitive programming.

•  For CPP, refer The Complete C++ Reference by Herbert Schildt. It covers all

the topics.

•  Almost all the big companies allow you to use STL. Make sure you know how
each data structure works in STL. You can refer Schildt’s book or articles on
GFG for this. Don’t worry if the syntax seems scary at first. With enough
practice, you should get the hang of things.

•  Have two or three projects on your preferred domain. Make sure you

•

understand each line of code.
If you are into data science or other related domains, check out Kaggle. Give
community-contests regularly.

 •  Before you put some fancy buzzword like AI, ML, blockchain, cloud computing
etc on your resume, make sure you have enough knowledge about it. You can
expect to be asked tons of questions on these topics if you put them on your
resume. My advice would be to put them in the interests section rather than
the skills section.

•  Work on your typing speed and accuracy. This will give you an edge over

others during contests and coding rounds.

•  For aptitude, refer Indiabix and puzzles on GFG. Devote enough time to this
as a majority of fintech companies have an aptitude section in the first round.

•  Focus on the core subjects like Operating Systems, Computer Networks,

DBMS, and Design & Analysis of Algorithms. For these topics, it is more than
enough if you pay attention in class. For last minute preparation, refer the
Frequently Asked Questions(FAQs) on takeuforward. You can also watch
Abdul Bari’s YT channel. He explains concepts in a simple, easy-to-
understand way.

•  Prepare for HR questions. This is the make-or-break part of interviews. Have

a solid introduction before your interview. This should cover your interests and
tell the interviewer who you are as a person rather than being an audio
version of your resume. Look up standard HR interview questions and
prepare for them ahead of time.

•  Refer company-wise questions on a website of your choice. Ideally, this

should be done a few days before the actual test and interview

•  Keep up with the C.O.D.E initiative of ACM-CEG and regularly give practice

contests

•  Familiarise yourself with various platforms such as hackerrank, hackerearth
etc. The online tests will happen in these platforms, and it really helps if you
know the layout of the platform.

•  You will be given a 50-day break after the fourth semester. Use this break to

prepare for the internship season that’s coming up. Aim to solve 3 problems a
day and slowly increase the number of problems you are solving towards the
end of the break.

•  Finally, stay hopeful. The intern season can be rough. You will get rejected for

no reason. It may seem like your hard work isn’t paying off. Please stay
optimistic. Give the online tests and interviews to the best of your abilities and
you will definitely crack them.

Preliminary round

We were asked to give the online tests in college. The test was conducted on
hackerrank platform. We were all given a different set of coding questions. The
questions were moderate. We didn’t have any OS, CN, DBMS questions in the
online test.

The questions asked were:

 •  Brute-force based question. The catch here was to optimise it sufficiently. The
question was moderate-hard. It was similar to the concept of limiting reagent
in chemistry. I was able to pass 14/16 test cases.

•  An easy-moderate question on arrays. I got this fully right.

No aptitude-based questions were asked.

Final Round

The final round happened on Microsoft Teams. The interview began with a quick
introduction from the interviewer, and I was asked to introduce myself. The interview
was primarily focused on my projects and interests. There was a tree-based question
on hackerrank code-pair. The interviewer was satisfied with my approach, so I wasn’t
asked to code. We continued to talk about my projects and interests. I was quizzed
on my achievements mentioned in my resume. I was also asked to explain the
working of a few of my projects. Plenty of HR questions were asked. The interview
went on for about 40 minutes.

Aswin Ramanathan

Batch of 2024

Preparation Strategy For Competitive Programming:

●

●

●

Starting off your preparation for Competitive Programming (CP) early is a big advantage. You get to explore
and learn slowly and an early starter gets to cover a vast expanse of problems.
For CP, first you have to find your niche i.e find your perfect programming language that you are strong at by
analyzing your strengths and liabilities while solving problems using various languages. Explore thoroughly
before you make a choice as shifting programming languages amidst your preparation might create havoc.
It is advised to stick to the order of recommended languages below :

○ C++/Python (Highly Recommended, especially C++)
○
○ C

Java (Also a good choice)

● My choice of language was Python as I really admire and revere the language and I was accustomed to it

since high school. Learning a new language like C++ from scratch was tough for me. Python was
comfortable and I never faced any issues with that. But a majority of people recommend C++. Java is a
good choice as well. On the other hand, C is quite less preferred.

2

 ● CP will take you to a completely different realm of coding and programming. CP requires a problem solving

approach which would be entirely different from the normal monotonous coding problems with more
remembering involved that you solve pertaining to the syllabus courses.
The biggest change begins from a small step. So start off by solving a few simple problems with difficulty
level ‘easy’ from the sites given below :

●

○ Hackerrank
○
Leetcode
○ Geeksforgeeks

● Develop a conceptual learning approach towards programming rather than a theoretical approach. Focus

●

more on building ideas rather than just getting the output by typing the code.
Before jumping into the perspective of coding it out, channelise your focus to crack the logic. After the logic
is figured out, coding it out is easy. Getting a strong foothold on CP is possible only when you have a
vehement flow of ideas as a culmination of ideas and logic forms the basic foundation of CP.

● Gradually bring in the time component as given below and see if you are able to solve a problem with the

target :

○
Easy : 5 Minutes
○ Medium : 15 Minutes
○ Hard : 30 Minutes

●
●
●

If you are unable to, strive to achieve it by strengthening your practice.
Slowly advance to higher difficulty levels and try solving more problems.
Then, gradually start expanding various concepts of CP as per the following order :

Arrays
Strings
Searching and Sorting Algorithms
Stacks And Queues
Linked Lists
Trees

○
○
○
○
○
○
○ Graphs
○ DP
○
○

Back-tracking
Bit Manipulation

●

●

●

The only key to unlock the skill of CP is rigorous practice. Consistently practice and solve more and more
problems. Attempt as many contests as you can to gain exposure. Explore and solve as much as you can.
You may not be able to always work on a daily basis given the tight schedules, but being consistent in
solving problems is what matters. Keep getting back to solving the next day even if you miss a day. Long
gaps will prove to be a great impediment to your flow of practice.
And, it is not advised to use solutions readily available on the internet. However, after you solve, you can
refer to a few sites (only trusted authentic sites) to see if your solution can be improvised.

Interview Preparation :
The above guidelines focus on how to prepare consistently on a daily basis. But the guidelines below throw light on
how to prepare for the approaching upcoming interviews :

General Preparation :

refresh the approaches used.

Interviewbit

3

 ● Refer the archives for the coding, technical and aptitude questions asked in online assessments and

interview rounds.

Selection Process :

Round 1 : Online Coding Assessment :
Duration : 1 hour
No. of Qs : 2

Question 1 :

Concept Covered : Arrays

Gist of The Question :
It was a very simple and direct team formation question. Given an array of strengths of various members, form teams
as pairs of people with the same strengths. The program finally returns the no. of teams that can be possibly formed.

Approach :
●
●

Take the frequencies of each element and divide by too (floor division) and add them.
To ensure no repetitions of iterations for same array elements, convert the python list into a set and compute
frequencies.

Estimated Time : Less Than 5 Minutes
No. of Test Cases Passed : All 15/15
Difficulty Level : Easy (In My Opinion)

Question 2 :

Concept Covered : Simple Logical Computations With Good Geometry Visualization

Gist of The Question :
Given the cartesian coordinates of 3 vertices of a triangle ABC and 2 points P and Q on the cartesian plane.

We had to find which one of these 5 cases is true for the given input :

1. The vertices A, B and C cannot form a triangle.
2. The vertices A, B and C can form a triangle and the points P and Q both lie inside the triangle.
3. The vertices A, B and C can form a triangle and the points P and Q lie inside and outside of the triangle

respectively.

4. The vertices A, B and C can form a triangle and the points P and Q lie outside and inside of the triangle

respectively.

5. The vertices A, B and C can form a triangle and the points P and Q both lie inside the triangle.

The function should return 5 different integers corresponding to those 5 cases.

To Find If Vertices A, B and C Can Form A Triangle :

Approach :

● Compute the distance of the edges using the distance formula.
●
●

Take the 3 possible combinations of pairs of edges.
The sum of the lengths of those 2 edges should be greater than the third edge.

To Find If A Point P Lies Inside The Triangle ABC :

4

 Approach 1:
●
●

Find the minimum and maximum x and y coordinate among A, B and C.
If x coordinates of P are lesser than the minimum x coordinate or greater than the maximum x coordinate or
if similarly y coordinates of P are lesser than the minimum y coordinate or greater than the maximum y
coordinate, then P is not situated inside the triangle.

Estimated Time For Approach 1 : Less Than 5 Minutes

Mistake in Approach 1 :

This concept of min and max draws a rectangle that inscribes the triangle which is wrong as P may also lie inside the
rectangle and outside the triangle.

Approach 2:

● Compute the equations of the 3 lines AB, BC and AC using their coordinates in the form ax+by=c.
●
● Considering the points A, B and C are in clockwise direction, LHS should be greater than c for AC and

Substitute P’s x and y in the LHS and see if it is lesser or greater than c.

lesser for AB and BC.

Estimated Time For Approach 2: 10 Minutes
No. of Test Cases Passed After Approach 2 : All 15/15
Difficulty Level of The Question : Medium (In My Opinion)

After the assessment, on resolving, A Better Approach :

● We can compute the area of a triangle with the 3 vertices’ coordinates.
●
●

Find the areas of ABC, ABP, ACP and BCP.
If the sum of areas of ABP, ACP and BCP equals the area of ABC, then P lies inside tri.ABC.

Round 2 : Interview (Technical + HR) : [ 14 Student Shortlisted]
Platform : Microsoft Teams
[ We were asked to turn on our videos ]

Introductory Interaction Questions Asked:

5

 Tell me about yourself.

●
● Highlight your technical and non-technical skillset.
●

Enunciate your strengths and weaknesses.

Tip :

It is advised to prepare for these questions and frame an answer in advance as you will face these questions in any
interview you take up.

Resume - Based Questions :

●
I was asked to explain any one completed project from my resume (I explained my DSA project).
● Questions : What did you learn from the project? & What problems did you face while working on it?

Coding Questions : (No. of Questions : 2)

How the Coding Round Was Carried :

●

●

A hackerrank code pair link was given to us where we can type our code and obtain the output. The
interviewer will have a live view of whatever we code.
A question was given to us and we were asked to solve it quickly without explaining.
Tip :
Usually it is advised to be interactive while coding out your solution. Always think aloud and discuss your
flow of ideas with the interviewer and they will help you out when you are stuck as well. Also, any mistake or
mishap in your logic will be spotted in advance and you can change it then and there without wasting time.
But, for us, due to the time constraint of only 1 hour for both technical and HR questionnaires.
The code that we put forth should be optimized.

●
● Once we were done, we should report it to the interviewer.
●

Then they would ask you to explain the code and answer the follow up questions.

Question 1 :

Concept Covered : Matrices, Binary Search

Tip : Always be thorough with commonly used searching and sorting algorithms.

Given an m x n matrix, we have to search for a value k.

Each row of the matrix is sorted and the last element of a row is always less than the first element of the next row.

Approach 1 :

●
●

Populate all the entries to a 1D array of size mn.
Perform binary search.

[ I was told that converting to a 1D array is not allowed ] So, to solve , Perform 1 binary search to find the row. Then,
Perform another binary search to locate the element k in that row.
(Requires 2 Loops)

Follow Up Q : Can this be done without using 2 loops? Ans : No, since we are not allowed to convert into 1D, there
should definitely be 2 searches, one to find the row and one more to locate the element in that row.

Question 2 :

Concept Covered : Arrays
The simple and very familiar two-sum question. To find pairs of integers in an array that yield a sum k.

6

 Approach :

● Convert the array to python set.
●
●
●

Traverse the set for each element i.
If k-i is also in i, add the key-value pair (i,k-i) to a dictionary.
Print the pairs.

[ Tip : In this interview, we had no questions from technical concepts like DSA, DBMS, OS, etc. But they are sure to
be asked in a majority of the interviews and cannot be neglected.]

Aptitude Question :

● Q : You have 2 ropes. Each rope will burn completely in 30 mins. Measure a time duration of 45 minutes

using the 2 ropes.
So, here we definitely burn one rope entirely to measure 30 minutes.
The catch is to find how to measure 15 mins with the second rope.

●
●
● My Answer : It is 15 minutes when the rope burns half its length.
●
● My Answer : Folding the rope to get midpoint.
●
●

[ But there is no instrument to measure half

[ But the rope doesn’t stay homogenous. It doesn't burn at a uniform rate throughout ]
After thinking for a minute, My Answer : Burn the rope at both ends (Irrespective of rate of burning, lighting it
at 2 ends doubles the speed at which the matter in the write burns, Speed doubled is time halved. So 15
mins can be measured) [ Right Answer ]

Company - Based Questions :

● Why VISA? ( Tip : As mentioned earlier, one should be aware of the company to answer such questions. )
● Do you have any questions for us? ( Tip : Here, do not say that you have no questions. Do ask something

about the company that you are curious to know about. [ Eg: I asked about the security mechanisms used in
VISA that have formed a foundation to instill a sense of trust and reliability to millions of users across the
globe. ] )

General Tips To Be Kept In Mind During The Interview.

●
●

Be calm and poised throughout the interaction.
Speak fluently without fumbling and put forth your points with confidence.

This phase may be tough. You may face rejections despite acing the interview rounds. Never see this rejection as a
factor that pulls you down. Stay motivated and never lose hope. A vehement learner gets gratified in the experiences
and doesn’t blindly expect good outcomes. Keeping that in mind, enjoy the course of learning and keep striving
tirelessly towards your goal. Achievers never quit until they achieve the impossible. Remember always that the best
awaits you. Keep up your perseverance in learning more and more and that indeed paves the way for you to triumph.

and ace your interviews!

Shankar Subramanian (2018115102)

Online Test:

Two questions, first one was a dynamic programming question where instead of finding the
minimum or maximum you find the GCD. I think i did this using python 3.9 as it has an inbuilt
GCD function, else i coded it manually.

Second question was a string question where you had to find the HCF of 2 strings (ababab,
abab) -> (ab) as that’s the smallest subset that when multiplied can form both. I did this using
java as it has a repeat function on strings.

Interview round 1:
Was asked questions about how I would find a pair and then a trio of numbers in a given list of
numbers that total 0, you use hashmaps here cuz the time complexity on reading that is O(1)
instead of arrays that would be an O(n). I think the exact code wasn’t needed, it had to sound
right, so i guess it was a logic test, the interviewer was helpful. This was done on hackerrank
codepair i think it was where your screen gets recorded and locked and you and the interviewer
share the same file in real time, its like google docs but code i guess

Interview round 2:
Got asked about my resume for a little bit and then asked what languages I am familiar with,
told them javascript, python, and java. Was asked basic questions about javascript like how I
would make cookies, and what is freezing an object. Syntax wasn't asked as IDEs nowadays
have that stuff. Was then asked to do some string manipulation, using python as slicing was one
of the options. This also happened on that codepair thing.

Interview round 3:
40 minutes of resume project analysis, was asked about the courses i did on coursera, why i did
different things, and what my interests had been, and what they are now. Then discussed a
random project on the resume for 15 minutes before getting into the nitty gritty of it and then
discussing about the ways in which it could be improved or how i would handle different
scenarios, you really have to know what you’re doing here as the replies must be convincing
and the interviewer builds upon your previous response. As it builds upon your previous
response you are in control of the direction of the interview, so think ahead and try to not let it go
to a domain or part where you are not comfortable with.

 Don’t do these:

1. don’t shell into your comfort zone and also don’t YOLO, there’s a fine line live life there
2. don’t try to scam the interviewers, spoiler alert they know stuff
3. know different languages i guess (this isn't hard it's just adding different shortcuts to your

arsenal as each language has its own special thing)

4. don’t tunnelvision yourself into max priority of getting the job, read the company, think
ahead of what the interviewer would ask, plan for most important scenarios, basically
game the system and you will be rewarded
If you don't know something, tell them what you’re thinking and as they aren’t sitting on
the other side to just RA you, they will help direct you towards the right answer (this
seems to be a recurring theme in most interviews).

5.

A paragraph of tips:
I wouldn’t recommend just reading G4G the prev night on a new topic (unless that’s your thing
and you’re real good at understanding theory), at least in my case that never works, i think
having coded it at some point in your life makes it a more memorable experience of pain or joy
and as you have a certain emotion attached to it it would last longer in your mind (this is some
psychology stuff i guess). Middle ground case of previous night's G4G and coding it as you
read, i don't know about this as I have not tried it, most of mine was from my practical
experience so I guess you could call it a YOLO based on past experiences.

It’s an interview not an interrogation room, it’ll be a cool experience if you want it to be, it could
be fun if you want it to be, basically it becomes what you want it to be, mine were straight up fun
(maybe i'm weird), we were casually talking about the answers and discussing topics and
scenarios. I guess the main take away is that they just want to check if you can think or design
something, for this you do need to know some computer theory stuff like trees, and maps, but
they’d help you with it if it’s more obscure (not splay tree kinda stuff levels, you’ll probably never
see things like these and if you do please let me know at cottonian99@gmail.com).

Like when you’re learning something and it feels important and you can think of a place it could
work (i'm not telling you to sit in a white room like socrates and force yourself to think) like know
it enough so that if you’re thinking about something else this should
pop-up-in-your-head-levels-of-importance, then know that thing and remember it. Some obscure
idea like idk getting the attendance of all students in the class could be made faster by asking all
students to turn their hotspots on or connect to the particular wifi and logging the IP/MAC->
student id (don't come at me saying this is a bad idea cuz of IP/MAC spoofing, it was just a
thought)

ps: take this as seriously as you would a grain of salt, as this is only my experience, and most of
it was based around my resume, as Visa i think does not exactly have a straight up viva like
Q&A session interview (i guess my first one was like that) i’d suggest knowing the stuff in your
resume well.

Srinikethan’s VISA Intern Selection Experience

**About the company:**

**What is VISA?**

VISA is the world's leader in digital payments technology. VISA’'s mission is to connect the world through the most creative, reliable and secure payment network - enabling individuals, businesses, and economies to thrive. Our advanced global processing network, VisaNet, provides secure and reliable payments around the world, and is capable of handling more than 65,000 transaction messages a second. The company's dedication to innovation drives the rapid growth of connected commerce on any device, and fuels the dream of a cashless future for everyone, everywhere. As the world moves from analog to digital, Visa is applying our brand, products, people, network and scale to reshape the future of commerce.

Company’s specifications:

![](data:image/png;base64...)

**Profile/Resume Building :**

Add all the projects you have done till 5th sem starting to your resume, that you are confident about. This can include the mini projects you have done as a part of your curriculum requirements. Do add certifications if you have done some online courses which you feel might be relevant to the company/role you are vying for. Just be thorough about what you have mentioned in your resume. Remember that the **quality** of the projects matter and not the quantity. Companies have low expectations and they just expect you to have your fundamentals clear and have good reasoning skills.You should be able to explain the whats, whys and hows of the project(s) concisely.

I had 1 mini project (Data Structures - Network request rate limiter using Hashtable) , 1 on-going project (in Deep Learning) and an nptel certification (not related to IT) in my resume.

**Preparation Strategy:**

Problem solving under DSA in any coding platform (Hackerrank, Leetcode, GeeksForGeeks, etc.)

Start with Easy problems under the topic Arrays in the language of your choice (C/C++,Python and Java are predominantly used). Starting early is definitely a key. Start with a single easy problem everyday. Make sure coding/problem solving becomes a daily habit. And then go on to strings, greedy, dynamic programming, backtracking, data structures, etc. at a slow pace (**ARRAYS** is the most important topic and forms the base for all others). Increase the no. of problems you do as you solve problems with ease and go on to Medium problems. Medium problems (majority) and easy problems will be asked in online tests. Hard questions are not asked thus you can omit it. As intern selection time nears, concentrate more on Medium questions.

In 3rd sem, concentrate on DBMS - Get your fundamentals intact. Important topics : Normalization, Transactions, SQL queries.

In 4th sem, make sure you conceptually understand OOPS and DAA(Algorithms) and can explain clearly what you have learnt.

Start revising DBMS, OOPS and DAA concepts during intern selection time when you are able to solve more than 1-1.5 / 2 questions in online tests. Concepts of OS shall be your last priority. This is enough to ace technical interviews.

**Preliminary Round:**

Online Coding Round:

No. Of questions : 2 (1 easy 1 medium)

Test Duration: 60 mins

Mode of test : Online

Qn 1 . Topic : Bit Manipulation

Easy Question - the concept of XOR and its implementation are the prerequisites needed to solve the problem. Coming up with a logic was quite easy.

All test cases passed

Qn. 2 Topic : Array multiplication

Medium question. Concepts: Array . It was a problem based on 1D array multiplication, addition and subtraction to produce a desired output array. Understanding the question was the key to crack it.

12/15 Test cases passed

Submitted the test with 15 min left on the timer.

**Interview round :**

14 students got shortlisted for this round

No. of interviews : 1 i.e. 1 Technical + HR round

**Technical + HR Interview :**

The Interviewer asked to introduce myself and started with a few questions based on my resume i.e. about the projects, especially the **motivation** and **novelty** of the projects.

The interview was more of a HR round than a Technical round. The interviewer asked doubts and questions from my answers. He was very keen on these 3 questions(which were asked indirectly) :

1. What does VISA do?
2. What are the technologies that are used by VISA and how do you plan to contribute to it?
3. What will you learn/will be the key takeaways from the internship opportunity if you get selected?

Just remember that an interview is just a conversation so it is important to be calm and be welcoming. Do think out loud while answering and try to be precise and specific.

Before attending the interview, do some basic research about the company(based on the questions above).

The interview lasted for 35 mins.

Final Verdict : **Selected**. (11 students were selected in total from AU)

Verizon Interview Experience 
Verizon is a leading global communications and technology company, ranked as the #1 
wireless provider in the United States and a global leader in next-generation technologies 
such as 5G and the Internet of Things (IoT). Verizon is recognized for its innovation, 
advanced network technology, and enterprise solutions. In 2024, Verizon was ranked as the 
8th Best Workplace Worldwide by LinkedIn, highlighting its strong culture, employee 
development, and leadership in technology. Working at Verizon provides opportunities for 
professional growth, exposure to cutting-edge tools, and being part of a globally respected 
brand. 
Recruitment Process 
1. Online Assessment (Aon Cocubes) 
The online assessment evaluated multiple skills: 
• 
Coding Round (2 Questions): 
1. Check if the sum of digits of a number N is prime  
2. Given N, T, energy gains , and jumps  calculate the minimum hours required 
to reach or exceed target energy. 
• 
Essay Writing: Tested critical thinking and writing skills. 
• 
Language Proficiency: A 4-minute rapid-fire section to answer as many grammar, 
spelling, and vocabulary questions as possible. Wrong answers had negative marks, 
but skipped questions carried no penalty—speed and accuracy were key. 
• 
Puzzle Section: Medium-level puzzles in a rapid-fire format tested logical reasoning 
and problem-solving speed. 
Outcome: 82 candidates were shortlisted for the GD round. 
2. Group Discussion (GD) 
• 
Topic: How India Serves as a Consumer Base for Global Market Industries 
• 
Format: 9 participants per panel, 20 minutes. 
Tips: 
• 
Focus on quality points, not quantity. 
• 
Don’t dominate but participate meaningfully. 
• 
Use examples or data to support your ideas. 
• 
Acknowledge and build on others’ points politely. 
• 
Maintain confident body language and show active listening. 
 Outcome: 50 candidates were shortlisted for the next round. 
3. AI Prompting Round 
This round was not preliminary but a key part of the process. Candidates solved problem 
statements (Realtime System failure concepts, Marketing Solution)using Prompt Engineering 
techniques such as one-shot prompting, role-based prompting, and structured prompts. 
Tips: 
• 
Understand the problem clearly before writing your prompt. 
• 
Use concise instructions with context. 
• 
Experiment with role-based prompts for better results. 
• 
Practice different prompting strategies in advance. 
• 
Stay updated on advancements in AI tools and methods. 
 Outcome: No eliminations took place in this round. 
4. Technical Interview 
My technical interview lasted 45 minutes, but durations can vary. If the panel finds you 
reliable and knowledgeable, they may extend the questioning. 
Key Areas Covered: 
• 
Programming Languages: Be thorough in the languages you mention. 
• 
Java Concepts: Core Java, OOP principles, and sometimes interface-related coding 
tasks. 
• 
Generative AI and Prompt Engineering: Questions on GenAI models, use cases, and 
prompt strategies. 
• 
Projects: Detailed discussions on project schemas, architecture, challenges, and 
contributions. 
• 
Panel Variation: Questions may vary depending on the panel. 
Tips: 
• 
Know your resume inside-out—anything you list can be asked. 
• 
Be well-prepared for programming fundamentals and AI advancements. 
• 
Understand your project schema and architecture in detail. 
• 
Revise Java basics, data structures, DBMS, and OS. 
• 
Be honest if unsure—explain your thought process. 
• 
Connect your experiences to Verizon’s technologies and values. 
 Outcome: 32 candidates advanced to the HR round, and all were finally selected. 
5. HR Round 
The HR round was casual and friendly, focusing on cultural fit. 
Questions Asked: Family background, hometown, interests, and flexibility for relocation. 
Tips: 
• 
Be genuine and confident. 
• 
Show enthusiasm for Verizon’s culture and opportunities. 
• 
Prepare thoughtful questions about growth and company culture. 
Outcome: Confirmation mails and shortlisted names were shared after HR—not immediate 
offer letters. 
 
Additional Tips for Success 
• 
Coding: Practice competitive programming, focus on time complexity and edge 
cases. 
• 
GD: Stay informed on current affairs and global market trends, especially India’s role 
in global trade. 
• 
AI Prompting: Learn few-shot, zero-shot, and role-based prompting. 
• 
Technical: Be thorough with programming languages, project schema, and AI tool 
advancements. Expect extended questioning if you’re deemed reliable. 
• 
General Advice: Stay calm, answer confidently, and maintain a positive attitude—
confidence matters as much as correctness. 
Summary 
Verizon’s recruitment process was smooth and transparent, testing coding skills, 
communication, 
AI 
prompting 
techniques, 
and 
technical 
expertise.
