ROYAL BANK OF SCOTLAND (RBS) 
 
 The RBS Group is a large international banking and financial services company. The Group 
provides a comprehensive range of financial services to retail, corporate, and financial 
institutions. 
Placement Process: 
 
The applicants were filtered based on CGPA and then the placement procedure took place in 
two rounds. The first online test round and an online face-to-face interview round. 
 
Round 1: Aptitude, Verbal, Technical Round: 
First round was for 2 hours on Mettl tests platform. It consisted of MCQs and 3 coding 
questions. 
MCQ Questions were based on Quantitative aptitude (profit loss, probability etc.), Logical 
Reasoning (directions, blood relations etc.), Verbal Aptitude (language related questions like 
synonyms, antonyms and comprehension type) and Technical Questions (mostly related to 
DBMS, OS and data structures). The coding questions were array-based.  
After completing the first round, the toppers were selected for the second interview round. 
 
Round 2: Technical and HR Round: 
This round consists of 2 panels (Technical and HR) 
Technical Questions: 
1. Introduce yourself. 
2. What are the subjects you are familiar with and which are you most comfortable? 
I chose Database Management Systems, so most of the questions were based on 
DBMS. 
3. What is Normalization? 
4. What is 2NF and explain with an example used in your project. 
5. What is a trigger and have you used it in your project? What are the types of triggers? 
6. What is a stored procedure? What are the advantages/disadvantages of using a stored 
procedure rather than executing as individual queries separately? 
7. What are some methods to improve the speed of execution of database queries? 
(Indexing etc.) 
 
 
 
Project Related questions: 
 
I had mentioned 3 projects in my resume and they chose two and asked to explain 
them in detail. 
The asked the following questions about the android app I had developed. 
1. What version of Android is your app compatible with? Why did you choose that 
version?  
2. What language is it developed in and what tools have you used? 
3. Did you have any way for clearing up the data to free up space once the data is not 
needed? (housekeeping) 
4. What is the difference between your application and the already existing ones? 
5. Have you released the application on the play store?  
HR Questions: 
1. What is good teamwork according to you? What are the characteristics of a good 
team? 
2. What is your aim? 
3. What do you know about RBS from PPT and what do you think you'll be working on? 
4. How do you keep yourself updated about technology? 
5. If people in your team listen to your idea, but do not implement it, how will you 
react? 
6. What is your favourite domain? What will you do if you get a project in a domain that 
you are not interested in? 
These were the questions asked to me. I had not answered 1-2 questions with clarity but 
overall it was a good experience.  
Topics to be focussed: 
 Database Management Systems 
 Data Structures and Algorithms 
 Operating Systems 
 
Takeaway: 
Having a good knowledge of the basics of DBMS, OS and Data structures, having clear idea 
about everything mentioned in the resume and confidence and clarity to answer the HR 
questions will help to get placed in RBS. 
Thank you. All the Best.

ABOUT THE COMPANY 
 
 
Aptiv PLC is an Irish-American automotive technology supplier with headquarters 
in Schaffhausen, Switzerland. Aptiv grew out of the now-defunct American company, Delphi

PROCESS 
 
Total Rounds: 4 (Each round was an eliminatory round- Once a round was cleared by the 
candidate, they received a link(with time slot mentioned) for the next round.) 
 
Round 1 : ONLINE ASSESSMENT (HACKERRANK – 18th sep 2025) 
 
This was a 90-minute round that consisted of both multiple-choice questions (18–20 questions 
focused on DSA, OOPs, DBMS, CN, and OS) and two coding problems (listed below). 
           1) First Lady of Software - You are given: n_processes: number of processes (for example, 2 
processes — A and B), n_intervals: number of time intervals (for example, 3 intervals). At each time 
interval, one process runs. You need to find how many different valid schedules are possible to assign 
the processes to these intervals. 
           2) Score of an array – You are given an array of integers. You can delete any elements                        
you want in any order. After all deletions, the array reindexes (positions shift). The score is the 
count of elements where the value equals its position (1-indexed). Find the maximum possible score 
you can achieve after any number of deletions. 
 
Round 2 : TECHNICAL INTERVIEW (MICROSOFT TEAMS – 29th sep 2025) 
            The interview round commenced with a brief self-introduction from both the interviewer and 
me. It then proceeded to a discussion of my projects, where I was asked to explain one in detail. The 
interviewer subsequently evaluated my understanding of core subjects, focusing on intermediate-level 
concepts. Following this, my programming proficiency was assessed through a coding problem 
(stated below) in my preferred language, along with three subsequent modifications to the same 
problem. The interviewer appeared satisfied with my responses (approaches) and concluded the 
session by inviting me to ask any questions.

set a time constraint for solving four coding problems(stated below) but deliberately did not disclose

       Be consistent in practicing Data Structures and Algorithms, and make sure to cover all core 
subjects such as OOPs, DBMS, CN, OS, and System Design. Explain every concept with real-life

About Arista Networks 
Arista Networks is an industry leader in data-driven, client-to-cloud networking for large 
data center, campus and routing environments. Arista is a well-established and profitable 
company with over $5 billion in revenue. Arista’s award-winning platforms, ranging in 
Ethernet speeds up to 800G bits per second, redefine scalability, agility, and resilience. 
Arista is a founding member of the Ultra Ethernet consortium. We have shipped over 20 
million cloud networking ports worldwide with CloudVision and EOS, an advanced network 
operating system. Arista is committed to open standards, and its products are available 
worldwide directly and through partners. 
Online Round: 
First, the students who had applied for the company were shortlisted, and only 83 students 
were selected to attend the online test. Since it was conducted in the department, the 
students wrote the exam using the department systems, not their personal laptops. 
The test was conducted on HackerRank, and the password was provided by the company. 
The test consisted of: 
 
15 technical MCQs covering topics like pointers, twisted output-prediction 
questions, and other programming-related concepts. 
 
3 coding questions, each with a separate time limit. 
You could access each section only during its allotted time, but if you completed a question 
early, you were allowed to save and proceed but oyu have to wait until the time ends. 
Next day the interview was in a hybrid mode which comprised of 2 rounds. 
Interview Round 1: 
Only nine students were shortlisted for the interview, and it was scheduled for the very next 
day, so I had very little time to prepare. My first round was virtual. It began with a quick self-
introduction, after which the interviewer explained the pair-coding process and asked me to 
share my screen. I was allowed to choose the programming language. 
First, he gave me an output-prediction question based on function overriding. I answered it 
incorrectly, but he guided me to the right answer and then asked multiple output-based 
questions by making small modifications to the same program. 
Next, he asked me to code a function that takes a string as input and returns another string 
sorted by the frequency of its characters. If two characters had the same frequency, they 
had to be sorted lexicographically. He handled the input and main function himself, and he 
asked me to explain my approach and use the assert function to test my implementation. 
After that, he asked me to code the flattening of a linked list, which I was already familiar 
with. Again, he took care of the main function and the printing of the linked list. 
He then asked how vectors are implemented internally, and I was only able to answer 
partially. 
Finally, he asked if I had any questions. 
Interview Round 2: 
Only five students were shortlisted for the second round. Everyone had a different experience 
in this round — some were asked technical questions again, while others faced project-based 
discussions. For me, it was mostly HR-style questioning. 
Since I was confident about my projects, I tried steering the conversation toward them. 
However, the interviewer felt that I hadn’t done anything new or impactful through my 
projects. He implied that I hadn't solved any real-world problem and even made me feel as 
though I had wasted my college life. From his tone, I felt he had already decided not to select 
me, but I didn’t lose hope. I kept trying to convince him that I was a good fit for the 
company. 
Eventually, the interview shifted into technical topics. He asked me what my strength was, 
and I said Data Structures and Algorithms. He then mentioned the name of an algorithm and 
asked if I knew it. I hadn't even heard of it before, so I said no. He then asked which 
algorithms I do know. I asked if he wanted something simple or complex. After his response, 
I first said Bubble Sort. He told me to choose a more optimal one, so I switched to Merge 
Sort. 
He then asked how I arrived at the time complexity of Merge Sort. I had forgotten the 
explanation, so I just described how Merge Sort works instead of giving the actual 
complexity derivation. 
In the end, he advised me to participate in more hackathons and work on personal projects. 
After two rounds only 2 members were selected for internship.

About the Company 
Western Digital is a major data storage company with a legacy of innovation, evolving from its 1970 
founding as a semiconductor maker to become a leader in hard disk drives (HDDs) and flash memory 
solutions for consumers, businesses, and cloud providers. Its long history includes a strategic pivots to 
focus on data storage technologies, leading to breakthroughs like OptiNAND and a continued 
commitment to developing reliable, high-capacity storage for the growing digital world. Western Digital 
has its only Indian office at Bangalore. 
 
Eligible Branches: ​
CSE, EEE, ECE 
Eligibility Criteria: ​
7.5 and above from 10th onwards, No history of arrear. 
Internship Location: ​
Bangalore 
Internship Duration: ​
8 weeks 
Stipend: ​
​
Rs. 40,000 
Roles:​ ​
​
Software Engineer (Embedded), Platform Engineer 
 
Selection Process 
1.​ Initial screening by Resume 
2.​ Online Assessment 
3.​ Interviews 
a.​ Technical Interviews 
b.​ HR Interviews 
 
Initial screening based on Resume 
From the applied candidates there was an initial screening with respect to resume and CGPA. It was not 
mentioned on what basis the resumes were screened although it was probably based on whether the 
candidate knows C/C++. I had a simple C++ project in my resume and included C and C++ in the 
languages known. 
 
Online Assessment 
Duration:  40 mins​
​
​
​
Location: CUIC 
The OA was conducted at CUIC Labs on the Hirepro website. Ensure your microphone and cameras are 
working properly if you are taking the assessment on your laptop. 
 
Software Engineer (Embedded) 
30 Aptitude Questions on numerical aptitude and general logical reasoning. The questions were not too 
hard. 
 
Platform Engineer 
Apart from the aptitude questions also included a segment with 20 coding MCQs based on C and C++. 
Although I did not attempt this assessment I heard that it was not too difficult. 
 
10 were shortlisted for the Software Engineer Embedded role and 30 for the Platform Engineer role from 
CEG and MIT. 
 
Interviews 
 
Technical Interview 1 
Duration: 1 Hour​
Location: CUIC 
 
I was assigned to an online interview although many had offline.  
Non-technical Questions asked: 
1.​ Why did you pick CEG and why CSE? 
2.​ Why are you interested in joining Western Digital? 
3.​ Your projects are not very related to the work here. So, why did you pick this role?  
4.​ If you had to pick between these in a decade's time what would you pick: Domain expert, a 
project manager, technical manager, general expert. 
5.​ What choice would you take between masters or placements and why? 
 
For the non-technical questions it's purely based on how you weave a convincing story. I myself had not 
explored much in embedded programming but tried to use smaller experiences like switching to a linux 
OS and using the Raspberry PI. 
 
I was asked what my favourite subjects were and I said DSA and Computer Architecture and hence was 
asked questions on DSA. I was also asked if I knew OS and mentioned that I was currently learning that.  
 
Technical Questions: 
1.​ Find the 2nd maximum element in the array 
(https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/dsa/find-second-largest-element-array/) 
I was asked to type out the code in notepad and was also asked to implement some in-built 
functions like max(a,b) 
​
 
2.​ Find the kth maximum element in the array 
(https://leetcode.com/problems/kth-largest-element-in-an-array/description/) 
For this I was not asked to code it but to only give the logic. I had given an approach of using 
bucket sort for O(n) time and space as well as a heap based method for O(nlogk) time. I had not 
mentioned QuickSelect which is the most efficient algorithm capable of solving this in O(n) time 
and constant space. 
 
3.​ Explain some of the scheduling algorithms in OS. 
 
The interviewer wanted to know if I had any questions and asked the following: 
1.​ How has AI affected your workflow? 
 
I was told that I wasn’t selected for the next round in the interviews for Software Engineer Embedded but 
was suggested for Platform Engineer and was asked to sit for those interviews. (I am not sure why this 
was done but yes I had to sit for another series of interviews with a lot of people already in the queue.) 
 
Technical Interview 2 
Duration: 30 mins​
Location: CUIC 
 
This interview was for the Platform Engineer role. As I had already done an interview before there 
weren’t many non-technical questions asked in this round. 
 
Technical Questions: 
1.​ Find if a linked list has a cycle 
(https://leetcode.com/problems/linked-list-cycle/description/) 
I was asked to write the full code on paper in either C or C++. 
 
2.​ Reverse words in a string 
(https://leetcode.com/problems/reverse-words-in-a-string/description/) 
I was again asked to write the full code on paper and was asked to do the reversal in-place. 
 
Techno-HR Interview 
This was a relaxed interview focussed on follow-ups based on my answers to questions. 
Things i talked about: 
1.​ Experience in projects and difficulties faced. 
2.​ My exploration of embedded programming and electronics (I had once broken my hard drive and 
tried to replace it back so I talked about that.) 
3.​ Leadership experiences in clubs 
4.​ Why Western Digital? 
 
I was then given the opportunity to ask questions to the interviewer and I asked the following: 
1.​ How has AI affected the products you make? 
2.​ Do you feel there is a market for developing AI products for internal use in companies? 
3.​ Why is WD focussed primarily on HDDs? Are SSDs a viable option for enterprise storage? 
 
HR Interview 
Duration: 10 mins 
Location: CUIC 
 
This was a quick round asking about my family background, my siblings, whether relocation to Bangalore 
would be an issue and about my experiences in clubs focussing on leading diverse teams. 
 
I was then selected for the Platform Engineer role along with 4 other MIT students and for the role of 
Software Engineer (Embedded) 3 were selected. 
 
Some tips: 
1.​ Try to pick a practice sheet for DSA quickly, I spent more time researching the best sheet than 
doing actual questions initially. Pick neetcode 150 or Striver A2Z. Also try to start practice during 
the 4th sem starting itself so that you can take a more relaxed learning approach. (It is tiring to try 
and do 5-6 problems a day at the end). 
2.​ Understand patterns and not just solutions. I initially tried to remember solutions and yes for 
some you just HAVE to remember but for many you can try understanding the pattern. Try to 
explain each step you take towards the solution (e.g. Why should this pointer move to the right , 
why try sorting this first and so on), doing so will make it easier to find patterns in unseen 
questions. Try to also not jump straight into coding a question after you see it; explain to yourself 
the logic behind your solution, that way you can start identifying loop holes. 
3.​ When asked what your favourite subject is, try to answer something related to the job description. 
Answering Full Stack Technology for a WD interview is not suitable as they don't really do much 
of full stack. Try to tune your resume also similarly if possible. 
4.​ Ensure you know all of the things mentioned in your resume. Your resume doesn’t have to be 
groundbreaking. A good and suitable set of projects (2-3), tools, languages related to the job, 
leadership experience if any and certifications and workshops can fill your resume. 
5.​ Please also practice aptitude. Aptitude is asked in almost all OAs and usually people skip out on 
that. 
6.​ Go through CS subjects like DBMS, OOPS, OS (if already done), C and C++. 
7.​ Be confident while speaking, you do not have to know everything beforehand, try to use whatever 
knowledge you have and tackle the questions. 
 
Do not chase number of leetcode/DSA questions and try to understand them. I had to redo 150 questions 
because I forgot all of them. You might face rejections along the way, do not worry about that at the end 
you only need 1 internship.  
Luck plays a major role and is often overlooked but unfortunately you cannot practice luck so do not 
worry if you are unlucky and be grateful if you are.  
Try your level best and leave the rest. Try to get an internship if not you can always try for placements. 
Do not stress out too much or you will get hairfall (and that is worse than not getting an intern). 
May the force be with you! 
​
Reach out if you have any doubts whatsoever :D

Tips:
Start the preparations early and focus on preparing for both DSA as
well as Core subjects ( DBMS, OS, Computer Networks).
I followed strivers-sde-sheet for the preparation. You can also make
use of the resources provided by ACM CODE.
Focus on understanding the concepts and practise pattern-specific

platform of your choice.
Be consistent in the preparation journey.
Do research about the company prior to the interviews and prepare
questions to ask the interviewer.

on mentioned technical skills.

unknown topics.

About the company:

Vivriti Capital

Vivriti Capital is an Indian fintech NBFC that focuses on debt finance for mid-market enterprises. It is
one of the leading startups based out of Chennai. They are mainly backed by TVS Credit, a company
that has been in this field for years. Vivriti has offices in Chennai, Bengaluru, Mumbai, Gurgaon and
Singapore although the tech team is based out of Chennai office only. They have other sister
companies like Vivriti Asset Management and Vivriti Next which offers fintech solutions. Tech hires
are considered as employees of Vivriti Capital only.

Role:
R&D Engineer – The role offers lot of scope for exploring new domains and tech stack. You’ll get to
experiment a lot and learn different stack. It involves more of ML-based solutions and latest
developments in the field and offers full ownership of the product.

Preparation Strategy:
Usual medium level DSA and other fundamentals. Be thorough with projects and skills in resume.

Placement Drive Experience:
First round – Online HackerRank test at CUIC
10 MCQs on fundamentals
3 DSA questions (1 easy, 2 medium. Arrays, strings and basic 1-D DP)

Second round – Technical + HR interview
Basic DSA, sorting techniques, time & space complexity, etc. Be thorough with your resume. Major
part of the interview will be based on what you put in resume, especially projects. Know everything
about your projects.

Tips and tricks:
Tips as in for first round, the usual DSA in Leetcode/GFG will do. As for the interview, it’ll be based on
your skill-set. The major advantage here is that they do not require you to be knowledgeable in a
specific tech stack but want you to be fundamentally strong in what you mention. The interviewer

gain industry experience while preparing for something else then this will be a good place. Don’t
expect it to be like other multinational banks since this is an Indian company and works differently.

 ROUND 3:HR

Round 3 was a very usual HR interview and the questions were “what
are your strengths and weakness”, “Is there anything that you do
apart from your academics”, “What will be your mindset when you
work as a team for projects?” “How confident are you about getting
placed in Wells?”.

I would suggest that it is not mandatory to use the same safe
answers available on the internet. That can at times make them feel
that you are reciting the answers. You can share your perspectives
confidently. Answer with a smile

And finally of the 22 selected for the interview, the company
provided internship for 12.

PREPARATION STRATEGY:

I started to prepare for the internship drives only during my 4th

semester holidays. I started CP somewhere during the mid of 3rd
semester but then I was not consistent at all. So my active
preparation started after 4th semester. ACM’s documents were the
only resource I followed. If possible, find a partner to practice with,
exchange your solutions and discuss with them. I think practising in
random doesn’t work..So follow a schedule starting from arrays,
strings and ending with DP and high-level topics. Easy and medium
level questions are more than sufficient is what I feel.

Following a schedule is the best tip I can give because we tend

to be focussed. ACM’s schedule will be helpful. Solve easy to medium
problems on each topic and attempt a quiz at the weekend. That is
sufficient and you are getting into some top companies. Don’t take
too much stress. We are never too late to start.

After companies started to come have this in mind,

 One thing we can do from our side is to work. If it turns
positive, fine else no problem; something big is waiting. Luck also
plays a big role in the process. So it doesn’t mean your works are not
benefitted. Never lose your confidence in any step of preparation.
We will work. Something big is always waiting for us!

Always feel free to text, call or meet us for any kind of help! We

will work together!

Stage 2
Interviews at CUIC, Anna University.
Technical Interview 1: (45-50 mins)
The shortlisted candidates assembled at CUIC. First round was a virtual one in
Microsoft Teams. There were totally 7 panels.
I was asked to share my resume with them, then the interview begun. First the
interviewer introduced himself and started asking questions. I was asked to give
overview of the projects mentioned in my resume, then he started clarifying his
questions on those projects and dug deep on to them.
Python:
· What is a decorator?
· What is flask and how do you handle a request?
· How do you handle JSON data in python?
· Threads in Python.
· Some Basics on Python in my project
DSA:
· Height of the Binary Tree (Easy)
· Frequency of a repeating char in a string (Easy)
React:
· What is the root page of React?
· How does React works?
· How do you manage states in React?
· Any idea on Use Memo?
· How do you use params in React?
Core:
· What are triggers and where did you use in your project?
· Threads and its applications
· DNS Caching
· Caching Techniques
· How do you manage multiple user requests?
· What are the security features that you have implemented in your projects?

· Do you have any idea on cloud computing and deployment?
SDLC:
· Do you have any idea on Software Design concepts?
· Do you know about SOLID principles?
· Have you used any Design concepts in your project?
I managed to answer most of the questions that were asked. I was true on things I don’t
have a clear idea on. I was asked to wait for the results of the next round. I came out,
within 10 mins I moved on to next round.
Technical Interview 2: (20-25 mins)
This interviewer is a cool guy and he is also an alumnus of our college. First he asked
me to introduce myself. I delivered what I have prepared. He asked about my hostel life
over the three years and my club activities.
Questions:
· Give a travel on your academics over the years.
· What are your favourite subjects?
· What is the difference between AI, ML and DL?
· Do you have any idea on python automation?
I mentioned using python for my regular stuffs hence he questioned me more on those
things. I didn’t feel like I was in an interview.
Technical Round 3: (10-15 mins)
This was supposed to be a Business Leadership round but we faced only technical
questions. I was asked to introduce myself. The interviewer told me to pick one project
that I was confident about and asked me present to him. I have done a realtime project
and I presented him that.
Questions:
· What are the languages you know?
· Which language you are confident on?
· Why do you use python?
· What type of language is python?
· Can you give me an example of Object Oriented Language?
· What is pure/strict Object Oriented Language?

Preparation Strategy:
I started my preparation on DSA only on summer intern drive and I was not consistent. I
would suggest to start as early as you can and be consistent.
Don’t wander around materials and many youtube sources. Fix onto one resource.
DSA: As everyone says Strivers Sheet.
SQL: Hackerrank SQL course (Awesome one).
Core Concepts: Your academic books (Yes, they cover in depth which is more than
enough). Last minute revision – Geeks for Geeks.
Projects: Know what you have done and why you have done. Even if you feel your
project is simple learn everything in it.
Resume: Know everything in it. Don’t add stuffs that you are not confident about. It
should showcase who you are. Add your leadership and extra-curriculars. This helped
me a lot in my interview.
Interview Tips:
Have a clear mind on the day of interview. Practise “Tell me about yourself” atleast 5
times. I did it for around 30mins and It boosted my confidence. Be cool, don’t frustate.
Result is the secondary thing, focus on delivery first.
Have a smile on your face inside the panel. If you are not sure what they are asking or
what they are expecting, make yourself clear asking “Could you be more specific
sir/ma’am? Or Do you want me to explain on this?”. Clarifying makes a good
impression.
Learn the values of the company and act accordingly and try to align yourself to it as
much as you can.
Prepare questions to ask your interviewer.
It’s all in you on what question you can get next. I drove the interview in such manner
that my answers can lead to another question that I have an idea on.
For Instance:
In my first round I was asked on, how to calculate the height of a binary tree. I wrote the
code and sent the answer in chat in python. Then he started questioning on python.
When I was asked questions on react I told him that it’s been a year I have worked on it.
So he stopped asking from react.

I told I have used triggers and implemented security features and so he asked questions
on them.
Final Note:
| Be Confident | and      | Honest.             |           |            |

| Luck plays   | a major  | role. But only luck | won’t get | you a job. |
| “Luck Played | Hardwork | Paid ”              |           |            |

**Wells Fargo Summer Intern Selection Process**

**About Company:**

Wells Fargo & Company (NYSE: WFC) is a leading global financial services company headquartered in San Francisco (United States). Wells Fargo has offices in over 30 countries and territories. Our business outside of the U.S. mostly focuses on providing banking services for large corporate, government and financial institution clients. We have worldwide expertise and services to help our customers improve earnings, manage risk, and develop opportunities in the global marketplace. Our global reach offers many opportunities for you to develop a career with Wells Fargo. Join our diverse and inclusive team where you will feel valued and inspired to contribute your unique skills and experience.

We are looking for talented people who will put our customers at the canter of everything we do. Help us build a better Wells Fargo. It all begins with outstanding talent. It all begins with you.

**About the Role:**

Internship includes performing various assignments to become familiar with the organization and gain basic work experience. Work assignments are augmented by classroom training, self-study assignments, workshops, networking and/or other events.

**Overview Of Selection Process:**

1. Online Test (Coding + Aptitude)
2. Interview Rounds:
3. Interview Round 1 (Tech)
4. Interview Round 2 (Tech + HR)
5. HR Round

**1) Online Test ( SHL Platform ) :**

The test comprised 4 rounds: 2 Coding Sections, 1 Verbal Aptitude and 1 Business Aptitude. Navigation was strictly linear – going back to previous question/section is not allowed.

* **Section 1 (Coding – 1 Hr) :**

For this Coding Section alone, **Java was the only programming language allowed**. The question did vary for others.

**My Question (Simplified Version) was:**

“ Given a currency value ( Ex: ₹2700), find the minimum number of notes( ₹2000, ₹500, ₹200, ₹100, ₹50, ₹20, ₹10, ₹5, ₹1 Notes Possible) and display them in the specified format ”.

**I/P :** 1950

**O/P :**

500 | 3

200 | 2

50 | 1

**Solution Logic:** Can be solved through 2 methods: One is using mod (Fast/Efficient) , and another is repeated subtraction till 0 (Easier to Understand)

* **Section 2 ( Verbal Aptitude – 12 Mins ) :**

10 Questions. This section tested English vocabulary and reading comprehension. Were asked to do:

* Synonyms and anonyms (6 Questions) for underlined words
* Given a comprehension passage, some questions(4 questions) related to that one passage.
* **Section 3 ( Business Aptitude – 25 Mins ) :**

12 questions in total. This round felt like the deal-maker among all sections. 4 Graphic Visualization problems were given:

Tables, Venn Diagrams, Bar Charts, and Candlestick charts.

Due to time constraint, I was able to attempt only about half the questions. It required quick math and logical relations.

* **Section 4 ( Automata – 40 Mins ) :**

2 Questions with any preferred languages allowed. (Again, questions varied for others) Questions I got was (Simplified) :

**Q1)** **Given m days and n profit entries, return the max profit for each day.**

**Number of Test Cases:** 14

**Q2)** **Very lengthy. Can recollect the total question:**
“ A house can be represented as a cell. Thus, a group of houses with each house having adjacent house can be said as group of cells, where each house can be either active (1) or inactive (0) . When it’s the next day, if only one of its either neighbour is active/inactive (not both), then it will be turned active. If both of it’s neighbours are active/inactive, it will be turned inactive. It can be assumed the left neighbour and right neighbour of 1st cell and last cell respectively are inactive always. Provided an array of n cells state and m days, find and return the cells state after m days. “

**Sample I/P :**

1 (No of days)

1 0 1 1 0 0 1 (Stream of data)

**Sample O/P :**

0 0 1 1 1 1 0 (States after 1 day)

**Number of Test Cases:** 122

**Approach I took:** I went for a simple logic, which is to find the XOR of each cell’s neighbours ( both left and right ) to determine its next day state. For the first and last cells, which have only one neighbour, I directly used that neighbour’s state (XOR of 0 and x = x).

**2) Interview Rounds:**

In total 3 rounds. **Every round required a self-introduction** and some question related to your resume was asked by the interviewers.

* **Tech Round:**

The interviewer began by asking what course subjects I liked the most. I mentioned Advanced Data Structures and DBMS. He followed up with questions from DBMS, including concepts like **ETL**, **ELT**, **MDM**, and **CDC**.

Then, we shifted to **Data Structures**. I was asked to open a notepad and write a pseudo code to check whether a given string is a palindrome using pointers. As I typed, I explained the logic and reasoning behind each line.

Satisfied with my explanation, He moved on to the difference between Singly Linked List and Double Linked List - how it differs actually in code, their use cases and real-world examples.

Then, we discussed sorting. He asked for a detailed comparison between bubble sort and merge sort, including procedures , strategies and time complexities. I explained both in Notepad with examples.

* **Tech + HR Round:**

The HR reviewed my whole resume and asked me to elaborate on my previous intern experience and some of the projects I worked on.

Toward the end of the discussion, he asked a single question:

“What’s the use of a pointer? From Programmer’s Perspective”.

He clarified he wanted an answer that’s not the usual one “To store it in a variable” and that a layperson can - without diving into topics like Operating Systems or Computer Architecture.

In a nutshell, I explained pointers by starting with a university system example—where we don’t know how many students will enrol in advance. First, I Gave a simplified, real-world explanation of pointers, finally tying it back to dynamic memory management. It went more like a formal conversation with some back-and-forth.

* **HR Round:**

The HR round was quite relaxed. After a brief self-introduction, they asked some questions related to my resume. Towards the end : two questions, “Why should we hire you?” and “Why choose us?”. I answered both together by stating my interest and my desire to contribute in finance domain through this role.

Before wrapping up, they advised me to **improve my Java Skills** as 80% of their systems rely on Java.

**Interview Advice:**

* Most interviews are quite chill. Just stay calm and focused throughout any interviews and you will do good.
* My interviewers weren’t worried much about not knowing some technical concepts. For me, they were looking at how well you could communicate and whether you could explain a topic clearly to anyone.

**My Preparation Strategy:**

* I actually started my intern prep quite late – just about a month before the whole process kicked off. While most of my peers had been practicing since the start, I knew I had to work in that month itself.
* My plan wasn’t rigid or structured. I took a few courses (Free ones) and relied heavily on GeeksforGeeks & ACM questions for brushing up concepts and solving problems. What I took seriously was not just solving questions—but **understanding them deeply**. I made sure I knew why a solution worked, not just how.
* My mindset was kind of simple: Keep working until you can crack the test. And at the same time, I made sure my communication skills are good enough, like I said, that mattered the most out of anything else.

**All the Best and Good Luck!**

Internship Experience at Wells Frago
                                -by Mahalakshmi B

About the Company:

Wells Fargo & Company, an American multinational financial services corporation
headquartered in San Francisco, has a significant presence in India. Wells Fargo India is a critical
part of the company's global operations, offering services in technology, operations, and business
process management.

India is a strategic hub for Wells Fargo, playing a vital role in enabling the company to achieve
operational efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and scalability. Wells Fargo in India actively engages
in CSR (Corporate Social Responsibility) initiatives, emphasizing education, community
development, environmental sustainability, and financial literacy.

Overview of the Process:

The overall process consisted of:

•  An Online coding contest (English, Aptitude, Coding)
•
•
•  HR Round – in CUIC

Interview Round 1 (Technical) – in CUIC
Interview Round 2 (Tech & Innovation) – in CUIC

Online coding contest :

The online test here was little different from those test that were conducted by other companies
since here we had questions related to Aptitude and English in addition to coding . From the
overall online test, I could understand that Wells Fargo is not only interested in your coding
knowledge, but also keen about your communication skills and logical thinking.

In the English section we had questions related to grammar and a basic Aptitude section which
involves logical thinking. I got 2 coding questions, one was easy (Sorting a nested vector) and
other was of medium (Case Study based) level and both where based on DSA. We didn’t have
any sectional time limit, so it was easy for us to grab sometime from the English and aptitude
section by answering them as fast as possible as.

 TIPS :

When the question is easy, the two major things to be considered is the time  and the number of
test cases that are passed . So rather than thinking of the optimal solution for the question, try to
complete the question as soon as possible, while ensuring that all test cases are passed

While question is of medium or difficult level, now the major concern will be the number of test
cases that are passed . So aim to make your solution as versatile enough to cover all the test
cases.

In the worst case scenario, at least use some logic to satisfy the open test cases. since, most of the
people tend to leave the difficult level questions unattended and you could gain advantage of the
same.

The major thing to be considered here is that most of the companies shortlist the people based
upon the time and the effectiveness of their solution in satisfying all the test cases.

Interview Round 1 (Technical) :

My first round was based on the fundamentals of the programming in any language. The
interview started with the first basic question of  “Introduce  yourself”, and he dug deep into my
academic history and my interest towards the field that I have applied for. He questioned me on
the basics like “what is an algorithm? Why do we need it and give some examples for the
same?.” Most of the first rounds starts with the basics where they test you on the fundamental.

The concepts that have been questioned intensively are Sorting ,OOPS,DSA .

He initially questioned me on this basic concepts, the logic behind its working and how we can
implement it . As far as I know, all of my friends were given a choice to choose their favorite
language to answer these questions ( you have to justify the same ) and then they would tell us to
1st explain the algorithm, how it works and question about its working with some special cases
and then he prompted me to write code snippets (pen & paper) implementing the same.

For DSA, rather to write the code, they asked me to explain about the AVL Tree and spanning
tree ,Their working with some illustrations using pen and paper.

They didn’t give much importance to the code syntax, but rather concentrated on the concept and
my fundamental understanding of how and why does it work? What happens when we remove
certain line from the algo ? What is its practical implication and what are the situations where
this will be much useful and where it is not ? etc…

TIPS :

•  Be well prepared for the “ introduce yourself “ coz it is the first impression that you

gonna make there .

 •  Be calm and patient while your answer the question that makes you look wiser.
•  Be thorough with the fundamentals of the programming language that you are strong with
and also make sure that you can logically reason the working of any algorithm or a data
structure.
•
revise the special cases, worst cases ,etc of every algorithm that you know
•  As far as I know, Wells Fargo is more interested on how strong you’re on your

fundamentals and your basics (majorly OOPS) rather than your strength in the advanced
concepts

•  Major tip to pass through the round is to speak about all the concepts that are related to
the question and try to cover most of the approached without giving the chance for the
interviewer to question about the leftover possibilities

•  Actually, covering all the approaches for the given question will make the interviewer to
dive deeper into that particular question, limiting the number of other questions to be
asked, and you may escape from the questions which might be challenging you .
•  Most of the interviewers question you based on what you utter so choose your words

wisely

Interview Round 2 (Tech & Innovation) – in CUIC

This round revolved around my resume, and it scaled up to 40 to 45 minutes, discussing about
the various projects that I did, which was very interesting and the most challenging project to me
? I was asked to discuss about the tech stack that I used and have come across ,their major
benefits and the cons of the same.

They look into the innovation that you try to achieve through your projects and also how familiar
you were with the overall working of the project, irrespective of which section that you
contributed .

The interviewer  analyzed my project and questioned me about the various other innovations that
can be implemented to scale up the project and also reason out why I  choose a particle
innovation in the field, and how will it help to get a better result? The questions were on How did
I choose a project? What was the ground work that I did  before starting the project ?  what were
the requirements that I gather to start the project? The challenges that I faced during the project
implementation and how did I overcome ?. The interviewer was very keen on understanding the
novelty behind the project.

The interviewer went through almost all of the projects and  languages that I have mentioned of
being knowledge about in my resume.

The interviewer questioned me on, how did I rank my skills in a particular language and my
knowledge about the financials and also about the company recently.

 TIPS :

•  Be accountable of whatever you write in the resume
•  Make sure you mention the best projects that you did in the Resume
•  Go through your projects and tech stack, and also how did you implement the same
•  Be confident with your projects and what are your plans in extending the project in future
•  Be very patient and take time to understand the questions so that you can give the

creative and innovative idea

•  Research about the company in the recent times, and how will you able to fit into the

same?

•  Prepare for some questions that you can ask the interviewer at the end .
•  Use your logical thinking and be confident with your answers, even if it goes wrong, be

wise enough to analyse and assure them to correct it in the future.

HR Round – in CUIC

This round lasted for 15 minutes where the HR questioned me on the grounds of my professional
ethics and they were very simple, like will you move to another company when you are offered
with higher package for the same role ?

The HR was very keen on analyzing my soft skills they include the leadership qualities,
communication skills, and social skills
Some of the basic questions were :

•  About my hobbies. How will I continue it during my work?
•  What is a career and how important it is in my life?
•  What is the difference between a passion and a career?
•  What did I expect from the company and what a company can expect from me?
•  Why did I choose Wells Fargo?
•  Why should Wells Fargo choose me?  (Give me 3 reasons to hire you and 3 to not hire

you)

•  Where do I see myself in the next five years?
•  The HR gave me a scenario to analyzes and give solution based on my leadership

qualities and critical thinking

•  The HR enquired me about my contribution to clubs in the college
•  What is life defined to me?
•  What is happiness and how will I achieve it in my life?

Believe in Yourself ! All The Best !

Padma Prabha M

Overview of the Process:

The overall process consisted of

•  An Offline coding contest in SHL platform (Conducted in IST, CEG)

•

•

Interview Round 1 (Based on Resume, Technical) – in MIT Campus

Interview Round 2 (general, HR) – in MIT Campus

I applied for the following position:

R-389220 Intern Analyst

Online coding contest in SHL platform:

The coding contest was conducted in SHL platform. All the instructions given by

the platform should be followed strictly like turning ON camera, microphones. The test is
of 4 sections namely 2 aptitude sections and 2 coding sections.

The technical rules are you can’t move to the next question without answering

the current question. And once you have moved from a question, you can’t move to that
question. No negative markings.

 The aptitude section:

•  English aptitude.
•  Business Analytics.

The 3rd section consisted of 2 coding questions. (40 mis)

1.  Find the count of non-repeating alphabets in the given string.

2.  You have two arrays with repeating positive integers. The count of the non-

common numbers from the two arrays must be returned.

The 4th section consisted of only one question. (10 mins)

1.  Graph Problem – The Famous Konigsberg Bridge Problem is described in detail
and our task is to find the given graph ends up like the given bridge or can it be
solvable. Then return true if solvable or false if unsolvable.

22 students were shortlisted for interview process of Intern Analyst.

TIPS for Aptitude Sections:

•  Have a basic English Knowledge. Connect the question with each of the 4 given
options. If you don’t know the answer then don’t get stressed, stay calm and
answer according to your conscious, believe on your 1st choice.

•  Logical Thinking is the key along with some maths. For business analytics kind of
questions, read the entire question because the answer will be just hidden in.

•  Practice speed math, just a transferable skill. Trust me it helps a lot.

Interview Round 1: (45-50 mins)

(Based on Resume)

This round was purely based on our Resume. The questions were general. There
were 4 panels (each with one member) 2 panel for Round 1 and 2 panel for Round 2.
Questions and their complexity depends on the panel you go. Some panel members can
test your deep technical knowledge in both rounds (purely depends upon the
interviewers).

Sir named Basker is my interviewer. After going through my resume he started to ask

the following questions:

•  First, are you ready to start the interview?

•

Introduce yourself.

 •  You have mentioned some projects in your resume, do you do these projects on

your own interest or they are just part of your curriculum?

•  Point the project that you have done apart from your curriculum (based on

interest)

Sir started reading about that project.

•  Why you chose hardware over software? (I like to work in embedded systems

and IoT, hence I chose to work on that project)

•  Explain me one project that you are confident on, from your resume.

•  Can you explain me about the workshops you have attended?

•

Is this project a continuation from the learning of the workshop?

•  Explain me the idea of the hackathon and your contribution in it.

•  Explain me the Gaming Projects that you have mentioned here.

•  Shall we move to the Technical Side?

 (Technical):

•

I am going to ask questions from OOPS, DB, DSA. Choose one to start. I chose

OOPS.

•  Why do we need structing?

•  What are the 4 main concepts of OOPS? Explain each in detail.

•  What is a subclass?

•  What is the main purpose for inheritance?

•  Can you give me a real-life scenario where inheritance can be used?

•  What do you know about file handling?

•  Syntax to open, read/write and close a file.

•  File handing – working mechanism.

•  What is the need of error handling?

•  Tell me some techniques of handling error.

DBMS

•  What do you know about DBMS?

•  What is querying?

 •  Do you have experience working with NoSQL?

•  What are the 3 major commands in querying (DDL, DML, DCL)

•  Explain each DDL, DML, DCL in detail.

•  Tell me some of the aggregate functions.

•  Write a Query to display the duplicates in field. (I used GROUP BY and COUNT)

•  What is the need of indexing? (along with B, B+ tress)

•  What is a view, why it is used?

•  What is JOIN? Tell me about all its types and explain each in detail.

•  Do you know about distributed DB?

DSA

•  Tell me the types of DS. (Your answer should be from the base classification like

linear and non-linear, then further explain each branch)

•  Explain the mechanism of Stack and Queue.

•  What do you know about tree DS?

•  Write code to perform inorder – traversal along with drawing the Tree.

•  Explain me about linked lists, its advantages and disadvantages over arrays, stack,

queues.

•  Sir asked me the draw all types of linked lists with max 3 nodes by mentioning

the pointers.

CLOUD COMPUTUING

•  Do you know about Cloud Computing?

•  What are the three major types in it?

•  Name some cloud computing services that you know.

SOFTWARE PROCESS

•  Have you studied software processes?

•  Can you explain about Agile and SDLC?

•  What are the main principles of Agile?

•  Any questions for him?

 TIPS:

•  Take your time to think and phrase your answer, whenever possible.
•  Answer short and crisp with confidence. (if mentioned to elaborate, then take

your time)

•  They weren’t bothered about the syntax. They were fine with that.
•

If you don’t know even a bit of the answer/ unable to recall, be straight to the
point and say “I don’t Know”/ “I can’t recall at this moment”. Because it gives a
hope on you that you are able to express your mind!

•  Since the interview is 45 -50 mins, drink water if you dry out and relax yourself

by breathing.

THEN: Round 2 has been conducted for all the 22 members.

Interview Round – 2: (30 mins)

NOTE: the entire convo went in mixed tamil(major) and english

Sir introduced himself, and told me to introduce myself.

•  Ram sir asked about my studies how it was going.

•  Then he told me that “lets reverse our roles”, which means I want to ask
questions (any question on earth) to him and he will be answering.

•

I felt so nervous and decided to ask the first question that hits my mind.

Question: How did you get into Wells Fargo Sir?

Answer: sir explained me about his previous startups and how he joined Wells Fargo.
He is working for like 15-18 years. (in wells fargo).

 (I started to think so hard for next question – where I realised asking questions is
more difficult than answering them!)

Question: How to improve communication in English sir?

Answer: he asked me “do anybody told that your English is bad or it is awesome?” I
replied NO, then sir said don’t hear what others say about you, you should be the
one who validate yourself then he said communication is like a sport like cycling,
swimming. The more you practice the more you improve.

Question: Sir this swapping of roles is interesting but why are you asking me to ask

questions sir? How to helps you in knowing me? Is there anything so deep in this?

Answer: Sir’s reply is like a life lesson. He said you are just a 4th semester completed
student and you are just 19 right? But I have an experience of 15-18 years. Hence there is
no point in asking technical questions to you. (I am frozen with a smile in my face)

 See I recently learned MongoDB and with what I learnt I written a code. (my code
worked correctly) But when I ask ChatGPT to do that it gave me 5 different answers
which is far better that my code in both time and space complexity.

What do you understand from this he asked. I replied: “Prompting (asking questions

and doubts) …”. He said, yes that’s why I want you to ask questions.

Next: for the next question I started to think…sir said ok it’s enough I will ask now

SIR STARTED ASKING HIS QUESTIONS

•  What tool would you like to learn and develop?
•  How was your round 1?
•  What are you parents?
•  Why you chose finance? What you know about it?
•  What do you think Wells Fargo does to people?
•  Again, any questions for me? (I said NO sir)

MY PREPARATION STRATEGY:

•

I had a thought of preparing for internships in my 4th semester holiday, I even
downloaded all the materials sent by our seniors for the first time, but I couldn’t
make it because I had to spend my holidays in my summer project, mostly family
time, license works etc…

•  When I got so bored I watched Striver’s DSA Series (TUF) on youtube. – This man

is everything in my preparation for CP.

•

 With some knowledge on competitive programming that I have practiced during
my 3rd and 4th semesters I took my first online test for D.E.Shaw.

•  After taking that test, coming out the lab, I was like “are you a paradoxical idiot?
You are getting a good idea to solve that question, but don’t know the syntax of
STL”

•

I clearly remember where I got stuck. Therefore, I decided to not repeat my
mistakes.

•  Next for company Vegrow, I improved somewhat and I am so happy that I didn’t

do any mistakes that I done for D.E.Shaw and but discovered some new mistakes!

•  Then I realised that, there are great inbuilt lib in C++, then why I am not using
them. So, I started learning all of them in the weekends I got (Saturday and
Sunday)

•  Then came Appian, I did my best (on understanding the question and solving)

 •  For Wells Fargo, the online test was on Monday, I got two days for my prep. And
wrote the exam with all my mind, I did not do any mistakes and I done what I
thought right for me.

I got selected for the interview (completed test at 7 pm – started at 5 pm,12th August,
results of interview shortlist came at 11:25 pm, where they mentioned tomorrow is your
interview so be there on MIT campus at 8:00 am)

I got no time to read about the job description or either recall the important points for an
interview (even introducing myself)

I went to MIT. They said interview will be starting at 10 am, then I started googling
important topics for interview, how to give self-introduction and also read some news
after a saturation point. They called me for round 1 at 11:20 am, I came out at 12:30 pm.

After lunch I got my second round, gave my best, then relaxed. (maximum try not to skip
lunch, eating a bit)

SOME ADDITIONAL TIPS:

For Bio-maths students like me who are not exposed to any kinda of

programming languages, tools or softwares during their 11th and 12th grade, there are lots
of online resources to learn coding, competitive programming and DSA.

Find your study method (the method by which you grasp new things quick and

easy) and follow that kind of online resources.

Make good connections with seniors (trust me some seniors are great helpers

they teach you by sharing their experiences).

Hope my words gave you a click about my experience, my prep and general interview
process, I suggest you to have a look on all others experiences so that you will get to
know different flavours of selection processes.

THANK YOU.

 Padma Prabha M (3rd year IT - purple)

RAM KRISHNA H S – INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY

-  CCIBT INTERN IN WELLS FARGO

About the Company:

Wells Fargo is a major American financial services company headquartered in
San Francisco, California, with significant operations across the United States.
Founded in 1852, it offers a wide range of services including banking,
mortgage, investments, and consumer and commercial finance. The company is
known for its extensive branch network and ATMs, making it one of the largest
banks in the U.S. by assets.

About the Role:

CCIBT – Commercial And Corporate Investment Banking Technology

Commercial and Corporate Investment Banking Technology (CCIBT) refers to
the specialized technological infrastructure and solutions that support the
operations of commercial and corporate investment banking services. This
sector involves developing, maintaining, and enhancing software platforms,
trading systems, risk management tools, and data analytics solutions that enable
banks to manage complex financial transactions, investment portfolios, and
corporate client relationships. CCIBT plays a critical role in ensuring the
efficiency, security, and compliance of banking operations, especially in areas
like automated trading, real-time data processing, and regulatory reporting. As
the financial industry increasingly adopts digital transformation, CCIBT is at the
forefront, driving innovation and enabling banks to remain competitive in a
rapidly evolving market.

supports  customers  who  conduct  business  in  the  global  economy.  The  company  employs
approximately  273,000  team  members  and  serves  one  in  three  households  in  the  United  States.
Wells Fargo’s vision is to satisfy its customers’ financial needs and help them succeed financially.

About the Role: Enterprise Global Services (EGS)

The role is part of the Enterprise Global Services (EGS) technology team. EGS is a crucial division
that  enables  global  talent  capabilities  for  Wells  Fargo.  It  supports  over  half  of  the  company's
business  lines  and  staff  functions  across  four  key  areas:  Technology,  Business  Services,  Risk
Services,  and  Knowledge  Services.  EGS  operates  from locations in Hyderabad, Bengaluru, and
Chennai in India, as well as Manila in the Philippines.

As a member of the team, you interact closely with multiple business and technology partners across
various Wells Fargo locations, contributing to a diverse and inclusive organizational culture.

Preparation Strategy

●  Data  Structures  &  Algorithms  (DSA):  The  online  test  included  a  medium-hard  dynamic
programming  problem.  While  the  interview  DSA  question  was  basic  (Height  of  a  Binary
Tree), a strong foundation is essential to clear the initial screening. Practice consistently on
platforms like HackerRank or LeetCode.

●  Core  CS  Fundamentals:  This  is  non-negotiable.  Interviewers  repeatedly  tested  concepts
from  OOPS,  DBMS,  Operating  Systems,  and  Computer  Networks.  Ensure  you  have  a
deep and clear understanding of the fundamentals.

 ●  Full-Stack  Development:  The  technical  interviews  heavily  emphasized  my  development
projects.  Be  prepared  to  discuss  your  tech  stack  (React,  Node.js,  Express),  backend
design, database schema, security policies, and API design in detail.

●  SQL:  The  online  test  featured  a  dedicated  SQL  question,  and  database  concepts  were
discussed in interviews. You must be comfortable writing complex queries involving joins,sub
queries and aggregate functions.

●  Software  Development  Life  Cycle  (SDLC):  This  topic  was  brought  up  in  every  single
interview  round.  It's  critical  to  understand  the  different  phases  of  SDLC  and  be  able  to
explain how you applied them to your projects.

●  Resume Deep Dive: Be prepared to explain every single line on your resume. Interviewers
will  ask  follow-up  questions  about  your  projects,  internships, hackathons, and skills. Know
your story and your work!

Placement Drive Experience

The entire process consisted of four rounds conducted sequentially.

Round 1: Online Test (HackerRank)

This round was purely focused on coding and problem-solving, deviating from the previous pattern of
including aptitude questions.

The test consisted of three problems:

1.  Regex:  Write  a regular expression to find all words where the starting and ending letters

are the same.

2.  Dynamic Programming (The Taxi Driver Problem):

A taxi driver receives ride requests, each with a pickup, drop, and tip. The earning for a ride
is calculated as:
earning=(drop[i]−pickup[i])+tip[i]
The driver can only accept non-overlapping rides (a new ride's pickup time must be greater
than  or  equal  to  the  previous  ride's  drop  time).  Given  the  three  arrays,  find  the  maximum
possible earnings.

3.  SQL Query:

Write  a query to find the total usage time (in seconds) for each task. The usage time is the
sum of the differences between end_dt and start_dt for all its associated processes. Return
each task's hash and its total usage time, ordered descending by the usage time.

Round 2: Virtual Technical Interview

(Duration: 50-55 minutes)

This interview was conducted by Varadan S - a developer who was very approachable. After a brief
self-introduction, he asked follow-up questions on my intro about the hackathons and project expos I
mentioned.

 The interview then progressed as follows:

●  Resume  Deep  Dive:  He  systematically  went  through  my  resume,  focusing  heavily  on  the

development side.

●  Technology Stack:

○  Frontend: In-depth questions on React.
○  Backend:  Questions  on  Node.js,  Express,  backend  design  structures,  security

policies, etc.

○  Database: Questions on DB design and writing SQL queries.

●  SDLC:  He  asked  how  I  incorporated  SDLC  methodologies  in  my  hackathon  and  personal
projects.  The  questions  ranged  from  easy  to  difficult,  designed  to  gauge  the  depth  of  my
knowledge.

●  DSA Check: He asked a simple question: "Find the height of a binary tree,"and asked a few

follow up questions on Trees primarily to check my basic coding fundamentals.

●  Comparison of different data structures and their search complexities.
●  Core Subjects: Covered the basics of OOPS, DBMS, OS, and Networks.
●  Explain OOPs concepts with simple real world examples rather than technical definitions.
●  Project  Discussion:  A  very  detailed  discussion  about my internship and projects. He was
keen  on  understanding  my  specific  contributions  and  assessing  my  teamwork  and
collaboration skills.

●  Behavioral  Question:  The  final  question was, "What are the major new challenges that
you faced in the development of a project?" He specifically asked me to take my time to
structure  a  good  answer.  I  explained  a  challenge  and  connected  my solution to the SDLC
framework, which he appreciated.

I concluded by asking him about his experience transitioning from college to a corporate role. Out of
25 students, around 14-16 were shortlisted for the next round.

Round 3: In-person Technical Interview

(Duration: 40-45 minutes)

This round was even more conversational. The interviewer skipped the self-introduction and started
by asking about my academic journey and curriculum.

●  My academic journey and curriculum: From how I entered CEG to Courses learnt.
●  Favorite  Subject  Deep  Dive:  He  asked  about  my  favorite  subject.  I  said  Computer
Networks, which set the direction for the next 15-20 minutes. I explained everything I knew,
and  he  listened  with  interest  before  asking  follow-up  questions  on  topics  like  TLDs,  SSL,
network security, IPv6, and Private Networks.

●  Favorite  Language Deep Dive: He asked for my favorite programming language and then

tested my knowledge from basic to advanced OOPS concepts within that language.

●  Project Discussion: He asked me to pick two projects from my resume and explain them in

detail. The focus was on potential improvements and the application of SDLC.

●  Agentic AI: While discussing ML and DL topics during the interview, he started asking about

Agentic AI.

 ●  Random CS Fundamentals: He asked several questions on various CS topics to ensure my

fundamentals were strong across the board.

●  Hobbies  &  Personality:  He  noticed  'Photography'  listed  as  a  hobby  on  my  resume  and
asked about it, including technical aspects like the "rule of thirds." This was a great way for
him to gauge my personality and passion outside of work.

This  interview  felt  like  a  friendly, technical conversation. Around 8 students, including myself, were
moved to the final round.

Round 4: Techno-Managerial Interview

(Duration: 15-20 minutes)

This was a final, brief round to assess my overall fit.

It began with a self-introduction.

●
●  He asked some basic questions on OOPS and DSA.
●  The  main  focus  was  on  my  projects  and  the  competitions  I  had  won.  I  selected  a  key

project and explained its architecture, challenges, and outcomes in detail.

●  Projects and the knowledge you possess on the topics of the projects is very important.
●  He completed by asking a few questions on AI, Agentic AI, ML, DL etc.
●

It  ended  with  me  asking  a  few  questions  to  the  interviewer  about  how  the  company  is
transforming due to the growth of AI.

Tips and Tricks

●  Honesty  is the Best Policy: During my first interview, the interviewer was trying to assess
how  honest  I  was  with  my  answers.  Don't  bluff.  If  you  don't  know  something, it's better to
admit it.

●  Know Your Resume: This cannot be stressed enough. Every single word on your resume is
fair  game  for  a  deep-dive  question.  Be  prepared  to  defend  and  elaborate  on  everything
you've written.

●  Master  the  Fundamentals:  Wells  Fargo  places  a high value on strong core CS concepts.

Don't just focus on DSA; revise OOPS, DBMS, OS, and Networks thoroughly.

●  Connect  Projects  to  SDLC:  Simply  building  a  project  is  not  enough.  Be ready to explain
how  you  built  it  using  principles  from  the  Software  Development  Life  Cycle.  This  shows
maturity and a professional mindset.

●  Be  Communicative  and  Confident:  The  interviewers  were  chill  and  appreciated  a
conversational style. Don't just give one-word answers. Explain your thought process clearly
and confidently.

●  Ask  Insightful  Questions:  Prepare  a  few  good  questions  to  ask  your  interviewers.  This

shows your interest in the role and the company.

 Recommended Preparation Resources

●  DSA: Striver's SDE Sheet, LeetCode (Easy/Medium), HackerRank (Problem Solving).
●  Core  Subjects: GeeksforGeeks articles for all core subjects (OS, DBMS, CN), and refer to

standard university textbooks for deep conceptual clarity.

●  SQL: Leetcode’s SQL 50 is excellent for practice.
●  Web  Development:  Official  documentation  for  React,  Node.js,  and  other  frameworks  you

have used. Building and deploying a full-stack project is the best preparation.

WELLS FARGO INTERN PROCESS

The Selection Process consisted of an online programming and aptitude round followed by 3 rounds of interview.

The online round consisted of questions based on logic driven thinking and DSA. These where on Medium level as DSA was not asked in depth. Along with that there were also aptitude questions which had a considerable weightage.

For the Offline Selection Process there were 3 rounds:

1. Technical round (around 30 to 40 min)
2. Technical round 2 (around 30 min)
3. HR round (10 min)

Technical Round 1:

Just gave a brief intro about myself and directly started into question answers. The interviewer asked questions from various domains such as OOPS, OS, DBMS, System Designs etc.
Also He Asked about how each language is different from each other, why do you prefer certain language over other in particular situations and so on. Asked about my projects briefly like what is the project, technologies, purpose etc..,. Also some questions where based on real life scenarios(application based) rather than direct questions from the topics. It went for about 30 to 40 min and the interviewer seemed pleased.

Technical Round 2:

Similar to 1st round with few more questions and also enquired about my IIT degree which I was pursuing simultaneously along with our btech course. Asked about the subjects that were there, what was the difference from here, what was the current subject that went there… what I liked there and what I learnt new etc (both generally and technically). It to went for around 30 min.

HR round:

It was a relaxed round which lasted for 10 – 15 min general convo with the HR interviewer. Asked general questions, Shared about her own experience with the company and so on. Finally for formality asked about why I choose Wells Fargo and the interview process came to a conclusion.

Tips that I recommend (personal view):

1. It is as important to concentrate on your aptitude along with your core as most of the companies have those in online round to filter people. So concentrate on them and train them side by side along with your coding and core subjects.
 2. If the questions are easy try to solve it as fast as u can as it will surely be a filtering criteria.

1. On the other side, if the questions are hard and if you can’t solve the problems, try to imply your logic as companies may do code review and will see your logic and u might have an upper hand on the selection process
2. Discuss with Your seniors/ refer youtube about the company specific interview process on what topic they ask etc and prepare accordingly as the preference change from company to company. For an example I attended D.E.Shaw and their interview process was more coding and DSA oriented whereas in wells it was more subject and fundamentals oriented.
3. Be strong with the fundamentals of Core subjects such as OOPS, OS, DBMS, CN etc.
   Before the day of interview brush up these topics.
4. Know about the company briefly, the role and its need, and prepare questions to ask them before going to the interview.
5. Be relaxed in the interview process, take your time for thinking and answer. They too know we are freshers. Don’t panic in any situation.

All The Best to All of You and hope You all do well

VASUMATHY V – INFORMATION TECHNOLOGY

TECH INTERN - WELLS FARGO (JUNE-JULY 2024)

About The Company

Wells Fargo & Company is an American multinational financial services company with a
significant global presence. It is a systemically important financial institution according to
the Financial Stability Board, and is considered one of the "Big Four Banks" in the United
States, alongside JPMorgan Chase, Bank of America, and Citigroup.

Founded in 1852 and headquartered in San Francisco, Wells Fargo provides banking,
investment and mortgage products and services, as well as consumer and commercial finance,
through more than 7,300 locations, 12,000 ATMs, the internet (wellsfargo.com) and mobile
banking, and has offices in over 40 countries and territories to support customers who
conduct business in the global economy. With approximately 250,000 team members, Wells
Fargo serves one in three households in the United States. Wells Fargo & Company was
ranked No. 41 on Fortune’s 2022 rankings of America’s largest corporations.

Wells Fargo has offices in the United States, India (Chennai, Bengaluru, Hyderabad) and the
Philippines.

About the Role

I worked as a Tech Intern in Wells Fargo, Chennai. The LOB (Line of Business) I worked
under was CT (Consumer Technology). Wells Fargo's consumer technology domain focuses
on enhancing digital banking through mobile and online platforms. It emphasizes
personalized customer experiences, leveraging data analytics, and improving UI/UX design
to ensure seamless and secure interactions, ultimately driving customer satisfaction and
operational efficiency in consumer banking.

Overview of the Process

1)  Online coding round

  Aptitude MCQ questions
  English MCQs
  Statistical MCQs
  2 coding questions

2)  Interview at CUIC

  Round 1 – Technical interview
  Round two – Technical / Critical thinking interview
  Round three – HR interview

 Online Coding Round

The online coding round first started off with the MCQs. It consisted of statistical graph-
based questions (analyzing bar graphs, statistical figures etc.). The MCQs also consisted of
English questions. The questions were of easy to medium level difficulty.

There were two coding questions. One question was easy, the other was of medium level
difficulty.

Tips: Read the question carefully. Once you understand the question completely, use a piece
of paper to write down your approach. If you can think of an optimal approach then well and
good, but if you can’t, then do not waste too much time on searching for an optimal result.
Start coding your approach soon and move on to the next question. Do not waste too much
time on a single question. Resort to brute force if you cannot think of any other approach in
the stipulated time.

Practice your DSA consistently. Leetcode is one of the main sources I used. Everyday, do
atleast 1-2 Leetcode questions. Do a mixture of easy, medium and hard level questions.
Choose your preffered language to code in (C++ or Java or python). I used python and C++,
depending on the question and my comfort level. Another important advice a senior of mine
gave me that I followed, DO NOT get disappointed if you don’t get shortlisted for the
interview round after the online coding round. Keep trying your best and practicing regularly.
Consistency is key.

Interview at CUIC

The interview happened over 3 rounds. All three rounds were offline at CUIC and were one-
on-one.

The first round was a technical interview. The time duration varied, depending on the
interviewer we were assigned to. Mine lasted for around 30 minutes. The interviewer touched
upon all basic topics such as OS, DBMS, OOPs. He gave me a piece of paper and asked me
to solve a couple of coding questions on the spot. He also asked me technical questions. For
example, he asked me the differentiate between the types of polymorphism, when do we use
a linked list and when an array. He also asked me to optimize the code for the question he
gave. Basic OS questions on scheduling were also asked.

The second round of interview was another technical interview. The ones who were selected
after the first round moved on to the second round. In the second round, the interviewer asked
me to briefly explain the projects that I had worked on. After this, he asked me critical
thinking questions. He expected me to answer his questions as creatively as possible.
Honestly speaking, the second round was one of my favourite experiences throughout the
interview process. The interview felt more like a conversation. I took my time to answer the
questions and interreacted freely with the interviewer.

The third round was an HR round. The ones selected from the second round of interview
were directed to the third round. The third round started of with questions about myself, my
hobbies, and my experiences. It was a short round and lasted for 5 minutes.

 Tips: Answer to the point. Be calm and collected. If you do not know the answer to a
question, try to answer the question but if you still do not reach the answer, then you can
politely let the interviewer know that this question is related to a topic you are yet to learn in
college or that your knowledge on this topic is limited.

The interviewer looked at my resume and asked question on the topics that I had listed as my
areas of interest (like data science, OS, DBMS). So kindly include only those topics in your
resume that you are confident in. Be strong in your basic OOPs, DBMS, OS, coding
knowledge.

For the second round, the interviewer asked me to be as creative as possible with my answers.
Hence, I had the liberty to answer creative and innovatively. I suggest that you first take a
moment to understand what the interviewer is asking and then answer accordingly.

I would suggest to work on your communication skills in English. This is key in acing an
interview.

Finally, every interviewer will ask you if you have any questions, at the end of the interview.
I would suggest you to ask some question, either about the company, the role or something
else. Never leave that question unattended.

Overall, the experience was smooth. I learned a lot from the entire interview process. Every
interview is an experience, regardless of the result.
