Worlder Team Interview Experience

Shreyas S – 2022115046

About Worlder Team:

[Worlder TEAM Pte. Ltd.](https://team.worldergroup.com/) is a Singapore based software company dedicated to empowering small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) through digital transformation and global expansion. Operating as part of the [Worlder Group](https://www.worldergroup.com/%22%20%5Ct%20%22_blank), the firm specializes in artificial intelligence, robotics, and machine learning to bridge the gap between traditional business operations and the global market, particularly within the food and beverage industry. Their suite of IT solutions includes products like **Wolonote**, **Improver**, and the **EDIE** inference engine, which provide real-time insights and operational automation. As a remote-first organization, Worlder TEAM maintains a diverse global workforce spanning Singapore, India, Indonesia, Japan, and the United States, fostering a collaborative culture focused on making global business as accessible as "playing an online game".

Role: AI Engineer

* Assist in the development and implementation of AI models and algorithms.
* Work on data preprocessing, curating, and transformation tasks.
* Collaborate with the AI team to design and develop process pipelines to test and evaluate machine learning models.
* Conduct experiments to evaluate and improve the performance of AI models.
* Assist in the integration of AI solutions into existing systems and applications.
* Participate in research and development projects to explore new AI techniques and applications.
* Help prepare technical documentation and presentations.

Selection process:

* Online Test
* Tech Interview

Online Test:

There were multiple domains to choose from, and I selected the AI Engineer domain and proceeded with the corresponding test link. The test was of moderate difficulty, with around 20% coding output MCQs and the remaining questions focused on AI concepts, use cases, algorithms, and scenario-based problems. It lasted approximately 30–40 minutes.

Tech Interview:

The technical interview lasted around 45 minutes and began with a quick discussion of my resume. Since I had mentioned federated learning as one of my projects, I explained it in detail, especially because it aligned well with my preferred domain. This was followed by AI/ML questions that started with fundamental concepts, such as types of learning, and gradually became more advanced. I was assessed across various areas, including ML evaluation metrics, CNNs, real-world scenarios, confusion matrices, regularization, and more.

The interview focused more on my perspective and problem-solving approach rather than textbook definitions, which made the experience particularly engaging. Toward the end, the interviewer invited questions from my side. I asked about the remote work culture and how teams collaborate across different time zones. A week later, the results were announced, and I was shortlisted.

Strategy:

I revised my AI/ML concepts thoroughly, especially since I had studied those subjects in the recent semester, which made them easier to grasp. Alongside that, I practiced DSA as well, not extensively, but I covered the commonly asked problems. I also made it a point to regularly go through my resume so I could speak with clarity and avoid rambling during the interview.

One important lesson I applied was about attitude. In a previous interview, I had been overconfident, and that didn’t work in my favor. This time, I consciously maintained the right balance - being confident yet grounded, while staying strong in both concepts and mindset. The rest, as they say, is history.

Tips:

I’ll make it quick, key suggestions and tips. I know DSA is a maze. I was not one of those HackerRank wizards, yet I made sure I practiced and revised the most common ones, and kept in touch with the job role and inclined towards it. Since mine was AI, I focused more on AI rather than other concepts like DBMS, OS, OOPs, and stuff. So yeah, this should make you confident.

And most importantly, attitude, stay grounded. The person in front of you is highly experienced; you’re just a new guy, so keep that in mind. With all this said, you’re good to go. Be positive, YOU ROCK!!

Fidelity Investments 
Interview Experience 
 
About the Company 
Fidelity Investments is a US-based organization, and one of the largest asset managers 
in the world. It operates a brokerage firm, manages mutual funds, provides fund 
distribution and investment advice, retirement services, index funds, wealth 
management, securities execution and clearance, asset custody, and life insurance. It 
offers brokerage clearing and back office support and software products for financial 
services firms. It is one of the largest providers of 401(k) plans and manages employee 
benefit programs for more than 28,800 businesses. 
 
Role 
2M Summer Internship 
5M Internship & PPO based on performance 
 
Process 
1. Pre-Placement Talk 
2. Online Assessment 
3. Technical Interview 
4. HR Interview 
 
Pre-Placement Talk 
A well-detailed introduction and discussion on the company. Took place online and 
lasted for about an hour. 
 
Online Assessment 
The assessment was for a period of 90 minutes, and consisted of 3 different sections: 
 
1. Coding – 40 minutes: 2 questions were asked. Requires basic DSA knowledge to 
easily get through. Check if a brute force approach passes all the test cases, and go 
for optimized solutions only if it doesn’t. 
2. Aptitude – 20 minutes: The usual aptitude questions. MCQ and numerical type. 
3. Algorithms – 30 minutes: 2 questions about relatively simple algorithms were 
asked. Be thorough with the different algorithms you’ve learned in your Data 
Structures and Design and Analysis of Algorithms classes. 
 
Around 350 candidates took this assessment, and about 50 were shortlisted for the 
interviews. 
 
Technical Interview 
The technical interview took place at CUIC, and lasted for about 60 minutes for me. 
However, on average, it was probably around 30 minutes. 
 
Tips 
1. Be thorough with your projects. Most questions will be from your projects. 
2. Any theoretical question asked will most probably be from a concept or tool that 
you have or could have used in the projects mentioned in your resume, so when 
answering the question, explain how you used the concept or tool in your project 
and how it made your design better. 
3. Have good understanding of the different concepts from core IT areas, mainly 
DBMS, Software Engineering, OS, Web Design, OOPs, Networks, and Algorithms. 
4. There is more importance given to system design and the understanding of core 
OOPs and DBMS concepts. There will be DSA questions, but they will be relatively 
simple, and mostly straightforward such that you can answer them on the spot. 
5. Python, C++, or Java are good choices if any specific coding questions are asked. 
 
Questions Asked to Me 
1. Explain IPC. 
2. What is the Critical Section problem? How do you solve it? 
3. What is Indexing? Explain its types. Why did you use it in your project (I used it for a 
simple project wherein the data was massive - around 500K records).  
4. Construct an ER diagram for the given scenario (it was a pretty simple scenario and I 
only needed 5 different entities to cover the requirements. A good balance of 
normalisation and redundancies is expected). 
5. Implement a stack using an array (my interviewer was familiar with C++, but any 
language of choice was offered, so I went with Python and used lists). 
6. Design the given circuit using HDL (the circuit was drawn in a paper, consisting of 
AND, OR, NAND, and XOR gates; I think very few were asked HDL questions, so don't 
worry much). 
7. Explain the mathematics behind linear regression. 
8. Provide a walkthrough of the OSI model and explain both top-down and bottom-up 
approaches. 
9. Explain the difference between TCP and UDP. 
10. How did you use Inheritance in your project (it was a simple OOPs-focused 
Supermarket System)? 
11. Some HTML tag questions, Agile vs Waterfall, and other pretty straightforward 
questions were asked.  
 
As you can see, the questions were pretty straightforward, but you have to convince the 
interviewer that you know what you're talking about. So just be strong with your basics. 
Thorough foundational knowledge is pretty much enough. 
HR Interview 
Around 30 candidates were shortlisted from the 50 who took the technical interview. My 
HR round lasted for about 20 minutes, which was the overall average as well. 
 
Tips 
1. Research the company.  
2. Research yourself. Don't be confident that you can answer anything about yourself. 
Sometimes, on the spot, you'll say something about yourself that is not true, and 
when a follow-up is asked, you'll put yourself in a tight spot. So, understand your 
strengths and weaknesses and focus primarily on your strengths whenever you're 
introducing yourself.  
3. Don't be too technical here. You can highlight that as your strength, sure, but this 
round is to assess your suitability in a workplace environment, so look for other 
strengths like communication, collaboration, etc. 
4. Fit the vibes of the interviewer - if he/she is friendly, feel free to have a friendly but 
respectful talk. If he/she is straight down to business, be crisp yet comprehensive 
with your answers. 
5. Some HR interviews may consist of technical questions, so be ready for that as well 
and don't be too shocked. 
 
Questions Asked to Me 
1. Introduce yourself. 
2. Suggest me a phone that I could buy, given I have an infinite budget (I said that I was 
a phone fanatic in my introduction, hence the follow-up). 
3. Why would you recommend this phone? 
4. What do you think about AI? How much do you use it? 
5. Have you read this book about AI? (I said that I loved reading) 
6. What kind of books do you read? 
7. Summarise your favourite book to me in one minute and convince me to read it. 
8. What is one quality of yours that you think would be amazing for the workplace in 
Fidelity? 
 
My interviewer was a pretty chill guy, and allowed me to ask him a couple of questions 
as well at the end, and he really liked the questions that I asked him, because they were 
aimed at his personal experiences and how I could be ready for the company. 
 
And that’s it! 12 candidates were selected after the HR round, and I was one of them. If 
you have any specific questions, feel free to reach out to me! 
 
Yuvaraj K

About the role:

Software Development Engineer – Tech at ZoomRx focuses on developing high-
performance  web  applications  and  APIs.  The  role  involves  collaborating  with
senior engineers to execute the software development lifecycle (SDLC), writing
efficient  code,  and  participating  in  code  reviews.  Key  tasks  include  debugging
software,  documenting  processes,  and  working  with  stakeholders  to  gather
requirements and design user-friendly interfaces. This position offers the chance
to  work  with  innovative  technologies  and  contribute  to  impactful  solutions
aligned with the company’s vision.

Overview of the process:

  Coding test (Online)
  Technical Interview (Online)
  HR Interview (Online)

 Online coding test:
Duration: 1 hr 30 mins

It  was  informed  that  the  online  test  would  be  conducted  in  the  morning,
followed by the technical interview in the afternoon on the same day.

The  online  coding  test  was  conducted  on  iMocha.  There  were  2  components:

1.Multiple Choice Question (Time limit: 30 mins)
2.Coding Problems (Time limit: 1 hr)

The 25 MCQs included 20 intermediate-level CS questions (DBMS, OOPS, DSA)
and 5 aptitude questions.

2nd section consisted of 2 coding problems. Both were medium level questions.

1)https://leetcode.com/problems/best-time-to-buy-and-sell-stock-
ii/description/

2) A 2-D array type problem (little harder than the previous qn).

I completed the test before 25 mins and got selected for the technical interview.

Technical Interview:
Duration: 2 hrs

For this round, 23 students were shortlisted: 10 in the first batch and 13 in the
second batch. I was in the 2nd batch.

The interview was conducted via Microsoft Teams, where two panel members
interviewed me. Questions included are

  Introduce about yourself
  How  was  your  coding  round?  [They  shared  my  code  of  the  2-D  array
problem and asked me to explain each line of it. Since it was on the same
day I done it easily.]

  Gave me 2 coding problems. First one was to find the sum of 2 octal strings
as  an  octal  string.  I  told  the  approach.  Then  the  2nd  question  is
https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/print-all-pairs-with-given-
sum/#expected-approach-using-hash-map-on-time-and-on-space.
I
explained  my  approach  before  coding  and  then  started  coding  using
HashMap with time complexity of O(n^2). They then asked me to optimize

 it. I told the solution with O(n) time complexity; didn’t asked me to code
it.

  Explain your recent project clearly
  How much would you rate yourself for OOPS, OS, DBMS and DSA?
  Questions in all four of them like

  OOPS – 4 main concepts’ short explanation, a real-life application

where 4 of them is used altogether

  OS – Deadlocks
  DBMS – Normalization, 2 SQL questions(One to write query using

JOINS, the other in oral like what will you use? - GROUPBY)

  Data Structures – List all DS you know, difference between Array and
ArrayList, Types of trees, BST, BFS and DFS, given an example what
DS will you use? (stack), real-life application of LinkedList (gave me
clues to find), DS you used in any of your projects

  How will you design an Elevator system using OOP principles? [Discussed
various  use-cases,  assumptions,  requirements,  class  diagrams  and
interfaces involved].

  Any questions?
  What  all  companies  you  attended  interview  before  and  few  of  its

experiences?

I felt like this two-hour session covered a wide range of topics. I tried answering
most of the questions and even when I struggled in few they helped me. It was
like a friendly conversation.

9 students were shortlisted for the next round.

HR Interview:
Duration: 1 hr

It was also a teams meet. This round consisted of both Technical questions and
HR  questions.  For  some  candidates,  the  round  focused  primarily  on  HR
questions.

  The  interview  began  with  questions  about  my  native  place  and  current

semester.

  Introduce about yourself
  Explain your recent project clearly

   What tech stacks you used? What new things have you learnt from your

project?

  Why  you  used MongoDB? Advantage of using MongoDB than  relational

DB. Difference between SQL and NoSQL.

  What are some new trending technologies? What is GPT?
  In what all ways will you learn any new skill?
  What all  happens from entering a  URL (eg:  https://www.udemy.com) in

the browser to get a page? What all protocols used?

  Difference between HTTP and HTTPS
  What is public key cryptography? List some algorithms used in it. Hashing?
  Why you have taken IT?
  One thing that you are pride of yourself? What are the steps taken by you

for that?

  If you want to go back and change something in your life, what will it be?
  One thing that you wish to improve yourself.
  Tell me about your family.
  Why you want to join ZoomRx?
  Any questions?
  What all companies you attended interview before? He asked about the
rounds  I  had  undergone,  the  concepts  discussed  during  the  group
discussion of my previous interview, my points, and the reason that I think
for my rejections in those.

Finally 3 candidates were selected and I’m one among them.

Tricks that I followed:

  Paying attention during the PPT helped me frame questions to ask during

the interview.

  I completed my online test as soon as possible as mentioned earlier.
  I  researched  the  company  before  the  tests  and  referred  to  previous
interview experiences on GFG. From that I came to know that DP problems
were not much asked in ZoomRx. So, I prepared based on that.

  Revised  the  topics  in  DBMS,  SQL,  OS,  OOPS  and  DSA.  My  previous

interview experiences helped me a lot.

 Preparation Strategy:

  I began preparing for placements during my 6th-semester holidays, which

I would not recommend.

  I  used  senior’s  preparation  materials  for  some  concepts  and  practiced

coding in HackerRank and LeetCode.

  Before  any  company’s  interview  I  used  to  refer  senior’s  placement
experience  for  that  company  (if  any)  or  in  GFG,  Glassdoor  or  other
websites.

  Even after I faced many rejections, I didn’t lose hope and soon made me

ready taking only the things to improve myself to get selected.

Suggestions:

  Start preparing at least before your internships in the 3rd year, and most
importantly, take the internships seriously and aim to get selected. That
makes  you  feel  less  burden  in  the  7th  semester.  Even  if  you  don’t  get
selected, the preparation will definitely help you.

  Only include what you know in your resume.
  Believe in yourself.. Don’t ever lose hope and Always be confident!
